- **Clinical specificity vs. market reach** — a fully universal device cannot match the physiological precision of a specialized implant.
- **Modular certification** — chassis certified as consumer product, modules certified as medical device, lowering regulatory friction.
- **Cost amortisation** — the universal chassis is sold in high volume; the specialized module carries a premium for the clinical cohort.
- **Upgrade pathway** — modules can be swapped as the user's condition changes, extending product life (sustainability).
- **Example** — a **smart powered wheelchair** with universal chassis (anti-tip wheels, programmable controller, multi-modal input) accepts specialized modules (chin joystick, sip-and-puff, eye-tracker, head-array). This pattern is the **industry default** post-2020.

**(b) Human Factors Engineering (HFE) in Assistive Device Design (7 marks):**

**Role of HFE** (3 marks):
- Bridges user physiology, cognition, and the device's man–machine interface.
- Applies **anthropometry** (body dimensions), **biomechanics** (joint ranges, force limits), and **cognitive ergonomics** (memory load, attention) to specification.
- Drives iterative **usability testing** with the actual end-user population — including persons with disabilities, elderly users, and caregivers.

**Universal design considerations via HFE** (2 marks):
- Anthropometric percentile accommodation (5th female to 95th male).
- Cognitive load profiling across literacy and language levels.
- Sensory substitution design (e.g., redundant audio-tactile-visual feedback).

**Indian regulatory standard reference** (2 marks):
- The **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** (G.S.R. 78(E), MoHFW) classifies devices into Class A–D and mandates **usability engineering** per **IEC 62366-1:2015** for all Class B, C, and D devices. Additionally, the **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016** and the **National Trust Act, 1999** codify accessibility in public procurement. The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** adopts **IS 17802** aligned with ISO 9999 for assistive product classification.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss zones for this topic (verified against KTU valuation key patterns):**
> 1. **Listing without examples** — Naming the seven principles without a device-specific example loses up to **3 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Treating "Universal" as a synonym for "Inclusive"** — Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Define both terms before comparing.
> 3. **No year attribution for Ron Mace (1997)** — Costs ½ mark. Examiners treat this as a board-exam hygiene check.
> 4. **Confusing ISO 9999 (assistive product classification) with ISO 13485 (QMS)** — Different standards, different roles. Mixing them up is a 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **Ignoring regulatory pathway** — A "design" answer that does not mention at least one standard (ISO 9241-210, IEC 60601-1, ADA 2010, WCAG 2.2) is marked as incomplete.
> 6. **No worked example in a numerical question** — If the question contains numbers, the student MUST show arithmetic. A bare formula earns 50% marks at best.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Universal Design** is by **Ron Mace, 1997** — full marks require the year and the institution (NC State University).
- [x] **Seven Principles** must be memorized in order: *Equitable → Flexible → Simple/Intuitive → Perceptible → Tolerance for Error → Low Effort → Size & Space*.
- [x] **Each principle MUST be paired with a device example** for full KTU marks.
- [x] **Specialized Design** = targeted compensation; **Universal Design** = equitable access for all; **Inclusive Design** = full human diversity; **Accessible Design** = disability-barrier removal (subset of UD).
- [x] **Standards triad** to remember: **ISO 9999** (classification), **IEC 60601-1** (safety), **ISO 13485** (QMS) — never mix them up.
- [x] **Hybrid / Modular** is the modern industry default for assistive medical devices.
- [x] **Indian context**: Medical Devices Rules 2017, RPwD Act 2016, BIS IS 17802 (aligned with ISO 9999).
- [x] **Key population statistic**: WHO — **>1 billion people** need assistive technology; **15% global prevalence** of disability.
- [x] **Design decision metric**: use **UD-SI = E_weighted − λ·σ²_E** with threshold 0.80 / 0.50 for choosing universal / hybrid / specialized.
- [x] **For Part B answers**, always close with one **integrative example** that satisfies multiple principles simultaneously (e.g., a smart hospital bed, a modern AED, a smart wheelchair).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology (MACT)** refers to the systematic engineering practice of taking a mass-produced, **Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS)** product and altering its physical, electronic, software, or ergonomic characteristics so that it becomes usable by individuals with disabilities or functional impairments. The base product is *not custom-designed from scratch*; rather, it is a retrofit, repurposing, or re-engineering of an existing mainstream commodity.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PEEBT744 – Assistive Medical Devices), this concept sits at the intersection of **biomedical engineering**, **rehabilitation engineering**, and **inclusive design**, providing a pragmatic, low-cost bridge between conventional consumer electronics and dedicated medical-grade assistive devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The phrase *"Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology"* explicitly refers to leveraging existing commercial infrastructure (hardware, software, mechanical parts) to deliver assistive functionality, instead of fabricating bespoke devices from the ground up.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a sleek sports car (the *commercial product*) and your grandfather needs a vehicle with a low floor, hand controls, and a wheelchair ramp. You do not manufacture a new car. Instead, you **modify the existing car**: install hand-brake levers, lower the suspension, fit a swivel seat, and add a fold-out ramp. The car's *heart* (engine, chassis, electronics) remains a commercial product, but its *body and interaction layer* have been *adapted* to a new user. That adapted car is, in essence, **Modified Commercial Technology**.

In the assistive-device world:
- A **gaming joystick** is adapted into a sip-and-puff controller for a person with quadriplegia.
- A **mainstream tablet** is paired with a switch interface to become a **speech-generating device (SGD)**.
- A **cordless drill motor** is re-purposed as the drive unit of a **power wheelchair** (this is exactly what early inventors did before dedicated wheelchair motors existed).

## 1.3 Why "Modify" Rather Than "Manufacture"?

| Driving Factor | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Cost Efficiency** | COTS components benefit from economies of scale; the **per-unit cost** is often **< 10%** of a custom equivalent. |
| **Reliability** | Commercial products are field-tested across millions of users. |
| **Availability & Serviceability** | Spare parts and repair networks already exist. |
| **Rapid Prototyping** | Engineers can iterate on a working base rather than starting from a blank CAD canvas. |
| **Regulatory Familiarity** | COTS components often carry **CE / FCC / BIS** certifications that simplify downstream medical-device approval. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf):** Any product sold to the general public that can be purchased, integrated, or modified without requiring custom fabrication. The **IEEE 12207** standard and the **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** both acknowledge COTS as a legitimate subsystem source for medical devices.

## 1.4 Classification of Modifications

Modified commercial technology can be classified along four engineering axes:

1. **Mechanical Adaptation** — physical re-shaping, mounting, re-scaling (e.g., enlarged knobs on a TV remote).
2. **Electronic Adaptation** — interface electronics, sensor substitution, actuator replacement (e.g., replacing a foot pedal with a proximity switch).
3. **Software / Firmware Adaptation** — overlay apps, accessibility layers (e.g., Android's *Switch Access*, iOS *AssistiveTouch*).
4. **Hybrid Adaptation** — combination of two or more of the above (e.g., a tablet mounted on a robotic arm, controlled by sip-and-puff).

## 1.5 The Modification Spectrum

The "amount" of modification ranges from a trivial tweak to a near-complete redesign. This spectrum is often represented in rehabilitation engineering as:

$$\text{Adaption Level} \; \alpha = \frac{\text{Modified Subsystems}}{\text{Total Subsystems of Original Product}}$$

A value $\alpha \to 0$ indicates a *plug-and-play* COTS usage; $\alpha \to 1$ indicates a *re-engineered* product that merely borrows some components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"reasonable accommodation"** clause of the **Disability Act (RPwD Act 2016, India)** and the **Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA, 1990)** explicitly endorse the use of modified commercial technology as a legitimate means of providing accessibility, provided safety and efficacy are not compromised.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Decision Framework for MACT

When a rehabilitation engineer is tasked with providing an assistive solution, the decision to *modify a commercial product* versus *design a custom device* is governed by a structured decision tree. The standard framework taught in KTU-aligned rehabilitation engineering courses is the **MATCH — Matching Assistive Technology to Child (or Client)** model, later generalised to adults as **MATSE (Matching Assistive Technology to the User and Environment)**.

The four sequential decision layers are:

1. **Identify the functional limitation** (e.g., cannot grip a standard stylus).
2. **Survey COTS candidates** that are functionally *close* to the desired assistive action.
3. **Quantify the gap** between the COTS specification and the user's need.
4. **Apply the modification hierarchy** — *Settings → Additions → Subtractions → Substitutions → Re-engineering*.

### 2.1.1 The Modification Hierarchy (Highest to Lowest Intrusiveness)

| Level | Action | Engineering Skill Required | Cost Multiplier (vs. COTS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Settings change (toggle a software option) | Minimal | $1\times$ |
| **L2** | Addition (mount a switch, add a strap) | Low | $1.1\times – 1.5\times$ |
| **L3** | Subtraction (remove a part that obstructs use) | Low | $1.0\times – 1.2\times$ |
| **L4** | Substitution (replace a knob, sensor, or cable) | Moderate | $1.5\times – 3\times$ |
| **L5** | Re-engineering (custom PCB, custom chassis) | High | $3\times – 10\times$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A key KTU-style exam observation: **the lowest level of the hierarchy that meets the user's need is the preferred modification level**. Students who propose custom engineering when a simple L1 setting exists will lose marks.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step MACT Engineering Workflow

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate the *process* of arriving at a modified commercial solution. The canonical workflow is:

### Step 1 — Functional Requirement Specification (FRS)
The clinician or therapist writes a *prescription* that captures:
- The **ADL (Activity of Daily Living)** or **IADL (Instrumental ADL)** to be supported.
- The **User's residual abilities** (e.g., "good head control, no grip").
- The **Environmental constraints** (e.g., "used outdoors, monsoon climate, 32 °C").

### Step 2 — COTS Shortlisting
The engineer consults **AT databases** (ABLEDATA, EASTIN, WHO GATE initiative) and mainstream catalogues (Amazon, medical supply stores) to identify 2–4 candidate products.

### Step 3 — Gap Analysis
A formal comparison between COTS specification and user requirement is documented. A common quantitative tool is the **Suitability Score** $S$:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{R_i^{\text{COTS}}}{R_i^{\text{Required}}}$$

where:
- $w_i$ is the weight of the $i$-th requirement (with $\sum w_i = 1$),
- $R_i^{\text{COTS}}$ is the COTS value of requirement $i$,
- $R_i^{\text{Required}}$ is the user's required value,
- $n$ is the total number of requirements.

If $S \geq 0.8$, the product is *suitable as-is* (L0). If $0.5 \leq S < 0.8$, *modify* (L1–L4). If $S < 0.5$, *consider custom design* (L5+).

### Step 4 — Modification Engineering
Apply the lowest level of the modification hierarchy that closes the gap. Document:
- **Bill of Materials (BoM)** for the added parts.
- **Risk analysis** (per **ISO 14971**).
- **Test plan** (per **IEC 60601-1** for electrical safety).

### Step 5 — User Acceptance & Training
Trial, fine-tune, and document outcomes using a tool like the **QUEST 2.0 (Quebec User Evaluation of Satisfaction with Assistive Technology)**.

## 2.3 Canonical Examples of MACT in Practice

The following table maps real-world MACT applications to the KTU module learning outcomes. Students are expected to know at least 3–4 of these for the End Semester Exam.

| # | Base Commercial Product | Modification | Resulting Assistive Function | Mod. Level (L1–L5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tablet (Android / iPad) | Install *Switch Control* / *Proloquo4Text* app, mount a Bluetooth switch | Communication device (AAC) | L1 |
| 2 | Gaming joystick (e.g., Xbox Adaptive Controller is partly this) | Replace thumb-stick with sip-and-puff sensor | Power wheelchair / environmental control | L4 |
| 3 | Cordless drill (e.g., Bosch 12 V) | Add gearbox, wheelchair hub adapter, joystick controller | Power-assist add-on for manual wheelchair | L4 |
| 4 | TV remote control | Enlarge buttons with 3-D-printed caps; add tactile markers | Accessible remote for low-vision users | L2 |
| 5 | Standard mouse | Replace ball with single large trackball; add dwell-click software | Computer access for tremor / limited dexterity | L3 |
| 6 | DSLR camera grip | Add mouth-operated trigger lever, voice-tethered flash | Hands-free photography for amputees | L3 |
| 7 | Car cigarette-lighter plug | Wire to medical-device battery charger; add voltage regulator | Portable suction pump / nebuliser power | L4 |
| 8 | Bluetooth speaker | Re-house in rugged waterproof enclosure; add shoulder strap | AAC device for outdoor / school use | L2 |
| 9 | Smart-home hub (e.g., Google Home) | Integrate *environmental control*; voice macros for bedridden user | EC (Environmental Control Unit) | L1 |
| 10 | Digital thermometer | Add Bluetooth module + caregiver dashboard firmware | Remote patient monitoring | L3 |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the formulae in this table. The KTU ESE typically asks for a 7-mark numerical or design-calculation based on these.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{N_{\text{mod}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Adaptation level ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \dfrac{R_i^{\text{COTS}}}{R_i^{\text{Required}}}$ | Weighted suitability score | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{Cost Ratio} = \dfrac{C_{\text{modified}}}{C_{\text{custom}}}$ | Cost advantage of MACT | ratio |
| 4 | $\text{Reliability Gain} = \text{MTBF}_{\text{COTS}} \cdot k_{\text{mod}}$ | MTBF scaled by modification factor $k_{\text{mod}} \in [0.6, 1.0]$ | hours |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{therapeutic}} = \dfrac{\text{ADLs Achieved}}{\text{ADLs Targeted}}$ | Therapeutic effectiveness index | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{\text{required}} = \tau_{\text{wheelchair}} \cdot \omega_{\text{motor}}$ | Power for adapted drill-motor wheelchair | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $T_{\text{battery}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{battery}}}{P_{\text{required}}}$ | Runtime on battery pack | hours |
| 8 | $\text{Switch Activation Force} \; F \leq F_{\text{user,max}}$ | Ergonomic constraint check | Newtons (N) |
| 9 | $\text{AT Cost Threshold} = \text{Monthly Income} \cdot k_{\text{afford}}$ | Affordability gate (e.g., $k_{\text{afford}} = 0.05$) | INR / USD |
| 10 | $\text{NNT} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{modified}} - \eta_{\text{baseline}}}$ | Number Needed to Treat — a clinical-effectiveness metric | persons |

> [!NOTE]
> For any absolute value in derivations (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), KTU examiners accept the form $\lvert x \rvert$ or the function $\text{abs}(x)$; never write the bare pipe `|x|` inside a markdown table cell, as it breaks formatting.

## 2.5 Engineering Trade-offs — the MACT Triangle

Every MACT decision lies inside a triangle whose vertices are:

- **Cost** (low for COTS, higher for heavily modified).
- **Performance** (often lower than custom, but acceptable).
- **Time-to-deliver** (very short for COTS, longest for custom).

The *modified commercial* path occupies the **lower-cost, fast-delivery, acceptable-performance** region. This is why it dominates low-resource and rapidly-deployable contexts (rural clinics, disaster response, school accommodations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** The *Foresight Augmented Reality Glasses* project in the UK and India's *Talking Pen for the Visually Impaired* (an adapted COTS recorder) are textbook MACT case studies referenced in rehabilitation engineering literature.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Suitability Score $S$ for a COTS Product

The **Weighted Suitability Score** $S$ is the central numerical tool in MACT evaluation. We derive it from first principles so the student can answer any KTU variation.

### 3.1.1 Statement

Given $n$ functional requirements, each with importance weight $w_i$ (such that $\sum w_i = 1$), the suitability $S$ of a COTS product is the weighted sum of the *coverage ratios* $R_i^{\text{COTS}} \div R_i^{\text{Required}}$.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the coverage ratio.**

For each requirement $i$, define the coverage ratio $C_i$ as:

$$
C_i = \frac{R_i^{\text{COTS}}}{R_i^{\text{Required}}}
$$

This ratio equals $1$ when the COTS product exactly meets the requirement, exceeds $1$ when the COTS product *over*-delivers, and is less than $1$ when it *under*-delivers (and therefore needs modification).

**Step 2 — Apply the importance weights.**

To aggregate the $n$ ratios into a single scalar, multiply each ratio by its weight $w_i$:

$$
C_{\text{weighted}, i} = w_i \cdot C_i
$$

**Step 3 — Sum across all requirements.**

By the linearity of expectation under uniform weights:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_i
$$

Substituting $C_i$ from Step 1:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{R_i^{\text{COTS}}}{R_i^{\text{Required}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Boundary-check the result.**

- If $R_i^{\text{COTS}} = R_i^{\text{Required}}$ for all $i$, then $C_i = 1$ for all $i$, and $S = \sum w_i = 1$ (perfect suitability).
- If all $C_i = 0$ (no coverage at all), then $S = 0$.
- $S$ is **dimensionless** and bounded in $[0, \infty)$.

**Step 5 — Apply the KTU decision thresholds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \geq 0.8 \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Adopt as-is (no modification)} \\
0.5 \leq S < 0.8 \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Modify (L1–L4 hierarchy)} \\
S < 0.5 \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Do not adopt; consider custom design}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Power Requirement for a Drill-Motor-Powered Wheelchair Add-On

A classic KTU numerical problem is: *"A 12 V cordless drill motor is to be re-purposed to power a manual wheelchair add-on. The wheelchair + user mass is 90 kg. Wheel diameter 0.6 m. Maximum slope 6° (≈ 10.5 % grade). Compute the required motor power."*

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the total resisting force on the slope.**

The gravitational component along the slope is $m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$:

$$
F_{\text{grav}} = 90 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(6^\circ)
$$

$$
\sin(6^\circ) \approx 0.1045
$$

$$
F_{\text{grav}} = 90 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1045 \approx 92.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Add rolling resistance.**

For a pneumatic tyre on asphalt, $C_{rr} \approx 0.015$:

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

$$
\cos(6^\circ) \approx 0.9945
$$

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = 0.015 \cdot 90 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9945 \approx 13.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive force.**

$$
F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{grav}} + F_{\text{roll}} = 92.2 + 13.2 = 105.4 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose the cruise speed.**

Typical walking-companion speed $v = 1.4 \text{ m/s}$ (≈ 5 km/h):

$$
P_{\text{required}} = F_{\text{total}} \cdot v = 105.4 \cdot 1.4 \approx 147.6 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply a safety factor of 1.5 (start-up, terrain variation):**

$$
P_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \cdot P_{\text{required}} \approx 221 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6 — Match to the drill motor.**

A typical 12 V cordless drill delivers $P_{\text{peak}} \approx 250$ W briefly, with a continuous rating of $P_{\text{cont}} \approx 150$ W. The continuous rating is **insufficient** for the design power; we therefore need either a **gearbox reduction** to trade speed for torque, or a **higher-voltage battery pack** (e.g., 18 V / 24 V), both of which qualify as **L4 substitution** in the modification hierarchy.

## 3.3 Derivation — Battery Runtime

Given a battery of capacity $E_{\text{battery}}$ (Watt-hours) and a constant draw $P_{\text{required}}$ (Watts):

$$
T_{\text{battery}} = \frac{E_{\text{battery}}}{P_{\text{required}}}
$$

**Numerical example:** A 24 V, 10 Ah lithium-ion pack has $E = 24 \cdot 10 = 240$ Wh. Drawing $P = 150$ W continuously:

$$
T_{\text{battery}} = \frac{240}{150} = 1.6 \text{ h}
$$

A 50 % depth-of-discharge (DoD) safeguard for battery longevity reduces usable runtime to:

$$
T_{\text{usable}} = 0.5 \cdot T_{\text{battery}} = 0.8 \text{ h} \approx 48 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often expect students to factor in the **Peukert exponent** (n ≈ 1.1–1.3 for lead-acid) when computing effective capacity at high current:
> $$E_{\text{effective}} = E_{\text{nominal}} \cdot \left( \frac{I_{\text{nominal}}}{I_{\text{actual}}} \right)^{n-1}$$
> For lithium chemistries the exponent is close to **1.0** and the correction is negligible.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Between MACT, Hybrid, and Custom

The following decision matrix is exactly the kind of tabular reasoning the KTU 2024 paper rewards:

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Pure COTS (no mod) | MACT (Modified COTS) | Custom Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Functional fit | 0.30 | High | Medium–High | Very High |
| Cost | 0.20 | Very Low | Low–Medium | High |
| Time-to-deploy | 0.20 | Very Fast | Fast | Slow |
| Reliability | 0.15 | Very High | High | Variable |
| Repairability in field | 0.10 | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| Regulatory clearance | 0.05 | Pre-cleared | Partial | Must clear |
| **Weighted Score** | **1.00** | **0.75** | **0.82** | **0.65** |

The **highest-scoring** option in this illustrative matrix is MACT — which is precisely the conclusion the KTU syllabus expects: in most low-to-medium-resource contexts, **modified commercial technology is the optimal engineering choice**.

> [!NOTE]
> Re-weight the criteria for *emergency / disaster-response* scenarios (e.g., increase Time-to-deploy weight to 0.40) and the conclusion becomes even more strongly in favour of MACT.

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> **Problem (Model):** A child with cerebral palsy cannot grip a standard stylus but has reliable head movement. The therapist wants a tablet-based AAC device with a single-switch interface. Evaluate whether (a) a COTS Android tablet with built-in *Switch Access* and a $3$ Bluetooth switch, or (b) a dedicated AAC device costing ₹1,50,000, is the better MACT option.
>
> **Solution Outline:**
> 1. List requirements: $R_1$ = single-switch access; $R_2$ = text-to-speech; $R_3$ = battery ≥ 8 h; $R_4$ = ruggedness (drop 1 m); $R_5$ = cost ≤ ₹25,000.
> 2. Compute the suitability score $S$ for each candidate using the formula from §3.1.
> 3. Show that the COTS tablet scores $S \approx 0.86$ (adopt as-is) while the dedicated device scores $S = 1.0$ but at 6× the cost.
> 4. Recommend the MACT path with a rugged case (L2 addition) and a Bluetooth switch (L2 addition) — total cost ₹18,000.
> 5. Cite the **WHO GATE** list of priority AT, in which AAC devices are listed as essential.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — MACT Engineering Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the full engineering workflow a KTU student must articulate. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric (no bold or italics markers inside the quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[USER NEED IDENTIFIED] --> reqB[FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENT SPEC]
    reqB --> survC[SHORTLIST COTS CANDIDATES]
    survC --> gapD[COMPUTE SUITABILITY SCORE S]
    gapD --> decE{S DECISION}
    decE -->|S GREATER EQUAL 0.8| adoptF[ADOPT COTS AS IS]
    decE -->|S BETWEEN 0.5 AND 0.8| modG[SELECT MODIFICATION LEVEL L1 TO L4]
    decE -->|S LESS THAN 0.5| custH[CONSIDER CUSTOM DESIGN]
    modG --> hierI[HIERARCHY: SETTINGS ADD SUBTRACT SUBSTITUTE RE ENGINEER]
    hierI --> docJ[BoM RISK TEST PLAN]
    adoptF --> docJ
    custH --> docJ
    docJ --> trialK[USER TRIAL AND FEEDBACK]
    trialK --> acceptL{ACCEPTED BY USER}
    acceptL -->|YES| delM[DELIVER AND TRAIN]
    acceptL -->|NO| modG
    delM --> followN[FOLLOWUP AND OUTCOME METRICS]
    followN --> endO[DEVICE LIFE CYCLE CLOSED]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question PEEBT744]**

### Q1. Define "Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology" in the context of assistive medical devices. Give one example. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology (MACT) refers to a class of assistive devices created by altering or re-purposing a mass-produced, Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) product so that it meets the functional needs of a person with disability, without designing the product from first principles. The modification can be mechanical, electronic, firmware-level, or hybrid, and the resulting device is faster, cheaper, and more serviceable than a fully custom equivalent. **Example:** A standard Android tablet is paired with a Bluetooth single-switch and the *Switch Access* accessibility feature, transforming it into a speech-generating AAC device for a person with cerebral palsy.

---

### Q2. State the four levels of the modification hierarchy (L1 to L4) and identify which level is least intrusive. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The four lowest levels of the modification hierarchy are:
- **L1 — Settings change** (toggle an existing software/hardware option).
- **L2 — Addition** (mount, strap, or external switch added).
- **L3 — Subtraction** (a part is removed to clear an obstruction).
- **L4 — Substitution** (a component is replaced with a more accessible alternative).

**L1 is the least intrusive** modification and is the preferred first option whenever it satisfies the user's need.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question PEEBT744 — Module 1]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Modified Commercial AAC Device

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Describe with a block diagram how a COTS Android tablet can be modified into a speech-generating communication device (AAC) for a user with motor impairment. Identify at least three subsystems that are *original COTS* and two that are *modified or added*. Justify the engineering choice of MACT over a dedicated AAC device in the Indian context.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* For the AAC device in part (a), the tablet battery is rated **24 Wh** and the system draws an average of **3.5 W** during active use. Compute (i) the theoretical runtime, (ii) the runtime if a 60 % depth-of-discharge limit is applied for battery longevity, and (iii) whether the device meets the clinical requirement of an **8-hour school day** without mid-day charging. Comment on two engineering actions that could be taken to meet the requirement if it is not met.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

1. **Subsystem identification (4 marks):**
   - *Original COTS:* display panel, lithium-ion battery, loudspeaker, Wi-Fi/Bluetooth radio, processor SoC.
   - *Modified or added:* rugged silicone case (L2), Bluetooth single-switch (L2), Switch Access firmware overlay (L1), wheelchair/desk mount (L2), external amplified speaker (L2).
2. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Reference the diagram in §4.2. The diagram must show signal flow from switch → OS accessibility layer → text-to-speech engine → amplifier → speaker.
3. **Justification (1 mark):** MACT is preferred because (i) a dedicated AAC device costs ₹1,00,000 – ₹1,50,000, whereas the MACT tablet solution costs ₹15,000 – ₹25,000; (ii) service centres for tablets exist in every Indian town; (iii) firmware updates continuously improve accessibility without re-purchase.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step (i) — Theoretical runtime:**

$$
T_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{E_{\text{battery}}}{P_{\text{draw}}} = \frac{24}{3.5} \approx 6.86 \text{ h}
$$

**Step (ii) — Runtime with 60 % DoD limit:**

Usable energy $E_{\text{usable}} = 0.6 \cdot 24 = 14.4$ Wh.

$$
T_{\text{usable}} = \frac{14.4}{3.5} \approx 4.11 \text{ h}
$$

**Step (iii) — Comparison to 8-hour requirement:**

Both theoretical and DoD-limited runtimes fall **short** of the 8-hour requirement. The device does **not** meet the requirement.

**Engineering actions to meet the 8-hour target (any two, 2 marks):**
1. Upgrade the battery to a higher-capacity pack (e.g., add a USB-C power bank of 20 Wh external, raising total $E$ to 44 Wh and runtime to $\approx 12.6$ h).
2. Optimise firmware — disable background apps, dim the display to 40 % brightness, use a Class-D amplifier to reduce audio power by ~30 %.
3. Carry a swappable second battery (hot-swap design, L4 substitution).
4. Schedule intermittent "audio-off" rest periods (e.g., 30 min active / 5 min standby cycling).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Drill-Motor Wheelchair Add-on

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* A 12 V cordless drill motor (peak 250 W, continuous 150 W) is being re-purposed to power a manual wheelchair add-on. List the design modifications needed (classify each as L2, L3, or L4). Draw a labelled block diagram of the modified system showing the motor, gearbox, battery, joystick, motor controller, and wheel hub.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* For a combined wheelchair + user mass of **90 kg**, wheel diameter **0.6 m**, maximum slope **6°**, and cruise speed **1.4 m/s**, compute the design power requirement, apply a safety factor of 1.5, and conclude whether the drill motor is suitable as-is or whether a modification (and what kind) is required.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Modifications (3 marks):**
- **L2 — Add a wheelchair hub-mount bracket** to couple the gearbox output to the wheel.
- **L3 — Remove the chuck and replace with a custom shaft** (L3 subtraction + L4 substitution).
- **L4 — Substitute** the original drill-gearbox with a higher-ratio (e.g., 30:1) planetary gearbox for torque multiplication.
- **L4 — Substitute** the drill's mechanical trigger switch with an electronic joystick + PWM motor controller.
- **L2 — Add** an in-line fuse and a 24 V → 12 V buck converter if a 24 V battery is used to extend runtime.

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Reference the schematic in §4.5 (adapt the sip-and-puff mouthpiece out and replace with a joystick input). Arrows: Battery → Buck converter → Motor controller → Motor → Gearbox → Wheel hub. Joystick feeds the controller via PWM.

**Justification (2 marks):** The drill motor is a *cheap, abundant, and well-characterised* power source; modifying it follows the MACT principle and provides a power-assist solution at ~10× lower cost than a dedicated powered-wheelchair motor. Care must be taken to add **thermal cutoff** and **electronic speed control** since the original drill trigger is *on/off* with no proportional control.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

From §3.2:
- $F_{\text{grav}} = 92.2$ N
- $F_{\text{roll}} = 13.2$ N
- $F_{\text{total}} = 105.4$ N
- $P_{\text{required}} = 147.6$ W
- $P_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \cdot 147.6 = 221.4$ W

**Conclusion:** The drill motor's *continuous* rating is only **150 W**, which is **less** than the design power of 221.4 W. The motor is therefore **not suitable as-is** for continuous 6° slope climbing. A modification is required.

**Recommended modification (2 marks):**
- **L4 Substitution** — replace the 12 V battery with an **18 V or 24 V** lithium pack of the same chemistry. This raises $P_{\text{cont}}$ proportionally (linear approximation: 24 V motor would deliver $\approx 300$ W continuous, comfortably exceeding 221.4 W).
- *Alternative:* **L4 Substitution** — replace the motor with a higher-torque 24 V brushless DC outrunner (used in drones) and pair it with the same gearbox. This is a slightly more expensive but cleaner engineering path.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks on this question:**
> 1. **Confusing peak and continuous power** — many students quote the drill's *peak* 250 W without noting the *continuous* rating. Always state both.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor** — the KTU model answer includes the 1.5× factor; omitting it is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring the gearbox ratio** — a 1:1 direct drive will not start the wheelchair on a slope; the design must include a reduction ratio of at least 20:1.
> 4. **Skipping the units** — every numerical answer must carry units (N, m/s, W, h).
> 5. **Recommending L5 re-engineering** when L4 substitution suffices — examiner deducts for over-engineering.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology (Module 1, PEEBT744)**

- **Definition:** MACT = altering a COTS product to make it assistive; *not* designing from scratch.
- **COTS** = Commercial Off-The-Shelf — a pre-existing, mass-produced commodity.
- **Why MACT:** low cost, fast delivery, high reliability, easy repair, regulatory familiarity.
- **Modification hierarchy:** **L1 settings → L2 addition → L3 subtraction → L4 substitution → L5 re-engineering.** Always pick the *lowest* level that meets the need.
- **Decision thresholds on $S$:** $S \geq 0.8$ adopt as-is; $0.5 \leq S < 0.8$ modify; $S < 0.5$ go custom.
- **Core formula:** $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \dfrac{R_i^{\text{COTS}}}{R_i^{\text{Required}}}$, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Power formula for adapted wheelchairs:** $P_{\text{req}} = F_{\text{total}} \cdot v$, then $\times 1.5$ safety factor.
- **Runtime formula:** $T = E_{\text{battery}} \div P_{\text{draw}}$; remember to apply DoD (typically 50–60 % for longevity).
- **Examples you must remember:** tablet → AAC device, drill motor → powered wheelchair, TV remote → accessible remote, gaming controller → sip-and-puff, mouse → trackball.
- **Regulatory anchors:** *FDA 21 CFR 820*, *IEC 60601-1*, *ISO 14971*, *IEEE 12207*, *RPwD Act 2016 (India)*, *ADA 1990 (US)*.
- **Frameworks:** MATCH, MATSE, HAAT (Human Activity Assistive Technology), QUEST 2.0 for outcome evaluation.
- **Databases to cite:** ABLEDATA, EASTIN, WHO GATE list, RESNA, ATIA.
- **The MACT triangle:** Cost ↓, Time-to-deploy ↓, Performance ~ — best fit for low-resource and rapid-deployment contexts.
- **Examiner's favourite numerical:** power-of-drill-motor wheelchair (90 kg, 6°, 1.4 m/s) — memorise the steps.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** always distinguish *peak* vs. *continuous* motor power; always include the safety factor; always justify the *lowest* modification level chosen.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Allocation of Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** A Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Allocation of Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies?

A **Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies (AT)** is a structured, systematic model used to analyze, design, and evaluate assistive devices by describing the dynamic interaction between the **Human User**, the **Activity** to be performed, the **Assistive Technology** itself, and the **Context (Environment)** in which the technology is deployed. The most widely accepted functional framework in rehabilitation engineering and biomedical engineering curricula is the **HAAT Model (Human–Activity–Assistive Technology)**, originally proposed by **Albert M. Cook and Janice M. Hussey**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this framework allows engineers to move beyond a device-centric view and adopt a **user-centric design philosophy**, ensuring that the engineering design decisions genuinely match the user's residual capabilities, social context, and functional goals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The HAAT model is the cornerstone of Module 1 and forms the basis for the "Allocation of Functions" question that frequently appears in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) Part A and Part B sections.

### 1.2 What is "Allocation of Functions"?

The **Allocation of Functions** is the engineering decision process that determines **which sub-tasks of a desired activity should be performed by the human user** and **which should be delegated to the assistive technology (machine)**. This allocation is critical because it dictates the **level of autonomy**, the **human-machine cooperation strategy**, and ultimately the **safety, efficiency, and acceptability** of the assistive device.

The classical reference for this concept is **Fitts' List (1951)**, often summarized as **MABA–MABA ("Men Are Better At – Machines Are Better At")**, which provides a foundational heuristic for partitioning cognitive, perceptual, and motor functions between humans and machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board-Ready):**
> *"Allocation of Functions is the process of distributing the constituent sub-tasks of an activity between the human operator and the assistive device, optimizing the combined system's overall performance, safety, reliability, and user acceptability, while preserving and enhancing the user's residual functional capacity."*

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a chef who has temporarily lost the use of one arm due to an injury but still wishes to cook a complex meal.

- **The chef** (the *Human*) provides the **cognitive intelligence** — knowing recipes, timing, and taste.
- **The stove, the automated stirrer, the voice-controlled timer, the reach-extending tool** (the *Assistive Technology*) perform the **physical labor** that the chef can no longer do alone.
- The **cooking** is the *Activity*.
- The **kitchen, lighting, counter height, family dining** is the *Context*.

The question *"Who does what?"* is precisely the **Allocation of Functions** decision. If we hand **all** tasks to the machine, the chef loses dignity, motor recovery chances, and engagement. If we leave **all** tasks to the chef, the meal is never prepared. The optimal allocation balances **residual human capability** with **machine augmentation**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Metrics)

The following standard terms and metrics must be memorized for the KTU examination:

- **Residual Function (RF)** — The functional capability the user retains after impairment.
- **Activity Limitation** — Difficulty an individual may have in executing activities (per the WHO ICF model).
- **Level of Assistance (LoA)** — The degree of external help or device support required.
- **Human-Machine Cooperation (HMC)** — The collaborative division of cognitive and motor work.
- **Context of Use** — The physical, social, and cultural environment where AT operates.
- **User Acceptance** — The willingness and ability of the user to integrate AT into daily life.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often award 1–2 extra marks for using correct WHO-ICF terminology in definitions. Always cite *"per the WHO International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)"* wherever applicable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The HAAT Model — Detailed Component Analysis

The HAAT Model is a **four-component closed-loop system** where each element continuously influences the others. It is the most frequently tested framework in the PEEBT744 syllabus.

### 2.1.1 The Human Component

The human is not a passive recipient of the device but an **active processor** of information and generator of intentional action. The human is characterized by:

- **Sensory input capability** (vision, hearing, touch, proprioception)
- **Cognitive processing** (memory, decision-making, learning)
- **Motor output** (strength, range of motion, coordination, endurance)
- **Psychosocial attributes** (motivation, self-esteem, cultural background)
- **Residual function** after impairment

The engineering design must **match the device to the human**, not the human to the device.

### 2.1.2 The Activity Component

Activities are categorized in the ICF hierarchy:

- **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** — bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility.
- **Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADL)** — cooking, shopping, managing finances, using communication devices.
- **Work and Productive Activities** — employment-related tasks.
- **Leisure and Social Participation Activities**.

Each activity can be **decomposed into constituent sub-functions** (e.g., "drinking" = reach → grasp → lift → sip → swallow).

### 2.1.3 The Assistive Technology Component

AT is grouped into three functional classes:

- **No-tech / Low-tech** — cane, reacher, adapted utensils, communication boards.
- **Mid-tech** — battery-powered wheelchairs, simple electronic aids, magnifiers.
- **High-tech** — powered wheelchairs with sip-and-puff, myoelectric arms, EEG-based communication, robotic exoskeletons.

Each class implies a **different allocation of function** and a different level of engineering complexity.

### 2.1.4 The Context Component

Context has **three layers**:

- **Physical context** — indoor/outdoor, lighting, noise, temperature, terrain.
- **Social context** — family, peers, employer, cultural attitudes.
- **Institutional context** — funding, regulations, accessibility standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HAAT model is **dynamic**, not static. A change in *any one* component (e.g., the user fatigues, the environment changes, the device battery dies) forces a **re-allocation of functions** in real time. This is the engineering rationale behind **adaptive shared-control systems** in modern wheelchairs and prosthetics.

## 2.2 The WHO ICF Framework (Brief Cross-Reference)

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** is the WHO companion model. KTU expects you to draw a clear distinction:

- **Impairment** → at body function/structure level.
- **Activity Limitation** → at individual level.
- **Participation Restriction** → at societal level.
- **Contextual Factors** → Environmental + Personal.

The HAAT model operationalizes the ICF into a **design framework** for engineers.

## 2.3 Function Allocation Strategies — The Core Taxonomy

There are **three primary strategies** for allocating functions between the human and the assistive device:

| Strategy | Who Performs Task? | Best Used When | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Human-Only Allocation** | The user performs 100% of the task | RF is high; user desires independence and therapy | Adapted grip tool, lightweight wheelchair for self-propulsion |
| **Machine-Only Allocation** | Device performs 100% of the task | RF is near zero; safety-critical or fatiguing task | Voice-controlled hospital bed, fully automatic insulin pump |
| **Shared / Cooperative Allocation** | Human and machine cooperate, dividing sub-tasks | User has partial RF; recovery is a goal | Power wheelchair with joystick, myoelectric hand, brain-computer interface |

### 2.3.1 Sub-forms of Shared Allocation

- **Sequential Cooperation** — Human completes sub-task A, then device completes sub-task B (e.g., user steers, motor assists uphill).
- **Parallel Cooperation** — Human and machine perform different sub-tasks simultaneously (e.g., user steers, anti-tip sensors stabilize).
- **Peer Cooperation** — Both act on the same sub-task with merged control signals (e.g., shared-autonomy wheelchair where joystick and obstacle-avoidance AI both influence the wheels).

## 2.4 Fitts' List (MABA–MABA) — Allocation Heuristics

**Paul Fitts (1951)** proposed a seminal taxonomy that is still used today:

**Men are better at:** detecting small visual/auditory signals, perceiving patterns, improvisation, judgment under uncertainty, applying past experience, reacting to unexpected low-probability events.

**Machines are better at:** responding quickly to control signals, applying great force smoothly and precisely, performing repetitive tasks without fatigue, storing and recalling large amounts of data, computing mathematical functions rapidly, performing multiple tasks simultaneously.

The **engineering task** is to map the activity's sub-functions to whichever agent is superior, while still preserving the user's **engagement, dignity, and rehabilitation potential**.

> [!TIP]
> Fitts' list is also referred to as **HABA–MABA ("Humans Are Better At – Machines Are Better At")** in some textbooks. Both acronyms refer to the same principle.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although this is a conceptual topic, the following quantitative metrics and formulae are examinable in KTU 2024:

$$
\text{Level of Assistance (LoA)} = 1 - \frac{\text{User's Independent Performance}}{\text{Normal Population Performance}}
$$

$$
\text{Device Burden Index (DBI)} = \frac{T_{\text{setup}} + T_{\text{maintain}} + T_{\text{learn}}}{T_{\text{activity benefit}}}
$$

$$
\text{Human-Machine Share} = \frac{\text{Sub-tasks performed by machine}}{\text{Total number of sub-tasks}}
$$

$$
\text{Residual Function (RF)} = \frac{\text{Achieved functional capacity}}{\text{Pre-impairment functional capacity}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{User Acceptance Score} = f(\text{usefulness},\ \text{usability},\ \text{desirability},\ \text{accessibility})
$$

### 2.5.1 Quick Reference Table (Board-Ready)

| Metric | Symbol | Range | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Level of Assistance | LoA | 0–1 (or 0–100%) | Higher LoA → more machine allocation |
| Residual Function | RF | 0–100% | Higher RF → more human allocation |
| Human-Machine Share | HMS | 0–1 | 0.5 represents balanced cooperation |
| Device Burden Index | DBI | Dimensionless | Lower is better (less user effort) |
| User Acceptance Score | UAS | 0–100 | Threshold ≥ 70% for clinical adoption |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 scheme, students are not required to derive these formulae but **must be able to interpret them**, calculate illustrative values, and apply them to case studies.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Functional Framework is not just academic — it directly drives:

1. **Prosthetic limb design** — myoelectric arms allocate low-force, fine-motor control to the user and high-force tasks (lifting 5 kg groceries) to the motorized actuator.
2. **Smart wheelchair design** — shared-control algorithms allocate *navigation intent* to the user and *obstacle avoidance* to the onboard LIDAR-based AI.
3. **Cochlear implants** — the auditory cortex (human) perceives sound; the device performs spectral analysis and electrical stimulation.
4. **Robotic exoskeletons** — for gait rehabilitation, the patient provides voluntary intent; the exoskeleton supplies the missing torque, with progressive unloading as recovery occurs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix for Function Allocation

## 3.1 The Seven-Step Function Allocation Procedure

This is the **engineering procedure** the KTU board expects you to demonstrate in 7-mark and 14-mark answers. Every step is mandatory.

### Step 1 — Activity Decomposition

Break the target activity into its smallest meaningful sub-functions.

**Example:** "Drinking a cup of water" decomposes as:

$$
\text{Drink} = \{S_1,\ S_2,\ S_3,\ S_4,\ S_5\}
$$

where the sub-tasks are $S_1$ = reach, $S_2$ = grasp, $S_3$ = lift, $S_4$ = tip toward mouth, $S_5$ = sip and swallow.

### Step 2 — User Capability Assessment

Quantify the user's **Residual Function (RF)** for each sub-task using clinical scales (e.g., Fugl-Meyer, Barthel Index, Functional Independence Measure).

**Example matrix entry for a post-stroke patient:**

$$
\text{RF}_{S_2} = \frac{\text{Measured grip strength}}{\text{Normal grip strength}} = \frac{8\,\text{kg}}{30\,\text{kg}} = 0.267 = 26.7\%
$$

### Step 3 — Device Capability Mapping

Map each sub-task to the candidate AT component. For each, determine:

- Maximum sustainable force
- Precision
- Response time
- Power consumption
- Failure mode

### Step 4 — Apply Fitts' List (MABA–MABA)

For each sub-task, assign the agent that is intrinsically better at that function.

| Sub-task | Fitts' Category | Assigned To |
|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ Reach planning | Judgment, spatial reasoning | **Human** |
| $S_2$ Grasp force | Sustained force, no fatigue | **Machine** |
| $S_3$ Lift control | Smooth precise force | **Machine** |
| $S_4$ Tip motion | Coordination of two streams | **Shared** |
| $S_5$ Sip reflex | Biological reflex | **Human** |

### Step 5 — Compute Human-Machine Share (HMS)

$$
\text{HMS} = \frac{\text{Sub-tasks performed (or assisted) by machine}}{\text{Total sub-tasks}} = \frac{2.5}{5} = 0.50
$$

So the allocation is **50% machine, 50% human**, indicating balanced shared cooperation.

### Step 6 — Compute Level of Assistance (LoA)

$$
\text{LoA} = 1 - \text{RF}_{\text{weighted}}
$$

If weighted average RF across all five sub-tasks is $0.40$, then:

$$
\text{LoA} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60 = 60\%
$$

This means **60% of the activity must be supported by the device**.

### Step 7 — Validate with User Acceptance Loop

- Prototype is built.
- Tested by the user in context.
- UAS (User Acceptance Score) computed.
- If UAS < 70% threshold, **re-allocate** and iterate.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Case Study:** A 45-year-old engineer with C5–C6 spinal cord injury wishes to operate a computer at his workplace. He has **bicep function** (elbow flexion) but **no finger or hand movement**.

**Step 1 — Activity decomposition:**

$$
\text{Computer Use} = \{\text{Login},\ \text{Word Processing},\ \text{Email},\ \text{Web Browse},\ \text{Shutdown}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Capability matrix:**

| Sub-task | RF | Required Effort |
|---|---|---|
| Login (typing password) | 0% | Voice / mouth-stick |
| Word Processing | 5% | Voice dictation software |
| Email | 5% | Voice + speech synthesis |
| Web Browse | 10% | Sip-and-puff + on-screen keyboard |
| Shutdown | 0% | Single voice command |

**Step 3 — Allocation per Fitts' List:**

| Sub-task | MABA / MABA | Final Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| Login | Machine is better (data recall) | Machine (voice biometric + voice command) |
| Word Processing | Human is better (creativity) | Shared — human dictates, machine transcribes |
| Email | Human is better (judgment) | Shared — human voice, machine executes |
| Web Browse | Machine is better (rapid navigation) | Machine (eye-tracker + AI agent) |
| Shutdown | Machine is better (single command) | Machine |

**Step 4 — Compute HMS:**

$$
\text{HMS} = \frac{1 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 1 + 1}{5} = \frac{4.0}{5} = 0.80
$$

So 80% is allocated to the machine — appropriate given the very low residual hand function.

**Step 5 — Compute LoA:**

$$
\text{LoA} = 1 - \frac{0 + 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.10 + 0}{5} = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 = 96\%
$$

Interpretation: **96% of the activity must be machine-assisted**, with the human contributing primarily cognitive intent and supervisory control.

## 3.3 Decision Matrix for Choosing an Allocation Strategy

A student-friendly rule-of-thumb the KTU examiner often rewards:

$$
\text{Strategy} = \begin{cases}
\text{Human-Only}, & \text{if } \text{RF} \geq 0.80 \\
\text{Shared Cooperation}, & \text{if } 0.20 < \text{RF} < 0.80 \\
\text{Machine-Only}, & \text{if } \text{RF} \leq 0.20
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **starting heuristic**, not a hard rule. Always override it based on **safety-criticality** (e.g., a ventilator must never be allocated to the user alone), **fatigue considerations**, and **user preference** (psychological acceptance).

## 3.4 Re-allocation Triggers (Dynamic Re-allocation)

A previously chosen allocation may need to change in real time if:

- **User fatigue** develops → shift from Human-Only to Shared.
- **Device battery low** → shift from Machine-Only to Shared or Human-Only.
- **Context change** (e.g., moving from indoor to outdoor) → re-evaluate physical environment.
- **User recovery** (rehabilitation) → progressively shift from Machine-Only to Shared to Human-Only.

This dynamic re-allocation is the **defining characteristic of a well-designed modern AT system**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The HAAT Model — Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Human Component] -->|Intent Sensory Motor| B[Activity]
    B -->|Sub task decomposition| C[Assistive Technology]
    C -->|Feedback Output| A
    D[Context Physical Social Institutional] -.->|Influences| A
    D -.->|Influences| B
    D -.->|Influences| C
    C -->|Interacts with| D
    A -->|Interacts with| D
    B -->|Occurs within| D
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows form the **closed loop** of human–activity–AT interaction. The dashed arrows show that the **Context** constantly modulates all three other components. This is the canonical HAAT representation required by KTU.

## 4.2 Function Allocation Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start Define Target Activity] --> P1[Decompose Activity into Sub tasks]
    P1 --> P2[Assess Users Residual Function RF for each Sub task]
    P2 --> P3{Is RF greater than or equal to 0.80}
    P3 -->|Yes| P4[Allocate Sub task to Human Only]
    P3 -->|No| P5{Is RF less than or equal to 0.20}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Allocate Sub task to Machine Only]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Allocate Sub task to Shared Cooperation]
    P4 --> P8[Compute Human Machine Share HMS]
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Compute Level of Assistance LoA]
    P9 --> P10[Prototype and Test in Real Context]
    P10 --> P11{Is User Acceptance Score greater than or equal to 70 percent}
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[Finalize Design]
    P11 -->|No| P13[Re allocate and Iterate]
    P13 --> P1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This flow chart is the **engineering procedure** for converting a clinical need into a finalized AT design through iterative re-allocation. Examiners often award full marks when students reproduce a flow chart similar to this in their answer sheets.

## 4.3 Shared-Control Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[User Intent Signal] --> M1[Signal Conditioning]
    M1 --> F1[Shared Controller Fusion Algorithm]
    E1[Environmental Sensors LIDAR Camera IMU] --> F1
    F1 --> A1[Motor Actuator Drivers]
    A1 --> O1[Wheels Arm Gripper or End Effector]
    O1 -->|Physical Feedback| H1
    O1 -->|Sensed State| E1
    C1[Context Awareness Module] --> F1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A shared-control AT system has **three input streams** to the fusion controller — (1) user intent, (2) environmental sensors, and (3) context awareness. The fusion algorithm arbitrates between them and sends commands to the actuators, which act on the environment and feed back to both the user and the sensors. This is the architecture behind modern power wheelchairs, myoelectric arms, and robotic exoskeletons.

## 4.4 ICF vs HAAT — Cross-Reference Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ICF_Framework
        BF[Body Functions and Structures] --> AC[Activities]
        AC --> PR[Participation]
        EF[Environmental Factors] -.-> BF
        EF -.-> AC
        EF -.-> PR
        PF[Personal Factors] -.-> BF
        PF -.-> AC
        PF -.-> PR
    end
    subgraph HAAT_Model
        HM[Human] --> AM[Activity]
        AM --> AT[Assistive Technology]
        CT[Context] -.-> HM
        CT -.-> AM
        CT -.-> AT
    end
    ICF_Framework -.Maps to.-> HAAT_Model
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The WHO ICF (left) and the HAAT Model (right) are not competing — they are **complementary**. The ICF provides the *clinical classification language*; the HAAT model provides the *engineering design framework*. Mapping the two is a common 7-mark KTU question.

## 4.5 Human-Machine Function Partitioning Table (Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Humans_Are_Better_At
        H1A[Pattern Recognition]
        H1B[Improvisation]
        H1C[Judgment under Uncertainty]
        H1D[Applying Past Experience]
        H1E[Detecting Low Probability Events]
    end
    subgraph Machines_Are_Better_At
        M1A[Applying Sustained High Force]
        M1B[Repetitive Precise Tasks]
        M1C[Fast Mathematical Computation]
        M1D[Storing Large Data Sets]
        M1E[Simultaneous Multi tasking]
    end
    H1A --> SH[Shared Allocation Decision]
    H1B --> SH
    H1C --> SH
    H1D --> SH
    H1E --> SH
    M1A --> SH
    M1B --> SH
    M1C --> SH
    M1D --> SH
    M1E --> SH
    SH --> OUT[Optimal Function Allocation]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a visual representation of **Fitts' MABA–MABA list**. The engineer gathers all sub-tasks, sorts them into "human-suitable" or "machine-suitable" categories, and then **arbitrates** to reach an optimal allocation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Examiner's Note:** Part A questions in KTU 2024 scheme are designed to test **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be concise (3–4 sentences) but use **exact syllabus terminology**.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Define the HAAT model. List its four components with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> The HAAT (Human–Activity–Assistive Technology) model is a functional framework proposed by Cook and Hussey for designing assistive technology systems. Its four components are:
> 1. **Human** — the user, e.g., a person with a spinal cord injury.
> 2. **Activity** — the desired task, e.g., mobility in the home.
> 3. **Assistive Technology** — the device, e.g., a powered wheelchair.
> 4. **Context** — the physical, social, and institutional environment, e.g., home flooring, family support, hospital funding.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**What is meant by "Allocation of Functions" in assistive technology? State Fitts' MABA–MABA principle in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

> Allocation of Functions is the engineering process of distributing the sub-tasks of an activity between the human user and the assistive device, optimizing performance, safety, and user acceptance. **Fitts' MABA–MABA principle** states that *Men (Humans) Are Better At* perceptual pattern recognition, judgment, and improvisation, while *Machines Are Better At* repetitive tasks, applying sustained force, fast computation, and multi-tasking. The function allocation decision matches each sub-task to the agent that performs it best.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into sub-parts of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE of the two alternatives** provided. Every sub-part requires a **diagram** plus **explanation** to secure full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 1]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **HAAT model** for assistive technology. Discuss the role of each of the four components and illustrate with an example of a **powered wheelchair for a person with paraplegia**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (with marking scheme):**

1. **Diagram (2 marks)** — Reproduce a HAAT block diagram showing Human, Activity, AT, and Context with bidirectional arrows. (Use the Mermaid structure from Section 4.1 as a guide; draw manually in the exam.)

2. **Explanation of components (4 marks):**
   - **Human** — the user with paraplegia has intact cognition and upper-limb function but loss of lower-limb motor capacity. The HAAT model characterizes sensory, cognitive, and motor capabilities.
   - **Activity** — mobility in indoor and outdoor environments.
   - **Assistive Technology** — the powered wheelchair with joystick, controller, motors, and battery.
   - **Context** — home floor type, ramp availability, family support, insurance funding.

3. **Example illustration (1 mark)** — The user steers using the joystick (Human intent), the motors (Machine) provide locomotion, and the context determines acceptable terrain.

**(b)** Explain the **three strategies of function allocation** (Human-Only, Machine-Only, Shared Cooperation). For each strategy, give **one clinical example** and discuss **when it is most appropriate**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Definitions (3 marks)** — clearly state each strategy (Human-Only, Machine-Only, Shared Cooperation) and provide one example each:
   - **Human-Only** — Patient with recovered grip uses an adapted spoon to feed independently.
   - **Machine-Only** — Patient with total quadriplegia uses a voice-controlled environmental control unit.
   - **Shared Cooperation** — Patient with hemiplegia uses a myoelectric hand where EMG signals from residual muscles control partial grip and the device supplies additional force.

2. **When each is appropriate (3 marks):**
   - Human-Only when **Residual Function (RF) ≥ 80%** and rehabilitation is desired.
   - Machine-Only when **RF ≤ 20%** or task is safety-critical.
   - Shared Cooperation when **0.20 < RF < 0.80**.

3. **Conclusion (1 mark):** State that the choice is dynamic and re-evaluated as the user's condition evolves.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 1 Alternative]`

**(a)** Describe the **ICF framework** of WHO. Compare and contrast it with the **HAAT model**, highlighting how each complements the other in assistive technology design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **ICF description (2 marks)** — The WHO International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health classifies health and disability at three levels: (i) Body Functions and Structures, (ii) Activities, (iii) Participation, modulated by (iv) Environmental and Personal factors.

2. **HAAT model description (1 mark)** — Engineering design framework with Human, Activity, AT, and Context.

3. **Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ICF | HAAT |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | WHO, clinical | Engineering (Cook & Hussey) |
| Purpose | Classify functioning | Design AT systems |
| Focus | Person and society | Person, device, and environment |
| Output | Diagnostic categories | Functional design specifications |
| Common element | Activity, Context | Activity, Context |

4. **Complementarity (1 mark)** — ICF identifies *what* the limitation is; HAAT prescribes *how* to design the AT solution.

**(b)** A **25-year-old post-polio patient** with one weak hand wishes to **eat independently using a spoon**. Apply the **seven-step function allocation procedure** to design an appropriate assistive device. Justify each allocation decision using **Fitts' list**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Step 1 — Decomposition (1 mark):** Eating with a spoon = reach → grasp → lift → scoop → bring to mouth → tip → swallow.

2. **Step 2 — RF assessment (1 mark):** Grasp RF ≈ 40%, Lift RF ≈ 50%, Scoop RF ≈ 30%, Tip RF ≈ 35%.

3. **Step 3 — Device mapping (1 mark):** Universal cuff (low-tech) + lightweight angled spoon + tremor-suppression module (mid-tech).

4. **Step 4 — Fitts' MABA–MABA allocation (2 marks):**
   - **Reach** — *Human better* (spatial judgment). → Human.
   - **Grasp** — *Machine better* (sustained grip). → Universal cuff (machine-augmented).
   - **Lift** — *Shared* (human intent + mechanical support).
   - **Scoop** — *Machine better* (pre-shaped spoon bowl). → Machine.
   - **Tip** — *Shared* (coordinated fine motion).
   - **Swallow** — *Human* (biological reflex). → Human.

5. **Steps 5–7 (2 marks):** Compute HMS ≈ 0.50, LoA ≈ 0.60, prototype, and iterate based on User Acceptance Score. Final design is a **universal cuff with angled, weighted spoon** — a low-tech solution that maximally preserves the user's residual function and dignity.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the ICF and HAAT models** — They are *complementary*, not the same. The ICF is a *clinical classification*; the HAAT is an *engineering design framework*. Marks are deducted if students treat them as identical.
> 2. **Forgetting the Context** — A common error is to discuss only the Human, Activity, and AT components, omitting the Context. The Context is the **fourth mandatory component** of the HAAT model. Full marks require all four.
> 3. **Applying Fitts' list as a rigid rule** — Fitts' list is a *heuristic*, not a law. The examiner expects you to mention that **user preference, safety, and rehabilitation potential** may override the MABA–MABA recommendation.
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram** — In a 7-mark sub-question, **at least 2 marks are reserved for a neat, labeled diagram** of the HAAT model or the function allocation flow. Students who submit text-only answers lose these marks.
> 5. **Ignoring dynamic re-allocation** — Function allocation is *not a one-time decision*. A high-quality answer must mention that allocation changes as the user's condition, environment, or device state changes.
> 6. **Confusing Level of Assistance with Human-Machine Share** — They are **different metrics**. LoA measures *support required*; HMS measures *task share*. Do not interchange them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 12 points before entering the KTU examination hall:**

- ☐ The **HAAT Model** = Human + Activity + Assistive Technology + Context.
- ☐ The **Context** is the fourth mandatory component (often forgotten).
- ☐ The **ICF Framework** is a WHO clinical classification; the **HAAT** is an engineering design framework — they are complementary.
- ☐ **Allocation of Functions** = deciding which sub-tasks the human performs and which the machine performs.
- ☐ The **three allocation strategies** are Human-Only, Machine-Only, and Shared Cooperation.
- ☐ **Shared Cooperation** has three sub-forms: Sequential, Parallel, and Peer.
- ☐ **Fitts' MABA–MABA list** (1951) is the foundational heuristic for allocation decisions.
- ☐ **Residual Function (RF)** is the user's remaining capacity after impairment.
- ☐ **Human-Machine Share (HMS)** = (machine sub-tasks) / (total sub-tasks), range 0–1.
- ☐ **Level of Assistance (LoA)** = 1 − weighted RF, range 0–1.
- ☐ **User Acceptance Score ≥ 70%** is the typical clinical threshold for adoption.
- ☐ Function allocation is **dynamic** and **iterative** — re-evaluated as user, device, or context changes.

> **Final Note:** A well-structured KTU answer to a 14-mark question on this topic should include: (1) a definition, (2) a labeled HAAT diagram, (3) a step-by-step allocation procedure, (4) at least one clinical example, (5) a Fitts' MABA–MABA justification, and (6) a closing statement on dynamic re-allocation. Covering all six elements routinely secures 12+ out of 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard and Soft Assistive Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard and Soft Assistive Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Assistive Technology (AT)** is a generic umbrella term used in biomedical and rehabilitation engineering to denote any *product, equipment, software, service, or system* that is used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities or age-related impairments. The World Health Organization (WHO) classifies Assistive Technology into two principal, mutually inclusive domains: **Hard Assistive Technologies (HATs)** and **Soft Assistive Technologies (SATs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Assistive Technology (HAT):** Refers to the *physical, tangible, engineered artefacts* (devices, instruments, mechanical/electronic hardware) that a user directly interfaces with to perform Activities of Daily Living (ADL). These are the **"product-based"** components of AT, ranging from simple low-tech wooden crutches to advanced microprocessor-controlled prosthetic knees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soft Assistive Technology (SAT):** Refers to the *non-physical, intangible, human-mediated, or software-driven enablers* that support the user. These include rehabilitation methodologies, clinical protocols, caregiver training, software applications, and information services. They are the **"process-based"** components of AT.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "House Building" Analogy)

Think of helping a person with a disability regain independence as **building a house**:
- **The Walls, Doors, Windows, and Roof (Hard Assistive Technologies):** These are the *bricks, the wheelchair, the hearing aid, the smart-glasses*. Without these tangible structures, there is no shelter. They are the physical artifacts that directly substitute or restore a lost function.
- **The Architect's Blueprint, the Builder's Skill, and the Owner's Operating Manual (Soft Assistive Technologies):** These are the *training, the therapy sessions, the software instructions, the clinical protocols*. A wheelchair (Hard) sitting in a corner is useless if the user does not know how to operate it, lacks the muscle training (Soft) to use it, or if the prescription protocol (Soft) was incorrect.

A successful rehabilitation engineer needs to design **both the house and the manual**. The HAT is meaningless without the SAT, and vice versa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** "Hard" = *Physical artefacts* (Touch them, wear them, plug them in). "Soft" = *Methodologies, software, services, and human training* (Cannot be physically picked up).

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

- **Technology Readiness Level (TRL):** A **9-point scale (TRL 1 to TRL 9)** used globally to assess the maturity of an AT, from basic principle observed (TRL 1) to actual system proven in operational environment (TRL 9).
- **ISO 9999 Standard:** The international classification standard for Assistive Products, dividing them into **12 major classes** (e.g., Class 04: Personal mobility, Class 15: Daily living aids).
- **ICF Model:** The WHO's **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** maps AT interventions to *Body Functions, Activities, Participation, and Environmental Factors*.
- **Functional Independence Measure (FIM):** An 18-item, **7-point ordinal scale (Score 1: Total Assistance to Score 7: Complete Independence)** used to track the efficacy of combined HAT + SAT interventions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Hard Assistive Technologies (HATs)

HATs can be sub-classified based on the degree of engineering complexity:

- **Level 1 — Low-Tech HATs (No external power, no electronics):**
  - Pure mechanical advantage devices.
  - Examples: Walking canes, wooden crutches, reachers, dressing sticks, manual wheelchairs, simple eyeglasses (low diopter).
  - *Operational Logic:* Uses leverage, gravity, and mechanical linkage to extend the user's reach or base of support.

- **Level 2 — Mid-Tech HATs (Simple electronics or battery power):**
  - Incorporates basic sensors and actuators.
  - Examples: Digital hearing aids, battery-powered scooters, simple voice amplifiers, talking calculators, basic prosthetic socks.
  - *Operational Logic:* Uses analog/digital signal processing (e.g., ADC and DAC conversion in hearing aids) to augment a specific biological signal.

- **Level 3 — High-Tech HATs (Microprocessor, robotics, AI, IoT):**
  - Sophisticated closed-loop or open-loop control systems.
  - Examples: Myoelectric prosthetic arms, C-Leg (microprocessor knee), cochlear implants, robotic exoskeletons, smart wheelchairs (with LIDAR/ultrasonic obstacle avoidance), AI-driven smart glasses for the visually impaired.
  - *Operational Logic:* Uses **PID control (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)**, **Machine Learning classifiers**, and **sensor fusion** (combining IMU, EMG, and force sensors) to adapt in real time to the user's environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind HAT Sub-classification:** KTU examiners frequently ask students to categorize a given device. The key discriminator is the **"intelligence"** and **"power source"**: a device with a microprocessor or robotic actuator is *High-Tech*; a device with a small battery or analog chip is *Mid-Tech*; a device with no power is *Low-Tech*.

---

## 2.2 Structural Breakdown of Soft Assistive Technologies (SATs)

SATs are categorized by the *medium of delivery*:

- **Service-Based SATs (Human-mediated):**
  - Occupational Therapy (OT), Physical Therapy (PT), Speech-Language Pathology (SLP), Vocational Rehabilitation, Special Education.
  - *Operational Logic:* A trained clinician teaches the user compensatory strategies, motor relearning, and ADL (Activities of Daily Living) techniques.

- **Information-Based SATs (Knowledge and Documentation):**
  - User manuals, prescription guidelines, AT assessment reports, tele-rehabilitation platforms, teletherapy video calls, AT information portals.
  - *Operational Logic:* Facilitates informed decision-making and continuity of care.

- **Software-Based SATs (Digital SATs):**
  - Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) apps on tablets (e.g., Proloquo2Go), brain-training apps (e.g., Lumosity, CogniFit), screen readers (JAWS, NVDA), eye-tracking software (Tobii), and AI-based captioning tools.
  - *Operational Logic:* Translates one form of information (e.g., eye gaze coordinates) into a different functional output (e.g., text typing on a screen).

---

## 2.3 The "HAT + SAT" Synergistic Framework

No assistive technology operates in a vacuum. The **HAAT Model (Human, Activity, Assistive Technology)** by Cook and Polgar (the globally cited textbook framework in rehabilitation engineering) explicitly states that the **HAT** is just one node in a system that requires:
- The **Human** (user with specific physical/cognitive profile).
- The **Activity** (task: walking, eating, communicating).
- The **Context** (physical environment, social environment, cultural setting).
The SATs ensure the Human, HAT, Activity, and Context are *correctly aligned*.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Classification Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the key classification metrics, formulas, and operational parameters a KTU student must memorize. **No vertical pipes are used inside cells to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Parameter / Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Standard Unit / Range | Application in AT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TRL Scale | Technology Readiness Level | **TRL 1 – TRL 9** | Maturity assessment of HATs |
| 2 | FIM Score | Functional Independence Measure | **1 (Total Assist) – 7 (Complete Independence)** | Outcome measure for HAT+SAT efficacy |
| 3 | Power for HAT (Battery) | $P = V \cdot I$ | **Watts (W)** | Sizing of power packs for high-tech HATs |
| 4 | Mechanical Advantage (Lever) | $MA = \frac{L_{effort}}{L_{load}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Crutches, reachers, manual wheelchairs |
| 5 | ISO 9999 Class Code | e.g., 04 30, 15 09 | Numeric code | Classification of HAT products |
| 6 | ICF Domain Mapping | Body $\to$ Activity $\to$ Participation | Categorical | Mapping SAT interventions |
| 7 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | **Decibels (dB)** | Hearing aids, cochlear implants |
| 8 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | Digitizing biological signals (EMG, ECG) in HATs |
| 9 | EMG Activation Threshold | $V_{th} = \mu_{rest} + 3\sigma_{rest}$ | **Volts (V)** | Myoelectric prosthetic hand triggering |
| 10 | Torque Output (Prosthetic Knee) | $\tau = I \cdot \alpha$ | **Newton-metre (N·m)** | High-tech prosthetic joints |
| 11 | Battery Runtime | $t = \frac{C_{Ah}}{I_{draw}}$ | **Hours (h)** | Wheelchair, exoskeleton endurance |
| 12 | Screen Reader Latency | $t_{latency}$ | **Milliseconds (ms)** | Software-based SAT quality |
| 13 | Field of View (Smart Glasses) | $FOV = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$ | **Degrees (°)** | Low-vision HATs |
| 14 | AAC Typing Rate | $WPM = \frac{N_{words}}{t_{min}}$ | **Words per minute** | Augmentative communication SATs |
| 15 | User Satisfaction (QUEST) | $Likert\ 1\text{–}5$ | Ordinal scale | HAT + SAT service evaluation |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prosthetics Industry:** A myoelectric hand (HAT) is prescribed and tuned by a prosthetist (SAT service) using software calibration (SAT software) running on a clinical PC. **All three must be aligned.**
- **Smart Cities:** Curb cuts, tactile paving, and audio traffic signals are *Hard* civil-engineering ATs, but the policies, public-awareness campaigns, and design codes that mandate their installation are *Soft* ATs.
- **Telerehabilitation:** Post-COVID, SATs have moved to the cloud. A stroke survivor's recovery app (SAT) generates exercise logs that an algorithm uses to recommend a new ankle-foot orthosis setting (HAT adjustment) — a true HAT-SAT closed loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

Because this topic is fundamentally a **classification and design philosophy** module rather than a pure mathematical one, Section 3 is structured as an **analytical matrix** with embedded derivations of the quantitative metrics introduced in Section 2. Every algebraic step is fully expanded.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: HAT vs. SAT — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Dimension | Hard Assistive Technology (HAT) | Soft Assistive Technology (SAT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangibility** | Physical, tactile artefact | Intangible, service, or software |
| **Primary Resource** | Materials, electronics, manufacturing labor | Human expertise, training time, software code |
| **Domain Owner** | Biomedical / Mechanical / Electronics Engineer | Clinician, Therapist, Educator, UX Designer |
