# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define Electronic Pointing Interface. List any four categories of EPIs with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Electronic Pointing Interface (EPI)** is an assistive device that enables individuals with motor disabilities to control a computer cursor through bioelectric, optical, mechanical, or physiological signal inputs, replacing the standard hand-operated mouse.

**Four categories:**

| # | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optical / Vision-based | IR-based eye tracker using PCCR |
| 2 | Bioelectric | EOG-based interface using skin electrodes |
| 3 | Mechanical / Pneumatic | Sip-and-puff system |
| 4 | Acoustic / Ultrasonic | Ultrasonic head tracker (time-of-flight) |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming categories + 1 mark for correct examples)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – December 2023]
**What is the "Midas Touch" problem in Electronic Pointing Interfaces? How is it typically mitigated?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Midas Touch problem** refers to the phenomenon where a dwell-based click mechanism generates an **unintended click** while the user is merely moving the cursor through a clickable region without intending to select it. The name is borrowed from the mythological King Midas, who turned everything he touched into gold.

**Mitigation strategies:**

1. **Increase dwell time** $T_d$ to 500–1000 ms (reduces false-positive rate).
2. **Reduce dwell radius** $R_d$ to ≤ 10 pixels.
3. **Adaptive dwell**, where the dwell time varies based on cursor velocity (faster cursor = longer dwell required).
4. **Confirmatory blink or sip-and-puff** as a secondary click trigger.

*(3 marks: 1 mark problem statement + 2 marks mitigation methods)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, Module 2 Part B questions carry 14 marks. Each sub-question is typically allocated 7 marks. An **internal choice** between two full questions is provided.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an Eye-Gaze-Based Electronic Pointing Interface**. Describe the **Pupil Center Corneal Reflection (PCCR)** method for gaze estimation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Eye Aspect Ratio (EAR) technique** for blink detection. Derive the EAR formula and describe how a blink can be mapped to a mouse click event. State one limitation of the technique. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(a):

**Block Diagram (descriptive — refer to SECTION 4.1):**

The eye-gaze EPI consists of the following stages:

1. **IR Illumination Stage:** Two or more IR LEDs (typically 850 nm) are mounted around a small CMOS camera to produce **on-axis** illumination.
2. **Image Acquisition:** The camera captures frames at 30–120 fps of the user's eye.
3. **Pre-processing:** Frame differencing and thresholding isolate the **pupil** (dark blob) and the **glint** (bright spot) regions.
4. **Pupil Center Detection:** Centroid of the dark pupil region is computed.
5. **Glint Detection:** Intensity-weighted centroid of the glint region is computed.
6. **PCCR Vector Computation:** The vector from glint to pupil center is the gaze feature.
7. **Calibrated Polynomial Mapping:** A 2nd-order polynomial maps the PCCR vector to screen coordinates $(X_s, Y_s)$.
8. **HID Output:** The mapped coordinate is sent to the host as a USB HID mouse-move report.

**PCCR Method (in detail):**

The PCCR method works on the principle that when an IR LED is placed **off-axis** from the camera, it produces a **specular reflection (glint)** on the cornea, whose position is **fixed relative to the head**. As the eye rotates within the orbit, the **pupil center moves relative to the glint** in a way that is approximately linear with gaze angle.

The PCCR vector is:

$$
\vec{v}_{PCCR} = (x_p - x_g, \; y_p - y_g)
$$

A second-order polynomial is used for calibration:

$$
X_s = a_0 + a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta y + a_3 \Delta x^2 + a_4 \Delta x \Delta y + a_5 \Delta y^2
$$

where the coefficients $a_i$ are determined during a **9-point calibration** routine.

**Scoring breakdown (7 marks):** Block diagram 3 marks + PCCR explanation 2 marks + Polynomial mapping 1 mark + Calibration mention 1 mark.

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(b):

**Eye Aspect Ratio Derivation:**

The EAR is defined as the ratio of the **vertical eye-opening distance** to the **horizontal eye width**, computed from six landmark points around the eye. Referencing the six points as $p_1$ (left corner), $p_2$ (upper-left lid), $p_3$ (upper-right lid), $p_4$ (right corner), $p_5$ (lower-right lid), $p_6$ (lower-left lid):

$$
EAR = \frac{\vert\vert p_2 - p_6 \vert\vert + \vert\vert p_3 - p_5 \vert\vert}{2 \cdot \vert\vert p_1 - p_4 \vert\vert}
$$

**Why This Works:**

- When the eye is open, both vertical distances $\vert\vert p_2 - p_6 \vert\vert$ and $\vert\vert p_3 - p_5 \vert\vert$ are nearly equal to the eye height $h$, giving $EAR \approx h / w$, a stable value (typically 0.25–0.35).
- During a blink, the eyelids close rapidly. Both vertical distances collapse to nearly zero, while the horizontal width remains unchanged. Hence, $EAR$ drops to a small value (typically $< 0.15$).
- The ratio is **scale-invariant**: moving the camera closer or farther does not change $EAR$.

**Blink-to-Click Mapping:**

$$
\text{Blink State} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{OPEN}, & EAR > 0.20 \\
\text{CLOSED}, & EAR \leq 0.20
\end{cases}
$$

A **short blink** (duration 100–300 ms) maps to a **left click**; a **long blink** (duration > 500 ms) maps to a **right click** or **drag**.

**Limitation:** The EAR technique fails when the user wears **thick eyeglass frames** that occlude the facial landmarks, and it is **sensitive to extreme head rotations** that push the eye out of the camera's field of view.

**Scoring breakdown (7 marks):** EAR formula derivation 2 marks + Geometric interpretation 2 marks + Blink-to-click mapping 1.5 marks + Limitation 1.5 marks.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – December 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **principle, working, and components of a Sip-and-Puff Electronic Pointing Interface.** Describe how sip-and-puff commands are mapped to cursor motion and click events. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the working of an **EMG-based cursor control system.** Derive the expression for the **envelope detector** used to smooth the rectified EMG signal. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(a):

**Principle:** A sip-and-puff EPI exploits the user's preserved **breath control** (often intact in high-level SCI patients at C3–C4 level). The user puffs (exhales) or sips (inhales) through a plastic tube, and a **pressure transducer** measures the pressure change.

**Components:**

1. **Mouthpiece / Plastic Straw** — Sterilizable, replaceable.
2. **Pressure Transducer** — Differential type, range $\pm 50$ cmH₂O, output 0–5 V.
3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplifier + low-pass filter (cutoff $\approx 10$ Hz, since breath signals are slow).
4. **Microcontroller** — Implements pressure-to-action mapping.
5. **HID Interface** — Emulates a USB mouse.

**Pressure-to-Action Mapping:**

| Pressure Range | Action | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Sip 5–15 cmH₂O | Soft sip | Cursor moves **LEFT** (slow) |
| Sip > 15 cmH₂O | Hard sip | Cursor moves **LEFT** (fast) or **MODE SWITCH** |
| Puff 5–15 cmH₂O | Soft puff | Cursor moves **RIGHT** (slow) |
| Puff > 15 cmH₂O | Hard puff | Cursor moves **RIGHT** (fast) or **CLICK** |

**Why a Threshold?** The threshold (typically 5 cmH₂O) prevents accidental activation due to ambient pressure fluctuations or the user's normal breathing cycle.

**Scoring breakdown (7 marks):** Principle 2 marks + Components list 2 marks + Mapping table 2 marks + Threshold logic 1 mark.

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(b):

**Working of EMG-Based Cursor Control:**

1. **Surface Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** placed on a residual muscle (e.g., sternocleidomastoid for left/right, frontalis for up/down).
2. **Instrumentation Amplifier** (gain $\approx 1000$) amplifies the differential EMG signal (amplitude $50\ \mu V$ to $5\ mV$).
3. **Bandpass Filter** (20 Hz – 500 Hz) removes DC offset, motion artifact, and high-frequency noise.
4. **Full-Wave Rectifier** converts bipolar EMG to a unipolar signal.
5. **Envelope Detector (Low-Pass Filter)** smooths the rectified signal to produce a slowly varying envelope proportional to muscle contraction strength.
6. **Threshold Comparator** converts the envelope into a binary decision.
7. **Direction Mapper** assigns each muscle to a direction (e.g., left SCM = cursor right, right SCM = cursor left).
8. **HID Mouse Report** is sent to the host.

**Envelope Detector Derivation:**

The envelope detector is a first-order RC low-pass filter applied to the rectified EMG. The input $x(t)$ is the rectified (unipolar) EMG signal; the output $y(t)$ is the smoothed envelope.

The governing ODE is:

$$
RC \cdot \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = x(t)
$$

Solving using the standard first-order step response with initial condition $y(0) = y_0$:

$$
y(t) = y_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC} + \frac{1}{RC} \int_0^t x(\tau) \cdot e^{-(t-\tau)/RC} \, d\tau
$$

For a constant rectified input $x(t) = V_{rect}$, this reduces to:

$$
y(t) = V_{rect} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) + y_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC}
$$

The **time constant** $\tau = RC$ determines the smoothness: larger $\tau$ gives a smoother envelope but slower response. A typical choice is $\tau = 100$ ms, corresponding to a cutoff frequency:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 0.1} \approx 1.59 \text{ Hz}
$$

This cutoff preserves the *voluntary contraction envelope* (which varies over hundreds of milliseconds) while attenuating the underlying EMG ripple at 20–500 Hz.

**Scoring breakdown (7 marks):** Block diagram 3 marks + Rectifier and threshold explanation 1 mark + Envelope derivation 2 marks + Numerical example of $\tau$ and $f_c$ 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the Pupil Center with the Glint.** The pupil is a *dark* region; the glint is a *bright* spot. Reversing them gives an inverted gaze vector.
> 2. **Do NOT write the EAR formula as a single eyelid distance divided by the eye width.** Examiners specifically look for the **sum of two vertical distances** in the numerator (multiplied by 1, not 2). The factor of 2 appears in the denominator.
> 3. **For Sip-and-Puff, do NOT confuse "sip" with "puff."** Sip = **inhale** → cursor LEFT (or in some conventions, RIGHT). Puff = **exhale**. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 4. **In EMG derivations, do NOT skip the time-constant calculation.** Examiners will deduct marks if you state the formula for $\tau = RC$ without computing the corresponding $f_c$ numerically.
> 5. **Always include the units** (cmH₂O for pressure, $\mu V$ or mV for EMG, pixels for screen coordinates). KTU evaluators mark generously for unit consistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- **Definition:** An EPI is an assistive device that produces HID-compliant cursor events from a non-manual physiological input.
- **Five-Stage Pipeline (memorize the order):** Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Feature Extraction → HID Emulation.
- **Five Major Categories:** Optical, Bioelectric (EOG/EMG/EEG), Mechanical-Pneumatic, Acoustic-Ultrasonic, Infrared/Proximity.
- **PCCR Method:** Glint (fixed) + Pupil (mobile) → vector $\rightarrow$ polynomial mapping $\rightarrow$ screen coordinate. Requires 9-point calibration.
- **EAR Formula:** Sum of two vertical eye distances divided by **twice** the horizontal eye width. Threshold $\approx 0.20$ for blink.
- **EOG Equation:** $V_{EOG} = k_{EOG} \cdot \sin(\theta)$, where $\theta$ is the eye rotation angle.
- **EMG Pipeline:** Surface electrodes → Instrumentation amplifier → 20–500 Hz BPF → Rectifier → Envelope detector ($\tau = 100$ ms, $f_c \approx 1.6$ Hz) → Threshold.
- **Sip-and-Puff:** Sip (inhale) and Puff (exhale) are detected by a pressure transducer. Threshold $\approx 5$ cmH₂O distinguishes voluntary action from ambient breathing. Hard sip/puff (>$15$ cmH₂O) is used for clicks or mode-switching.
- **Dwell-Click:** Triggered when cursor remains within radius $R_d \approx 10$ pixels for $T_d \approx 500$ ms. Main pitfall: **Midas Touch** (unintended clicks).
- **Joystick Sigmoid:** $v_{norm} = \text{sign}(v_n) \cdot \left(\frac{\vert v_n \vert - d_z}{1 - d_z}\right)^k$ — deadzone $d_z \approx 0.1$, exponent $k \approx 1.5$–$2.0$.
- **Coordinate Mapping:** $X_s = X_{\min} + \frac{(u - u_{\min})(X_{\max} - X_{\min})}{u_{\max} - u_{\min}}$.
- **Performance Metrics:** Throughput $TP = ID / MT$, Index of Difficulty $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$, Pointing Error $e = \sqrt{(X_t - X_c)^2 + (Y_t - Y_c)^2}$.
- **SNR Formula:** $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$. Target $> 20$ dB for biosignal EPIs.
- **First-Order LPF:** Cutoff $f_c = 1 / (2\pi RC)$, time constant $\tau = RC$, discrete smoothing factor $\alpha = \Delta t / (\tau + \Delta t)$.
- **Standard IR Wavelength:** **850 nm** (invisible to user, strongly reflected by cornea).
- **Clinical Mapping (high-yield):**
  - Locked-in syndrome → **EOG or IR eye tracker** (eye motion only)
  - High SCI (C3–C4) → **Sip-and-puff or head tracker** (no hand use, head motion intact)
  - Cerebral palsy with tremor → **Single-switch scanning** (avoids continuous motor control)
  - Advanced ALS → **EEG/BCI** (no reliable motor output at all)
- **Communication Protocols:** USB HID (most common), Bluetooth HID (wireless), Serial/JOYSTICK (legacy).
- **Recent Trend (for 2-mark "current trends" questions):** AI-based intent prediction using LSTM/Transformer models to anticipate cursor targets, reducing Midas Touch and improving throughput.
- **Regulatory:** Most medical-grade EPIs require **FDA 510(k) clearance** in the US and **CE marking** in the EU.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Electronic Pointing Interfaces**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joysticks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Joysticks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **joystick** is an *input transducer device* that converts physical displacement (linear or angular) or applied force by a user into a proportional electrical control signal, which is then processed by an actuator controller to govern the motion of an external device such as a powered wheelchair, robotic manipulator, or assistive mobility platform.

In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices (PEEBT744, Module 2)**, the joystick functions as the **primary Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** for individuals with motor disabilities, enabling intuitive control over powered mobility devices. The device essentially transforms a user's residual motor capability into a directed command signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Within the scope of Module 2, joysticks are studied as one of the foundational **control input mechanisms** for assistive mobility systems, alongside sip-and-puff systems, head arrays, and switch controls.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy: The Fishing Rod and the Boat**

Imagine you are steering a small boat with a single outboard motor using a **fishing rod mounted at the back**. The rod is anchored at one end (the *pivot*), and you hold the free end (the *handle*).

- When you **push the handle forward**, the boat moves forward.
- When you **pull it back**, the boat reverses.
- When you **tilt it left or right**, the boat turns in that direction.
- The **further** you push or tilt, the **faster** the boat goes.

A joystick operates on exactly this principle — but instead of pushing a boat directly, it *senses* the direction and magnitude of your input and translates it into an electrical command that an electronic controller uses to drive motors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric:**
> A clinically graded assistive joystick must respond to a deflection of **±15° to ±30°** (mechanical) or a force of **~50 g to ~150 g** (isometric), with a typical signal linearity of **±5%** across the working range.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Joysticks (Taxonomy)

```
                    JOYSTICKS
                       │
       ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐
       │               │               │
   MECHANICAL      ISOMETRIC        DISCRETE
   (Position)      (Force)        (Microswitch)
       │               │               │
   ┌───┴───┐       ┌───┴───┐        ┌──┴──┐
   │       │       │       │        │     │
 Potentio-  Encoder  FSR   Hall-    4-way  8-way
 meter             (Force Effect
                   Sensing
                   Resistor)
```

The three primary categories used in **medical assistive technology** are:

| Category | Operating Quantity | Output Type | Common Use |
|----------|--------------------|-------------|------------|
| **Mechanical (Positional)** | Angular/Linear displacement | Analog voltage proportional to position | Standard powered wheelchairs |
| **Isometric (Force-Sensing)** | Applied force (no displacement) | Analog voltage proportional to force | Tremor-affected users, Sip-and-puff alternatives |
| **Discrete (Digital/Microswitch)** | Directional push (4 or 8 positions) | Binary ON/OFF per direction | Severe motor-impairment users, scanning control |

---

## 1.4 Why Joysticks Are Critical in Assistive Technology

A joystick is often the **only viable control interface** for patients with:
- **Spinal cord injury (SCI)** — quadriplegia, paraplegia.
- **Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)**.
- **Muscular dystrophy**.
- **Cerebral palsy** (with appropriate mounting/torque adjustment).
- **Multiple sclerosis** with progressive motor loss.

Because of this clinical centrality, joysticks must meet strict biomedical standards:
- **Operating force:** adjustable (typically **0.5 N to 3 N**).
- **Dead-zone handling:** for users with hand tremors.
- **Durability:** minimum **1 million cycles** (per ISO 7176 series).
- **Ingress Protection:** typically **IP54** or higher for outdoor wheelchairs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Block-Level Decomposition

A joystick operates as a **multi-axis electromechanical transducer**. The following pipeline describes its functional behavior:

### Step 1 — Mechanical Input (User Action)
The user applies either a **deflection** (positional joystick) or a **force** (isometric joystick) along the X-axis (left/right) and Y-axis (forward/reverse).

### Step 2 — Sensing Element Activation
The mechanical input is converted to an electrical signal by a transducer. The most common transducers are:

1. **Potentiometer (Voltage Divider)** — used in classic mechanical joysticks.
2. **Force-Sensing Resistor (FSR)** — used in isometric joysticks.
3. **Hall-Effect Sensor** — used in contactless proportional joysticks.
4. **Optical Encoder** — used in digital/encoder-based joysticks.

### Step 3 — Signal Conditioning
The raw signal is amplified, filtered (low-pass to suppress hand-tremor noise, typically with a **cut-off frequency of 1–3 Hz**), and normalized to a **0 to Vref** range (commonly **0–5 V** or **0–3.3 V**).

### Step 4 — Coordinate Transformation
The X and Y analog signals are converted to a **polar coordinate pair** (magnitude *r* and angle *θ*), since wheelchair motion is best described in polar terms — speed and direction.

### Step 5 — Motor Command Generation
The magnitude *r* controls motor speed (PWM duty cycle), and the angle *θ* controls the **differential steering ratio** between left and right wheels.

---

## 2.2 The Potentiometer-Based Mechanical Joystick (Detailed)

A **potentiometer** is a three-terminal variable resistor. In a joystick, two potentiometers are ganged orthogonally (one for X, one for Y). Each potentiometer operates as a **voltage divider**:

> **Voltage Divider Equation:**
> When the wiper moves along a potentiometer of total resistance $R_{total}$, the output voltage at the wiper is:
>
> $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{total}}$$

where $R_x$ is the resistance between the wiper and the ground terminal.

The output voltage thus varies **linearly** with the joystick deflection angle:

> **Linear Relationship:**
> $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta_x}{\theta_{max}}$$

where $\theta_x$ is the instantaneous deflection angle in the X-direction and $\theta_{max}$ is the maximum mechanical deflection (typically **±25° to ±30°** for assistive devices).

---

## 2.3 The Hall-Effect Isometric Joystick (Detailed)

In modern clinical-grade joysticks, **Hall-effect sensors** are widely preferred because:
- They are **contactless** (no mechanical wear).
- They are immune to dust and moisture.
- They have a typical operational life exceeding **10 million cycles**.

### Physical Principle
When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the current, a **transverse voltage (Hall voltage)** is generated:

> **Hall Voltage Equation:**
> $$V_{H} = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

where:
- $R_H$ = Hall coefficient of the material.
- $I$ = current through the sensor (mA).
- $B$ = magnetic flux density perpendicular to the sensor (Tesla).
- $t$ = thickness of the semiconductor element (mm).

When a small permanent magnet attached to the joystick stick moves closer/farther from the Hall sensor, the magnetic flux density $B$ changes proportionally, modulating $V_H$.

### Joystick-Specific Mapping
$$V_{out,x} = V_{0} + k \cdot B_{x}$$

where $V_0$ is the resting (null) voltage (typically **2.5 V** for a 0–5 V supply), and $k$ is a sensitivity constant.

---

## 2.4 Force-Sensing Resistor (FSR) Isometric Joystick

An **FSR** is a polymer thick-film device whose resistance decreases non-linearly with applied force:

$$R_{FSR} = \frac{k}{F^n}$$

where:
- $F$ = applied force (N).
- $k$ = device constant.
- $n$ = exponent (typically **1.0 to 1.5** for most commercial FSRs).

The FSR is integrated into a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration, and the differential output is proportional to applied force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Note (Tremor Compensation):**
> FSR-based isometric joysticks are preferred for users with **Parkinson's disease** or essential tremor, because the user exerts force without deflecting the stick — tremors produce minimal net force (due to inertial averaging over a stiff mechanical platform), whereas a position-sensing joystick would register tremor directly as motion noise.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------|------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_{total}}$ | Potentiometer voltage divider | $R_x$ wiper resistance, $R_{total}$ full resistance | V, Ω |
| 2 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_x}{\theta_{max}}$ | Linear position-voltage mapping | $\theta_x$ deflection, $\theta_{max}$ max deflection | V, ° |
| 3 | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$ | Hall-effect voltage | $R_H$ Hall coefficient, $I$ current, $B$ flux density, $t$ thickness | V, m³/C, A, T, m |
| 4 | $R_{FSR} = \dfrac{k}{F^n}$ | FSR resistance-force law | $k$ constant, $F$ force, $n$ exponent | Ω, N |
| 5 | $r = \sqrt{V_x^{\,2} + V_y^{\,2}}$ | Polar magnitude from X-Y voltages | $r$ magnitude (speed), $V_x, V_y$ axis voltages | V |
| 6 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_y}{V_x}\right)$ | Polar angle from X-Y voltages | $\theta$ direction angle | rad |
| 7 | $V_{L} = V_{ref} - \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2}(1 - \sin\theta)$ | Left motor speed (differential steering) | $V_L$ left motor command | V |
| 8 | $V_{R} = V_{ref} - \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2}(1 + \sin\theta)$ | Right motor speed (differential steering) | $V_R$ right motor command | V |
| 9 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Low-pass tremor filter cutoff | $R$ filter resistance, $C$ filter capacitance | Hz |
| 10 | $F_{min} = m_{stick} \cdot a_{max}$ | Minimum detectable force (isometric) | $m$ stick mass, $a$ acceleration | N |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Why Joystick? |
|-------|-------------|---------------|
| **Powered Wheelchairs** | Primary drive controller | Proportional speed & direction from a single intuitive input |
| **Electric Prosthetic Arms** | End-effector control | 2-axis deflection → 2 degrees of freedom |
| **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci, etc.)** | Master console manipulator input | Force feedback and high-precision proportional control |
| **CCTV / Electric Bed Controls** | Bed positioning, camera panning | Simple 4-directional motion in a small footprint |
| **Rehabilitation Robotics (Lokomat, Armeo)** | Game-based motor therapy | Engaging, intuitive control for pediatric/adult rehab |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Potentiometer Output vs. Deflection Angle

### Statement
Show that the voltage at the wiper of a linear potentiometer is linearly proportional to the deflection angle of the joystick stick.

### Setup
A linear potentiometer has total resistance $R_{total}$ with a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ applied across it. The wiper position corresponds to the stick angle.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
Let the joystick stick pivot at its base. When the stick is at its neutral position, the wiper is at the center of the potentiometer track, so the resistance on each side is $R_{total}/2$.

When the stick deflects by an angle $\theta_x$, the wiper moves along the track. The fraction of the total track length traversed is:

$$\frac{\Delta x}{L} = \frac{\theta_x}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Resistance as a function of deflection.**
Since the track resistance is uniformly distributed:

$$R_x = R_{total} \cdot \frac{\theta_x}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage divider rule.**
With $R_x$ between the wiper and ground, and $R_{total} - R_x$ between the wiper and $V_{ref}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_x + (R_{total} - R_x)}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_{total}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**
Substituting the expression for $R_x$ from Step 2:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{total} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_x}{\theta_{max}}}{R_{total}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta_x}{\theta_{max}}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The output voltage varies linearly with the deflection angle. For example, with $V_{ref} = 5~\text{V}$, $\theta_{max} = 30°$:
- At $\theta_x = +30°$ (full forward) → $V_{out} = +5~\text{V}$
- At $\theta_x = 0°$ (neutral) → $V_{out} = +2.5~\text{V}$
- At $\theta_x = -30°$ (full reverse) → $V_{out} = 0~\text{V}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polar Coordinate Transformation (Cartesian to Speed/Direction)

### Statement
Convert the two-axis analog voltages ($V_x$, $V_y$) from a joystick into a speed magnitude and a direction angle for differential wheelchair steering.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the inputs.**
The joystick outputs:
- $V_x$ — left/right axis voltage, centered at $V_{ref}/2$.
- $V_y$ — forward/reverse axis voltage, centered at $V_{ref}/2$.

**Step 2 — Normalize to a [-1, +1] range.**
Define normalized coordinates:

$$x = \frac{2 V_x}{V_{ref}} - 1$$

$$y = \frac{2 V_y}{V_{ref}} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude (speed).**
$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

This is the **speed command**, mapped linearly to PWM duty cycle (or motor voltage). It ranges from 0 (no motion) to 1 (full speed).

**Step 4 — Compute the direction angle.**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

This is the **steering direction**, where $\theta = 0$ corresponds to pure forward motion, $\theta = \pm\pi/2$ corresponds to pure turning, and $\theta = \pi$ corresponds to pure reverse.

**Step 5 — Apply a dead-zone (tremor suppression).**
If $r < r_{dead}$, set $r = 0$. Typical clinical values: $r_{dead} = 0.05$ to $0.15$.

**Step 6 — Apply a shaping function (progressive gain).**
To give finer low-speed control, a non-linear shaping is applied:

$$r_{final} = r^{p}, \quad p > 1$$

Typical values: $p = 1.5$ to $2.0$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential Steering Motor Commands

### Statement
For a two-wheel differential-drive wheelchair, derive the left and right motor commands as functions of the joystick speed $r$ and direction $\theta$.

### Setup
For a differential-drive robot, the left and right wheel velocities are:

$$v_L = v \cdot (1 - \sin\theta \cdot k)$$

$$v_R = v \cdot (1 + \sin\theta \cdot k)$$

where $v = r \cdot v_{max}$ is the forward speed and $k$ is a steering gain (typically $k = 1$ for in-place turning at $\theta = \pm \pi/2$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the motor command voltages.**
The motor driver accepts PWM inputs $V_L$ and $V_R$ ranging from $0$ to $V_{ref}$.

**Step 2 — Derive the forward-motion case.**
When $\theta = 0$ (pure forward), $\sin\theta = 0$:
- $V_L = V_{ref} \cdot v_{L}/v_{max} = V_{ref} \cdot r$
- $V_R = V_{ref} \cdot v_{R}/v_{max} = V_{ref} \cdot r$
Both motors are equal → straight-line motion.

**Step 3 — Derive the in-place turn case.**
When $\theta = +\pi/2$ (pure left turn), $\sin\theta = +1$:
- $V_L = V_{ref} \cdot r \cdot (1 - 1) = 0$
- $V_R = V_{ref} \cdot r \cdot (1 + 1) = 2 V_{ref} \cdot r$

To keep the output within $V_{ref}$, we normalize:

$$\boxed{V_L = V_{ref} \cdot r \cdot \frac{(1 - \sin\theta)}{2} \cdot 2 = V_{ref} \cdot r \cdot (1 - \sin\theta)}$$

$$\boxed{V_R = V_{ref} \cdot r \cdot (1 + \sin\theta)}$$

A common safer formulation:

$$V_L = \frac{V_{ref}}{2} \cdot r \cdot (1 - \sin\theta)$$

$$V_R = \frac{V_{ref}}{2} \cdot r \cdot (1 + \sin\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.**
Let $V_{ref} = 24~\text{V}$ (typical wheelchair battery bus), $r = 0.6$, $\theta = 30°$ ($\sin\theta = 0.5$):

$$V_L = \frac{24}{2} \cdot 0.6 \cdot (1 - 0.5) = 12 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.5 = 3.6~\text{V}$$

$$V_R = \frac{24}{2} \cdot 0.6 \cdot (1 + 0.5) = 12 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1.5 = 10.8~\text{V}$$

The right wheel spins faster than the left → the wheelchair curves to the **left**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Low-Pass Filter for Hand Tremor Suppression

### Statement
Design a first-order RC low-pass filter to suppress Parkinsonian hand tremor (typical frequency **4–6 Hz**) while preserving intentional joystick motion (typically **< 1 Hz**).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Filter transfer function.**
For a first-order RC low-pass filter:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2 — Choose the cutoff frequency.**
The cutoff should lie **between** the tremor band and the intentional-motion band. A common clinical value is $f_c = 2~\text{Hz}$.

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 3 — Choose the capacitor.**
Let $C = 0.1~\mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}~\text{F}$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2 \cdot 10^{-7}}$$

$$R \approx \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 795{,}775~\Omega \approx 796~\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Attenuation check.**
At $f = 5~\text{Hz}$ (tremor):

$$|H| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (5/2)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{7.25}} \approx 0.371$$

So tremor is attenuated to about **37%** — a significant reduction. At $f = 0.5~\text{Hz}$ (intentional motion):

$$|H| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (0.5/2)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.0625}} \approx 0.970$$

Intentional motion passes through with **97%** fidelity.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — End-to-End Joystick-to-Wheelchair Calculation

**Given:**
- Joystick: potentiometer type, $V_{ref} = 5~\text{V}$, $\theta_{max} = \pm 30°$.
- User deflection: $\theta_x = +15°$, $\theta_y = -10°$.
- Low-pass filter cutoff: $f_c = 2~\text{Hz}$ (already applied).
- Wheelchair bus voltage: $V_{bat} = 24~\text{V}$.

**Find:** Left and right motor commands.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute raw voltages.**

$$V_x = 5 \cdot \frac{15}{30} = 2.5~\text{V}$$

$$V_y = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{30} = 1.667~\text{V}$$

(The signs indicate direction — assume positive = forward and right.)

**Step 2 — Normalize.**

$$x = \frac{2 \cdot 2.5}{5} - 1 = 0 \quad (\text{pure left/right axis no motion})$$

Wait — for the centered case we should re-center. Assuming the neutral is at 2.5 V:

$$x = \frac{2.5 - 2.5}{2.5} = 0$$

$$y = \frac{1.667 - 2.5}{2.5} = -0.333 \quad (\text{slight reverse})$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude and angle.**

$$r = \sqrt{0^2 + (-0.333)^2} = 0.333$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-0.333}{0}\right) = -\frac{\pi}{2} \quad (\text{pure reverse, since x = 0})$$

**Step 4 — Motor commands.**

$$V_L = \frac{24}{2} \cdot 0.333 \cdot (1 - \sin(-\pi/2)) = 12 \cdot 0.333 \cdot 2 = 8.0~\text{V}$$

$$V_R = \frac{24}{2} \cdot 0.333 \cdot (1 + \sin(-\pi/2)) = 12 \cdot 0.333 \cdot 0 = 0~\text{V}$$

So the wheelchair reverses by **spinning the left wheel only**, with the right wheel stationary — a tight reverse turn to the right.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Joystick Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER HAND] -->|Applies force or deflection| B[MECHANICAL STICK]
    B --> C[SENSING ELEMENT]
    C -->|Potentiometer or FSR or Hall sensor| D[SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    D --> E[LOW-PASS FILTER]
    E --> F[ADC / MICROCONTROLLER]
    F --> G[POLAR COORDINATE TRANSFORM]
    G --> H[DIFFERENTIAL STEERING COMPUTATION]
    H --> I[LEFT MOTOR DRIVER]
    H --> J[RIGHT MOTOR DRIVER]
    I --> K[LEFT WHEEL]
    J --> L[RIGHT WHEEL]
    F --> M[DISPLAY FEEDBACK]
    M --> N[LCD OR BUZZER]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Modern Isometric Joystick

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Force Input X-axis] --> Q1[FSR Sensor X]
    P2[Force Input Y-axis] --> Q2[FSR Sensor Y]
    Q1 --> R1[Wheatstone Bridge X]
    Q2 --> R2[Wheatstone Bridge Y]
    R1 --> S1[Instrumentation Amplifier X]
    R2 --> S2[Instrumentation Amplifier Y]
    S1 --> T1[Tremor Low-Pass Filter X]
    S2 --> T2[Tremor Low-Pass Filter Y]
    T1 --> U[Microcontroller ADC Input]
    T2 --> U
    U --> V[Polar Transform and Dead-Zone]
    V --> W[Output to Motor Controller]
    W --> X[CAN bus or PWM output]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Joystick Classification (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[JOYSTICK DEVICE] --> B{Sensing Principle}
    B -->|Mechanical displacement| C[Positional Joystick]
    B -->|Applied force| D[Isometric Joystick]
    B -->|Discrete switch| E[Digital Joystick]
    C --> C1[Potentiometer based]
    C --> C2[Optical Encoder based]
    C --> C3[Hall Effect based with magnet on stick]
    D --> D1[FSR based]
    D --> D2[Strain Gauge based]
    D --> D3[Hall Effect force sensor]
    E --> E1[4-Way Microswitch]
    E --> E2[8-Way Microswitch]
    E --> E3[Membrane Keypad Type]
    C1 --> F1[Clinical Use: Standard powered wheelchair]
    D1 --> F2[Clinical Use: Tremor affected users]
    E1 --> F3[Clinical Use: Severe motor impairment]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Flow — From Stick Deflection to Wheel Motion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Stick at Neutral] --> S1[Resting Position]
    S1 --> S2[Potentiometer reads Vref divided by 2]
    S2 --> S3[Microcontroller reads 2.5 V on both axes]
    S3 --> S4[Compute r and theta]
    S4 --> S5{r greater than deadzone}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Motors remain off]
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Compute left and right voltages]
    S7 --> S8[Send PWM signals to motor drivers]
    S8 --> S9[Wheelchair accelerates in commanded direction]
    S9 --> S10[Continuous feedback loop at 50 to 100 Hz]
    S10 --> S3
```

---

## 4.5 Comparative Architecture — Joystick vs. Other Assistive Inputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER WITH DISABILITY] --> B{Residual Motor Capability}
    B -->|Hand and arm intact| C[JOYSTICK CONTROL]
    B -->|Limited hand use| D[HEAD ARRAY or CHIN JOYSTICK]
    B -->|No hand use, breathing OK| E[SIP AND PUFF]
    B -->|No hand use, no breath control| F[EYE TRACKING or BRAIN COMPUTER INTERFACE]
    C --> G[Wheelchair, Robot Arm, Computer]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of an isometric joystick with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **isometric joystick** is a force-sensing input device that produces an output signal proportional to the **force applied** by the user, rather than the displacement of the stick. The mechanical stick is mounted on a stiff spring or flexure, so it does **not visibly deflect** when force is applied.

The working principle involves:

1. The user applies a force $F$ on the stick handle along the X or Y axis.
2. This force is transmitted to a **Force-Sensing Resistor (FSR)** or **strain gauge** mounted at the base.
3. The FSR's resistance changes according to $R_{FSR} = k/F^n$, unbalancing a **Wheatstone bridge**.
4. The differential bridge voltage is amplified by an instrumentation amplifier to produce a proportional analog signal in the range $0$ to $V_{ref}$.
5. The microcontroller reads this signal and computes speed/direction commands.

**Key advantage:** Because the stick does not move, hand tremor produces minimal net force, making it ideal for **Parkinson's patients** and users with intention tremor.

*(Examiner's note: A neat diagram showing the stick, FSR, and bridge circuit fetches full marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare mechanical (positional) and isometric joysticks. Mention one clinical advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Mechanical (Positional) Joystick | Isometric Joystick |
|-----------|----------------------------------|---------------------|
| Input quantity | Stick displacement (angle) | Applied force |
| Sensing element | Potentiometer / Hall sensor | FSR / Strain gauge |
| Stick movement | Visible deflection | Negligible movement |
| Tremor sensitivity | **High** (tremor = noise) | **Low** (tremor averaged out) |
| Wear and tear | Higher (moving parts) | Lower (no significant motion) |

- **Clinical advantage of mechanical joystick:** Provides direct **visual feedback** of position to the user — easier for users with good hand control and cognitive ability (e.g., spinal cord injury patients).
- **Clinical advantage of isometric joystick:** Ideal for users with **Parkinson's disease or essential tremor**, since the lack of stick motion filters out tremor.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a **powered wheelchair joystick control system**. Explain each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for the **left and right motor voltages** of a differential-drive wheelchair in terms of the joystick's normalized X-Y voltages. Apply your derivation to compute the motor commands when $V_x = 3.0~\text{V}$, $V_y = 4.0~\text{V}$, and $V_{ref} = 5.0~\text{V}$, $V_{bat} = 24~\text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

The functional architecture of a powered wheelchair joystick control system consists of the following sequential blocks:

**Block 1 — Joystick (Mechanical Input Stage):**
The joystick is a two-axis proportional input device. The user applies a deflection or force along the X (left/right) and Y (forward/reverse) axes.

**Block 2 — Sensing Element:**
Two potentiometers (mechanical type) or two Hall-effect sensors (modern type) are mounted orthogonally. Each produces a voltage proportional to its axis deflection:
$V_x = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_x}{\theta_{max}}, \quad V_y = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_y}{\theta_{max}}$

**Block 3 — Signal Conditioning:**
The two voltages are buffered, amplified, and passed through a **first-order low-pass RC filter** with $f_c \approx 2~\text{Hz}$ to remove hand tremor noise.

**Block 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):**
The conditioned analog signals are digitized by the microcontroller's ADC (typically 10-bit or 12-bit resolution).

**Block 5 — Coordinate Transformation:**
The digital X-Y values are re-centered (subtracting the neutral value $V_{ref}/2$) and normalized to a $[-1, +1]$ range.

**Block 6 — Polar Transform:**
The speed $r$ and direction $\theta$ are computed:
$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$
A dead-zone ($r < 0.1$) suppresses tremor-induced drift.

**Block 7 — Differential Steering Computation:**
Left and right motor voltages are calculated using:
$V_L = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{2} \cdot r \cdot (1 - \sin\theta)$
$V_R = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{2} \cdot r \cdot (1 + \sin\theta)$

**Block 8 — Motor Driver and Motors:**
PWM signals derived from $V_L$ and $V_R$ drive H-bridge motor drivers (e.g., VNH2SP30), which in turn drive the left and right DC motors.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (already in Section 3.3):**
We use:
$$x = \frac{2V_x}{V_{ref}} - 1, \quad y = \frac{2V_y}{V_{ref}} - 1$$
$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$$

**Numerical Application:**

**Step 1 — Normalize.**
$$x = \frac{2 \cdot 3.0}{5.0} - 1 = 1.2 - 1 = 0.2$$
$$y = \frac{2 \cdot 4.0}{5.0} - 1 = 1.6 - 1 = 0.6$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude.**
$$r = \sqrt{0.2^2 + 0.6^2} = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.36} = \sqrt{0.40} \approx 0.632$$

**Step 3 — Direction.**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.6 / 0.2) = \tan^{-1}(3) \approx 71.57°$$
$$\sin\theta \approx 0.949$$

**Step 4 — Motor voltages.**
$$V_L = \frac{24}{2} \cdot 0.632 \cdot (1 - 0.949) = 12 \cdot 0.632 \cdot 0.051 \approx 0.387~\text{V}$$

$$V_R = \frac{24}{2} \cdot 0.632 \cdot (1 + 0.949) = 12 \cdot 0.632 \cdot 1.949 \approx 14.78~\text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The right motor spins much faster than the left → wheelchair turns sharply to the **left** while moving forward.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the **Hall-effect principle**. Describe how it is used in a modern contactless joystick. State two advantages over the potentiometer-based joystick. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **first-order RC low-pass filter** to suppress a hand tremor of frequency 5 Hz. Choose $C = 0.1~\mu\text{F}$ and justify your cutoff frequency choice. Compute the attenuation at the tremor frequency and at 0.5 Hz (intentional motion). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Hall-Effect Principle (3 marks):**
When a current $I$ flows through a conductor or semiconductor placed in a magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the direction of current flow, a **transverse voltage** is generated across the conductor in the direction perpendicular to both $I$ and $B$. This is the **Hall effect**, and the generated voltage is:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $B$ is the magnetic flux density, $I$ is the current, and $t$ is the thickness of the sensing element.

This effect was discovered by **Edwin Hall** in 1879.

**Use in a Contactless Joystick (3 marks):**
In a Hall-effect joystick, a small **permanent magnet** is mounted on the lower end of the joystick stick. As the user deflects the stick, the magnet moves in a defined arc over a fixed **semiconductor Hall sensor** (usually a silicon IC such as the SS49E or Allegro A1302). The magnetic flux density $B$ at the sensor changes with the deflection, modulating the Hall voltage $V_H$. This voltage is internally amplified and offset (typically to $V_{ref}/2$ at the null position), producing an analog output:

$$V_{out} = V_0 + k \cdot B_{x}$$

A second Hall sensor is mounted perpendicular for the Y-axis.

**Two Advantages over Potentiometer-Based Joystick (1 mark):**
1. **No mechanical contact** → longer life (typically > 10 million cycles vs. ~1 million for potentiometer).
2. **Immune to dust, moisture, and oxidation** → higher reliability, suitable for outdoor wheelchairs and harsh clinical environments.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Choose the cutoff frequency (2 marks).**
Hand tremor (Parkinsonian) typically lies in the band **4–6 Hz**. Intentional joystick motion is **below 1 Hz**. The cutoff should lie **between** these two bands. We choose:

$$f_c = 2~\text{Hz}$$

This provides at least a 2:1 frequency separation, with a smooth roll-off.

**Step 2 — Compute the resistor value (2 marks).**
Given $C = 0.1~\mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}~\text{F}$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2 \cdot 10^{-7}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 795{,}775~\Omega \approx 796~\text{k}\Omega$$

Use a standard value of **$R = 820~\text{k}\Omega$**.

**Step 3 — Attenuation at tremor frequency (1.5 marks).**
At $f = 5~\text{Hz}$:

$$|H(f)| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (5/2)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{7.25}} \approx 0.371$$

So tremor is reduced to **37.1%** of its original amplitude — a **62.9% reduction**.

**Step 4 — Attenuation at intentional motion (1.5 marks).**
At $f = 0.5~\text{Hz}$:

$$|H(f)| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (0.5/2)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.0625}} \approx 0.970$$

Intentional motion passes through with **97%** fidelity — a **3% reduction**, which is imperceptible.

**Conclusion:** The filter effectively suppresses tremor while preserving intentional control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in $\sin\theta$:** Students often reverse the sign convention, causing the wheelchair to turn in the wrong direction. Always verify with the convention: "Positive $\theta$ = left turn → right motor faster."
> 2. **Forgetting the dead-zone:** Writing $r$ directly to motors without applying a dead-zone makes the wheelchair drift at rest — a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing up $V_{ref}$ (joystick supply, e.g., 5 V) with $V_{bat}$ (wheelchair battery, e.g., 24 V) in the differential steering formula. The joystick voltages are normalized, then scaled to $V_{bat}$.
> 4. **Confusing $r$ and $\theta$:** $r$ = speed magnitude, $\theta$ = direction. They are independent quantities — do not multiply them as if they were a single product before polar decomposition.
> 5. **Hall-effect formula errors:** Forgetting that $t$ is in the denominator — students write $V_H = R_H \cdot I \cdot B \cdot t$ instead of the correct $V_H = R_H \cdot I \cdot B / t$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- A **joystick** is the most common assistive input device — converts user deflection/force into electrical control signals for powered mobility.
- Three main types: **mechanical (positional)**, **isometric (force-sensing)**, and **discrete (digital/microswitch)**.
- The **voltage divider** equation $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot R_x / R_{total}$ is the foundation of potentiometer-based joysticks.
- **Hall-effect sensors** are preferred in modern clinical devices due to contactless operation and longer life (>10 M cycles).
- **FSR-based isometric joysticks** are ideal for tremor-affected users because the lack of motion naturally filters tremor.
- The **polar transform** $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$ converts Cartesian joystick voltages to speed and direction.
- The **differential steering equations** $V_L = \frac{V_{bat}}{2} r (1 - \sin\theta)$ and $V_R = \frac{V_{bat}}{2} r (1 + \sin\theta)$ map the polar values to left and right motor commands.
- A **low-pass filter** with cutoff around **2 Hz** is standard for tremor suppression; tremor (4–6 Hz) is attenuated while intentional motion (<1 Hz) is preserved.
- The **dead-zone** ($r < 0.05$–$0.15$) prevents drift at neutral and is essential for users with involuntary micro-motions.
- Joystick clinical standards follow **ISO 7176** (wheelchairs) and **IEC 60601** (medical electrical equipment safety).
- Modern joysticks use **microcontroller-based signal processing** with **ADC**, **DSP filtering**, and **CAN bus** communication to motor controllers.
- Adjustable operating force (typically **0.5 N to 3 N**) is a key clinical feature for accommodating users with varying strength.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eye-Gaze Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eye-Gaze Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

An **Eye-Gaze System** (also termed a *gaze-tracking system* or *eye-tracker based assistive interface*) is a non-invasive, contact-less biomedical instrumentation device that detects, measures, quantifies, and interprets the orientation and movement of a user's ocular globe to derive a real-time point-of-regard (POR) vector. In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices**, it functions as a Human-Computer Interface (HCI) for individuals with severe motor impairments — such as Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS), locked-in syndrome, quadriplegia, high-level spinal cord injury, or advanced cerebral palsy — enabling them to operate computers, speech-generating devices (SGDs), powered wheelchairs, and environmental control units (ECUs) solely through voluntary eye movements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** The KTU syllabus frames the eye-gaze system as an *alternative pointing and selection modality* — replacing the mouse/keyboard/joystick — rather than merely a diagnostic ophthalmic instrument. Remember this distinction for ESE answers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a person lying in bed who is fully conscious and intelligent, but whose hands, arms, and even voice are paralyzed — like the character in the film *The Diving Bell and the Butterfly*. The only part of their body they can reliably move and control is their **eyes**. Now imagine you attach a small, polite "observer" — like a tiny security camera — just below their computer screen. This camera watches which spot on the screen the person is *looking at*, and the computer automatically moves the cursor to that spot. If the person simply *stares* at a letter (called a **dwell-time** trigger) or blinks deliberately, the letter is "typed." The whole room has effectively turned their eyes into a wireless, hands-free mouse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world deployment example:** The Tobii Dynavox I-series and I-13 are clinical-grade eye-gaze AAC devices used by patients with ALS; the user simply looks at a symbol grid and the device speaks it out. Sampling rates in commercial systems are typically **60 Hz to 1200 Hz** with gaze accuracy around **0.4° to 1.0°** of visual angle.

## 1.3 Core Functional Goals of an Eye-Gaze System

| Functional Goal | Engineering Translation |
|---|---|
| **Pointing** | Map gaze vector → screen coordinates (x, y) |
| **Selection** | Detect dwell (≥ 300–1000 ms) or intentional blink |
| **Communication** | Trigger on-screen keyboard or symbol-based AAC board |
| **Mobility** | Drive powered wheelchair via gaze-based direction selection |
| **Environmental Control** | Send IR/relay commands to TV, lights, bed via gaze + blink |
| **Diagnosis** | Quantify saccades, fixation stability, nystagmus, smooth pursuit |

## 1.4 Physiological & Biomechanical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a 14-mark KTU question, you **must** be able to relate the engineering system to the underlying eye anatomy. Examiners award marks for this linkage.

The human eye rotates within the orbit. Three pairs of extra-ocular muscles (LR, MR, SR, IR, SO, IO) — innervated by Cranial Nerves III, IV, and VI — control horizontal, vertical, and torsional rotations. A **saccade** (ballistic jump) typically lasts **30–80 ms** and spans **20°–40°** in amplitude, while **fixations** (voluntary gaze lock) last **200–600 ms**. A standard eye-gaze system exploits these characteristics by recording the eye's pose during *fixations* and rejecting saccadic noise.

The principal measurement axes of ocular movement are:
- **Horizontal gaze angle (θₕ):** Left–Right rotation, ±35° physiological range
- **Vertical gaze angle (θᵥ):** Up–Down rotation, ±30° physiological range
- **Torsional angle (φ):** Rotation about the line of sight, ±10°
- **Pupil diameter (d):** Autonomic indicator, 2–8 mm range

A standard clinical specification: **0.5° gaze accuracy** corresponds to roughly **1 cm on a screen at 1 m viewing distance**, which is sufficient to disambiguate keys on a 17-inch QWERTY keyboard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Layered Architecture

An eye-gaze system operates in **four sequential engineering layers**, each performing a distinct signal-processing role. KTU expects you to be able to *draw and label* this block chain in a 7-mark sub-question.

**Layer 1 — Illumination & Image Acquisition**
- Near-Infrared (NIR) LEDs (typically **850 nm** or **940 nm** wavelength) flood the eye to produce a *bright-pupil* or *dark-pupil* effect.
- A CMOS/CCD camera sensor captures eye images at **30–1200 fps** depending on the model class.
- A *hot mirror* or dichroic filter allows the camera to see reflected IR while the user sees an unobstructed display.

**Layer 2 — Feature Extraction**
- **Pupil Centre (PC)** is localized via ellipse-fitting (e.g., RANSAC, Hough transform) on the dark-pupil image.
- **Corneal Reflection (CR)**, also called the *glint*, is the reflection of the IR LED off the corneal apex.
- The **Pupil–CR vector (PC–CR)** is the geometry that *mathematically encodes* where the user is looking.

**Layer 3 — Gaze Estimation / Mapping**
- A polynomial regression or geometric 3D model maps the PC–CR vector to a screen point (xₛ, yₛ).
- Calibration is performed by asking the user to sequentially look at 1, 5, 9, or more on-screen points.

**Layer 4 — Application / Action Translation**
- Dwell-timer or blink-detector produces a "click" event.
- The host OS receives a standard mouse event (HID-compliant) and acts on it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NIR (850–940 nm) and not visible light?**
> - **Invisible to the user** — does not distract.
> - Produces a **high-contrast pupil** because the retina retro-reflects IR strongly (red-eye effect).
> - Safe at low power — eye-safety limit is **< 10 mW/cm²** for continuous exposure per IEC/ISO 15004-2.

## 2.2 Two Dominant Gaze-Estimation Paradigms

### (a) 2D Regression-Based (Appearance / Interpolation)
A polynomial function maps the *image-plane coordinates* of pupil and glint directly to screen coordinates. Simple, fast, head-motion intolerant.

$$x_s = f_1(u_p, v_p, u_g, v_g)$$

$$y_s = f_2(u_p, v_p, u_g, v_g)$$

### (b) 3D Model-Based (Geometric)
A 3D eye model reconstructs the optical axis and visual axis. Uses corneal sphere geometry, pupil ellipse, and multiple glints. Head-motion tolerant, computationally heavy.

## 2.3 EOG (Electro-Oculography) — A Sub-Technique Worth Knowing

Before video-based trackers, EOG was the classical medical method. Skin electrodes placed around the orbit measure the **corneo-retinal standing potential** (≈ **0.4 to 1.0 mV**). When the eye rotates, this dipole rotates, producing a voltage difference proportional to gaze angle.

$$V_{EOG} = k \cdot \theta_{gaze}$$

where the proportionality constant $k$ is in the range **5–20 µV per degree** of gaze. Useful for KTU Part-A 3-mark questions on *recording ocular movements*.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below — these are the equations most often asked in 14-mark derivations or 3-mark Part-A questions.

| # | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Unit / Range | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{EOG} = k \cdot \theta_{gaze}$ | Standing-potential voltage proportional to gaze angle | $k \approx 5\text{–}20\ \mu\text{V}/°$ | EOG-based systems |
| 2 | $\theta_{gaze} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)$ | Visual angle subtended by a screen offset $d$ at distance $D$ | degrees, radians | Geometric mapping |
| 3 | $\Delta = \left\vert \vec{P} - \vec{G} \right\vert$ | Pupil–glint displacement vector in image plane | pixels | PC-CR vector |
| 4 | $\vec{g} = \dfrac{\vec{P} - \vec{C_c}}{R_c}$ | Normalized gaze direction from corneal center $C_c$ of radius $R_c$ | unitless | 3D model |
| 5 | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\vec{g} \cdot \vec{s}}{\vert \vec{g} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{s} \vert}\right)$ | Angular offset between gaze vector $\vec{g}$ and screen-point vector $\vec{s}$ | degrees | Accuracy metric |
| 6 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion for saccade capture | Hz; saccade $f_{max} \approx 50$ Hz, so $f_s \geq 100$ Hz | Sampling design |
| 7 | $A_{err} = \sqrt{(x_s - x_t)^2 + (y_s - y_t)^2}$ | Pointing accuracy error between predicted $x_s$ and true target $x_t$ | cm or degrees | Calibration metric |
| 8 | $T_{dwell} = 300\text{–}1000$ ms | Time user must fixate to trigger a "click" | ms | Selection logic |
| 9 | $P_{IR} < 10\ \text{mW/cm}^2$ | Eye-safety IR irradiance limit (IEC/ISO 15004-2) | mW/cm² | Safety compliance |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio of pupil-detection image | dB; typical $\geq 20$ dB | Image processing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical symbol-convention:** In the equations above, $u_p, v_p$ = pupil-center pixel coordinates; $u_g, v_g$ = glint pixel coordinates; $x_s, y_s$ = predicted screen coordinates; $x_t, y_t$ = true target coordinates.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond AAC, eye-gaze systems are central to:
- **Wheelchair control** via Tobii Pro Spectrum → controller → drive electronics
- **ALS clinical trials** as a primary outcome measure (e.g., ALSFRS-R eye subscores)
- **Neuroscience research** — reading studies, autism diagnostics, ADHD screening
- **Avionics & automotive** — driver drowsiness detection (PERCLOS metric)
- **Surgical robotics** — surgeon gaze-contingent view zooming (Da Vinci systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners award full marks only when the answer connects the *device principle* to the *clinical/disability context*. Always end a 14-mark answer with one sentence on the **clinical beneficiary population**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Gaze Angle from Pupil–Glint Geometry (2D Case)

We derive the horizontal gaze angle $\theta_h$ as a function of the pupil-center pixel coordinate $u_p$ and the corneal-reflection (glint) coordinate $u_g$, assuming a head-stabilized user.

**Step 1 — Define the camera's image-plane coordinate system.**
Let the optical axis of the camera be the $z$-axis. The image sensor's horizontal axis is the $u$-axis (in pixels). The user is at distance $D$ (in metres) from the camera lens.

**Step 2 — Relate a 3D gaze vector to a 2D image-plane displacement.**
When the eye rotates by a small angle $\theta_h$, the pupil appears to shift in the image. For small angles, the pixel shift of the pupil centre relative to the *fixed glint* is linearly proportional to $\theta_h$.

$$u_p - u_g = m \cdot f \cdot \tan(\theta_h)$$

where:
- $m$ = camera magnification factor
- $f$ = focal length of the camera lens (in pixels)
- $u_g$ is treated as the *reference anchor* because the glint is on the cornea, which is *stationary* during pure eye rotation.

**Step 3 — Apply the small-angle approximation.**
For gaze angles less than $20°$ to $25°$, $\tan(\theta_h) \approx \theta_h$ (in radians). The approximation is justified because voluntary computer use rarely exceeds $15°$ from straight ahead.

$$u_p - u_g \approx m \cdot f \cdot \theta_h$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the gaze angle.**

$$\theta_h = \dfrac{u_p - u_g}{m \cdot f}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to screen coordinates using the viewing geometry.**
If a screen point lies at a horizontal offset $X_s$ from the screen centre and the user sits at perpendicular distance $D$ from the screen, the visual angle to that point is:

$$\theta_{h,target} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_s}{D}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Equate the eye's measured angle to the target angle to compute the screen point.**

$$\theta_h = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_s}{D}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and solve for $X_s$.**

$$X_s = D \cdot \tan(\theta_h)$$

**Step 8 — Replace $\theta_h$ with the camera-pixel expression.**

$$X_s = D \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{u_p - u_g}{m \cdot f}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Final compact form, with the same derivation holding for the vertical axis:**

$$X_s = D \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{u_p - u_g}{m \cdot f}\right)$$

$$Y_s = D \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{v_p - v_g}{m \cdot f}\right)$$

This is the *core mapping equation* of a 2D polynomial-regression eye-gaze system. A KTU 14-mark question will frequently ask you to derive it.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Accuracy Calculation for a Dwell-Based Selection

Suppose the user fixates a screen icon for $T_{dwell}$. The system samples the gaze at $f_s$ Hz. Compute the standard deviation of the cursor's position noise and the resulting *effective accuracy*.

**Step 1 — Number of samples during dwell.**

$$N = f_s \cdot T_{dwell}$$

**Step 2 — For a 1° gaze-noise standard deviation (typical for entry-level trackers) and a 1-metre viewing distance, the spatial noise on the screen is:**

$$\sigma_{screen} = D \cdot \tan(1°) \approx 0.01745 \cdot D$$

**Step 3 — Plug in $D = 0.6$ m (typical monitor distance).**

$$\sigma_{screen} = 0.6 \cdot 0.01745 = 0.01047\ \text{m} \approx 1.05\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 4 — Averaging over $N$ samples reduces noise by $\sqrt{N}$:**

$$\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_{screen}}{\sqrt{N}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $f_s = 60$ Hz and $T_{dwell} = 0.5$ s:**

$$N = 60 \cdot 0.5 = 30$$

$$\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{1.05\ \text{cm}}{\sqrt{30}} \approx 0.19\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion: a 60 Hz tracker with 0.5 s dwell can localize the gaze point to within ±0.4 cm (2σ) on a 60 cm monitor — sufficient to disambiguate keys spaced 1.5 cm apart.**

## 3.3 Derivation C — Pupil–Glint Vector Stability Under Head Motion

One of the central engineering challenges is that *both* the pupil and the glint translate together if the head moves. We now show that the *difference vector* is invariant to rigid head translation.

**Step 1 — Define the pupil center in 3D space as $\vec{P_0}$ and the glint center as $\vec{G_0}$.**

**Step 2 — Under a head translation $\vec{T}$, both points translate identically:**

$$\vec{P} = \vec{P_0} + \vec{T}$$

$$\vec{G} = \vec{G_0} + \vec{T}$$

**Step 3 — Form the PC-CR vector:**

$$\Delta = \vec{P} - \vec{G} = (\vec{P_0} + \vec{T}) - (\vec{G_0} + \vec{T})$$

**Step 4 — The translation $\vec{T}$ cancels:**

$$\Delta = \vec{P_0} - \vec{G_0}$$

**Step 5 — Result: the pupil–glint vector is invariant under pure head translation.** This is the single most important theoretical reason why eye-gaze systems use the *PC-CR* vector instead of the pupil position alone. KTU examiners frequently test this in 3-mark conceptual questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Video-Based Eye-Gaze System

The diagram below is the canonical *signal-flow* representation. KTU examiners expect you to reproduce this — or a close variant — in any 14-mark question on the topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER EYE] --> B[IR LED ILLUMINATOR 850 nm]
    B --> C[EYE IMAGE ONTO CMOS SENSOR]
    C --> D[FRAME BUFFER FRAME GRABBER]
    D --> E[PUPIL LOCALIZATION ELLIPSE FIT]
    D --> F[GLINT LOCALIZATION CENTROID]
    E --> G[PC CR VECTOR COMPUTATION]
    F --> G
    G --> H[CALIBRATED MAPPING POLYNOMIAL OR 3D MODEL]
    H --> I[SCREEN COORDINATES xS yS]
    I --> J[DWELL OR BLINK DETECTOR]
    J --> K[SELECTION EVENT CLICK OR KEY]
    K --> L[HOST COMPUTER AAC WHEELCHAIR ECU]
    L --> M[USER FEEDBACK AUDIO OR VISUAL CUE]
```

## 4.2 Workflow of a Calibration Session

A user must look at a sequence of points so the system can learn the mapping from eye pose to screen coordinates. Below is the canonical 5-point calibration workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START CALIBRATION] --> S2[DISPLAY POINT 1 CENTER OF SCREEN]
    S2 --> S3[CAPTURE PUPIL GLINT PAIR FOR 1 SEC]
    S3 --> S4[RECORD PC CR VECTOR V1]
    S4 --> S5{ALL N POINTS COLLECTED N TYPICALLY 5 OR 9}
    S5 -- NO --> S6[DISPLAY NEXT POINT SEQUENTIAL OR RANDOMIZED]
    S6 --> S3
    S5 -- YES --> S7[FIT POLYNOMIAL OF DEGREE 1 OR 2 TO MAP VECTOR TO X Y]
    S7 --> S8[VALIDATE ACCURACY SHOW TARGETS USER MUST LOOK AT]
    S8 --> S9{ERROR BELOW THRESHOLD TYPICALLY 1 DEG}
    S9 -- NO --> S10[REPEAT CALIBRATION OR WARN USER]
    S9 -- YES --> S11[CALIBRATION COMPLETE ENTER TRACKING MODE]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Mapping Components to Clinical Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHYSIOLOGICAL_LAYER
        P1[EXTRAOCULAR MUSCLES CN III IV VI]
        P2[CORNEA APEX]
        P3[PUPIL APERTURE]
        P4[RETINA RODS CONES]
    end

    subgraph OPTICAL_LAYER
        O1[IR LED 850 nm]
        O2[HOT MIRROR DICHROIC]
        O3[CAMERA LENS f]
        O4[CMOS SENSOR]
    end

    subgraph SIGNAL_PROCESSING_LAYER
        SP1[PUPIL ELLIPSE FIT]
        SP2[GLINT CENTROID]
        SP3[PC CR VECTOR]
        SP4[POLYNOMIAL MAPPER]
    end

    subgraph APPLICATION_LAYER
        A1[ON SCREEN KEYBOARD]
        A2[SPEECH GENERATING DEVICE]
        A3[POWERED WHEELCHAIR CONTROLLER]
        A4[ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROL UNIT]
    end

    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    O1 --> P2
    O1 --> P3
    P2 --> O2
    P3 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
    O4 --> SP1
    O4 --> SP2
    SP1 --> SP3
    SP2 --> SP3
    SP3 --> SP4
    SP4 --> A1
    SP4 --> A2
    SP4 --> A3
    SP4 --> A4
```

## 4.4 Eye-Gaze Versus EOG — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EO_G_SYSTEM
        E1[5 SKIN ELECTRODES] --> E2[DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER GAIN 1000]
        E2 --> E3[BANDPASS 0.1 TO 30 HZ]
        E3 --> E4[ADC SAMPLING 100 Hz OR HIGHER]
        E4 --> E5[EOG VOLTAGE PROPORTIONAL TO GAZE]
    end

    subgraph VIDEO_BASED_SYSTEM
        V1[IR LED] --> V2[EYE CAMERA]
        V2 --> V3[FRAME BUFFER]
        V3 --> V4[PUPIL GLINT EXTRACTION]
        V4 --> V5[GAZE POINT MAPPING]
    end

    E5 --> Z[GAZE DIRECTION OUTPUT]
    V5 --> Z
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU answer scripts:** Always include a hand-drawn equivalent of Section 4.1 in your answer paper. Even a rough block diagram earns 2–3 easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define an eye-gaze system. List the two principal non-invasive ocular measurement techniques used in assistive medical devices.**

**Model Answer:**
An eye-gaze system is a non-contact biomedical instrumentation device that determines a user's *point of regard* (POR) on a display by detecting and interpreting voluntary eye movements, thereby enabling hands-free control of a computer, communication device, or powered wheelchair for users with severe motor disabilities.
The two principal non-invasive techniques are:
1. **Video-oculography (VOG) / Video-based tracking** — uses IR illumination and a camera to image the pupil and corneal reflection.
2. **Electro-oculography (EOG)** — measures the corneo-retinal standing potential (≈ 0.4–1.0 mV) using skin electrodes around the orbit.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**Why is the difference between the pupil centre and the corneal reflection (PC–CR vector) preferred over the absolute pupil position in gaze estimation?**

**Model Answer:**
Because the PC–CR vector is **invariant under rigid head translation**. When the head translates, both the pupil image and the glint translate by the same pixel offset; the *difference* cancels the head motion, leaving only the rotation-induced component. Using absolute pupil position would falsely interpret head movement as gaze shift, causing severe inaccuracy. This is the single most important design principle behind modern video-based eye trackers and a frequent KTU Part-A question.

---

## 5.2 PART-B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a video-based eye-gaze system. Describe the role of infrared illumination and corneal reflection. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the relationship between the gaze angle, the pupil–glint displacement, and the on-screen coordinates for a head-stabilized user. State all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

A video-based eye-gaze system is a contact-less optical instrument. The block diagram is as follows:

**User → IR LEDs (850 nm) → Eye (pupil + cornea) → Camera sensor → Frame grabber → Pupil localization + Glint detection → PC–CR vector → Calibrated mapping polynomial → Screen coordinates → Dwell/blink detector → Selection event → Host application (keyboard, AAC, wheelchair).**

- The **infrared LEDs** flood the eye with light that is invisible to the user. The cornea acts as a convex mirror and produces a bright **corneal reflection (glint)**. The pupil, in contrast, appears dark (in dark-pupil configuration) because IR is absorbed by the iris and the retina retro-reflects only weakly along the camera axis.
- The **camera** captures images at 30–1200 fps. Each frame is processed in real time. The pupil centre is localized by fitting an ellipse to the dark region; the glint is localized by finding the bright centroid.
- The **PC–CR vector** encodes the gaze direction. A **calibration polynomial**, learned from N (typically 5 or 9) on-screen points the user looks at, maps this vector to a screen point $(x_s, y_s)$.
- A **dwell detector** (300–1000 ms of stable fixation) or an **intentional-blink detector** produces a "click" event. The host computer then executes the chosen action.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Assumptions:**
1. The user's head is stationary during the measurement.
2. The gaze angle is small (≤ 20°) so that small-angle approximation holds.
3. The camera is mounted fixed relative to the screen.
4. The corneal sphere has a known radius $R_c$ and centre $C_c$.

**Derivation** (full step-by-step, matches Section 3.1 of these notes):

Step 1: In the image plane, the horizontal pixel offset of the pupil centre from the glint is:

$$u_p - u_g = m \cdot f \cdot \tan(\theta_h)$$

Step 2: Using small-angle approximation $\tan(\theta_h) \approx \theta_h$ (radians):

$$u_p - u_g = m \cdot f \cdot \theta_h$$

Step 3: Solve for the gaze angle:

$$\theta_h = \dfrac{u_p - u_g}{m \cdot f}$$

Step 4: Relate the gaze angle to a screen point at horizontal offset $X_s$ viewed at distance $D$:

$$\theta_h = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_s}{D}\right)$$

Step 5: Equate and solve:

$$\dfrac{u_p - u_g}{m \cdot f} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_s}{D}\right)$$

Step 6: Take tangent of both sides:

$$X_s = D \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{u_p - u_g}{m \cdot f}\right)$$

Step 7: Similarly for the vertical axis:

$$Y_s = D \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{v_p - v_g}{m \cdot f}\right)$$

The pair of equations above is the **calibrated mapping** from pupil–glint pixels to screen coordinates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted]**

> **(a)** Explain the principle of electro-oculography (EOG). Sketch the electrode placement and derive the relation between the EOG voltage and the gaze angle. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare EOG and video-based eye-gaze systems on the basis of accuracy, invasiveness, drift, sampling rate, and clinical application. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Principle:**
The eye behaves as an electrical **dipole** — the cornea is positive (≈ +0.4 to +1.0 mV) relative to the retina. When the eye rotates within the orbit, this dipole rotates, and the resulting potential difference can be measured across pairs of skin electrodes placed around the orbit.

**Electrode placement (standard configuration):**
- Two electrodes at the **lateral canthi** (outer corners) — measure horizontal movement.
- Two electrodes **above and below** one eye — measure vertical movement.
- A **reference electrode** on the forehead.

**Derivation:**
Let $V_R$ and $V_L$ be the potentials at the right and left canthal electrodes. The differential output of the amplifier is:

$$V_{EOG} = V_R - V_L = k \cdot \theta_h$$

where $k$ is the EOG sensitivity, typically in the range $5$–$20\ \mu\text{V/degree}$, and $\theta_h$ is the horizontal gaze angle. The voltage is amplified (gain ≈ 1000), band-pass filtered (0.1–30 Hz), and digitized.

To convert the voltage to a screen coordinate:

$$\theta_h = \dfrac{V_{EOG}}{k}$$

$$X_s = D \cdot \tan(\theta_h) = D \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{V_{EOG}}{k}\right)$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

| Parameter | EOG | Video-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | 1°–2° (lower) | 0.4°–1.0° (higher) |
| Invasiveness | Skin electrodes (mild) | Fully non-contact |
| Drift | Suffers from baseline drift (corneo-retinal potential changes with adaptation) | Drift-free if pupil-glint vector is used |
| Sampling rate | 100–1000 Hz | 30–1200 Hz |
| Lighting sensitivity | Insensitive to light (a key advantage) | Sensitive to ambient IR/visible light; needs controlled IR illumination |
| Robust to glasses | Yes | Sometimes struggles with thick frames or reflective lenses |
| Clinical use | Sleep studies, vestibular testing, nystagmus | AAC, wheelchair control, HCI |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Calibration | Simple | Mandatory multi-point |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing *visual axis* with *optical axis*.** The optical axis passes through the centre of the lens; the visual axis passes through the fovea. They differ by **≈ 5°** — the *angle kappa*. Failing to mention this will cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the head-motion-invariance proof** when asked "why PC–CR?" Always state the translation-cancellation derivation.
> 3. **No mention of dwell time** in selection questions. Without dwell/blink, the system cannot *click*.
> 4. **Unit errors:** Mixing pixels, degrees, and cm without conversion. Always show dimensional consistency.
> 5. **Skipping the clinical context.** A 14-mark answer that ends with engineering alone is marked down. Always close with one sentence on the *patient population served*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2 / Eye-Gaze Systems**

- **Definition:** Eye-gaze system = non-contact device that maps voluntary eye movements to a screen coordinate or device command.
- **Clinical context:** Used by patients with **ALS, locked-in syndrome, high-level SCI, advanced CP, muscular dystrophy**.
- **Two measurement methods:** (1) Video-based (VOG/IR pupil tracking) — preferred; (2) EOG — older, electrode-based.
- **Key features in the image:** **Pupil centre (PC)** and **Corneal Reflection (CR/glint)**.
- **Why PC–CR vector?** It is invariant under rigid head translation — *the* single most important design rationale.
- **Why IR (850–940 nm)?** Invisible, produces high-contrast pupil, safe below **10 mW/cm²**.
- **Sampling rate:** Must satisfy Nyquist for saccades: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot 50 = 100$ Hz minimum; commercial devices use 60–1200 Hz.
- **Selection mechanism:** **Dwell** (300–1000 ms fixation) or **intentional blink**.
- **Calibration:** User looks at **5 or 9** sequential on-screen points; polynomial of degree 1 or 2 maps PC–CR → screen.
- **Accuracy metric:** $A_{err} = \sqrt{(x_s - x_t)^2 + (y_s - y_t)^2}$, typical target **≤ 1° of visual angle**.
- **Mapping equation (must memorize):**

$$X_s = D \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{u_p - u_g}{m \cdot f}\right)$$

- **EOG signal:** $V_{EOG} = k \cdot \theta_{gaze}$, with $k \approx 5\text{–}20\ \mu\text{V/°}$ and standing potential 0.4–1.0 mV.
- **Drawn in the answer sheet:** Always include the block diagram of the VOG system — it is a guaranteed 2–3 marks.
- **Angle kappa** ≈ 5°: the offset between visual axis and optical axis — mention it whenever discussing gaze estimation geometry.
- **Safety limit:** IR irradiance < 10 mW/cm² (IEC/ISO 15004-2).
- **End every long answer with the *clinical beneficiary population*.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Interfaces for Indirect Selection - Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control Interfaces for Indirect Selection - Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indirect Selection** is an alternative access paradigm in assistive technology wherein a user with motor impairments cannot directly point to, touch, or manipulate a target on an interface. Instead, the user employs a **binary control interface** (most commonly a *switch*) to sequentially navigate and confirm a desired target from a presented set of options.

A **Switch** in the context of Assistive Medical Devices is a *low-force, low-travel, binary-state electromechanical or physiological transducer* that converts a controlled voluntary physical action (muscle twitch, breath pressure, eye blink, etc.) into a discrete ON/OFF electrical signal. This signal is then processed by an Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) device, environmental control unit (ECU), or computer access system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are watching TV but have broken both arms. You cannot press the channel button directly. Instead, you are given **one large button** mounted near your cheek. The TV automatically highlights channels one-by-one on screen. Whenever the channel you want is highlighted, you press your cheek-button to lock it in. That single cheek-button is your **switch**, and the auto-highlighting process is **indirect selection**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Direct selection = "I point at what I want." Indirect selection = "The system shows it to me, and I say YES when I see it."

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (KTU Specifications)

| Metric | Standard Value Range |
|---|---|
| Activation Force $F_a$ | **0.1 N – 2.0 N** |
| Switch Travel $d_t$ | **0.5 mm – 5 mm** |
| Debounce Time $t_d$ | **5 ms – 50 ms** |
| Rated Lifespan | **> 1 million activations** |
| Operating Voltage | **3.3 V – 24 V DC** |

> [!NOTE]
> For users with severe motor limitations (ALS, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy), the switch becomes the *only* channel of communication and control, making its design specifications clinically critical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle: The Switch as a Binary Transducer

A switch behaves as a single-pole single-throw (SPST) device with two stable states:
