Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of a Wheelchair A wheelchair supporting structure is functionally decomposed into **four hierarchical load paths**: 1. **Primary Load Path** — Rider weight → Seat sling/pan → Seat rails → Side frames → Axle plates → Wheels → Ground. 2. **Secondary Load Path** — Backrest reactions → Back canes → Seat rails → Frame. 3. **Tertiary Load Path** — Footrest loads → Footplate hangers → Front frame → Casters → Ground. 4. **Reactive Path** — Camber-induced lateral forces during turning → Camber tube → Axle plate. ## 2.2 Frame Materials & Their Engineering Trade-Offs | Material | Density (kg/m³) | Yield Strength (MPa) | Cost | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Mild Steel (1020) | 7850 | 350 | Low | Entry-level hospital chairs | | Aluminium Alloy (6061-T6) | 2700 | 240 | Medium | Lightweight manual chairs | | Aluminium Alloy (7000-series) | 2800 | 500 | Medium-High | Sports/active chairs | | Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) | 4430 | 880 | Very High | Custom-moulded active chairs | | Carbon Fibre Composite | 1600 | 600–1500 | Very High | Racing/performance chairs | | Magnesium Alloy | 1740 | 200 | High | Rare, ultra-lightweight | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Structural Mechanics of the Supporting Structure > [!NOTE] > The following formulas govern static stability, stress, and dimensional selection in a wheelchair frame. ### 2.3.1 Anti-Tip Stability (Longitudinal) The rear anti-tip moment must exceed the forward tip moment caused by braking inertia: $$M_{anti-tip} \geq M_{forward}$$ $$W \cdot L_{rear} \geq \frac{W}{g} \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}$$ $$\boxed{L_{rear} \geq \frac{a \cdot h_{cg}}{g}}$$ where: - $W$ = rider weight (N) - $L_{rear}$ = horizontal distance from rear axle to centre of gravity (m) - $h_{cg}$ = height of combined rider + chair centre of gravity (m) - $a$ = maximum deceleration (m/s²) - $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** ### 2.3.2 Lateral Stability (Tipping on a Side Slope) $$\tan \theta_{max} = \frac{T_{width}/2}{h_{cg}}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_{max} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{T_{width}}{2 \, h_{cg}}\right)}$$ where $T_{width}$ = track width between rear wheels. ### 2.3.3 Bending Stress in Seat Rail (Treated as Simply Supported Beam) For a uniform distributed load $w$ (N/m) over span $L$ (m) with the load centred: $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{w L^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{d/2}{\frac{\pi (d^{4} - d_i^{4})}{64}}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{4 \, w \, L^{2}}{\pi \, (d^{4} - d_i^{4}) / d}}$$ where $d$ = outer tube diameter, $d_i$ = inner tube diameter. ### 2.3.4 Buckling Load (Euler) for Vertical Frame Members $$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{(K L_{eff})^{2}}$$ $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{K^{2} L_{eff}^{2}}}$$ where: - $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa) - $I$ = second moment of area (m⁴) - $K$ = effective length factor (**K = 1** for pinned-pinned, **K = 0.5** for fixed-fixed, **K = 2** for cantilever) - $L_{eff}$ = effective unsupported length ### 2.3.5 Camber Geometry — Turning Radius Correction $$\Delta R = R_{inner} - R_{outer} = -T_{width} \cdot \sin(\gamma)$$ where $\gamma$ = camber angle. Negative $\Delta R$ means the wheel rolls along a smaller inner arc, **reducing turning effort** by approximately: $$\tau_{reduction} \approx \mu \cdot \sin(\gamma) \cdot W_{rear}$$ ### 2.3.6 Seat Depth, Width, and Ergonomic Sizing $$L_{seat} = 0.85 \cdot \text{Buttock-Popliteal Length (BPL)}$$ $$W_{seat} = \text{Hip Width} + 2 \times (20 \text{ to } 40 \text{ mm})$$ $$H_{seat} = \text{Popliteal Height} + (25 \text{ to } 50 \text{ mm cushion clearance})$$ ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Crashworthiness**: Aircraft aisle wheelchairs (e.g., the WCH-21 used by airlines) require frames rated to **16 g forward, 8 g rearward** per **ISO 7176-19**. - **Sports Wheelchairs**: Basketball/racing frames are designed for **lateral acceleration peaks of 4–6 g** during sharp cuts. - **Bariatric Care**: Heavy-duty frames use **double cross-braces** and **reinforced axle plates** to carry riders **>136 kg**. - **Pediatric Rehabilitation**: Growth-adjustable frames use **telescoping seat rails** to extend depth by **50–75 mm** without replacing the chair. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Anti-Tip Margin of Safety We will derive the **anti-tip factor** for a manual wheelchair from first principles. ### Step 1 — Define the system Treat the wheelchair + rider as a **rigid body** on two rear contact points. The forward tip axis is the line joining the two front caster-wheel ground contact points. ### Step 2 — Establish the restoring moment The combined mass $m = (m_{rider} + m_{chair})$ acts at the centre of gravity located at horizontal distance $L_{rear}$ behind the front caster axis and vertical height $h_{cg}$ above the ground. Restoring moment (anti-tip moment, taken about the front caster axis): $$M_{restore} = m \, g \, L_{rear}$$ ### Step 3 — Establish the tipping moment during braking When the rider applies brakes or pushes against an obstacle, a horizontal deceleration $a$ creates an inertial force at the CG: $$F_{inertia} = m \, a \quad (\text{acting forward at height } h_{cg})$$ Tipping moment about the front caster axis: $$M_{tip} = m \, a \, h_{cg}$$ ### Step 4 — Equate for marginal stability For marginal stability, $M_{restore} = M_{tip}$: $$m \, g \, L_{rear} = m \, a \, h_{cg}$$ The masses cancel: $$g \, L_{rear} = a \, h_{cg}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for the minimum required wheelbase projection $$\boxed{L_{rear,\,min} = \frac{a \, h_{cg}}{g}}$$ ### Step 6 — Compute the Factor of Safety (FoS) $$\text{FoS} = \frac{L_{rear,\,actual}}{L_{rear,\,min}}$$ A safe design requires **FoS ≥ 1.5** (per ISO 7176-8 stability testing recommendations). ### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A powered wheelchair with rider total mass **90 kg** has a centre of gravity located at height **h_cg = 0.55 m** above the ground and **L_rear = 0.42 m** behind the front caster axis. The maximum programmed deceleration is **1.8 m/s²**. Compute: (a) the minimum required L_rear, (b) the actual factor of safety, and (c) the maximum deceleration before forward tip. **Given:** - $m = 90$ kg - $h_{cg} = 0.55$ m - $L_{rear} = 0.42$ m - $a_{max} = 1.8$ m/s² - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **(a) Minimum L_rear:** $$L_{rear,\,min} = \frac{a \, h_{cg}}{g} = \frac{1.8 \times 0.55}{9.81}$$ $$L_{rear,\,min} = \frac{0.99}{9.81} = 0.1009 \text{ m}$$ $$L_{rear,\,min} \approx 101 \text{ mm}$$ **(b) Actual Factor of Safety:** $$\text{FoS} = \frac{L_{rear,\,actual}}{L_{rear,\,min}} = \frac{0.42}{0.1009}$$ $$\text{FoS} \approx 4.16$$ **(c) Maximum deceleration before forward tip:** $$a_{max,\,tip} = \frac{g \, L_{rear}}{h_{cg}} = \frac{9.81 \times 0.42}{0.55}$$ $$a_{max,\,tip} = \frac{4.1202}{0.55} = 7.491 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$ Convert to g: $$\frac{a_{max,\,tip}}{g} = \frac{7.491}{9.81} \approx 0.764 \, g$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree | Design Question | Governing Equation | Typical Range | Source Standard | |---|---|---|---| | Will the chair tip forward under braking? | $L_{rear} \geq \dfrac{a \, h_{cg}}{g}$ | $L_{rear}$ = 0.30–0.50 m | ISO 7176-8 | | Will the chair tip sideways on a slope? | $\theta_{max} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{T_{width}}{2 \, h_{cg}}\right)$ | $\theta_{max}$ ≥ 15° | ISO 7176-1 | | Will the seat rail yield under the rider? | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{4 w L^{2}}{\pi (d^{4}-d_i^{4})/d}$ | $\sigma_{max} < \sigma_{yield}/1.5$ | ISO 7176-8 | | Will the front frame column buckle? | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{K^{2} L_{eff}^{2}}$ | $P_{cr} \geq 3 \cdot W$ | Fatigue per ISO 7176-8 | | Is the camber angle optimal? | $\tau_{reduction} \approx \mu \cdot \sin(\gamma) \cdot W_{rear}$ | $\gamma$ = 0°–6° (manual), 0°–3° (power) | RESNA WC-1 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Wheelchair Supporting Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["USER BODY LOADS
Static weight + Postural reactions"] nodeB["SEAT SYSTEM
Sling or solid pan + cushion"] nodeC["BACKREST SYSTEM
Slung fabric or rigid panel"] nodeD["SIDE FRAME TUBES
Primary load-bearing columns"] nodeE["CROSS BRACE
X-link folding / rigid weldment"] nodeF["AXLE PLATE
Camper tube + axle bearing"] nodeG["FRONT FRAME
Castor housing + footrest hanger"] nodeH["REAR WHEEL
Handrim propulsion interface"] nodeI["FRONT CASTOR
Swivel + fork"] nodeJ["GROUND REACTION
Vertical + traction + braking forces"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeI nodeF --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeJ nodeK["FOOTREST ASSEMBLY
Hanger tube + footplate"] nodeG --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeJ nodeL["ARMREST ASSEMBLY
Side pad + desk or full length"] nodeD --> nodeL ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of Frame Geometry and Critical Dimensions ```mermaid flowchart LR dim1["L_rear
(Wheelbase)"] dim2["h_cg
(CG height)"] dim3["T_width
(Track width)"] dim4["Camber gamma
(Wheel tilt)"] dim5["Seat Depth"] dim6["Backrest Height"] relA["L_rear and h_cg determine
forward anti-tip margin"] relB["T_width and h_cg determine
lateral slope tolerance"] relC["Camber gamma determines
lateral push effort"] relD["Seat Depth + Backrest Height
define ergonomic envelope"] dim1 --- relA dim2 --- relA dim2 --- relB dim3 --- relB dim4 --- relC dim5 --- relD dim6 --- relD ``` ## 4.3 Load-Path Flow Under a 100 kg Rider ```mermaid flowchart TD mass1["Rider 90 percent of 100 kg
Vertical load through pelvis"] mass2["Cushion absorbs 15 to 30 percent peak"] mass3["Seat sling or pan
Distributes to side rails"] mass4["Side rail bending stress
sigma max = 4 w L squared over pi d bracket"] mass5["Cross brace converts
vertical to frame torsion"] mass6["Axle plate reaction
Transmits to rear wheels"] mass7["Ground reaction
Equals rider weight at rest"] mass1 --> mass2 --> mass3 --> mass4 --> mass5 --> mass6 --> mass7 ``` ## 4.4 Component Map of the Supporting Structure ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GroupA["Frame Subsystem"] partA1["Main side frame"] partA2["Cross brace"] partA3["Axle plate"] partA4["Front frame / hanger"] end subgraph GroupB["Seat Subsystem"] partB1["Seat sling / pan"] partB2["Cushion"] partB3["Seat rail"] end subgraph GroupC["Back Subsystem"] partC1["Back cane"] partC2["Back upholstery"] partC3["Push handle"] end subgraph GroupD["Limb Subsystem"] partD1["Armrest"] partD2["Footrest hanger"] partD3["Footplate"] partD4["Heel loop"] end GroupA --> GroupB GroupA --> GroupC GroupA --> GroupD ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > Define the **wheelbase (L_rear)** of a manual wheelchair and state its role in **longitudinal anti-tip stability**. **Model Answer:** The wheelbase $L_{rear}$ is the horizontal distance between the **front caster ground contact axis** and the **rear wheel ground contact axis**, measured at the ground level. In longitudinal anti-tip stability, $L_{rear}$ directly provides the **restoring moment arm** about the front caster tipping axis during braking or downhill deceleration. The condition for stability is: $$L_{rear} \geq \frac{a \, h_{cg}}{g}$$ A larger $L_{rear}$ produces a higher restoring moment $m \, g \, L_{rear}$, allowing the chair to safely tolerate greater braking decelerations without forward tip-over. KTU valuation credit is given for explicitly mentioning the inertia term $m \, a \, h_{cg}$ and the gravitational restoring term $m \, g \, L_{rear}$. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > Compare **sling upholstery** and **rigid seat pan** wheelchair seats with respect to **postural support, pressure distribution, and durability**. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Sling Upholstery | Rigid Seat Pan | |---|---|---| | Postural Support | Poor — pelvis sinks, encourages posterior tilt | Excellent — provides stable base of support | | Pressure Distribution | Acceptable for short-duration use; hammocking effect | Superior when combined with contoured cushion | | Durability | 2–4 years under daily use | 8–15 years; pan does not deform | | Weight | Negligible (~200 g) | Adds ~1.0–2.5 kg | | Cost | Low | Moderate to high | | Best Use | Lightweight active chairs, occasional users | Long-term users, paediatric CP, spinal injuries | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]* > **(a)** With the aid of a free-body diagram, derive the **expression for minimum wheelbase projection (L_rear)** required to prevent **forward tip-over** of a wheelchair during emergency braking. Define each variable clearly. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A manual rigid-frame wheelchair has a rider-plus-chair mass of **95 kg**, centre of gravity height **0.58 m**, and rear axle located **0.40 m** behind the front caster axis. If the rider applies hand-rim brakes producing a deceleration of **2.2 m/s²**, compute (i) the minimum safe wheelbase projection, (ii) the factor of safety against forward tip, and (iii) the maximum deceleration the chair can sustain without tipping. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation** — Refer to Section 3.1 derivation above. Present the following in the answer script: (i) Define system as a rigid body on two caster contact points and one rear-wheel pair. (ii) Restoring moment about the front caster axis: $$M_{restore} = m \, g \, L_{rear}$$ (iii) Tipping moment from braking inertia: $$M_{tip} = m \, a \, h_{cg}$$ (iv) Equate for marginal stability: $$m \, g \, L_{rear} = m \, a \, h_{cg}$$ (v) Cancel $m$: $$\boxed{L_{rear,\,min} = \frac{a \, h_{cg}}{g}}$$ (vi) Define each variable: $m$ = total mass, $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**, $L_{rear}$ = wheelbase projection, $a$ = deceleration, $h_{cg}$ = centre-of-gravity height. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $m = 95$ kg, $h_{cg} = 0.58$ m, $L_{rear,\,actual} = 0.40$ m, $a = 2.2$ m/s², $g = 9.81$ m/s² **(i) Minimum safe wheelbase projection:** $$L_{rear,\,min} = \frac{a \, h_{cg}}{g} = \frac{2.2 \times 0.58}{9.81}$$ $$L_{rear,\,min} = \frac{1.276}{9.81} = 0.1301 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{L_{rear,\,min} \approx 130 \text{ mm}}$$ **(ii) Factor of Safety:** $$\text{FoS} = \frac{L_{rear,\,actual}}{L_{rear,\,min}} = \frac{0.40}{0.1301}$$ $$\boxed{\text{FoS} \approx 3.08}$$ **(iii) Maximum sustainable deceleration:** $$a_{max} = \frac{g \, L_{rear}}{h_{cg}} = \frac{9.81 \times 0.40}{0.58}$$ $$a_{max} = \frac{3.924}{0.58} = 6.766 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{a_{max} \approx 6.77 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$ In g-units: $a_{max}/g \approx 0.69 \, g$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]* > **(a)** Explain the function of the **cross-brace mechanism** in a folding manual wheelchair. Discuss the relationship between **brace geometry, seat rigidity, and frame fatigue life**. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** For a folding aluminium wheelchair, the seat rail is a hollow circular tube of outer diameter **25 mm**, wall thickness **2 mm**, and unsupported length between the cross-brace pivot points of **380 mm**. A rider weighing **80 kg** sits centred on the rail. Estimate the **maximum bending stress** in the tube and comment on its adequacy assuming **Al-6061-T6 yield strength = 240 MPa**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Cross-brace function:** (i) The cross-brace is an **X-shaped or scissor linkage** joining the two side frames beneath the seat. Its functions are: - **Folding action:** Allows the two side frames to collapse toward each other, reducing chair width for transport. - **Load transfer:** Converts vertical seat loads into axial loads along the brace members, which then transfer reaction to the axle plate. - **Seat plane rigidity:** A well-tightened brace minimises seat "splay" (lateral opening under load). - **Torsional resistance:** Counteracts twisting moments during uneven-propulsion strokes. (ii) **Trade-offs:** - A **longer cross-brace** → smaller angle from horizontal → better lateral rigidity, but slower fold action. - A **shorter cross-brace** → easier fold, but lateral splay increases → rider pelvic obliquity. - **Fatigue:** The pivot pin is the highest-stress region; ISO 7176-8 mandates 200,000 cycles without failure. --- **(b) Stress Computation:** **Given:** - Outer diameter $d = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m - Wall thickness $t = 2$ mm - Inner diameter $d_i = d - 2t = 21$ mm $= 0.021$ m - Length $L = 380$ mm $= 0.380$ m - Rider weight $W = 80$ kg, so $W = 80 \times 9.81 = 784.8$ N - Distributed uniformly across both rails → load per rail $w = W/(2L) = 784.8 / (2 \times 0.380)$ $$w = \frac{784.8}{0.760} = 1032.6 \text{ N/m}$$ **Step 1 — Maximum bending moment for simply supported beam with UDL:** $$M_{max} = \frac{w L^{2}}{8} = \frac{1032.6 \times (0.380)^{2}}{8}$$ $$M_{max} = \frac{1032.6 \times 0.1444}{8} = \frac{149.11}{8}$$ $$M_{max} = 18.64 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2 — Second moment of area for hollow tube:** $$I = \frac{\pi (d^{4} - d_i^{4})}{64} = \frac{\pi ((0.025)^{4} - (0.021)^{4})}{64}$$ $$I = \frac{\pi (3.906 \times 10^{-7} - 1.944 \times 10^{-7})}{64}$$ $$I = \frac{\pi \times 1.962 \times 10^{-7}}{64}$$ $$I = \frac{6.164 \times 10^{-7}}{64} = 9.631 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{4}$$ **Step 3 — Section modulus:** $$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{I}{d/2} = \frac{9.631 \times 10^{-9}}{0.0125} = 7.705 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{3}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum bending stress:** $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{18.64}{7.705 \times 10^{-7}}$$ $$\sigma_{max} = 2.419 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 24.19 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5 — Adequacy check:** $$\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{240}{24.19} = 9.92$$ The stress is well below the yield strength, yielding a factor of safety of approximately **9.9** — the design is **highly adequate** for static loading. The frame will, however, be governed by **fatigue** rather than static yield at the cross-brace pivot pins. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. Students commonly forget to **divide the rider's weight by 2** when computing per-rail loading — losing 2–3 marks immediately. > 2. For Euler buckling questions, mixing up **K = 1** (pinned-pinned) with **K = 2** (cantilever) is a frequent error worth 1–2 marks. > 3. Do **not** confuse "wheelbase projection" ($L_{rear}$) with the total wheelbase (front-caster-to-rear-axle); the *projection* is the horizontal component at ground level. > 4. Always convert mm to m before substituting into SI equations; otherwise the stress will appear **10⁶ times** too large. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Supporting structure** = **frame + seat + backrest + armrests + footrests + cross-brace** — all work as an integrated load path. - The **wheelbase projection** $L_{rear}$ and **centre-of-gravity height** $h_{cg}$ together define longitudinal anti-tip margin. - The **track width** $T_{width}$ and $h_{cg}$ define lateral slope tolerance. - **Camber angle** $\gamma$ (typically 0°–6°) reduces lateral push effort but slightly increases rolling resistance. - **Sling seat** is lightweight but provides poor long-term postural support; **rigid seat pan** is preferred for permanent users. - Frame materials progress from **steel (heavy, cheap)** → **aluminium (mid-weight, mid-cost)** → **titanium/carbon-fibre (light, expensive)**. - **Euler buckling** formula governs vertical frame columns; **simply-supported beam bending** governs horizontal seat rails. - Ergonomic sizing uses **buttock-popliteal length** for seat depth and **hip width + clearance** for seat width. - **ISO 7176 series** is the governing international standard for wheelchair structural testing. - Always apply a **Factor of Safety ≥ 1.5** for static loads and ≥ 3 for fatigue-loaded joints such as cross-brace pivots. - Standard test rider mass per ISO 7173-1 = **100 kg static / 130 kg dynamic**. ---

topics.title: Frame Types
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# FRAME TYPES IN ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frame Types

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Assistive Medical Devices (AMDs)**, particularly mobility aids such as **manual wheelchairs, powered wheelchairs, walkers, and standing frames**, the **Frame** constitutes the primary load-bearing skeletal architecture that:

- Supports the user's body mass and dynamic physiological loads.
- Provides mounting platforms for wheels, seating systems, cushions, armrests, footrests, and electronic actuators.
- Transmits propulsive, braking, and impact forces safely to the ground via the wheel-ground interface.
- Defines the **geometric configuration** (wheelbase, track width, seat-to-floor height, camber angle) of the device.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT744)** syllabus, **Frame Types** refers to the classification of assistive device frames based on structural configuration, folding mechanism, rigidity, adjustability, and intended clinical application.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the frame as the "skeleton of a human body."** Just as our skeleton holds muscles, organs, and skin in place while letting us walk, run, and jump, the frame of a wheelchair or walker holds the user, wheels, and seating components together. A strong skeleton means a strong body; a well-engineered frame means a safe, durable, and efficient assistive device.

A simple real-world analogy:
- **Rigid frame** = A fixed bicycle frame (stiff, light, energy-efficient).
- **Folding frame** = A folding bicycle (portable, compact, slightly heavier).
- **Tilt-in-space frame** = A reclining office chair (allows posture change without unloading the user).

### 1.3 Why Frame Selection Matters

| Parameter | Impact of Frame Choice |
|---|---|
| **Weight** | Determines propulsion effort (rolling resistance is mass-dependent) |
| **Foldability** | Determines portability for transport in cars, buses, aircraft |
| **Adjustability** | Determines accommodation of growth, postural changes, and clinical needs |
| **Durability** | Determines service life under cyclic loading |
| **Cost** | Material and manufacturing complexity drive end-user affordability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Frame classification is critical because **the choice of frame directly influences propulsion biomechanics, energy expenditure, postural support, and risk of pressure injuries.** For patients with spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, or muscular dystrophy, an inappropriate frame type can cause secondary complications.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Codes

- **HCPCS (Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System)** classifies manual wheelchairs as **K0001 through K0005** based on frame type, weight, and adjustability. Although this is a US Medicare standard, it is widely used in global rehabilitation engineering curricula, including KTU.
- **ISO 7176** series (International Organization for Standardization) governs wheelchair structural testing, including **static, impact, and fatigue strength of frames**.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, students must remember both the **HCPCS K-code classification** and the **ISO 7176 testing standard** since both are referenced in Module 2 of the syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Classification of Frame Types

#### 2.1.1 Based on Structural Configuration

**A. Rigid (Non-Folding) Frame**
- Welded single-piece chassis.
- **Lowest weight** (typically 7–10 kg for manual wheelchairs).
- **Highest energy efficiency** since no folding mechanism absorbs propulsive force.
- Examples: Sports wheelchairs, racing chairs, ultralight rigid chairs.
- **Disadvantage**: Cannot be folded for transport.

**B. Folding (Cross-Fold) Frame**
- Uses an **X-shaped cross-brace** mechanism (typically two interleaved bars pivoting at the center).
- Folds horizontally to reduce footprint for storage.
- Slightly heavier than rigid frames due to additional cross-brace hardware.
- Examples: Standard manual wheelchairs used in hospitals.

**C. Rigid Foldable (Hybrid) Frame**
- Combines a rigid main frame with a folding backrest or folding footrest.
- Modern innovation offering compromise between rigidity and portability.

**D. Tilt-in-Space Frame**
- Entire seat frame pivots backward (typically 0°–45°) while maintaining seat-to-back angle.
- Used for users with poor trunk control (e.g., cerebral palsy, advanced ALS).
- **Mechanical principle**: The user's center of mass stays over the wheelbase, allowing gravity redistribution.

**E. Reclining Frame**
- Backrest angle is adjustable independently (typically 90°–180°).
- The seat frame tilts backward; shear forces are managed via sliding backrest.

**F. Standing Frame**
- Allows the user to assume a vertical posture.
- Frame supports the knees, hips, and torso via pads and straps.
- Critical for **bone density preservation, circulation, and psychological wellbeing** in SCI patients.

#### 2.1.2 Based on User Population (HCPCS Classification)

| K-Code | Category | Frame Type | Weight Target | Adjustability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **K0001** | Standard wheelchair | Folding, fixed-frame | ≥ 36 lbs (16.3 kg) | None |
| **K0002** | Standard hemi (low seat) | Folding | ≥ 36 lbs | Adjustable seat height |
| **K0003** | Lightweight | Folding | < 36 lbs (16.3 kg) | Limited adjustability |
| **K0004** | High-strength lightweight | Rigid or folding | < 34 lbs (15.4 kg) | Adjustable rear axle, multi-adjustable |
| **K0005** | Ultralight | Rigid (typically) | < 30 lbs (13.6 kg) | Fully custom-adjustable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Question Pattern:** Students are frequently asked to *compare K0001 and K0005* or to *justify the choice of a specific frame type for a clinical case* (e.g., a 25-year-old paraplegic active user).

#### 2.1.3 Based on Materials

| Material | Density (g/cm³) | Yield Strength (MPa) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon Steel (1020)** | 7.85 | 350 | Standard frames |
| **Aluminum Alloy (6061-T6)** | 2.70 | 240 | Lightweight frames |
| **Titanium Alloy (Ti-6Al-4V)** | 4.43 | 880 | Premium / sports frames |
| **Carbon Fiber Composite** | 1.55–1.85 | 500–1500 | Ultralight sports |
| **Magnesium Alloy** | 1.74 | 200 | Rare, experimental |

### 2.2 Structural Mechanics of Frames

#### 2.2.1 Load Analysis

A wheelchair frame must support three categories of loads:

1. **Static Load** — Body weight at rest.
2. **Dynamic Load** — Propulsive forces, curb drops, and braking.
3. **Impact Load** — Sudden collisions or falls (governed by ISO 7176-8).

Per **ISO 7176-8**, the frame must withstand:
- A **static load test** of **1.5× the user's declared mass** applied at the seat without permanent deformation.
- A **fatigue test** of **2 × 10⁶ cycles** of vertical loading at the seat without failure.

#### 2.2.2 Critical Stress Concentrations

Stress concentrations typically occur at:
- **Rear axle mounting brackets** (high bending moment during propulsion).
- **Cross-brace pivot joints** (cyclic loading).
- **Front caster stem connections** (impact loading from kerbs).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{prop} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Axial stress in frame tube | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_{bend} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending stress (M = bending moment, c = distance to outer fiber, I = second moment of area) | Pa |
| 4 | $I_{circular} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | Second moment of area for solid circular tube | m⁴ |
| 5 | $I_{hollow} = \dfrac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{64}$ | Second moment of area for hollow circular tube | m⁴ |
| 6 | $P_{crit} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(KL)^2}$ | Euler buckling load | N |
| 7 | $F_{impact} = m \cdot a = m \cdot \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | Impact force during curb drop | N |
| 8 | $S_{f} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{applied}}$ | Factor of safety | dimensionless |
| 9 | $W_{frame} = \rho_{mat} \cdot V_{frame}$ | Frame weight | kg |
| 10 | $F_{test} = 1.5 \cdot m_{user} \cdot g$ | ISO 7176-8 static test load | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used in formulas:** All mass $m$ is in kg, acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, distances in meters, forces in Newtons, stresses in Pascals.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The structural frame is the **single largest determinant** of:
- **Propulsion efficiency** (rigid frames transmit ~95% of input energy; folding frames lose 5–15% to joint compliance).
- **User fatigue** over a day of mobility.
- **Long-term joint degeneration** in the shoulder (a leading secondary injury in long-term wheelchair users).
- **Service life** and **total cost of ownership**.

> [!TIP]
> In Module 2, KTU examiners often expect students to **link the frame type to a clinical condition**. Memorizing just the names is insufficient; you must be able to **justify** the choice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Estimating Frame Weight from Material Selection

**Problem:** A wheelchair frame is approximated as 8 cylindrical tubes, each of length 0.8 m, outer diameter 25 mm, and wall thickness 2 mm. Compute the frame weight when manufactured from (a) aluminum 6061-T6 and (b) titanium Ti-6Al-4V. Comment on the K-code suitability.

**Given:**
- Number of tubes: $N = 8$
- Length per tube: $L = 0.8 \text{ m}$
- Outer diameter: $d_o = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Wall thickness: $t = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$
- Inner diameter: $d_i = d_o - 2t = 0.025 - 2(0.002) = 0.021 \text{ m}$
- Density of Al 6061-T6: $\rho_{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of Ti-6Al-4V: $\rho_{Ti} = 4430 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of one hollow tube**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( d_o^2 - d_i^2 \right)
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.025)^2 - (0.021)^2 \right)
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 6.25 \times 10^{-4} - 4.41 \times 10^{-4} \right)
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 1.84 \times 10^{-4} \right)
$$

$$
A = 1.445 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Volume of all tubes**

$$
V = N \cdot A \cdot L = 8 \times 1.445 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.8
$$

$$
V = 9.248 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Frame weight for aluminum**

$$
W_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot V = 2700 \times 9.248 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
W_{Al} = 2.497 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4: Frame weight for titanium**

$$
W_{Ti} = \rho_{Ti} \cdot V = 4430 \times 9.248 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
W_{Ti} = 4.097 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5: K-code suitability comment**
- Total chair weight is typically **2× to 3× the bare frame weight** when wheels, footrests, and upholstery are added.
- Aluminum-based total chair mass ≈ 8–10 kg → qualifies for **K0004 or K0005**.
- Titanium-based total chair mass ≈ 12–14 kg → qualifies for **K0003 to K0004** (heavier but stronger).

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation demonstrates the **density-driven trade-off**: aluminum gives a lighter frame but titanium gives 3.7× the yield strength, justifying its use in sports and high-impact applications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: ISO 7176-8 Static Test Load

**Problem:** A user weighing 90 kg is prescribed a wheelchair. Compute the minimum static test load the frame must withstand per ISO 7176-8. If the rear axle tube is hollow (outer diameter 28 mm, wall 3 mm), compute the induced bending stress when the test load is applied as a point load at a moment arm of 0.30 m from the axle support.

**Given:**
- User mass: $m = 90 \text{ kg}$
- Safety factor (ISO 7176-8): $1.5\times$
- Moment arm: $L_{arm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- Outer diameter: $d_o = 0.028 \text{ m}$
- Inner diameter: $d_i = 0.028 - 2(0.003) = 0.022 \text{ m}$
- Yield strength of Al 6061-T6: $\sigma_y = 240 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1: Test load**

$$
F_{test} = 1.5 \cdot m \cdot g = 1.5 \times 90 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F_{test} = 1324.35 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Bending moment**

$$
M = F_{test} \cdot L_{arm} = 1324.35 \times 0.30
$$

$$
M = 397.305 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3: Second moment of area for hollow tube**

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( d_o^4 - d_i^4 \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( (0.028)^4 - (0.022)^4 \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 6.1466 \times 10^{-7} - 2.3426 \times 10^{-7} \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 3.804 \times 10^{-7} \right)
$$

$$
I = 1.867 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 4: Maximum bending stress**

$$
\sigma_{bend} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} \quad \text{where } c = d_o/2 = 0.014 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\sigma_{bend} = \frac{397.305 \times 0.014}{1.867 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{bend} = \frac{5.562}{1.867 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{bend} = 2.979 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 297.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Factor of safety**

$$
S_f = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{bend}} = \frac{240}{297.9}
$$

$$
S_f = 0.806
$$

**Interpretation:** The factor of safety is **less than 1.0**, meaning the proposed 28 mm × 3 mm aluminum tube **fails** the ISO 7176-8 static test for a 90 kg user at 0.30 m moment arm.

**Step 6: Redesign — Try 32 mm × 4 mm tube**

$$
d_o = 0.032 \text{ m}, \quad d_i = 0.032 - 0.008 = 0.024 \text{ m}
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( (0.032)^4 - (0.024)^4 \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 1.0486 \times 10^{-6} - 3.3178 \times 10^{-7} \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 7.168 \times 10^{-7} \right)
$$

$$
I = 3.518 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
\sigma_{bend} = \frac{397.305 \times 0.016}{3.518 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{bend} = 1.807 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 180.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
S_f = \frac{240}{180.7} = 1.328
$$

**Result:** The redesigned 32 mm × 4 mm tube gives a factor of safety of **1.33**, which is acceptable for a static load application (typical design target: $S_f \geq 1.25$ for static loading).

> [!TIP]
> This type of derivation is a **KTU favourite** for the 14-mark question. Notice how we (1) compute the test load, (2) translate it to a moment, (3) compute I, (4) compute stress, and (5) check against yield strength. Examiners award marks for *each step*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rolling Resistance Comparison Between Rigid and Folding Frames

**Problem:** Compare the rolling resistance force for a 75 kg user on (a) a rigid frame chair and (b) a folding frame chair, both with 24-inch rear wheels on a tiled floor. Assume coefficient of rolling resistance $\mu_r$ on tiles = 0.012.

**Given:**
- $m = 75 \text{ kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\mu_r = 0.012$

**Step 1: Rigid frame**

$$
F_{rigid} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.012 \times 75 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F_{rigid} = 8.829 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Folding frame (with 10% energy loss due to joint compliance)**

$$
F_{folding,eff} = F_{rigid} \times 1.10 = 9.712 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Energy expenditure over 1 km**

$$
W_{rigid} = F_{rigid} \times 1000 = 8829 \text{ J}
$$

$$
W_{folding} = F_{folding,eff} \times 1000 = 9712 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4: Difference**

$$
\Delta W = 9712 - 8829 = 883 \text{ J per km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical significance:** Over 5 km of daily mobility, a rigid-frame user expends ~4.4 kJ less energy than a folding-frame user. For a long-term wheelchair user (paraplegic), this translates to reduced shoulder joint loading and lower risk of rotator cuff injury.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Frame Type vs. Clinical Indication

| Clinical Profile | Recommended Frame | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Active paraplegic, age 20–40 | **K0005 Rigid ultralight** | Minimum weight, max adjustability, lowest energy cost |
| Elderly, occasional indoor use | **K0001/K0002 Standard folding** | Affordable, foldable for transport, low adjustment need |
| Cerebral palsy child | **K0004 Tilt-in-space** | Postural support, pressure relief |
| Bilateral lower limb amputee | **K0004 Rear axle adjustable** | Shifts rear axle backward for stability |
| ALS / progressive weakness | **K0005 + power assist** | Customizable, accommodates decline |
| Stroke hemiplegia | **K0002 Hemi low-seat folding** | Allows foot propulsion on one side |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wheelchair Frame Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Assessment] --> B{Activity Level}
    B -->|High Active| C[K0005 Rigid Ultralight]
    B -->|Moderate| D[K0004 High-Strength Lightweight]
    B -->|Low Indoor| E[K0001 Standard Folding]
    B -->|Hemi Need| F[K0002 Low Seat Hemi]
    A --> G{Postural Control}
    G -->|Poor Trunk| H[Tilt-in-Space Frame]
    G -->|Reclining Need| I[Reclining Frame]
    G -->|Standing Therapy| J[Standing Frame]
    A --> K{Transport Need}
    K -->|Frequent| L[Folding / Rigid Foldable]
    K -->|Rare| M[Rigid Performance Frame]
    C --> N[Carbon or Titanium Material]
    D --> O[Aluminum 6061-T6]
    E --> P[Steel / Aluminum]
    H --> Q[ISO 7176-8 Tested]
    I --> Q
    J --> Q
    Q --> R[Final Prescribed Device]
```

### 4.2 Frame Type Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Frame Selection] --> Q1{User Weight?}
    Q1 -->|Over 100 kg| Bariatric[Bariatric Reinforced Frame]
    Q1 -->|Under 100 kg| Q2{Daily Distance?}
    Q2 -->|Over 2 km| Q3{Portable?}
    Q2 -->|Under 2 km| Q4{Adjustability?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Hybrid[Hybrid Rigid-Foldable]
    Q3 -->|No| Rigid[Rigid Ultralight K0005]
    Q4 -->|High| MultiAdj[Multi-Adjustable K0004]
    Q4 -->|Low| Standard[Standard K0001]
```

### 4.3 Load Path Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User[User Body Weight] --> Seat[Seat Pan]
    Seat --> Backrest[Backrest Tube]
    Seat --> Sideframe[Side Frame Rails]
    Backrest --> CrossBrace[Cross Brace Junction]
    Sideframe --> CrossBrace
    Sideframe --> RearAxle[Rear Axle Bracket]
    Sideframe --> CasterStem[Front Caster Stem]
    RearAxle --> RearWheel[Rear Wheel]
    CasterStem --> CasterWheel[Front Caster]
    RearWheel --> Ground[Ground Reaction Force]
    CasterWheel --> Ground
```

### 4.4 Folding Mechanism Detail

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Closed[Closed Frame] --> Pivot1[Upper Pivot Pin]
    Closed --> Pivot2[Lower Pivot Pin]
    Pivot1 --> XBarA[Cross Bar A]
    Pivot2 --> XBarB[Cross Bar B]
    XBarA --> Center[Center Hinge]
    XBarB --> Center
    Center --> Folded[Folded Configuration]
    Folded --> Transport[Vehicle Stowage]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each node represents a structural component or load path element. The arrows show **direction of force or action flow**. For KTU exams, students should annotate such diagrams with **stress concentrations** and **failure zones**.

### 4.5 Material Comparison Radar (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Material[Material Properties Comparison]
    direction LR
    M1[Steel] -->|Density 7.85| D1[Heavy]
    M2[Aluminum 6061] -->|Density 2.70| D2[Light]
    M3[Titanium] -->|Density 4.43| D3[Medium Heavy]
    M4[Carbon Fiber] -->|Density 1.55-1.85| D4[Ultra Light]
    end
    M1 --> E1[Cost: Low]
    M2 --> E2[Cost: Medium]
    M3 --> E3[Cost: High]
    M4 --> E4[Cost: Very High]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "wheelchair frame" and list any four types of frames used in assistive mobility devices.**

**Model Answer:**

A wheelchair frame is the **primary load-bearing structural chassis** of a wheelchair that supports the user, mounting hardware, and transmits propulsive and reactive forces to the wheels. It defines the device's geometry (wheelbase, seat height, camber) and determines weight, portability, and clinical suitability.

**Four types of frames:**
1. **Rigid (non-folding) frame** — welded single-piece chassis.
2. **Folding (cross-fold) frame** — uses X-brace for collapsibility.
3. **Tilt-in-space frame** — seat pivots backward as a unit.
4. **Standing frame** — supports the user in vertical posture.

*(Any four correct types accepted; ½ mark each for definition, ½ mark each for types.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the significance of HCPCS K-codes K0001 through K0005 in wheelchair frame classification?**

**Model Answer:**

The **HCPCS K-codes** are a U.S. Medicare classification system used internationally to categorize manual wheelchairs by **frame type, weight, and adjustability**. They enable:
- Standardized prescription across clinicians.
- Insurance reimbursement mapping.
- Engineering specification matching to user need.

| Code | Category |
|---|---|
| K0001 | Standard wheelchair (≥ 36 lbs, no adjustability) |
| K0002 | Standard hemi (low seat height) |
| K0003 | Lightweight (< 36 lbs, limited adjustability) |
| K0004 | High-strength lightweight (multi-adjustable rear axle) |
| K0005 | Ultralight (custom adjustable, active user) |

---

### 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Classify wheelchair frames based on **structural configuration**, **material**, and **user population (HCPCS)**. Discuss the engineering trade-offs involved in selecting each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 75-year-old patient weighing 80 kg, post-stroke with right hemiplegia, requires a wheelchair for indoor use and occasional car transport. Justify your choice of frame type, K-code, and material. Compute the ISO 7176-8 minimum static test load for this frame and verify whether an aluminum tube of 25 mm outer diameter and 2 mm wall thickness (cross-brace) can sustain it at a moment arm of 0.25 m. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Classification by structural configuration:**
- **Rigid frame**: Welded, non-folding, lightest, highest energy efficiency. Used in K0005 active chairs.
- **Folding (cross-brace) frame**: X-brace pivot allows horizontal fold. Used in K0001–K0003.
- **Rigid foldable hybrid**: Combines rigid main with folding backrest.
- **Tilt-in-space frame**: Seat pivots backward (0°–45°) for pressure relief.
- **Reclining frame**: Adjustable backrest angle (90°–180°).
- **Standing frame**: Supports user vertically.

**Classification by material:**
- **Steel**: Cheap, heavy, durable → K0001.
- **Aluminum 6061-T6**: Light, mid-cost, weldable → K0003–K0004.
- **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V**: Strong, light, expensive → K0005 sports.
- **Carbon fiber**: Ultra-light, very expensive → racing/sports.

**Classification by HCPCS K-code:** (As per Section 2.1.2 table above.)

**Engineering trade-offs:**

| Trade-off | Description |
|---|---|
| Weight vs Strength | Lighter materials (Al, Ti, carbon) cost more. |
| Rigidity vs Portability | Rigid frames are stiffer but non-foldable. |
| Cost vs Adjustability | K0005 is most adjustable but costliest. |
| Manufacturing vs Service | Welded frames are cheaper but harder to repair. |
| Fatigue Life vs Weight | Hollow tubes balance both via optimized $I/A$ ratio. |

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Clinical Justification:**
- Patient: 75-year-old, post-stroke, right hemiplegia, indoor use, occasional transport.
- Need: **Low seat height** for foot-propulsion on unaffected side → **K0002 Hemi wheelchair**.
- Material: **Aluminum 6061-T6** for moderate weight and reasonable cost.
- Frame type: **Folding frame** for car transport.

**ISO 7176-8 Static Test Load:**

$$
F_{test} = 1.5 \times m \times g = 1.5 \times 80 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F_{test} = 1177.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Bending Moment:**

$$
M = F_{test} \times L_{arm} = 1177.2 \times 0.25 = 294.3 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Second Moment of Area (25 mm OD, 2 mm wall):**

$$
d_o = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad d_i = 0.025 - 0.004 = 0.021 \text{ m}
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( (0.025)^4 - (0.021)^4 \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 3.906 \times 10^{-7} - 1.9448 \times 10^{-7} \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 1.961 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
I = 9.626 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Maximum bending stress:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{294.3 \times 0.0125}{9.626 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{3.679}{9.626 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.822 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\sigma = 382.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Factor of Safety:**

$$
S_f = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma} = \frac{240}{382.2} = 0.628
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $S_f < 1.0$, the **25 mm × 2 mm aluminum cross-brace FAILS** the static test for this user. The design must be upgraded to either a thicker wall (e.g., 3 mm) or larger diameter (e.g., 30 mm) to achieve $S_f \geq 1.25$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **mechanical working principle** of a **folding cross-brace frame** and a **tilt-in-space frame**. Discuss the clinical indications for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **frame weight** for a wheelchair manufactured from **titanium Ti-6Al-4V** assuming 6 cylindrical tubes of length 0.7 m, outer diameter 22 mm, and wall thickness 1.5 mm. Using this result, comment on the suitability of the chair for K0004 vs K0005 classification. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Folding Cross-Brace Frame:**

- Consists of two **interleaved X-shaped bars** mounted horizontally under the seat.
- Each bar has pivot pins at the front and rear of the seat rails and meets the other bar at a **central hinge**.
- When the user lifts the seat, the cross-brace flattens, drawing the side frames together and folding the chair.

**Working principle:** The central hinge is **offset**, so as the bars rotate, they translate horizontally — converting rotational motion into linear fold action.

**Clinical indications:**
- Users needing **frequent transport** (car, bus, aircraft).
- Short-term or **temporary users** (post-surgery, fracture).
- **Cost-sensitive** prescriptions (K0001–K0003).

**Tilt-in-Space Frame:**

- The entire seat unit (seat pan + backrest + armrests + legrests) is mounted on a **pivot mechanism** at the rear of the frame.
- A **gas strut or hydraulic cylinder** controls tilt angle (0°–45°).
- The user's **center of gravity remains over the wheelbase**, so the chair does not become unstable when tilted.

**Working principle:** A simple four-bar linkage pivots the seat around a fixed point behind the rear axle.

**Clinical indications:**
- **Cerebral palsy**, **muscular dystrophy**, **ALS**, advanced **spinal cord injury**.
- Users unable to independently shift weight → **pressure relief**.
- Users with poor **trunk control** requiring supported posture.

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Cross-Brace Folding | Tilt-in-Space |
|---|---|---|
| Portability | High | Low |
| Weight | Lower | Higher |
| Pressure Relief | Limited | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Adjustability | Limited | High |

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- Number of tubes: $N = 6$
- Length per tube: $L = 0.7 \text{ m}$
- Outer diameter: $d_o = 22 \text{ mm} = 0.022 \text{ m}$
- Wall thickness: $t = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m}$
- Inner diameter: $d_i = 0.022 - 0.003 = 0.019 \text{ m}$
- Density of Ti-6Al-4V: $\rho_{Ti} = 4430 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( d_o^2 - d_i^2 \right)
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.022)^2 - (0.019)^2 \right)
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 4.84 \times 10^{-4} - 3.61 \times 10^{-4} \right)
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.23 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
A = 9.660 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Volume**

$$
V = N \cdot A \cdot L = 6 \times 9.660 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.7
$$

$$
V = 4.057 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Frame weight**

$$
W_{frame} = \rho_{Ti} \times V = 4430 \times 4.057 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
W_{frame} = 1.797 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4: Total chair weight (estimated 2.5× bare frame weight)**

$$
W_{total} \approx 2.5 \times 1.797 = 4.49 \text{ kg} \text{ (theoretical minimum)}
$$

**Note:** In practice, titanium frames include additional brackets, cross-braces, and joints, so realistic total chair weight with titanium frame is **8–11 kg**.

**Suitability Comment:**

| Classification | Required Total Weight | Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| **K0004** | < 15.4 kg (34 lbs) | ✅ Suitable — titanium frame well within limits |
| **K0005** | < 13.6 kg (30 lbs) | ⚠️ Borderline — only if full ultralight configuration |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** when computing ISO test load → examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up HCPCS K-codes** (K0001 vs K0005) → major conceptual error; 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Skipping the $I = \pi d^4/64$ formula** in derivation → examiners expect this explicit step.
> 4. **Choosing wrong frame type for clinical case** (e.g., prescribing rigid K0005 for an elderly low-activity user) → clinical reasoning marks lost.
> 5. **Not stating units** in final numerical answers → typically ½ mark deducted per missing unit.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Frame** = load-bearing skeleton of the assistive mobility device.
- ✅ **Five HCPCS K-codes** (K0001–K0005) define manual wheelchair categories by weight and adjustability.
- ✅ **Rigid frames** are lightest and most energy-efficient; **folding frames** trade energy for portability.
- ✅ **Tilt-in-space** frames preserve wheelbase geometry while redistributing pressure.
- ✅ **Standing frames** provide physiological and psychological benefits for SCI users.
- ✅ **Material trade-off**: Steel (cheap, heavy) → Aluminum (mid) → Titanium (premium) → Carbon fiber (elite).
- ✅ **ISO 7176-8** mandates static test load = **1.5 × user mass × g**.
- ✅ **Euler buckling** governs long slender frame tubes; hollow circular sections maximize $I/A$ ratio.
- ✅ **Second moment of area**: $I_{hollow} = \dfrac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{64}$.
- ✅ **Bending stress**: $\sigma = \dfrac{Mc}{I}$.
- ✅ **Factor of safety** for static wheelchair frames should be $\geq 1.25$.
- ✅ **Clinical reasoning** > memorization — examiners reward frame-to-condition justification.
- ✅ **Folding mechanism** = X-brace with offset central hinge.
- ✅ **Tilt mechanism** = four-bar pivot at rear axle.
- ✅ **Camber angle** (typically 0°–12°) affects lateral stability and propulsion biomechanics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** Always structure your 14-mark answers as **(1) classification, (2) clinical/engineering justification, (3) numerical verification, (4) conclusion**. Examiners look for this flow and award step marks generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propelling Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Propelling Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Propelling Structure** in the context of assistive medical devices refers to the engineered mechanical assembly — comprising drive wheels, push rims, levers, gears, motors, cranks, or hand-driven contact surfaces — that converts a user's applied biomechanical input (or external electrical input) into the translational or rotational motion required to move an assistive mobility device, most commonly a **manual or powered wheelchair**, a **mobility scooter**, or a **prosthetic limb drive train**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, the propelling structure is studied as the **kinematic and kinetic interface** between the user and the assistive device. It encompasses:

- The **driving wheel** (where the propulsion torque is applied)
- The **coupling mechanism** (hand rim, lever, crank, motor shaft)
- The **transmission system** (gears, chains, belts, hubs)
- The **frame interface** that transmits reaction forces to the ground

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The "Propelling Structure" topic is positioned between wheel design and braking systems, indicating that students are expected to understand how user input is converted into rolling motion, what biomechanical efficiencies are involved, and how the structure affects user energy expenditure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a shopping cart in a supermarket. The handle is your interface, your arms are the input, and the wheels do the rolling. If you push on the wheel itself versus pushing on a small handle offset from the wheel center, you get very different mechanical advantages. 

A **propelling structure** works exactly the same way:
- The **hand rim** on a wheelchair is a smaller wheel concentric with the main drive wheel — you push on the outer rim, and a mechanical advantage is created based on the **ratio of the hand rim diameter to the wheel diameter**.
- A **lever-drive** wheelchair works like oars on a boat — long handles multiply the torque you apply.
- A **power wheelchair** works like a remote-controlled toy car — a DC motor + gear train + battery replaces your arm's muscular effort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** Every propelling structure is essentially a **torque converter**. The user's goal is to produce enough *torque* at the wheel-axle to overcome rolling resistance, friction, and terrain gradient. The propelling structure's geometry dictates *how* the user must apply force (linear push, rotational push, lever action, or electrical energy) to produce that torque.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard wheelchair drive wheel diameter:** **610 mm (24 inches)** — most common global standard.
- **Hand rim diameter (standard):** **480–530 mm (19–21 inches)**.
- **Mechanical Advantage Ratio (MAR) of hand rim:** typically **0.75–0.87** (ratio of hand rim radius to wheel radius).
- **Push frequency for manual wheelchair propulsion:** **0.6–1.2 Hz (36–72 cycles/min)** for typical self-propulsion.
- **Wheelchair user energy expenditure (METs):** **4.5–6.5 METs** during self-propulsion on level ground.
- **Rolling resistance coefficient (μᵣ):** **0.008–0.015** for pneumatic tires on smooth surface.
- **Standard motor power for powered wheelchairs:** **150–350 W** per drive motor (typical class).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept — Step by Step

The propelling structure functions on the following structured logic chain:

- **Step 1 — Force/Torque Application:** The user (or motor) applies a force at a defined point on the propelling structure. This generates a **moment arm (r)** and hence a **torque (τ = F × r)**.

- **Step 2 — Transmission Through Interface:** This torque is transferred to the drive wheel axle either:
  - *Directly* (in direct-drive push-rim systems), or
  - *Through a gear/chain/belt train* (in geared hub or lever-driven systems), changing the effective torque and angular velocity.

- **Step 3 — Wheel-Ground Interaction:** The torque at the axle applies a tangential force at the tire contact patch. If this force exceeds the static friction limit, the wheel rolls without slipping.

- **Step 4 — Locomotion:** The wheel rolls forward, and the ground reaction force (rolling resistance, gradient component) opposes the motion. The user must sustain a torque greater than the resisting torque to maintain acceleration or constant velocity.

- **Step 5 — Energy Storage and Recovery (Advanced Systems):** Some modern propelling structures (e.g., push-rim-activated power-assist wheelchairs) use **regenerative braking** to recover kinetic energy during deceleration.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why moment arm matters:** A larger moment arm (larger hand rim) reduces the force the user must apply, but requires longer stroke — a classic force-vs.-distance trade-off.
- **Why direct-drive is preferred in sports wheelchairs:** It provides high efficiency (≈ 90–95%) and responsive handling, despite requiring more user force.
- **Why lever drives are used for users with limited grip:** They redistribute effort to the shoulder and biceps, where many users retain strength.
- **Why powered systems use gear reduction:** DC motors produce high speed but low torque. A gear train trades speed for torque to make propulsion feasible.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Torque from applied force | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | F = tangential force (N), r = moment arm (m), τ in N·m |
| 2 | Mechanical Advantage Ratio (hand rim) | $MAR = \dfrac{R_{rim}}{R_{wheel}}$ | Ratio of hand rim radius to wheel radius; standard ≈ 0.79 |
| 3 | Effective push force at tire contact | $F_{tire} = F_{hand} \cdot \dfrac{R_{rim}}{R_{wheel}}$ | Conservative, no slip, no losses |
| 4 | Rolling resistance force | $F_{r} = \mu_{r} \cdot W$ | W = total weight on wheel (N); μᵣ is dimensionless |
| 5 | Grade resistance force | $F_{g} = W \cdot \sin\theta$ | θ = slope angle; ≈ 0.05–0.10 for typical ramps |
| 6 | Power required at constant velocity | $P = F_{total} \cdot v$ | $F_{total} = F_{r} + F_{g} + F_{a}$ (rolling + grade + aerodynamic) |
| 7 | Push frequency | $f_{push} = \dfrac{v}{2\pi R_{wheel}}$ | Approximate, assuming smooth rolling |
| 8 | Gear ratio in powered systems | $G = \dfrac{N_{driven}}{N_{driver}} = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{\omega_{wheel}}$ | Speed reduction when G > 1 |
| 9 | Motor torque at wheel | $\tau_{wheel} = \tau_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta$ | η = gear train efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95) |
| 10 | Energy per stroke | $E_{stroke} = F_{hand} \cdot d_{push}$ | d_push = stroke length on hand rim (m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** Students often confuse the *hand rim radius* with the *wheel radius*. The mechanical advantage is always **less than 1** for hand-rim propulsion (because R_rim < R_wheel), meaning the user must push **harder** than the rolling resistance force. This is the fundamental energetic burden of manual wheelchair propulsion.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sports wheelchairs (basketball, rugby):** Use **cambered wheels** (camber angle 12°–20°) for lateral stability during sharp turns; the propelling structure must accommodate the camber geometry.
- **Push-rim-activated Power-Assist Wheelchairs (PAPAW):** Combine manual and powered propulsion — sensors in the hand rim detect push force and engage motors proportionally.
- **Lever-driven wheelchairs (e.g., Magic Wheels):** Use a two-gear hub; one push on the lever produces **two wheel rotations**, reducing push frequency by 50%.
- **Standing wheelchairs:** Their propelling structure must accommodate a changing center of mass during sit-to-stand transition.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mechanical Advantage of a Hand-Rim Propulsion System

**Problem Statement:** A wheelchair has a drive wheel of radius $R_{wheel} = 305$ mm and a hand rim of radius $R_{rim} = 240$ mm. A user applies a tangential force of $F_{hand} = 80$ N on the hand rim. Determine the effective tangential force at the tire contact patch and the mechanical advantage ratio.

**Step 1 — Identify the torque applied by the user at the hand rim:**

The user pushes tangentially on the hand rim. The moment arm is the hand rim radius.

$$\tau_{applied} = F_{hand} \cdot R_{rim}$$

Substituting values (using SI units: $R_{rim} = 0.240$ m):

$$\tau_{applied} = 80 \cdot 0.240 = 19.2 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the same torque at the wheel axle:**

Since the hand rim is rigidly mounted to the drive wheel (concentric, same axle), the torque is transmitted 1:1 to the wheel.

$$\tau_{wheel} = \tau_{applied} = 19.2 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the tangential force at the tire contact patch:**

The contact patch is at radius $R_{wheel}$ from the axle. The tangential force at the contact patch is:

$$F_{tire} = \dfrac{\tau_{wheel}}{R_{wheel}}$$

$$F_{tire} = \dfrac{19.2}{0.305} = 62.95 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Mechanical Advantage Ratio (MAR):**

$$MAR = \dfrac{F_{tire}}{F_{hand}} = \dfrac{R_{rim}}{R_{wheel}}$$

$$MAR = \dfrac{0.240}{0.305} = 0.787$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The mechanical advantage is **less than 1**, meaning the user must push with a force **larger** than the actual rolling resistance force. Specifically, for every **1 N** of useful propulsion force at the tire, the user must exert **1.27 N** at the hand rim (since $1/0.787 = 1.27$).

$$\boxed{F_{tire} = 62.95 \ \text{N}, \quad MAR = 0.787}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A common student error is to assume MAR should be > 1 for "advantage." In hand-rim propulsion, MAR < 1 because the hand rim is *smaller* than the wheel — but this geometry is necessary to keep the hand rim within the wheel's footprint and to maintain a reasonable push cadence.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Required for Level Constant-Velocity Propulsion

**Problem Statement:** A 70 kg user (including wheelchair) moves at constant velocity v = 1.4 m/s on a level smooth surface. The drive wheels have rolling resistance coefficient μᵣ = 0.010 and pneumatic tire radius 0.305 m. Compute the required push power, push frequency, and force per stroke.

**Step 1 — Compute the total weight on the drive wheels:**

For a two-wheel manual chair, the total weight is supported by both drive wheels (caster wheels carry small load, neglected):

$$W = m \cdot g = 70 \cdot 9.81 = 686.7 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rolling resistance force:**

$$F_{r} = \mu_{r} \cdot W = 0.010 \cdot 686.7 = 6.867 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total propulsive force (level ground, constant velocity):**

At constant velocity, $F_{a} = 0$ (no acceleration) and $F_{g} = 0$ (level):

$$F_{total} = F_{r} = 6.867 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the power required at the contact patch:**

$$P_{wheel} = F_{total} \cdot v = 6.867 \cdot 1.4 = 9.61 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Adjust for the mechanical disadvantage at the hand rim:**

The user must apply more force than the rolling resistance:

$$F_{hand} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{MAR} = \dfrac{6.867}{0.787} = 8.72 \ \text{N}$$

Power at the hand rim is conserved (no losses assumed):

$$P_{hand} = P_{wheel} = 9.61 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the push frequency:**

Wheel angular velocity:

$$\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{v}{R_{wheel}} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.305} = 4.59 \ \text{rad/s}$$

Convert to revolutions per second:

$$f_{wheel} = \dfrac{\omega_{wheel}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{4.59}{2\pi} = 0.731 \ \text{rev/s}$$

If one full push = 1/2 revolution (typical start-recover cycle), push frequency is half the wheel rotation frequency:

$$f_{push} = \dfrac{f_{wheel}}{2} = 0.365 \ \text{Hz} \approx 22 \ \text{cycles/min}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the energy per push stroke:**

Arc length on hand rim per stroke (half-revolution):

$$d_{push} = \pi \cdot R_{rim} = \pi \cdot 0.240 = 0.754 \ \text{m}$$

Energy per stroke:

$$E_{stroke} = F_{hand} \cdot d_{push} = 8.72 \cdot 0.754 = 6.57 \ \text{J}$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Rolling resistance force | 6.87 | N |
| Hand rim force | 8.72 | N |
| Power at wheel | 9.61 | W |
| Push frequency | 0.365 | Hz |
| Energy per stroke | 6.57 | J |
| Mechanical Advantage Ratio | 0.787 | – |

$$\boxed{P_{propulsion} = 9.61 \ \text{W}, \quad f_{push} \approx 22 \ \text{cycles/min}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque and Speed Relationship in a Powered Wheelchair

**Problem Statement:** A powered wheelchair uses two 24 V DC motors, each rated 200 W. The motor output shaft runs at 3000 rpm, and a single-stage spur gear reduction with G = 30:1 drives the wheel. Gear efficiency η = 0.90. Wheel radius is 0.305 m. Compute the motor torque, wheel torque, and maximum theoretical velocity.

**Step 1 — Compute the motor shaft angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{motor} = 3000 \ \text{rpm} = \dfrac{3000 \cdot 2\pi}{60} = 314.16 \ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the motor torque from rated power:**

$$\tau_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{\omega_{motor}} = \dfrac{200}{314.16} = 0.637 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the wheel angular velocity after gear reduction:**

$$\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{G} = \dfrac{314.16}{30} = 10.47 \ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the wheel torque (after gear reduction and efficiency loss):**

$$\tau_{wheel} = \tau_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta = 0.637 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.90 = 17.20 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum theoretical velocity:**

$$v_{max} = \omega_{wheel} \cdot R_{wheel} = 10.47 \cdot 0.305 = 3.19 \ \text{m/s} \approx 11.5 \ \text{km/h}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the propulsive force at the contact patch:**

$$F_{tire} = \dfrac{\tau_{wheel}}{R_{wheel}} = \dfrac{17.20}{0.305} = 56.4 \ \text{N}$$

Since this is far greater than the typical rolling resistance (~7 N for 70 kg user), the chair has substantial margin to climb ramps or accelerate.

$$\boxed{\tau_{wheel} = 17.20 \ \text{N·m}, \quad v_{max} \approx 3.19 \ \text{m/s}, \quad F_{tire} = 56.4 \ \text{N}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Propelling Structures

| Feature | Direct Hand-Rim | Lever Drive | Power Assist (PAPAW) | Fully Powered |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User input | Muscular | Muscular | Muscular (assisted) | Joystick/control |
| MAR | 0.75–0.87 | 1.5–2.5 | 0.75–0.87 (boosted) | N/A (motor-direct) |
| Push frequency | High (1 Hz) | Low (0.5 Hz) | High (1 Hz) | N/A |
| User energy cost | High (5.5 METs) | Moderate (4 METs) | Low (3 METs) | Very low (2 METs) |
| Shoulder injury risk | High | Moderate | Low | Very low |
| Speed | 0.5–1.5 m/s | 0.4–1.0 m/s | 1.0–2.0 m/s | 1.5–3.0 m/s |
| Best for | Athletic users | Arthritic users | Long-distance users | Users with limited upper body |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Propelling Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Input Source]:::input --> B[Coupling Interface]:::interface
    B --> C[Torque Transmission]:::transmission
    C --> D[Drive Wheel Axle]:::axle
    D --> E[Tire Contact Patch]:::contact
    E --> F[Ground Reaction Forces]:::ground
    F --> G[Rolling Resistance fr]:::resist
    F --> H[Grade Component fg]:::resist
    F --> I[Aerodynamic Drag fa]:::resist
    G --> J[Net Propulsive Force Fnet]:::net
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Locomotion Result]:::output

    subgraph Modes
        M1[Manual Hand-Rim Mode]
        M2[Lever-Drive Mode]
        M3[Push-Rim Power-Assist Mode]
        M4[Fully Motorized Mode]
    end

    A -.uses.-> M1
    A -.uses.-> M2
    A -.uses.-> M3
    A -.uses.-> M4

    classDef input fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef interface fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef transmission fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef axle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef contact fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ground fill:#CD853F,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef resist fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Hand-Rim Propulsion Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheel Center Axle]:::axle --> B[Wheel Radius Rw = 305 mm]:::wheel
    A --> C[Hand Rim Radius Rr = 240 mm]:::rim
    C --> D[Applied Force Fhand at Tangent]:::force
    B --> E[Tangential Force Ftire at Contact Patch]:::force

    F[Torque τ = Fhand × Rr]:::torque --> G[Transmitted to Axle 1:1]:::trans
    G --> H[Effective Force at Tire = τ / Rw]:::output

    classDef axle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef wheel fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFF
    classDef rim fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFF
    classDef force fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef torque fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Power Train Flow — Motorized Wheelchair

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery Pack 24V DC]:::battery --> B[DC Motor]:::motor
    B --> C[Motor Shaft ωmotor τmotor]:::shaft
    C --> D[Gear Train G=30:1 η=0.90]:::gear
    D --> E[Wheel Axle ωwheel τwheel]:::axle
    E --> F[Drive Wheel R=0.305m]:::wheel
    F --> G[Ground Contact]:::ground

    H[Joystick or Push-Rim Sensor]:::control --> B

    classDef battery fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef shaft fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFF
```
