> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The red links in the diagrams represent the *force/torque flow*; the green links represent *output*; the blue links represent *control or signal paths*. In KTU examinations, students are often asked to *trace the energy flow* in a powered wheelchair — the battery → motor → gear → wheel → ground chain is the standard answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define a propelling structure in the context of a manual wheelchair. List its three main functional components with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **propelling structure** is the mechanical assembly in a wheelchair or assistive mobility device that converts the user's applied force (or external energy input) into the rolling motion of the drive wheels.

The three main functional components are:

1. **Drive Wheel (Component 1 mark):** The main wheel that contacts the ground and transmits the propulsive force. *Example: 610 mm diameter spoked wheel with pneumatic tire.*

2. **Coupling Interface (Component 1 mark):** The surface or mechanism where the user applies force. *Example: A 530 mm diameter hand rim mounted concentrically to the drive wheel.*

3. **Transmission / Mounting (Component 1 mark):** The structural element that supports the wheel and transmits reaction forces to the frame. *Example: A quick-release axle mounted on the wheelchair frame.*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**What is the Mechanical Advantage Ratio (MAR) of a hand-rim wheelchair? Why is it always less than 1, and what is its typical range?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mechanical Advantage Ratio (MAR)** is defined as the ratio of the hand rim radius to the drive wheel radius:

$$MAR = \dfrac{R_{rim}}{R_{wheel}}$$

It is **always less than 1** (1 mark) because the hand rim is necessarily *smaller* than the drive wheel — the hand rim sits inside the wheel circumference so the user can reach it from a seated position. Since $R_{rim} < R_{wheel}$, the MAR is always < 1.

The typical range is **0.75–0.87** (1 mark). This means the user must apply 1.15–1.33 times the actual useful propulsive force at the tire contact patch.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A: `[KTU ESE - Model Paper 1]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the applied hand-rim force, hand-rim radius, and the effective propulsive force at the tire contact patch for a direct-drive manual wheelchair. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A user weighing 75 kg (including chair) propels a wheelchair on a 5° inclined surface. The drive wheel radius is 320 mm, the hand rim radius is 250 mm, and the rolling resistance coefficient is 0.012. Calculate: (i) Total resistance force, (ii) Required hand-rim force, (iii) Power required at 1.5 m/s constant velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. No slip between the tire and ground (pure rolling).
2. No friction losses in the axle bearings.
3. Constant velocity (no acceleration considered).
4. Hand rim is rigidly attached to the drive wheel (1:1 torque transmission).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The user applies a tangential force $F_{hand}$ at the hand rim, which is at radius $R_{rim}$ from the wheel center. The torque generated at the axle is:

$$\tau_{applied} = F_{hand} \cdot R_{rim} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Since the hand rim and drive wheel are concentric and rigidly coupled, this torque is transmitted directly to the wheel axle:

$$\tau_{wheel} = \tau_{applied} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

The effective tangential propulsive force at the tire contact patch is obtained by dividing the torque by the wheel radius:

$$F_{tire} = \dfrac{\tau_{wheel}}{R_{wheel}} = \dfrac{F_{hand} \cdot R_{rim}}{R_{wheel}} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{F_{tire} = F_{hand} \cdot \dfrac{R_{rim}}{R_{wheel}} = F_{hand} \cdot MAR}$$

The Mechanical Advantage Ratio is therefore:

$$MAR = \dfrac{R_{rim}}{R_{wheel}} < 1$$

This means $F_{hand} > F_{tire}$, i.e., the user must push harder than the useful propulsive force. (Conclusion 1 mark)

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $m = 75$ kg
- $\theta = 5°$
- $R_{wheel} = 0.320$ m
- $R_{rim} = 0.250$ m
- $\mu_r = 0.012$
- $v = 1.5$ m/s

**Step 1 — Total weight (1 mark):**

$$W = 75 \cdot 9.81 = 735.75 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Rolling resistance force (1 mark):**

$$F_r = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.012 \cdot 735.75 = 8.83 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Grade resistance force (1 mark):**

$$F_g = W \cdot \sin\theta = 735.75 \cdot \sin(5°) = 735.75 \cdot 0.0872 = 64.13 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Total resistance at constant velocity (1 mark):**

$$F_{total} = F_r + F_g = 8.83 + 64.13 = 72.96 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Required hand-rim force (1.5 marks):**

$$MAR = \dfrac{R_{rim}}{R_{wheel}} = \dfrac{0.250}{0.320} = 0.781$$

$$F_{hand} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{MAR} = \dfrac{72.96}{0.781} = 93.42 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Power required at 1.5 m/s (1.5 marks):**

$$P = F_{total} \cdot v = 72.96 \cdot 1.5 = 109.4 \ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{F_{total} = 72.96 \ \text{N}, \quad F_{hand} = 93.42 \ \text{N}, \quad P = 109.4 \ \text{W}}$$

---

### Question B: `[KTU ESE - Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a push-rim-activated power-assist wheelchair (PAPAW). Compare its energy efficiency with a fully manual wheelchair. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A powered wheelchair uses two 24 V DC motors rated 250 W each, with a gear reduction of 25:1 and efficiency 0.88. Wheel radius is 0.305 m. The motor no-load speed is 3500 rpm. Compute: (i) Motor torque, (ii) Wheel torque, (iii) Maximum theoretical velocity, (iv) The maximum grade the chair can climb assuming total vehicle weight 80 kg and rolling coefficient 0.010. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

##### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Efficiency Discussion (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[User Push] → [Push-Rim Force Sensors] → [Controller] → [Battery Power] → [DC Motors] → [Gear Train] → [Drive Wheels] → [Motion]
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- The user pushes on the hand rim as in a normal manual wheelchair.
- Force/contact sensors embedded in the hand rim detect the magnitude and direction of the user's applied force.
- The sensor signals are sent to a microcontroller, which commands the battery-powered DC motors to deliver assistive torque proportional to the user's input.
- The motors are coupled to the wheel axles (typically through gears), and their torque is added to the user's manual torque.

**Energy Efficiency Comparison (2 marks):**
- **Manual wheelchair:** 5.5–6.5 METs (≈ 4–5× resting metabolic rate). Energy is entirely supplied by the user's upper limbs; propulsion efficiency ≈ 6–8% (most energy is spent on shoulder stabilization, not on wheel torque).
- **PAPAW:** 2.5–3.5 METs — energy efficiency of the *propulsion* action is effectively 30–50%, because the motor supplies the bulk of the propulsive torque. Net energy cost to the user is **roughly halved** compared to fully manual propulsion, with significantly reduced shoulder injury risk.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $P_{motor} = 250$ W, $V = 24$ V
- $G = 25$, $\eta = 0.88$
- $R_{wheel} = 0.305$ m
- $N_{no-load} = 3500$ rpm
- $m = 80$ kg, $\mu_r = 0.010$

**Step 1 — Motor no-load angular velocity (0.5 mark):**

$$\omega_{motor} = \dfrac{3500 \cdot 2\pi}{60} = 366.52 \ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Motor torque at rated power (1.5 marks):**

$$\tau_{motor} = \dfrac{P}{\omega} = \dfrac{250}{366.52} = 0.682 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Wheel torque after gear reduction (1 mark):**

$$\tau_{wheel} = \tau_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta = 0.682 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.88 = 15.00 \ \text{N·m (per motor)}$$

For two motors, total:

$$\tau_{total} = 2 \cdot 15.00 = 30.00 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum theoretical velocity (1 mark):**

$$\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{G} = \dfrac{366.52}{25} = 14.66 \ \text{rad/s}$$

$$v_{max} = \omega_{wheel} \cdot R_{wheel} = 14.66 \cdot 0.305 = 4.47 \ \text{m/s} \approx 16.1 \ \text{km/h}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum grade (2 marks):**

The maximum tractive force per motor is:

$$F_{tire} = \dfrac{\tau_{wheel}}{R_{wheel}} = \dfrac{15.00}{0.305} = 49.18 \ \text{N per motor}$$

Total tractive force:

$$F_{tire,total} = 2 \cdot 49.18 = 98.36 \ \text{N}$$

For a static (just-climbs) condition on a slope, neglecting acceleration:

$$F_{tire,total} = W \cdot \mu_r + W \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$98.36 = (80 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.010) + (80 \cdot 9.81) \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$98.36 = 7.85 + 784.8 \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$\sin\theta = \dfrac{98.36 - 7.85}{784.8} = \dfrac{90.51}{784.8} = 0.1153$$

$$\theta = \arcsin(0.1153) = 6.62° \approx 11.5\% \ \text{grade}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{motor} = 0.682 \ \text{N·m}, \quad \tau_{total} = 30.0 \ \text{N·m}, \quad v_{max} = 4.47 \ \text{m/s}, \quad \theta_{max} \approx 6.62°}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mark Loss #1 — Unit Conversion:** Many students write $R_{wheel} = 305$ mm in formulas requiring SI units. Always convert to meters *before* plugging into equations.
> 2. **Common Mark Loss #2 — Confusing MAR direction:** A student may write $F_{tire} = F_{hand} \cdot R_{wheel}/R_{rim}$ (inverted ratio). Remember: $F_{tire} = F_{hand} \cdot (R_{rim}/R_{wheel})$.
> 3. **Common Mark Loss #3 — Forgetting gear efficiency:** $\eta$ is *not* 1.0 in real systems. Skipping this is a 1–2 mark deduction in numerical problems.
> 4. **Common Mark Loss #4 — Slope formula:** Use $W \cdot \sin\theta$, NOT $W \cdot \tan\theta$. On small slopes (< 15°), these are similar, but for ramp compliance and KTU standards, $\sin\theta$ is the accepted convention.
> 5. **Common Mark Loss #5 — Motor count:** In a two-motor wheelchair, remember to *multiply by 2* for total torque and tractive force; do not forget the *combined* output.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Propelling structure = torque converter:** Converts user input (force/lever/motor) into wheel rotation.
- ✅ **Hand-rim MAR is ALWAYS < 1** (typically 0.75–0.87) because the rim is *inside* the wheel.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $F_{tire} = F_{hand} \cdot (R_{rim}/R_{wheel})$
- ✅ **Rolling resistance:** $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W$, $\mu_r \in [0.008, 0.015]$ for pneumatic tires.
- ✅ **Grade resistance:** $F_g = W \sin\theta$ — use sine, not tangent.
- ✅ **Power at constant velocity:** $P = F_{total} \cdot v$ — energy in equals energy out at steady state.
- ✅ **Push frequency** ≈ 0.5–1.0 Hz for manual users; lever drives halve it.
- ✅ **Powered wheelchair gear ratio** trades motor speed for torque; never forget efficiency $\eta$.
- ✅ **PAPAW (push-rim power assist)** reduces user MET cost from 5.5 → 2.5–3.5 — major clinical benefit.
- ✅ **Lever-drive systems** (e.g., Magic Wheels) provide mechanical advantage > 1, unlike hand-rim systems.
- ✅ **Maximum grade calculation** must include both rolling AND grade components in the resistance force balance.
- ✅ **Cambered wheels** in sports wheelchairs: camber angle 12°–20°, affects lateral stability and rolling dynamics.
- ✅ **Energy per stroke** = $F_{hand} \cdot d_{push}$, where $d_{push} = \pi R_{rim}$ for half-revolution push.
- ✅ **KTU standard units:** N, m, s, rad, W, Hz, N·m — always include units in numerical answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specialized Bases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specialized Bases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the discipline of **Prosthetics and Orthotics**, the term **"Specialized Bases"** refers to custom-engineered prosthetic socket–interface systems designed to optimize load transfer, suspension, alignment, and residual limb biomechanics for specific amputation levels, limb geometries, and patient pathologies. Unlike generic sockets, specialized bases incorporate anatomical landmarks (e.g., patellar tendon, ischial tuberosity, femoral condyles), biomechanical principles (hydrostatic pressure, skeletal locking, suction suspension), and material engineering (carbon fiber, silicone liners, thermoplastic resins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT744 Module 2):**
> "Specialized Bases encompass purpose-built prosthetic socket designs—including PTB, PTS, KBM, CAT-CAM, Ischial Containment, Quadrilateral, Syme's, and Hip/Knee Disarticulation bases—engineered to distribute physiological loads, achieve anatomical suspension, and restore kinetic alignment unique to each level of amputation."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine the prosthetic socket as the **"shoe" of an artificial limb**. A regular shoe may fit generally, but specialized bases are like **custom-molded orthopedic insoles**—each shaped to a specific foot arch, pressure point, and gait pattern. Just as a marathon runner's shoe differs from a mountaineer's boot, a transfemoral amputee's quadrilateral socket differs fundamentally from a transtibial amputee's PTB socket. The "specialized" part means the base is **biomechanically tailored** to where the body can bear load, where it cannot, and how the limb must be suspended in space.

### 1.3 Classification of Specialized Bases (Overview)

| Level of Amputation | Specialized Base(s) | Load-Bearing Region |
|---|---|---|
| Partial Foot | Slipper socket, Chopart, Lisfranc bases | Plantar surface |
| Syme's | Syme's socket (medial opening) | Patellar tendon + tibial flare |
| Transtibial (Below Knee) | PTB, PTS, KBM, TSB | Patellar tendon, medial flare, popliteal area |
| Knee Disarticulation | KD socket (bulbous end) | Distal femur condyles |
| Transfemoral (Above Knee) | Quadrilateral, Ischial Containment (CAT-CAM) | Ischial tuberosity, gluteal region |
| Hip Disarticulation | Canadian-type, saucer-type | Ischial seat + pelvic band |
| Transradial | Münster, Northwestern, triceps cuff | Humeral condyles, olecranon |
| Transhumeral | Socket with figure-8 harness, locking liner | Acromion, humeral shaft |

### 1.4 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

- **Patellar Tendon Bar Pressure:** Typically **5–7 kg/cm²** (PTB design standard)
- **Socket Fit Tolerance:** **0.5–1.0 cm** circumferential clearance (varies with liner)
- **Residual Limb Length Ratio (RLLR):** **0.4–0.6** for optimal transtibial leverage
- **Knee Center Alignment:** **9–12 mm** anterior to the trochanteric line (transfemoral)
- **Suction Seal Vacuum:** **Negative pressure of 40–60 kPa** for active vacuum suspension
- **Standard Build Height:** **12–18 cm** for endoskeletal modular bases

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The "specialized" nature of a base is defined by **three engineering parameters** — (1) load transfer mechanism, (2) suspension principle, and (3) alignment geometry. Memorize this triad.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Engineering Principles of Specialized Base Design

Every specialized base must satisfy **four fundamental engineering objectives**:

1. **Load Distribution** — Transfer ground reaction forces (GRF) to tolerant anatomical regions.
2. **Suspension** — Hold the residual limb within the socket against gravitational and inertial forces.
3. **Stability & Alignment** — Restore kinematic chain (e.g., knee center, hip center) for symmetrical gait.
4. **Comfort & Skin Integrity** — Avoid pressure ulcers, shear, and thermal injury.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — How a PTB (Patellar Tendon Bearing) Base Works

The PTB base, introduced by the **University of California, Berkeley (1957–1959)**, redistributes load to the patellar tendon, medial tibial flare, and popliteal region.

**Step 1 — Pressure Mapping:**
The residual limb is mapped for pressure tolerance. The **patellar tendon** is identified as a primary weight-bearing area (similar to a kneeling posture).

**Step 2 — Infrapatellar Bar Construction:**
A **3–5 cm wide concave bar** is modeled over the patellar tendon to concentrate pressure (5–7 kg/cm²), forcing the patellar tendon to bear significant load.

**Step 3 — Medial Flare (Wedge) Build-up:**
A **medial wedge** is added over the medial tibial flare to offload the distal end and prevent distal edema (choking).

**Step 4 — Popliteal Relief:**
The **popliteal area** is relieved to prevent hyperextension moment and allow hamstring function.

**Step 5 — Suspension:**
Achieved via a **supracondylar strap or cuff** above the femoral condyles (NOT a suction seal — this is *suspension*, not *suction*).

### 2.3 Comparison: PTS vs. KBM vs. TSB

- **PTS (Patellar Tendon Supracondylar):** PTB + medial and lateral supracondylar extensions that lock onto the femoral condyles → **skeletal suspension**, good for short residual limbs.
- **KBM (Kondylen Bettung Münster):** German design; medial wedge built *into* the socket wall, accepting a removable medial brim → **sleeker cosmetic profile**.
- **TSB (Total Surface Bearing):** Uses a **silicone liner with locking pin**; uniform hydrostatic pressure across the entire residual limb surface (no preferential loading).

### 2.4 Transfemoral Specialized Bases: Quadrilateral vs. Ischial Containment (CAT-CAM)

**Quadrilateral Socket (Traditional, 1950s American design):**
- 4-sided cross-section: **anterior wall 5 cm above ischial tuberosity**, posterior wall horizontally.
- Weight is borne on the **ischial tuberosity + gluteal muscle mass**.
- The **ischial tuberosity sits ON the posterior brim** (ischial seat) — this creates a "sitting on a shelf" feel.
- Femur is **adducted** to align with the knee center.

**Ischial Containment / CAT-CAM (Modern, 1980s design by Sabolich):**
- The **ischial tuberosity and ramus are INSIDE the socket** (containment, not shelf).
- Femur is held in **adduction and slight flexion** to mimic anatomical weight-bearing.
- Provides a **narrower mediolateral dimension**, giving better muscular control.
- Better for **long stumps and active patients**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patellar Tendon Pressure | $P = \frac{F}{A}$ | Force over tendon bar area | $\text{kg/cm}^2$ |
| Hydrostatic Pressure (TSB) | $P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Uniform pressure on residual limb | $\text{kPa}$ |
| Suction Vacuum Pressure | $P_v$ (active range) | Active vacuum suspension | $\text{kPa}$ |
| Socket Volume | $V_s = \pi r^2 h$ | Approximate cylindrical socket | $\text{cm}^3$ |
| Build Height (BH) | $BH = L_{stump} + L_{socket} + L_{pylon}$ | Total endoskeletal length | $\text{cm}$ |
| Knee Center Offset | $d_{knee} = 9 \text{ to } 12$ | Anterior to trochanteric line | $\text{mm}$ |
| Distal End Load % | $L_d = 5 \text{ to } 15$ | Allowed on PTB distal end | \% |
| TSB Liner Compression | $\sigma = \frac{E \cdot \Delta L}{L_0}$ | Silicone liner axial stress | $\text{kPa}$ |
| Brim Height (Quadrilateral) | $h_b = 5$ | Above ischial tuberosity | $\text{cm}$ |
| Adduction Angle | $\theta_{add} = 5 \text{ to } 10$ | Femoral adduction in IC socket | degrees |
| Flexion Angle | $\theta_{flex} = 5 \text{ to } 10$ | Femoral flexion in IC socket | degrees |
| Residual Limb Ratio | $RLR = \frac{L_{stump}}{L_{intact}}$ | For prosthetic candidacy | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In KTU examinations, numerical values from the table above are frequently asked. The patellar tendon pressure range of **5–7 kg/cm²** and the knee center offset of **9–12 mm** are the most commonly tested numerical values.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAD/CAM Socket Fabrication:** Modern specialized bases are digitally carved from **polypropylene, polyethylene, or carbon-fiber pre-preg laminates** using CNC carvers, with ±0.5 mm accuracy.
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** Pressure distribution in PTB and IC sockets is analyzed using FEA to optimize brim geometry.
- **3D Printing:** Polyamide (PA12) and flexible TPU sockets are now 3D-printed with lattice structures for breathability.
- **Outcome Statistics:** Patients fitted with **Ischial Containment sockets** report **~15–20% reduction in energy expenditure** during walking compared to quadrilateral sockets (industry data).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Patellar Tendon Bar Geometry

We need to determine the **width and depth of the patellar tendon bar** such that the pressure $P$ falls within the safe range of **5–7 kg/cm²**, given the typical axial load $F$ transmitted through the patellar tendon during mid-stance.

**Given Data:**
- Axial load through residual limb (mid-stance): $F = 60 \text{ kg}$ (≈ 60% body weight of a 100 kg individual)
- Patellar tendon width: $w = 3 \text{ cm}$ (anatomical constant)
- Safe pressure range: $P \in [5, 7] \text{ kg/cm}^2$

**Step 1 — Express pressure in terms of bar area.**

The pressure on the tendon bar is the load divided by the contact area of the bar.

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Express the area in terms of bar width and length.**

The bar is rectangular (approximated), so:

$$A = w \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the pressure equation.**

$$P = \frac{F}{w \cdot L}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the bar length $L$ to achieve the target pressure.**

Rearranging for $L$:

$$L = \frac{F}{w \cdot P}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the required length for the lower bound of pressure (5 kg/cm²).**

Plugging in the values:

$$L = \frac{60 \text{ kg}}{3 \text{ cm} \times 5 \text{ kg/cm}^2} = \frac{60}{15} = 4 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the required length for the upper bound of pressure (7 kg/cm²).**

$$L = \frac{60}{3 \times 7} = \frac{60}{21} \approx 2.86 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result.**

For a 60 kg axial load, the patellar tendon bar length must lie in the range:

$$L \in [2.86, 4.00] \text{ cm}$$

This is why standard PTB bars are designed at **3–5 cm in length** — this matches the KTU textbook specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *longer* bar spreads load and reduces pressure. A *shorter* bar concentrates pressure but increases risk of tissue trauma. The **3–5 cm range is a clinical sweet spot** balancing load transfer and tissue safety.

### 3.2 Derivation: Knee Center Alignment Offset in Transfemoral Bases

For a transfemoral (AK) amputee using a quadrilateral or IC socket, the **knee center** of the prosthetic knee unit must be aligned **9–12 mm anterior to the trochanteric line** to restore the **weight-line** passing through the knee during mid-stance.

**Given:**
- Trochanter-knee distance (intact side): $D = 45 \text{ cm}$
- Trochanter-ankle distance (intact side): $L = 90 \text{ cm}$
- Required knee center offset: $d_k = 10 \text{ mm}$ (midpoint of 9–12 mm)

**Step 1 — Define the weight-line angle.**

The weight-line (gravity line) passes from the trochanter to the floor, creating an angle $\theta$ with the vertical:

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_k}{D}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{10 \text{ mm}}{450 \text{ mm}}\right) = \arctan(0.0222)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the angle in degrees.**

$$\theta \approx 1.27^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the weight-line position at the ankle.**

The horizontal displacement of the weight-line at the ankle is:

$$x_{ankle} = D \cdot \tan(\theta) \approx L \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

$$x_{ankle} = 90 \text{ cm} \times 0.0222 = 2.0 \text{ cm anterior}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The **knee center offset of 10 mm** at the femur translates to a **2 cm anterior weight-line** at the ankle. This matches the natural weight-line of the intact limb and ensures the prosthetic knee remains **stable in mid-stance** (a posteriorly placed knee would buckle; an anteriorly placed knee would hyperextend).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Specialized Base

| Patient Condition | Recommended Base | Reason | Mark / Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long transtibial stump, active patient | TSB with locking liner | Hydrostatic, full-surface load | Modern, comfortable |
| Short transtibial stump (<10 cm) | PTS or KBM | Supracondylar suspension needed | Better skeletal lock |
| Medium transtibial, standard | PTB | Time-tested, low cost | Classical solution |
| Knee disarticulation | KD socket (bulbous) | Anatomic, leverages condyles | Distal end-bearing |
| Transfemoral, sedentary, geriatric | Quadrilateral | Easy donning, ischial shelf | For low mobility |
| Transfemoral, young, athletic | Ischial Containment (CAT-CAM) | Better femoral control | Narrower M-L |
| Syme's amputation | Syme's (medial opening) | Bulbous end accommodated | Distal weight-bearing |
| Partial foot, Chopart | Slipper socket with toe-filler | Preserves ankle motion | Conservative |
| B/K with sensitive skin | Silicone liner + PTB | Shear reduction | Tissue protection |
| B/K with poor hand dexterity | Suction socket (pin lock) | No straps needed | Self-donning |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Specialized Prosthetic Base


### 4.4 Block Diagram: Transtibial PTB Cross-Section Schematic (Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PTB[PTB Socket Cross Section]
        direction TB
        A1[Anterior Wall Patellar Tendon Bar] --> A2[Infrapatellar Pressure Zone 5-7 kg per cm2]
        B1[Medial Wall Tibial Flare Wedge] --> B2[Load Sharing Region]
        C1[Lateral Wall Counter Pressure] --> C2[Stabilization Zone]
        D1[Posterior Wall Popliteal Relief] --> D2[Hamstring Clearance]
        E1[Distal End Minimal Load 5-15 percent] --> E2[Choking Prevention]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid block diagrams use **alphanumeric node IDs only** (e.g., `A1`, `B2`) and plain uppercase text labels — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML inside the labels — to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Define the Patellar Tendon Bearing (PTB) socket. List its three primary load-bearing anatomical regions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Patellar Tendon Bearing (PTB) socket** is a specialized transtibial prosthetic base that transfers axial load primarily through the patellar tendon rather than the distal end of the residual limb. It was developed to prevent distal skin breakdown and improve weight-bearing comfort.

The **three primary load-bearing regions** are:

1. **Patellar tendon** (infrapatellar bar) — primary weight-bearing zone.
2. **Medial tibial flare** (medial wedge) — secondary load-sharing zone.
3. **Popliteal fossa region** (posteriorly, with relief) — counter-pressure for stability.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing 3 regions + 1 mark for correct anatomical naming.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between a Quadrilateral socket and an Ischial Containment (CAT-CAM) socket in transfemoral prosthetics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Quadrilateral | Ischial Containment (CAT-CAM) |
|---|---|---|
| Ischial position | **Sits ON** the posterior shelf | **INSIDE** the socket (contained) |
| Weight-bearing | Ischial tuberosity + gluteal mass | Ischial ramus + medial femoral shaft |
| Mediolateral width | Wider (muscle containment) | Narrower (for active control) |
| Femoral alignment | Adduction only | Adduction + slight flexion |
| Best for | Sedentary / geriatric | Young, athletic, long stumps |

*(Mark split: 1 mark per distinguishing feature, 3 features expected.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU End-Semester Exam Model]**

**(a)** Explain the biomechanical principles underlying the design of a **Total Surface Bearing (TSB) socket** with a silicone locking liner. Describe how it differs from the classical PTB design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transtibial amputee has a residual limb length of 12 cm and a body weight of 75 kg. Calculate the required **patellar tendon bar length** for a PTB socket, assuming the patellar tendon bar is 3 cm wide and the target pressure is 6 kg/cm². Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) TSB vs PTB — Biomechanical Principles (7 marks):**

The **Total Surface Bearing (TSB) socket** operates on the principle of **hydrostatic pressure distribution**, in which axial load is transmitted *uniformly* across the entire surface of the residual limb (in contact with the silicone liner), rather than being concentrated at specific anatomical landmarks.

**Key Engineering Points:**

1. **Hydrostatic Pressure Principle:** A fluid (or fluid-like elastomer liner) transmits pressure equally in all directions. Hence, when the residual limb is encased in a silicone liner and inserted into a snug-fitting socket, the ground reaction force is distributed over the entire limb surface. This is expressed as:
$$P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$
where $\rho$ is the effective density of the limb–liner system, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $h$ is the hydrostatic column height.

2. **Silicone Liner Function:** The silicone liner (typically 3–9 mm thick, Shore A 30–50) acts as a **cushion and load diffuser**. It deforms conformally to bony prominences, reducing point pressures below the tissue-damage threshold of **~15 kg/cm²**.

3. **Locking Pin Suspension:** A pin at the distal end of the liner engages a **shuttle lock** in the socket, providing secure mechanical suspension — a more reliable system than supracondylar straps.

4. **Comfort and Skin Integrity:** Uniform pressure reduces pistoning, shear, and distal edema.

**Difference from PTB:**

| Aspect | PTB | TSB |
|---|---|---|
| Load transfer | Localized (patellar tendon, flare) | Distributed (total surface) |
| Pressure profile | Peak at 5–7 kg/cm² on tendon | Uniform, <2 kg/cm² anywhere |
| Liner | Optional (foam or sock) | Mandatory (silicone) |
| Suspension | Strap or supracondylar wedge | Pin lock or suction |
| Distal end | Off-loaded (relieved) | Loaded (pressure-tolerant) |
| Indication | Medium stumps, traditional | Sensitive skin, modern patients |

*(Mark split: 3 marks for hydrostatic principle, 2 marks for liner function, 2 marks for PTB comparison.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — PTB Bar Length (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Body weight: $W = 75 \text{ kg}$
- Axial load at mid-stance: $F = 0.6 \times W = 0.6 \times 75 = 45 \text{ kg}$ (60% body weight)
- Patellar tendon bar width: $w = 3 \text{ cm}$
- Target pressure: $P = 6 \text{ kg/cm}^2$

**Step 1 — Recall the pressure formula.**

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{w \cdot L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$6 = \frac{45}{3 \cdot L}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $L$.**

$$L = \frac{45}{3 \times 6} = \frac{45}{18} = 2.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Verify against the safe range.**

The bar length $L = 2.5 \text{ cm}$ falls within the textbook-accepted range of **2.5–4 cm**, confirming the design is safe and within the prescribed pressure window.

**Step 5 — Note on the residual limb length.**

The stump length of 12 cm is adequate (RLR = 12/40 ≈ 0.30 for an assumed intact limb of 40 cm). Note: 12 cm is on the shorter side; if the stump were <8 cm, a PTS or KBM would be preferred to gain supracondylar suspension. However, the calculation above remains valid for a 12 cm stump.

**Final Answer:** Required patellar tendon bar length = **2.5 cm**.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for substitution and solving, 1 mark for verification against range, 3 marks for clinical interpretation and judgment.)*

---

#### Alternative Part B Choice (Module Internal Choice):

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU End-Semester Exam Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **Ischial Containment (CAT-CAM) socket** design in detail. Explain the rationale for femoral adduction and flexion in this design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transfemoral amputee requires socket fabrication. Given that the trochanter-knee distance is 40 cm and the recommended knee center offset is 10 mm anterior to the trochanteric line, compute the resulting weight-line angle. State the clinical significance of this offset. **(7 marks)**

*(Note: Detailed model solution is provided in §3.2 above for part (b). The weight-line angle computes to **θ ≈ 1.43°** using the exact formula; the clinical significance is that the weight-line passes anterior to the knee center, providing extension moment and stability in mid-stance.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PTB and PTS:** Students often state that PTB has "supracondylar suspension." It does NOT. Only **PTS and KBM** have supracondylar suspension. PTB uses a strap or wedge. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
>
> 2. **Wrong Patellar Tendon Pressure Range:** The range is **5–7 kg/cm²**, NOT 10–15. Examiners specifically test this number. *(Lose 1 mark if written as 10+.)*
>
> 3. **Confusing Ischial Containment with Quadrilateral:** Many students say "ischial containment means ischial tuberosity sits on a shelf." This is the **quadrilateral** design. In IC, the ischial ramus is **INSIDE** the socket. *(Lose 2 marks.)*
>
> 4. **Forgetting Units in Calculations:** Always specify kg/cm², cm, or mm. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
>
> 5. **Not Mentioning Suspension Method:** Every socket description MUST mention the suspension method. A description of PTB without mentioning "supracondylar strap or medial wedge suspension" is incomplete. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
>
> 6. **Skipping the Clinical Justification:** Numerical answers must end with a clinical interpretation sentence, not just the number.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **PTB** = load on patellar tendon, medial flare, popliteal relief; **pressure 5–7 kg/cm²**; strap suspension.
- ✅ **PTS** = PTB + supracondylar extensions → **skeletal lock**, ideal for short stumps.
- ✅ **KBM** = German design, medial wedge *built into* socket wall, removable medial brim.
- ✅ **TSB** = hydrostatic, total surface bearing, silicone liner with locking pin.
- ✅ **Quadrilateral** = ischial tuberosity ON shelf, **5 cm brim height**, older design.
- ✅ **Ischial Containment (CAT-CAM)** = ischial ramus INSIDE socket, **adduction + 5–10° flexion**, modern.
- ✅ **Syme's** = bulbous distal end, medial opening, distal weight-bearing.
- ✅ **Knee Disarticulation (KD)** = bulbous socket leveraging femoral condyles.
- ✅ **Hip Disarticulation** = Canadian or saucer type, ischial seat + pelvic band.
- ✅ **Knee center offset** = **9–12 mm anterior to trochanteric line** (transfemoral).
- ✅ **Patellar tendon bar** = 3 cm wide, 2.5–4 cm long, concave shape.
- ✅ **Distal end load in PTB** = only 5–15% of total (rest taken by proximal structures).
- ✅ **Residual limb ratio (RLR)** = stump length / intact limb length → useful for prosthetic candidacy.
- ✅ **Materials** = polypropylene, polyethylene, carbon fiber, silicone, TPU.
- ✅ **Suction suspension** = passive (seal) or active (vacuum pump, 40–60 kPa).
- ✅ **For all derivations:** state the formula, substitute values, solve, and add a **clinical interpretation sentence**.

---

**End of Module 2 — Specialized Bases Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme examination pattern and Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels 2–5: Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Smart Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart Wheelchair (SW)** is defined as a **motorized, mechatronically-integrated mobility platform** that augments a conventional powered wheelchair with a suite of **embedded sensors, intelligent controllers, and human-machine interfaces** to assist users with severe physical, cognitive, or sensory-motor disabilities. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for Assistive Medical Devices, a smart wheelchair transcends passive locomotion by incorporating **semi-autonomous or fully autonomous navigation capabilities**, real-time environmental perception, and adaptive control strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A smart wheelchair is an assistive robotic mobility system that combines a traditional powered wheelchair base with embedded intelligence — typically comprising **microcontroller units (MCUs), sonar/IR/LiDAR sensors, IMUs, vision modules, and AI-driven control algorithms** — enabling obstacle avoidance, autonomous path planning, and shared-control human-machine interaction.

**Core Functional Pillars of a Smart Wheelchair:**

| Pillar | Function | Example Hardware |
|--------|----------|------------------|
| **Perception** | Environmental sensing | Ultrasonic HC-SR04, IR arrays, LiDAR, RGB-D cameras |
| **Cognition** | Decision-making & path planning | ARM Cortex-M4, Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson |
| **Actuation** | Motor control & motion | BLDC motors, DC gear motors, motor drivers (L298N/BTS7960) |
| **Interaction** | User-interface | Joystick, sip-and-puff, EEG/EOG, voice recognition |
| **Communication** | IoT & telemetry | Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, CAN bus |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Smart Wheelchair = Self-Driving Car + Traditional Wheelchair"**

Imagine a **conventional powered wheelchair** as a *basic Maruti 800* — it drives only when the user turns the steering wheel. Now overlay onto it the **autonomy of a Tesla Model S** that senses traffic, avoids pedestrians, parks itself, and follows voice commands — but scaled down for indoor use, hospital corridors, and personalized mobility.

**Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a smart wheelchair as a *"perceptive co-pilot"*. The user provides the **high-level intent** ("take me to the kitchen"), and the wheelchair's onboard intelligence handles the **low-level details** — turning left to avoid a chair, slowing down on a ramp, alerting about a wet floor, or gently braking before a doorway.

**Real-World Use Cases:**
- Patients with **ALS, muscular dystrophy, spinal cord injury (SCI), cerebral palsy**.
- **Elderly geriatric** users with limited reflexes.
- **Cognitive assistance** for users with visual impairment or memory issues.
- Hospital logistics and **rehabilitation robotics**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded for KTU Emphasis):**
> - **Operating Voltage:** **24 V DC** (standard) or **48 V DC** (high-power models).
> - **Sensor Range:** **Ultrasonic: 2 cm – 400 cm**, **IR: 10 cm – 80 cm**, **LiDAR: 0.1 m – 30 m**.
> - **Wheelchair Mass:** **30 kg – 120 kg** (with payload).
> - **Maximum Speed:** **6 km/h – 10 km/h** (indoor safe).
> - **Wheelbase (L):** Typically **0.5 m – 0.7 m**.
> - **Battery Endurance:** **15 km – 30 km per charge** (Li-Ion / Sealed Lead-Acid).
> - **Sonar Speed of Sound Constant:** **c ≈ 343 m/s** (at **20 °C**).

---

## 1.4 Evolution & Classification

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies smart wheelchairs into **four generations**:

1. **Generation 1 (Sensor-Augmented):** Add-on sensors with simple reactive obstacle-avoidance.
2. **Generation 2 (Map-Based):** Pre-loaded environmental maps for waypoint navigation.
3. **Generation 3 (SLAM-Enabled):** Real-time *Simultaneous Localization and Mapping* with dynamic obstacle handling.
4. **Generation 4 (AI-Cognitive):** Deep-learning driven intent prediction, emotion-aware interaction, and **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface)** control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Smart Wheelchair — Modular Layered Model

A smart wheelchair follows a **classic sense-plan-act triad** inherited from mobile robotics, with an additional **human-in-the-loop shared-control layer**.

### Layer 1: Sensor Perception Layer
This layer **digitizes the world** around the wheelchair into numerical vectors. Typical modalities:

- **Proximity Sensors:** HC-SR04 ultrasonic transducers (transmit a 40 kHz burst, listen for echo).
- **Infrared Sharp GP2Y0A21YK0F:** Analog distance output, 10–80 cm range.
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** MPU6050 — 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis accelerometer.
- **Vision:** Raspberry Pi Camera V2, Intel RealSense D435 (RGB-D).
- **LiDAR:** RPLIDAR A1 — 360° 2D scan, 12 m radius.

### Layer 2: Embedded Computation Layer
Runs the **localization, mapping, and decision algorithms**. Common platforms:
- **Arduino Mega / Uno** (entry-level, 16 MHz ATmega2560).
- **STM32 Nucleo-F401RE** (ARM Cortex-M4, 84 MHz).
- **Raspberry Pi 4B** (Linux, OpenCV, ROS).
- **NVIDIA Jetson Nano** (GPU-accelerated deep learning inference).

### Layer 3: Motion Control Layer
- **Motor Drivers:** L298N (dual H-bridge, 2 A per channel), BTS7960 (43 A high-current).
- **PID Control Loops** for velocity regulation and heading correction.
- **Differential Drive Kinematics** for omnidirectional maneuvering.

### Layer 4: Shared-Control Arbitration Layer
Fuses **user intent** with **autonomous decisions** using weighted blending. The arbitration weight $\alpha \in [0,1]$ determines autonomy level:
- $\alpha = 0$ → Full user control.
- $\alpha = 1$ → Full autonomous control.
- $\alpha = 0.5$ → Shared control (most common clinically).

### Layer 5: Communication & Telemetry Layer
- **Bluetooth HC-05** for smartphone-app control.
- **Wi-Fi ESP8266/ESP32** for IoT dashboards.
- **CAN bus** for multi-node communication in advanced chairs.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1: Sensor Data Acquisition**
Each ultrasonic sensor fires a trigger pulse ($10\,\mu s$ HIGH), emits a 40 kHz burst, and measures the echo return time $t$.

**Step 2: Distance Computation**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$
where $c = 343$ m/s and division by 2 accounts for round-trip travel.

**Step 3: Sensor Fusion (Kalman Filter)**
Multiple noisy sensor readings are fused to obtain an optimal state estimate $\hat{x}_k$:

$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$$

where $K_k$ is the **Kalman gain**, $z_k$ is the measurement, and $H$ is the observation model.

**Step 4: Path Planning (A* or D* Lite)**
The planner searches a grid-based occupancy map for an optimal collision-free path from start $S$ to goal $G$ using the cost function:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where $g(n)$ is the cost-so-far and $h(n)$ is the heuristic (e.g., Euclidean distance).

**Step 5: Obstacle Avoidance (Dynamic Window Approach or VFH)**
Real-time reactive steering using **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** selects the steering direction with the lowest obstacle density in a polar histogram.

**Step 6: Motor Command Generation**
Wheel velocities $(v_L, v_R)$ are computed from the desired $(v, \omega)$ pair via inverse differential kinematics.

**Step 7: Closed-Loop PID Correction**
Wheel encoders feed back actual velocity, and a PID controller compensates for slip, slope, and load variation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MUST MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Ultrasonic distance measurement | meters |
| 2 | $v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2}$ | Linear velocity (differential drive) | m/s |
| 3 | $\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L}$ | Angular velocity (differential drive) | rad/s |
| 4 | $R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$ | Instantaneous turn radius (ICC) | meters |
| 5 | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Forward kinematics — x | meters |
| 6 | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Forward kinematics — y | meters |
| 7 | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t$ | Forward kinematics — heading | radians |
| 8 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A* search evaluation function | cost units |
| 9 | $F_{att} = -k_a (q - q_{goal})$ | Artificial potential field — attractive | Newtons |
| 10 | $F_{rep} = k_r \left(\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d^*}\right) \frac{1}{d^2}$ | Artificial potential field — repulsive | Newtons |
| 11 | $P_{motor} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power output of motor | Watts |
| 12 | $E_{batt} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Battery energy capacity | Watt-hours |
| 13 | $K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | Shared-control arbitration weight | dimensionless |
| 15 | $T_{sample} = \frac{1}{f_{sensor}}$ | Sensor sampling period | seconds |

> **Constraint Note:** All table entries use $\vert$ notation-free symbols to preserve KTU PDF rendering. The wheelbase $L$ is fixed at the wheelchair's geometric center between drive wheels.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Logistics:** Autonomous transport of medical supplies, reducing staff workload.
- **Rehabilitation Robotics:** Quantitative gait-and-push monitoring via embedded load cells.
- **Disability Inclusion:** UN SDG 3 (Good Health) & SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities) compliance.
- **AI Research Testbed:** Smart wheelchairs are excellent platforms for **ROS-based SLAM, multi-agent coordination, and human-robot interaction (HRI)** research.
- **Smart City Integration:** IoT-connected wheelchairs can report their GPS location to emergency services, enhancing **elderly care** and **patient monitoring**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Drive Forward Kinematics

A standard smart wheelchair uses **two independently driven rear wheels** and **caster wheels** for stability. We derive the position update equations that govern its motion.

### Assumptions:
- Wheel radius: $r$ (meters).
- Wheelbase (distance between drive wheels): $L$ (meters).
- Right wheel angular velocity: $\omega_r$ (rad/s).
- Left wheel angular velocity: $\omega_l$ (rad/s).
- Sampling period: $\Delta t$ (seconds).
- Initial pose: $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$ in world frame.

### Step 1: Express wheel linear velocities

The linear velocity of each wheel is the product of its angular velocity and radius.

$$v_r = r \cdot \omega_r$$

$$v_l = r \cdot \omega_l$$

*Conversion Logic:* A wheel rotating at $\omega_r$ rad/s with radius $r$ traces a circumference of $2\pi r$ per revolution, hence linear speed = angular speed × radius.

### Step 2: Compute the linear velocity of the wheelchair's center

The center moves at the average of the two wheel speeds because the wheelchair cannot translate differentially at the wheelbase.

$$v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}$$

### Step 3: Compute the angular velocity about the ICC

Rotation about the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) occurs when the two wheels have unequal speeds.

$$\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$$

*Conversion Logic:* The differential wheel speed divided by the wheelbase gives the rate of heading change (curvature).

### Step 4: Compute the Instantaneous Turn Radius (ICC distance)

By geometry, the ICC lies on the line connecting the two wheels, perpendicular to the heading:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$$

### Step 5: Update the pose over one sampling interval $\Delta t$

Using the bicycle-model approximation (small-angle Euler integration):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The position update projects the linear velocity $v$ along the current heading angle $\theta_k$ for time $\Delta t$. Heading update is simply angular velocity × time.

### Step 6: Special case — Pure rotation

If $v_r = -v_l$ (wheels spin in opposite directions), then $v = 0$ and $\omega = 2v_r/L$. The wheelchair rotates in place — useful for **tight indoor maneuvering**.

### Step 7: Special case — Straight line

If $v_r = v_l$, then $\omega = 0$ and $v = v_r$. The wheelchair moves in a **straight line**, with $\theta$ remaining constant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement

### Given:
- Speed of sound: $c = 343$ m/s (at 20 °C dry air).
- Echo round-trip time: $t$ (seconds).

### Derivation:

The ultrasonic pulse travels from the transmitter to the obstacle and back. The total distance covered is $c \cdot t$. Since this is a **round-trip**, the one-way distance to the obstacle is:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):

> A smart wheelchair's front-left ultrasonic sensor returns an echo after $t = 5.83$ ms. Compute the distance to the obstacle.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{c \cdot t}{2} \\
&= \frac{343 \times 5.83 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= \frac{343 \times 0.00583}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.99969}{2} \\
&= 0.9998 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 1.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The obstacle is **1.0 meter** away. The wheelchair should reduce speed since it is below the safe threshold of 1.5 m.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Modalities vs. Application

| Sensor Type | Range | Accuracy | Cost | Use Case in Smart Wheelchair |
|-------------|-------|----------|------|------------------------------|
| **Ultrasonic HC-SR04** | 2 cm – 400 cm | ±3 cm | ₹60–100 | Front/rear bump detection |
| **IR Sharp GP2Y0A21** | 10 cm – 80 cm | ±1 cm | ₹250 | Side proximity, edge detection |
| **LiDAR RPLIDAR A1** | 0.15 m – 12 m | ±2 cm | ₹8,000 | 360° SLAM, room mapping |
| **IMU MPU6050** | N/A | ±0.05°/s | ₹120 | Heading, tilt, fall detection |
| **RGB-D Camera** | 0.2 m – 4 m | ±1% | ₹15,000+ | Object recognition, gesture control |
| **Wheel Encoder** | N/A | 360–2000 CPR | ₹80 | Odometry, dead reckoning |
| **Force Sensor (FSR)** | 0 – 100 N | ±5% | ₹200 | Seat pressure, posture monitoring |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem: Energy Budget for a Smart Wheelchair

**Problem:** A smart wheelchair runs on a **24 V, 30 Ah Li-Ion battery**. The motors draw an average current of **15 A** at typical indoor speed. The embedded electronics (sensors + controller) draw an additional **2 A**. Compute the total operational endurance in hours.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= I_{motor} + I_{electronics} \\
&= 15 + 2 = 17 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{endurance} &= \frac{\text{Battery Capacity (Ah)}}{I_{total} \text{ (A)}} \\
&= \frac{30 \text{ Ah}}{17 \text{ A}} \\
&= 1.765 \text{ hours} \\
&\approx 1 \text{ hour } 46 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Energy delivered:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= V \cdot I \cdot t \\
&= 24 \times 17 \times 1.765 \\
&= 720 \text{ Wh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** To extend endurance, designers use **regenerative braking**, **low-power MCUs** (e.g., STM32L4), and **sleep modes** for sensors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Smart Wheelchair Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER_LAYER["USER INTENT LAYER"]
        u1["Joystick Input"]
        u2["Voice Command"]
        u3["EEG / BCI Signal"]
        u4["Sip-and-Puff Sensor"]
    end

    subgraph PERCEPTION["SENSOR PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        s1["Ultrasonic Array"]
        s2["IR Proximity"]
        s3["LiDAR Scanner"]
        s4["IMU 6-Axis"]
        s5["Wheel Encoders"]
        s6["RGB-D Camera"]
    end

    subgraph COGNITION["EMBEDDED COMPUTATION LAYER"]
        c1["Data Fusion Kalman Filter"]
        c2["SLAM Module"]
        c3["Path Planner A Star"]
        c4["Obstacle Avoidance VFH"]
        c5["Shared Control Arbiter"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION["MOTION CONTROL LAYER"]
        a1["PID Velocity Controller"]
        a2["Motor Driver L298N"]
        a3["Left BLDC Motor"]
        a4["Right BLDC Motor"]
    end

    subgraph COMM["COMMUNICATION LAYER"]
        com1["Bluetooth App"]
        com2["WiFi IoT Cloud"]
        com3["Emergency Beacon"]
    end

    u1 --> c5
    u2 --> c5
    u3 --> c5
    u4 --> c5

    s1 --> c1
    s2 --> c1
    s3 --> c2
    s4 --> c1
    s5 --> a1
    s6 --> c4

    c1 --> c2
    c1 --> c5
    c2 --> c3
    c3 --> c4
    c4 --> c5
    c5 --> a1

    a1 --> a2
    a2 --> a3
    a2 --> a4

    c5 --> com1
    c5 --> com2
    c5 --> com3
```

---

## 4.2 Obstacle Avoidance Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start(["Sensor Triggered"]) --> meas["Compute Distance d"]
    meas --> comp{"d less than D_safe?"}
    comp -- "NO" --> move["Continue Forward Motion"]
    comp -- "YES" --> eval["Evaluate Left and Right Clearance"]
    eval --> turn{"Clearest Direction?"}
    turn -- "LEFT" --> left["Steer Left at omega_left"]
    turn -- "RIGHT" --> right["Steer Right at omega_right"]
    turn -- "NONE" --> stop["Emergency Stop and Reverse"]
    left --> loop["Re-acquire and Re-plan"]
    right --> loop
    stop --> loop
    move --> loop
    loop --> start
```

---

## 4.3 PID Closed-Loop Motor Control Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref["Reference Velocity v_ref"] --> sumNode["Summing Junction"]
    sumNode --> ctrl["PID Controller Kp Ki Kd"]
    ctrl --> driver["Motor Driver"]
    driver --> motor["BLDC Motor"]
    motor --> wheel["Wheel Output v_actual"]
    wheel --> fb["Encoder Feedback"]
    fb --> sumNode
    wheel --> plant["Wheelchair Dynamics"]
```

---

## 4.4 Shared-Control Arbitration Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userIn["User Command u_user"] --> mulU["Multiply by alpha_minus_1"]
    autoIn["Autonomous Command u_auto"] --> mulA["Multiply by alpha"]
    mulU --> blend["Weighted Blend Sum"]
    mulA --> blend
    blend --> final["Final Command u_final"]
    final --> motor["Motor Controller"]

    context["Context Awareness Module"] --> alphaGen["Generate Alpha"]
    alphaGen --> mulU
    alphaGen --> mulA
```

**Explanation of Arbitration Math:**

$$
u_{final} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot u_{user} + \alpha \cdot u_{auto}
$$

When the user is in a clear corridor, $\alpha \to 0$ (user in control). Near a stairwell or detected hazard, $\alpha \to 1$ (autonomous override).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define a Smart Wheelchair. List any four sensors commonly used in it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Smart Wheelchair** is a mechatronically enhanced powered wheelchair equipped with embedded sensors, intelligent controllers, and human-machine interfaces to assist users with severe disabilities through semi-autonomous or autonomous navigation.

**Four Common Sensors:**

1. **Ultrasonic Sensor (HC-SR04)** — for short-range obstacle detection (2–400 cm).
2. **IR Proximity Sensor (Sharp GP2Y0A21YK0F)** — for side and edge sensing.
3. **IMU (MPU6050)** — for heading, tilt, and fall detection.
4. **LiDAR (RPLIDAR A1)** — for 360° 2D mapping and SLAM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU examiners award full 3 marks only if the **definition is precise** and **at least 4 sensors are listed with their function**. A vague answer scores 1.5 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between a Powered Wheelchair and a Smart Wheelchair.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Powered Wheelchair | Smart Wheelchair |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Control** | Manual joystick only | Joystick + autonomous AI |
| **Sensors** | None (or basic bumpers) | Ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, IMU, camera |
| **Navigation** | User-driven | Semi/fully autonomous with SLAM |
| **Obstacle Avoidance** | Reactive (bump sensors) | Predictive (VFH, A*, potential fields) |
| **Cost** | ₹30,000 – ₹1,50,000 | ₹1,50,000 – ₹10,00,000+ |
| **User Population** | Mild to moderate disability | Severe motor / cognitive impairment |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a smart wheelchair** and the function of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward kinematic equations of a differential drive smart wheelchair** and discuss the special cases of straight-line motion and in-place rotation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The architecture of a smart wheelchair follows a **five-layer modular design**:

**Layer 1 — Sensor Perception Layer:**
Comprises ultrasonic sensors, IR arrays, IMU, wheel encoders, and vision modules that digitize the environment into numerical vectors (distance, heading, image).

**Layer 2 — Embedded Computation Layer:**
The "brain" — typically an ARM Cortex microcontroller or Raspberry Pi — runs SLAM, sensor fusion (Kalman filter), and path-planning algorithms.

**Layer 3 — Motion Control Layer:**
Drives the BLDC motors via H-bridge motor drivers. Implements PID loops for velocity regulation and differential-drive inverse kinematics.

**Layer 4 — Shared Control Arbitration Layer:**
Blends user input with autonomous commands using the weight $\alpha$. Decides whether the user or the AI is in control at any moment.

**Layer 5 — Communication Layer:**
Provides Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, and IoT cloud connectivity for remote monitoring, caregiver alerts, and emergency beacons.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete Mermaid architecture.

**Conclusion:** The layered architecture ensures **modularity, fault isolation, and upgradeability** — a KTU-favored design philosophy.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** Wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $L$, angular velocities $\omega_r$ and $\omega_l$, sampling time $\Delta t$.

**Step 1:** Linear velocities of wheels:

$$v_r = r \omega_r \qquad v_l = r \omega_l$$

**Step 2:** Center linear velocity:

$$v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Angular velocity about ICC:

$$\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L}$$

**Step 4:** Turn radius:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$$

**Step 5:** Pose update (Euler integration):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v \cos\theta_k \, \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v \sin\theta_k \, \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \, \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special Case 1 — Straight Line Motion:**
If $v_r = v_l$, then $\omega = 0$ and $v = v_r$. The wheelchair translates without rotation, $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k$.

**Special Case 2 — In-Place Rotation:**
If $v_r = -v_l$ (wheels spin in opposite directions), then $v = 0$ and $\omega = \frac{2v_r}{L}$. The wheelchair rotates about its own center with $R = 0$.

**Conclusion:** The differential drive kinematics is the **mathematical foundation** of any smart wheelchair motion planner, and KTU expects students to derive all four primary equations (steps 2–5).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Artificial Potential Field (APF) method** of obstacle avoidance in smart wheelchairs. Derive the attractive and repulsive force equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A smart wheelchair uses **three ultrasonic sensors** (front, left-front, right-front) and operates at **24 V DC**. The echoes return at $t_1 = 2.92$ ms (front), $t_2 = 1.46$ ms (left), and $t_3 = 4.38$ ms (right). Compute the obstacle distances and determine the **steering decision**. Speed of sound = 343 m/s. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method treats the wheelchair as a point moving in a force field where the **goal** exerts an **attractive force** and **obstacles** exert **repulsive forces**.

**Attractive Force:**

The attractive potential is typically a quadratic function of distance to the goal:

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_a \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^2$$

where $k_a$ is the attractive gain. The force is the negative gradient:

$$F_{att} = -k_a (q - q_{goal})$$

This force **pulls the wheelchair toward the goal** with magnitude proportional to distance.

**Repulsive Force:**

The repulsive potential grows as the wheelchair approaches an obstacle:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \frac{1}{d(q)} - \frac{1}{d^*} \right)^2 & \text{if } d(q) \leq d^* \\ 0 & \text{if } d(q) > d^* \end{cases}$$

where $d(q)$ is the distance to the nearest obstacle, $d^*$ is the influence threshold, and $k_r$ is the repulsive gain. The repulsive force is:

$$F_{rep} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d^*} \right) \frac{1}{d^2}$$

**Total Force:**

$$F_{total} = F_{att} + \sum_{i} F_{rep,i}$$

The wheelchair steers in the direction of $F_{total}$.

**Limitation:** APF can suffer from **local minima** (the wheelchair gets stuck behind a U-shaped obstacle). Solutions include **random walks, virtual goal relocation, or hybrid A* + APF** planners.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

Using the ultrasonic distance formula $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$:

**Front Obstacle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \frac{343 \times 2.92 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= \frac{343 \times 0.00292}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.00156}{2} \\
&= 0.5008 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 0.50 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Left-Front Obstacle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_2 &= \frac{343 \times 1.46 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= \frac{343 \times 0.00146}{2} \\
&= \frac{0.50078}{2} \\
&= 0.2504 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 0.25 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Right-Front Obstacle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_3 &= \frac{343 \times 4.38 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= \frac{343 \times 0.00438}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.50234}{2} \\
&= 0.7512 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 0.75 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steering Decision:**

| Sensor | Distance | Status |
|--------|----------|--------|
| Front | 0.50 m | **BLOCKED** (below 0.8 m safe threshold) |
| Left | 0.25 m | **HIGHLY BLOCKED** (below 0.5 m) |
| Right | 0.75 m | **CLEAR** (above 0.5 m) |

**Decision:** The wheelchair should **steer RIGHT** to avoid the front obstacle, since the right side has the maximum clearance (0.75 m). If right is also blocked, an **emergency stop and reverse maneuver** is initiated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by 2** in the ultrasonic formula — this is the **#1 mistake** in KTU exams. Always remember: the echo is a **round-trip**, so one-way distance = (c × t) / 2.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Time must be in **seconds** (not ms). Convert ms to seconds by multiplying by 10⁻³ before plugging in.
> 3. **Skipping the comparison step:** KTU wants the **decision logic**, not just the distance. Always state the **steering direction** explicitly.
> 4. **Not showing the intermediate arithmetic:** Even small numerical problems require **3–4 explicit lines** of working. KTU valuation strictly follows a step-by-step rubric.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — SMART WHEELCHAIRS**

✅ **Definition:** Mechatronically augmented powered wheelchair with sensors + AI for autonomous/shared navigation.

✅ **Five-Layer Architecture:** Perception → Cognition → Actuation → Shared Control → Communication.

✅ **Differential Drive Kinematics (MUST MEMORIZE):**
- $v = (v_r + v_l) / 2$
- $\omega = (v_r - v_l) / L$
- $R = (L/2) \cdot (v_r + v_l) / (v_r - v_l)$
- Pose update via Euler integration.

✅ **Ultrasonic Distance Formula:** $d = c \cdot t / 2$, with $c = 343$ m/s at 20 °C.

✅ **Path Planning Algorithms:** A* (offline), D* Lite (online), RRT (continuous space).

✅ **Obstacle Avoidance:** APF, VFH, Dynamic Window Approach (DWA).

✅ **Sensor Fusion:** Kalman Filter for combining IMU + encoder + LiDAR.

✅ **Shared Control Equation:** $u_{final} = (1 - \alpha) u_{user} + \alpha \cdot u_{auto}$, with $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.

✅ **Standard Metrics:** 24 V DC, 6–10 km/h, 0.5–0.7 m wheelbase, 15–30 km range.

✅ **Generations:** Sensor-Augmented → Map-Based → SLAM-Enabled → AI-Cognitive (BCI).

✅ **KTU Hot Topics:** Differential drive derivation, APF derivation, ultrasonic distance problem, shared-control arbitration, numerical battery endurance, sensor comparison matrix.

✅ **Real-World Tie-In:** Hospital logistics, rehabilitation robotics, UN SDG-3 & SDG-10, smart city IoT.

---

**END OF NOTES — SMART WHEELCHAIRS (MODULE 2)**

*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PEEBT744 — Assistive Medical Devices*
*Examiner-aligned | Outcome-Based Education | NEP 2020 Compliant*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Considered for Selecting a Wheelchair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Factors Considered for Selecting a Wheelchair # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Wheelchair Selection** is a systematic, multidisciplinary clinical-engineering decision process in which the physical, functional, postural, and environmental requirements of an end-user are matched against the technical specifications of an available wheelchair to prescribe the most appropriate assistive mobility device. As defined by the **WHO Guidelines on the Provision of Manual Wheelchairs in Less-Resourced Settings (2008)** and the **ISO 7176 / ISO 16840** standards, a wheelchair is considered "appropriate" only when it meets the user's *body, life, and environment* in a safe, durable, and affordable manner. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > Wheelchair selection is **not** the act of buying a chair. It is a **user-centred prescription process** involving (1) user assessment, (2) wheelchair–user match, (3) fitting, and (4) training & follow-up, in alignment with the **8 Steps of Wheelchair Service Delivery** recommended by WHO. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine buying a pair of shoes. You do not just walk into a shop and grab the first pair — you consider your foot size, the shape of your arch, where you will walk (office vs mountain), how long you will stand, and your budget. A wheelchair works the same way, except the "feet" are the entire body, the "ground" ranges from smooth hospital floors to muddy village paths, and the user may have **paralysis, weakness, deformities, or sensory loss** that makes the wrong choice life-threatening (e.g., pressure sores, falls, or shoulder injuries). > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Selecting a wheelchair is like tailoring a suit for a person with unique measurements *and* a unique lifestyle — the cut, fabric, and fit must all be customized, not off-the-rack. ## 1.3 Core Categories of Selection Factors (At a Glance) The selection of a wheelchair is governed by **six primary factor clusters**, which must be evaluated in parallel: | # | Factor Cluster | Core Question Answered | |---|----------------|------------------------| | 1 | **User Physical Factors** | What can the user's body do and not do? | | 2 | **User Functional/Postural Factors** | How does the user sit, transfer, and propel? | | 3 | **Lifestyle & Environmental Factors** | Where, how often, and over what terrain? | | 4 | **Wheelchair Technical/Design Factors** | Does the chair's geometry match the user? | | 5 | **Seating & Support System Factors** | Will the cushion and backrest protect tissue and posture? | | 6 | **Service, Economic & Cultural Factors** | Can the user obtain, maintain, and accept the device? | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** A common board question is *"List the factors considered for selecting a wheelchair."* The expected answer always covers **user factors, environmental factors, wheelchair factors, and cost/maintenance** — never just one category in isolation. ## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Bold-Listed Constants) The following clinically validated dimensions and tolerances are used universally in wheelchair prescription: - **Seat width tolerance:** **2 – 3 cm (≈ 1 inch)** clearance on each side of the widest point of the hips. - **Seat depth tolerance:** **2 – 3 cm** clearance between the front edge of the seat and the popliteal fossa (back of the knee). - **Backrest height:** Shoulder height to seat surface (for tilt/postural support users); inferior angle of scapula (for active self-propellers). - **Standard cushion pressure threshold:** **≤ 32 mmHg** (≈ 4.27 kPa) capillary closing pressure to prevent ischial pressure ulcers. - **Standard wheelchair slope-climb capability:** **1:12 (≈ 4.8°)** for unassisted manual propulsion on a ramp. - **WHO 8-Step Wheelchair Service Delivery (2008):** Reference, Assessment, Prescription, Funding & Ordering, Product Preparation, Fitting, User Training, Follow-up & Maintenance/Repairs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Selection Factors ### A. User-Related Physical Factors - **Age:** Pediatric wheelchairs require growth-adjustable frames; geriatric users need low-effort propulsion and easy transfers. - **Body weight & height:** Determines frame strength, seat width/depth, and wheel size. - **Diagnosis & Disability Type:** Spinal cord injury (level of lesion), cerebral palsy (spasticity), polio, muscular dystrophy, amputation, arthritis, etc. - **Range of Motion (ROM):** Hip, knee, and ankle ROM dictates seat-to-back angle, leg rest length, and footplate angle. - **Muscle strength & tone:** Hypertonia (spasticity) → tilt-in-space chair; hypotonia → postural support systems. - **Sensation:** Loss of sensation (SCI, diabetic neuropathy) → mandatory pressure-relief cushion. - **Skin integrity:** History of pressure ulcers → off-loading cushion (e.g., Roho, Jay). - **Cognitive & visual status:** Affects ability to operate powered wheelchair or self-propel safely. ### B. Postural & Functional Factors - **Sitting balance:** Good trunk control → standard chair; poor control → tilt/recline + postural supports. - **Ability to transfer:** Independent (folding, lightweight) vs dependent (rigid, drop-arm, commode-style). - **Method of propulsion:** - *Two-arm manual:* Standard rear-wheel-drive self-propeller. - *One-arm drive (hemiplegia):* One-arm-drive (OAD) hub or low-effort lever-drive. - *Foot propulsion:* Used by amputees or those with upper limb deficiency. - *Powered wheelchair:* For users with insufficient upper-limb function. - **Activities of Daily Living (ADL):** Eating, working, school access, cooking, toileting. ### C. Environmental & Lifestyle Factors - **Indoor vs Outdoor use:** Indoor → narrow, maneuverable; Outdoor → larger rear wheels, suspension, knobby tyres. - **Terrain:** Smooth tile vs rough village path vs kerbs. - **Climate & humidity:** Affects cushion material, upholstery, and corrosion of frame. - **Transport & storage:** Folding for car boot; rigid for active users. - **Distance travelled per day:** Long distances → ultra-lightweight rigid chair; short distances → standard folding. ### D. Wheelchair Technical / Design Factors - **Frame type:** Folding (cross-brace) vs Rigid (open or closed). - **Frame material:** Steel (heavy, cheap), Aluminium (light), Titanium/Carbon-fibre (ultra-light, expensive). - **Wheel configuration:** - *Rear-wheel drive (RWD)*: stable, fastest. - *Mid-wheel drive (MWD)*: tightest turning radius (used in powered chairs). - *Front-wheel drive (FWD)*: climbs obstacles. - **Wheel size:** Large rear wheels (60 cm / 24 inch) for self-propulsion; small transit wheels for attendant-propelled. - **Tyre type:** Solid (puncture-proof) vs Pneumatic (shock-absorbing, smoother ride). - **Adjustability:** Growth kits for children; modular adjustability for long-term use. ### E. Seating & Support System Factors - **Cushion type:** Foam, gel, air, honeycomb, or hybrid — based on pressure-mapping and risk level. - **Backrest type:** Sling (standard) vs Solid (postural) vs Custom-moulded (seating insert). - **Lateral supports, headrests, pelvic belts, anti-tippers** as required. ### F. Service, Economic & Cultural Factors - **Cost & funding:** Personal budget, government scheme (e.g., **ADIP scheme of Govt. of India**), insurance, or charity. - **Availability of spares & service centre** in the user's locality. - **Durability & warranty** in the operating environment. - **User's and family's acceptance** — cultural stigma, aesthetics, social acceptance. - **Training of the user and caregiver** for safe operation and basic maintenance. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes the **clinically standardized wheelchair dimension formulas** that examiners frequently test. All measurements are taken with the user in the **standard seated posture (90° hip, 90° knee, feet flat)**. | Parameter | Formula / Rule of Thumb | Clinical Justification | |---|---|---| | **Seat Width** | $SW = Hip_{width} + 2 \times \Delta_{w}$ where $\Delta_{w} \approx 1\text{ cm} \text{ to } 1.5\text{ cm per side}$ | Total clearance ≈ 2–3 cm; prevents hip rubbing & pressure on greater trochanters. | | **Seat Depth** | $SD = Thigh_{length} - \Delta_{d}$ where $\Delta_{d} \approx 2\text{ cm to } 3\text{ cm}$ | Prevents popliteal fossa compression and posterior pelvic tilt. | | **Seat Height (Front)** | $SH = LowerLeg_{length} - \text{Cushion thickness}$ | Allows foot flat on floor for self-propulsion or on footplate at 90° knee. | | **Backrest Height** | For active user → $BH = Inferior\ angle\ of\ scapula$
For postural user → $BH = Shoulder\ height$ | Allows free scapular movement for push-stroke; provides trunk support when needed. | | **Armrest Height** | $AH = Elbow\ height + 2\text{ cm} \text{ to } 3\text{ cm}$ | Supports forearm with shoulder relaxed, no upward shrug. | | **Footrest Length** | $FL = Popliteal\ height - \text{Cushion thickness}$ | Maintains thigh-on-seat contact for pressure distribution. | | **Seat Plane Angle** | $SPA = 0°$ (level) or $+5°$ to $+15°$ (posterior tilt) | Posterior tilt reduces sliding & shear; aids posture in spastic users. | | **Back-to-Seat Angle** | $BSA = 90°$ (standard) or $95°$ to $120°$ (recline for postural support) | Open angle accommodates kyphosis, hip contractures, or low trunk control. | | **Pressure Threshold** | $P_{interface} \leq 32\text{ mmHg}$ | Below capillary closing pressure to prevent ischaemic pressure ulcers. | | **Ramp Slope for Self-Propulsion** | $Slope \leq 1:12\ (\approx 4.8^{\circ})$ | Beyond this, attendant or powered chair is required for safety. | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Seat Dimensions — "WIDTH, DEPTH, HEIGHT" (WDH):** > - **W**idth = Hips + **2 cm per side** > - **D**epth = Thigh − **2 to 3 cm** > - **H**eight = Lower-leg − cushion thickness > - **B**ackrest = Scapula (active) / Shoulder (postural) > - **A**rmrest = Elbow + **2 to 3 cm** ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Drives design of modular, adjustable, growth-friendly wheelchairs (e.g., **Whirlwind RoughRider**, **Motivation Multisize**). - **Manufacturing & R&D:** Specifies tolerances for ISO 7176 testing (static stability, brake efficiency, fatigue, rolling resistance). - **Clinical Practice:** Underpins prescription protocols used by occupational therapists (OTs) and physiotherapists (PTs) in hospitals and district rehabilitation centres (DRCs). - **Public Health:** ADIP (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances) under **Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment, Govt. of India** — selection criteria align with these factors. - **KTU Relevance:** Directly maps to **Course Outcomes CO2** (*"Design and select appropriate assistive devices for persons with disabilities"*) and **CO3** (*"Evaluate the biomechanical and ergonomic aspects of mobility devices"*). # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — The WHO 8-Step Selection Process > [!IMPORTANT] > The **WHO 8-Step Wheelchair Service Delivery** is the gold-standard algorithm tested in KTU exams. Walk through it step-by-step. ## Step 1 — Referral The user is referred to a wheelchair service by a doctor, therapist, or social worker. **Information collected:** diagnosis, prognosis, urgency, and any contraindications (e.g., skin breakdown, severe fixed deformities). ## Step 2 — Assessment A holistic assessment of the user across three domains: $$ Assessment = \left\{ A_{physical},\ A_{functional},\ A_{environmental} \right\} $$ Where each domain is scored against the factors in Section 2.1. Output: a written **"Wheelchair Prescription"** with body measurements and feature list. ## Step 3 — Prescription The clinician matches the assessment to a specific wheelchair type. The decision logic can be expressed as: $$ Chair_{prescribed} = \underset{c \in Catalog}{\arg\max} \Big[ U_{user}(c) \cdot S_{safety}(c) \cdot A_{accessibility}(c) \cdot C_{cost}^{-1}(c) \Big] $$ Where: - $U_{user}(c)$ = user-need match score for chair $c$ - $S_{safety}(c)$ = safety rating of chair $c$ (from ISO 7176 tests) - $A_{accessibility}(c)$ = environmental accessibility score - $C_{cost}(c)$ = total cost of ownership (purchase + 5-year spares) **Practical decision tree used by clinicians:** $$ \text{If } TrunkControl \geq Good \ \land\ UL_{strength} \geq Fair: \quad \rightarrow \text{Manual Self-Propelled Chair} $$ $$ \text{If } TrunkControl = Poor \ \lor\ UL_{strength} = Poor: \quad \rightarrow \text{Powered Wheelchair (RWD or MWD)} $$ $$ \text{If } Skin_{integrity} = HighRisk: \quad \rightarrow \text{Cushion}_{pressure-relief} + \text{Tilt-in-space} $$ $$ \text{If } Transfers = Dependent: \quad \rightarrow \text{Rigid frame + Drop-arm + Attendant brakes} $$ ## Step 4 — Funding and Ordering Identify the funding source (ADIP, NHFDC loan, insurance, personal).
