Confirm availability of the prescribed model in the local supply chain. ## Step 5 — Product Preparation - Inspect the wheelchair against the prescription checklist. - Adjust axle plate, backrest height, armrest height, footrest length. - Fit the prescribed cushion. ## Step 6 — Fitting - User sits in the prepared chair. - Verify the 2–3 cm clearances, pressure mapping (if available). - Check pelvic positioning, knee angle, foot placement. ## Step 7 — User Training - Teach safe transfers, propulsion, braking, pressure relief (push-ups / tilt), kerb climbing, folding, and basic maintenance. - Caregiver training for attendant-propelled chairs. ## Step 8 — Follow-up, Maintenance and Repairs - First follow-up within 1 month, then every 6 months. - Address growth, wear, postural changes, or pressure-sore recurrence. > [!NOTE] > **Why this algorithm matters in KTU exams:** A 14-mark question may ask you to *"Explain the steps involved in wheelchair selection"* — answering with all 8 WHO steps in order, with at least one engineering or clinical justification per step, fetches full marks. ## 3.1 Decision Matrix (Snapshot) | User Profile | Propulsion | Postural Need | Pressure Risk | Recommended Chair | |---|---|---|---|---| | Active paraplegic, young adult | Manual | Good trunk | Moderate | Ultra-light rigid, foam/air cushion | | Tetraplegic (C5–C6) | Powered | Poor trunk | Very High | Powered tilt-in-space, ROHO cushion | | Hemiplegic stroke | One-arm drive or Powered | Fair | Moderate | Folding OAD chair or mid-wheel power chair | | Cerebral palsy child | Attendant | Poor | Moderate | Postural tilt-in-space, growth-adjustable | | Elderly arthritis | Attendant | Good | Low | Folding standard, padded cushion | | Lower-limb amputee | Manual with rear axle set back | Good | Low | Rigid, amputee axle position, cushion | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Wheelchair Selection Decision Flow (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["User Referral"] --> B["Clinical and Functional Assessment"] B --> C{"Upper Limb Strength Sufficient?"} C -- "Yes" --> D{"Trunk Control Good?"} C -- "No" --> E["Powered Wheelchair"] D -- "Yes" --> F["Manual Self Propelled Chair"] D -- "No" --> G["Manual with Postural Support or Tilt"] F --> H{"Pressure Risk High?"} G --> H E --> H H -- "Yes" --> I["Pressure Relief Cushion and Tilt in Space"] H -- "No" --> J["Standard Foam or Gel Cushion"] I --> K["Fitting and Training"] J --> K K --> L["Follow up and Maintenance"] ``` ## 4.2 Wheelchair Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USER["User Domain"] U1["Physical Assessment"] U2["Functional Assessment"] U3["Environmental Assessment"] end subgraph CLINICIAN["Clinician Domain"] C1["Prescription Logic"] C2["Dimension Calculations SW SD SH BH AH"] end subgraph CHAIR["Wheelchair Domain"] W1["Frame and Wheels"] W2["Seating and Cushion System"] W3["Postural Supports and Accessories"] end subgraph SERVICE["Service Domain"] S1["Funding and Procurement"] S2["Fitting and User Training"] S3["Follow up and Repairs"] end U1 --> C1 U2 --> C1 U3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> W1 C2 --> W2 C2 --> W3 W1 --> S2 W2 --> S2 W3 --> S2 S1 --> W1 S2 --> S3 S3 --> U1 ``` ## 4.3 Factor Cluster Map (Mermaid Mind-Map) ```mermaid mindmap root((Wheelchair Selection Factors)) User Age and Diagnosis Strength and ROM Sensation and Skin Postural Control Functional Propulsion Method Transfer Ability ADL Needs Environment Indoor Outdoor Terrain and Climate Transport and Storage Wheelchair Frame Type and Material Wheel Configuration Dimensions and Adjustability Seating Cushion Type Backrest and Supports Service Cost and Funding Spares and Maintenance Cultural Acceptance ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** > - The first flowchart implements the **WHO 8-step decision logic** in compact form. > - The block diagram shows the **closed-loop relationship** between user, clinician, chair, and service — emphasizing that selection is not a one-time event but a cyclic process. > - The mind-map is the **"exam-day quick recall"** tool — reproduce this structure in 2-mark and 3-mark short-answer questions. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > **Q1. List the major factors that must be considered while selecting a wheelchair for a person with disability. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The major factors considered for selecting a wheelchair are: 1. **User-related physical factors** — age, weight, height, diagnosis, muscle strength, range of motion, sensation, and skin integrity. 2. **Postural and functional factors** — sitting balance, transfer ability, method of propulsion, and activities of daily living. 3. **Environmental factors** — indoor/outdoor use, terrain, climate, transport, and storage requirements. 4. **Wheelchair design factors** — frame type (folding vs rigid), material, wheel configuration, dimensions (seat width, depth, height, backrest, armrest), and adjustability. 5. **Seating and support factors** — cushion type for pressure relief, backrest, and postural supports. 6. **Service and economic factors** — cost, funding (ADIP/NHFDC), availability of spares, durability, and user/caregiver acceptance. --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2]** > **Q2. Explain the importance of seat width, seat depth, and backrest height in wheelchair selection. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** - **Seat width** must be the **hip width plus 2–3 cm clearance** (≈ 1 cm per side). Too narrow causes hip pressure and skin injury; too wide causes postural instability and difficulty propelling. - **Seat depth** must be the **thigh length minus 2–3 cm** clearance behind the knee. Too long causes popliteal compression and posterior pelvic tilt; too short shifts body weight to the ischial tuberosities, increasing pressure-sore risk. - **Backrest height** should reach the **inferior angle of the scapula** for active self-propellers (to allow free arm push), or **shoulder height** for users with poor trunk control (to provide full postural support). Correct backrest height is critical for trunk stability, scapular mobility, and prevention of kyphotic collapse. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q3A]** > **Q3. (A) With a neat flowchart, explain the WHO 8-step wheelchair service delivery process. List the key factors assessed in the *Assessment* step. (14 Marks: 7 + 7)** ### Part (a) — 7 Marks: WHO 8-Step Flowchart **Model Answer:** The WHO 8-step process for wheelchair service delivery is: 1. **Referral** — The user is referred to the wheelchair service by a doctor, therapist, social worker, or self/family. 2. **Assessment** — A holistic evaluation of the user's physical, functional, postural, and environmental needs, plus body measurements. 3. **Prescription** — Matching the assessment findings to a specific wheelchair type, dimensions, cushion, and postural supports. 4. **Funding and Ordering** — Identifying the funding source (ADIP, insurance, personal) and placing the order with the supplier. 5. **Product Preparation** — Inspecting the chair, assembling it, and making initial adjustments (axle position, footrest length, armrest height) and fitting the cushion. 6. **Fitting** — The user sits in the prepared chair; clearances (2–3 cm at hips, knees), pelvic position, foot placement, and pressure distribution are verified. 7. **User Training** — Teaching transfers, propulsion, braking, pressure relief (push-ups, tilt, recline), kerb technique, folding, and basic care. 8. **Follow-up, Maintenance, and Repairs** — Reviewing fit and function at 1 month, 6 months, and yearly; addressing wear, growth, postural change, or new skin issues. > A neat flowchart showing the sequential flow from Referral → Assessment → Prescription → Funding → Preparation → Fitting → Training → Follow-up, with feedback loops from Follow-up back to Assessment (3 loops), fetches full marks. ### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Key Factors in the Assessment Step **Model Answer:** The *Assessment* step evaluates the user across three domains: **(i) Physical / Medical Factors (2.5 marks)** - Age, height, weight. - Diagnosis and prognosis (SCI level, CP type, amputation, etc.). - Range of motion of hip, knee, ankle; muscle strength and tone (spasticity, flaccidity). - Sensation and skin integrity (history of pressure ulcers). - Cognitive status, vision, hearing, and behavioural factors. **(ii) Postural & Functional Factors (2.5 marks)** - Sitting balance (trunk and head control). - Method of propulsion (two-arm manual, one-arm drive, foot-propulsion, attendant-propelled, powered). - Transfer ability (independent, assisted, dependent). - Activities of daily living — eating, working, school, toilet, leisure. - Posture in sitting (pelvic tilt, scoliosis, kyphosis, windswept deformity). **(iii) Environmental / Lifestyle Factors (2 marks)** - Indoor / outdoor / mixed use. - Terrain — smooth floor, rough path, kerbs, ramps. - Climate, humidity, transport, storage, and availability of attendant. - Cultural acceptance and family support. The output of the assessment is a **written wheelchair prescription** specifying the chair type, dimensions, cushion, and postural supports. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q3B]** > **Q3. (B) Describe the various types of wheelchairs based on propulsion method. Discuss in detail the clinical, environmental, and economic factors that govern wheelchair selection, with suitable examples. (14 Marks: 7 + 7)** ### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Types of Wheelchairs by Propulsion **Model Answer:** 1. **Manual Self-Propelled Wheelchair** — Large rear wheels (60 cm / 24 inch) with hand-rims; user pushes both rims. Suitable for users with good upper-limb strength and trunk control (e.g., paraplegics with T6–T12 lesions, post-polio). 2. **Attendant-Propelled Wheelchair** — Small rear wheels (30 cm / 12 inch), push-handles, attendant brakes. For users who cannot self-propel (elderly, severely disabled, young children). 3. **One-Arm-Drive (OAD) Wheelchair** — Two hand-rims on one side linked by a lever system. For hemiplegic users (e.g., post-stroke, brachial plexus injury). 4. **Foot-Propelled (Amputee) Wheelchair** — Lower seat height, rear axle set further back for stability, foot-propulsion loop. For bilateral lower-limb amputees. 5. **Powered Wheelchair** — Battery-driven motors; controlled by joystick, sip-and-puff, or head-array. For users with poor upper-limb function (tetraplegics C4–C6, advanced MS, MND, severe arthritis). Available in rear-wheel, mid-wheel, and front-wheel drive configurations. 6. **Sports / Active Wheelchair** — Ultra-lightweight rigid frame, cambered rear wheels. For basketball, racing, rugby. 7. **Postural / Tilt-in-Space Wheelchair** — Seat frame tilts 0–45° relative to the ground. For users with poor trunk control, spasticity, or pressure-sore risk. 8. **Pediatric / Growth-Adjustable Wheelchair** — Modular frame with multiple width/depth/height settings. For children with CP, muscular dystrophy, etc. ### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Clinical, Environmental & Economic Factors with Examples **Model Answer:** **Clinical Factors (3 marks)** - **Diagnosis:** A C5–C6 tetraplegic lacks hand-grip → cannot use a manual hand-rim → needs a **powered wheelchair with hand-control joystick**. A T10 paraplegic has good arms → a **lightweight manual self-propelled** is ideal. - **Postural control:** Poor trunk control (advanced CP, high-level SCI) → **tilt-in-space + lateral supports + headrest**. - **Skin integrity:** Loss of sensation + previous pressure ulcer → **ROHO/air cushion + regular pressure-relief training**. - **Age & growth:** A 6-year-old with CP → **growth-adjustable pediatric chair** (e.g., Motivation Multisize), not an adult chair. **Environmental Factors (2 marks)** - **Indoor narrow spaces** → narrow chair with small wheelbase (MWD powered chair). - **Rough rural terrain** → large rear wheels, pneumatic tyres, robust frame (e.g., Whirlwind RoughRider, Jaipur Foot-style chassis). - **Public transport / car boot** → **folding chair** rather than rigid. - **Hot humid climate** → rust-proof aluminium frame, breathable cushion cover. **Economic Factors (2 marks)** - **Cost & funding:** Standard folding steel chair ~₹5,000–8,000 (affordable, ADIP-supported); powered wheelchair ₹50,000–2,50,000 (needs sustained funding). - **Spares availability:** Pneumatic tyres and tubes vs solid tyres — solid preferred where puncture repair is unavailable. - **Durability & maintenance:** In less-resourced settings, WHO recommends a **3-year minimum service life** with locally available spares. - **Cultural acceptance:** User and family must accept the chair's appearance, size, and use; otherwise abandonment occurs (~30% wheelchair abandonment rate globally if acceptance is poor). > A 1-mark conclusion linking these factors to the **principle of "appropriate wheelchair"** (meeting user's body, life, and environment) fetches the final mark. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Wheelchair-Selection Questions:** > 1. **Listing only 3–4 factors** when 6 clusters are expected → aim for *User, Postural, Environmental, Wheelchair, Seating, Service/Economic* (6 clusters) for full 3-mark coverage. > 2. **Forgetting dimensions** — Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the *seat width, seat depth, backrest height* formulas and tolerances (2–3 cm clearance). Memorize them. > 3. **Skipping the 2–3 cm clearances** in dimension answers → instant 1-mark loss. > 4. **Conflating "wheelchair selection" with "wheelchair types"** — selection is the *process* (WHO 8 steps); types is the *output* (manual, powered, etc.). Examiners differentiate sharply. > 5. **Ignoring economic/cultural factors** — under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned outcome-based assessment, *cost, spares, and acceptance* carry weight. Skipping them costs 1–2 marks. > 6. **Not drawing a flowchart in 14-mark Q** — a properly labelled block/flowchart fetches 1–2 easy marks; without it, the answer feels incomplete. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Wheelchair selection = WHO 8-step process:** Referral → Assessment → Prescription → Funding → Preparation → Fitting → Training → Follow-up. - **Six factor clusters:** User, Postural/Functional, Environmental, Wheelchair, Seating, Service/Economic. - **Memorize the dimension formulas:** - Seat Width = Hip + 2 × (1 to 1.5 cm) - Seat Depth = Thigh − (2 to 3 cm) - Seat Height = Lower leg − cushion thickness - Backrest = Scapula (active) / Shoulder (postural) - Armrest = Elbow + (2 to 3 cm) - **Standard pressure threshold:** ≤ **32 mmHg** (capillary closing pressure) for pressure-sore prevention. - **Standard slope for self-propulsion:** ≤ **1:12 (≈ 4.8°)**. - **Drive configurations:** RWD (stable/fast), MWD (tightest turning), FWD (climbs obstacles). - **Key cushions:** Foam (low risk), Gel (moderate), Air/ROHO (high risk), Honeycomb/hybrid. - **Postural solutions for poor trunk control:** Tilt-in-space, recline, lateral supports, headrest, pelvic belt, anti-tippers. - **Funding in India:** **ADIP scheme** (Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment), **NHFDC loans**, state-level schemes, charitable trusts. - **Appropriate wheelchair criteria (WHO):** Meets the user's *body, life, and environment*; safe, durable, affordable, available, acceptable. - **Global statistic to quote in answers:** WHO estimates < **10%** of the 75 million wheelchair users worldwide have access to an *appropriate* wheelchair — a strong "why this topic matters" line for introduction. - **Standards to remember:** **ISO 7176** (wheelchair testing), **ISO 16840** (seating), **RESNA WC-19** (wheelchair transport safety). - **Common confusion to avoid:** A *standing* wheelchair, *stair-climbing* wheelchair, and *off-road* power chair are NOT the same — know the differences for descriptive answers. - **Exam-day one-liner:** *"Selection is user-centred and evidence-based; the wheelchair must fit the user, not the other way around."*

topics.title: Safe Transportation of Individuals in Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safe Transportation of Individuals in Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Safe Transportation of Individuals in Wheelchairs** refers to the engineered, standards-compliant methodology of securing a wheelchair-seated occupant inside a moving vehicle such that, during routine driving, sudden braking, or frontal/rear/lateral collision events, the wheelchair itself does not become a hazardous projectile, the occupant is not subjected to injurious loads on the spine or viscera, and the wheelchair remains structurally intact enough to serve as a survivable seat.

The discipline is governed internationally by three principal standards:

| Standard | Scope |
|----------|-------|
| **ISO 7176-19** | Performance requirements for wheelchairs used as seats in motor vehicles (crash-tested "WC19" chairs) |
| **ISO 10542-1** | Wheelchair Tie-down and Occupant Restraint Systems (WTORS) — minimum 4-point tiedown + 3-point belt |
| **ANSI/RESNA WC-19 / WC-20** | US equivalent of crash-tested wheelchairs and tiedown hardware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A wheelchair is **NOT** an automotive seat by default. Only wheelchairs explicitly labelled **"WC19 / ISO 7176-19 compliant"** are crash-tested as vehicular seats. Using a non-crash-tested chair in a vehicle is equivalent to strapping an office chair into a car — the chair fails structurally before the belts can protect the user.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Car-Seat Principle"

Imagine buckling a fragile glass vase onto a roller-coaster car using only a piece of string. Two things can go wrong:

1. The **string breaks** because it wasn't engineered for the violent jerk.
2. The **vase shatters against the back wall** because nothing stopped it from translating.

A wheelchair in a vehicle faces the exact same twin hazards: the **tiedown system** must not break under crash loads (anchorage strength), and the **restraint belt** must decelerate the occupant gently enough that internal organs and the spine survive (biomechanical loading limits). The whole engineering exercise — therefore — is to engineer the *chair*, the *straps*, and the *belts* as **one unified crash-energy-management system**.

> [!NOTE]
> The reference peak deceleration in a frontal crash test is **20 g** (≈ **196.2 m/s²**), while rear impacts are tested at **15 g**. All WTORS hardware must maintain integrity at these loads with no failure.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics Used in This Topic

| Metric | Standard Value |
|--------|---------------|
| Reference test dummy mass | **75 kg** (Hybrid III 50th percentile male) |
| Peak frontal crash pulse | **20 g** |
| Peak rear crash pulse | **15 g** |
| Anchor load (4-point tiedown) | **≥ 8,000 N** minimum per strap |
| Maximum permissible spinal compression ( Nij ) | **6,000 N** at T12 |
| Maximum permissible femoral load | **10,000 N** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Safety Architecture

Safe wheelchair transportation is achieved through **three coordinated layers**. Any single layer failing compromises the entire system.

### Layer 1 — The Wheelchair Itself (Crashworthy Frame)
- Must have **four factory-engineered securement points** (two front, two rear) integrated into the frame.
- Must have a **crash-tested backrest** that resists spinal compression under 20 g loading.
- Must NOT use lightweight magnesium or unreinforced aluminium structural members at the tie-down zone.

### Layer 2 — The Tie-down System (Anchorage)
- **4-Point Tie-down (Standard):** Two straps at the front lower frame + two straps at the rear lower frame. Each strap must anchor the chair to the vehicle floor in a ±45° cone.
- **3-Point Tie-down (Alternative):** Adds a single upper strap that constrains rearward rotation/tipping.
- Strap material: **Polyester webbing, ≥ 48 mm width**, with cam-buckle or ratchet tensioner.

### Layer 3 — The Occupant Restraint System (ORS)
- **3-Point Belt:** Pelvic (lap) belt anchored to the vehicle floor (NOT the wheelchair) + shoulder belt anchored to the vehicle's B-pillar.
- **4-Point Belt:** Used for paediatric or high-risk occupants — adds a second shoulder anchor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER** anchor the occupant belt to the wheelchair frame. The wheelchair itself is the "seat", but it is *not* the restraint anchor — the belt must go to the **vehicle chassis**, otherwise the chair + occupant become a single projectile.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Engineering Reason |
|-------|--------------------|
| 4-point lower tiedown | Prevents forward & rearward translation of the chair under crash pulse |
| Upper tiedown (if 3-point) | Prevents rearward pitch/rotation of the unoccupied chair mass |
| Pelvic belt (lap) | Restrains pelvis — the strongest bony anchor in the body — preventing "submarining" (sliding under the belt) |
| Shoulder belt | Distributes crash load across the clavicle and sternum, bypassing the lumbar spine |
| Crashworthy frame | Provides a survivable "crumple cage" so the chair does not collapse onto the occupant |

## 2.3 Biomechanical Tolerance (The Human Constraint)

Even if every strap holds, the **occupant's body has limits**. The relevant injury criteria are:

- **Nij (Normalized Injury Criterion)** — combined axial + flexional loading on the neck.
- **Lumbar spinal compression limit** — must stay below **6,000 N** at T12.
- **Femur compression limit** — must stay below **10,000 N**.

These limits are why **proper belt geometry matters more than belt strength**. A correctly-rated belt installed at the wrong angle can still injure the occupant.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's 2nd law — crash force on occupant | N |
| 2 | $a_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | Average crash deceleration | m/s² |
| 3 | $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ | Standard gravity (exact for all KTU problems) | m/s² |
| 4 | $n = \dfrac{a}{g}$ | Deceleration in "g"s (e.g., 20 g) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $F_{\text{crash}} = m \cdot n \cdot g$ | Crash force on occupant at *n* g | N |
| 6 | $F_{\text{anchor,total}} = m \cdot n \cdot g$ | Total anchor force (4-point share it equally) | N |
| 7 | $F_{\text{anchor,each}} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot g}{k}$ | Per-anchor load (k = number of tie-down points) | N |
| 8 | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy to be absorbed by restraint system | J |
| 9 | $SF = \dfrac{F_{\text{ult}}}{F_{\text{applied}}}$ | Safety Factor (WTORS requires SF ≥ **1.5**) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{\text{stop}} = \dfrac{v}{n \cdot g}$ | Time to stop under constant deceleration | s |
| 11 | $\sigma_{\text{webbing}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{ult}}}{A_{\text{cross}}}$ | Stress in polyester webbing | Pa |
| 12 | $\theta_{\text{strap}} \leq 45°$ | Maximum tiedown angle from vertical | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Trick:** In a 4-point tiedown, students often divide the load by **4 anchors**. This is correct **only** if the load is shared symmetrically. In practice, due to impact asymmetry, the standard requires each anchor to be rated for **the FULL crash load**, not a quarter. Always re-read whether the question is asking for the *design rating* or the *average distribution*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Crash Force on a Wheelchair Occupant

**Problem Statement:** A 75 kg wheelchair-bound occupant is involved in a frontal crash with a peak deceleration of 20 g. Calculate (a) the peak force on the occupant, (b) the total anchor load on the tiedown system, and (c) the per-anchor load for a 4-point tiedown.

### Given Data
- Mass of occupant + chair: $m = 75 \, \text{kg}$
- Peak deceleration: $n = 20 \, g$
- Number of anchor points: $k = 4$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert deceleration to SI units.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= n \cdot g \\
  &= 20 \times 9.81 \\
  &= 196.2 \, \text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Multiplying g-units by 9.81 m/s² converts them to absolute deceleration in m/s².

**Step 2 — Calculate peak crash force on occupant using Newton's 2nd law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{crash}} &= m \cdot a \\
                 &= 75 \times 196.2 \\
                 &= 14{,}715 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the inertial load the occupant experiences. The restraint belts must apply an equal and opposite force to decelerate the occupant over the same time period.

**Step 3 — Total anchor load on the tiedown system.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{anchor,total}} &= F_{\text{crash}} \\
                       &= 14{,}715 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* By Newton's 3rd law, the tiedown straps must collectively resist the same 14,715 N that is trying to translate the chair forward.

**Step 4 — Per-anchor load (average distribution).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{anchor,each}} &= \frac{F_{\text{anchor,total}}}{k} \\
                       &= \frac{14{,}715}{4} \\
                       &= 3{,}678.75 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* If the load shares equally among 4 anchors, each carries ~3.68 kN. But ISO 10542 requires each anchor be rated to the **full** crash load (≈ 14,715 N) to handle asymmetric loading.

### Final Answer Block

$$
F_{\text{crash}} = 14{,}715 \, \text{N} \quad ; \quad F_{\text{anchor,each (avg)}} = 3{,}678.75 \, \text{N}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Time to Stop and Kinetic Energy

**Problem Statement:** A wheelchair user in a vehicle traveling at 60 km/h experiences a frontal collision with a sustained deceleration of 20 g. Calculate (a) the stopping time, (b) the kinetic energy that must be absorbed by the restraint system.

### Given Data
- Initial velocity: $v = 60 \, \text{km/h}$
- Peak deceleration: $n = 20 \, g$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert velocity to m/s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 60 \times \frac{1000}{3600} \\
  &= 60 \times 0.27778 \\
  &= 16.667 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The standard conversion factor is 1000 m / 3600 s = 0.27778.

**Step 2 — Calculate stopping time under constant deceleration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{stop}} &= \frac{v}{n \cdot g} \\
                &= \frac{16.667}{20 \times 9.81} \\
                &= \frac{16.667}{196.2} \\
                &= 0.0849 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* From kinematics, $v = u + at$ with final velocity = 0, so $t = v/a$. The occupant stops in under 85 milliseconds — faster than a human reflex blink.

**Step 3 — Calculate kinetic energy to be absorbed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KE &= \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
   &= \frac{1}{2} \times 75 \times (16.667)^2 \\
   &= 0.5 \times 75 \times 277.78 \\
   &= 10{,}416.75 \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the total energy the belts and the chair's deformable zones must dissipate. For reference, 10.4 kJ is roughly the muzzle energy of a high-powered hunting rifle.

### Final Answer Block

$$
t_{\text{stop}} \approx 85 \, \text{ms} \quad ; \quad KE \approx 10.42 \, \text{kJ}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Safety Factor Verification

**Problem Statement:** A polyester tiedown strap has an ultimate tensile strength of 22,000 N and cross-sectional area of 60 mm². During the crash test above, it experiences 14,715 N. Calculate (a) the safety factor, (b) the working stress, and (c) verify it passes the ISO 10542 minimum (SF ≥ 1.5).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate safety factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF &= \frac{F_{\text{ult}}}{F_{\text{applied}}} \\
   &= \frac{22{,}000}{14{,}715} \\
   &= 1.495
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* SF = ultimate / applied. A value of 1.495 is dangerously close to the 1.5 minimum.

**Step 2 — Calculate working stress.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{F_{\text{applied}}}{A_{\text{cross}}} \\
       &= \frac{14{,}715}{60 \times 10^{-6}} \\
       &= 245{,}250{,}000 \, \text{Pa} \\
       &= 245.25 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Stress = Force / Area. 60 mm² = 60 × 10⁻⁶ m².

**Step 3 — Verification verdict.**

$$
SF = 1.495 \; < \; 1.5 \; \Rightarrow \; \textbf{STRAP FAILS ISO 10542 — REJECT}
$$

*Logic:* This strap is *unacceptable* for use. A 22 kN strap barely fails the 1.5 safety factor; one should select a strap with **F_ult ≥ 22,500 N** to provide a comfortable engineering margin.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Tie-down Configurations

| Configuration | Anchor Count | Crashworthiness | Use Case | KTU Exam Favourite? |
|---------------|--------------|-----------------|----------|---------------------|
| 4-point lower tiedown + 3-point belt | 4 + 2 belt anchors | Highest (20 g frontal) | Standard public transit & personal vehicles | ✅ Very common |
| 3-point tiedown (2 lower + 1 upper) + 3-point belt | 3 + 2 belt anchors | High (15 g frontal) | Lightweight chairs, low-speed shuttles | ✅ Common |
| 4-point lower + 4-point belt | 4 + 4 belt anchors | Highest (20 g + paediatric) | Children, high-risk occupants | Occasional |
| Docking system (EZ Lock, Q'Straint) | Mechanical lock + belt | Equivalent to 4-point | Frequent-stowage fleets | Often asked |
| No tiedown, lap belt only on chair | 0 | **None — illegal** | Never permitted | Rare (safety trap) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WTORS Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Floor Anchor Rails] --> B[Four-Point Tie-down Strap System]
    A --> C[Three-Point Occupant Belt Anchor]
    B --> D[Wheelchair Lower Frame Securement Points]
    D --> E[Crashworthy Wheelchair Frame]
    E --> F[Crash-Tested Backrest]
    C --> G[Occupant Pelvic Belt Anchor]
    C --> H[Occupant Shoulder Belt Anchor]
    G --> I[Lap Belt Across Pelvis]
    H --> J[Shoulder Belt Across Sternum]
    E --> K[Posture Belt on Chair]
    I --> L[Survivable Crash Envelope]
    J --> L
    F --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Crash Force at 20 g Decelerated over 85 ms]
    M --> N[Injury Criteria Within Tolerance]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The diagram shows the four functional subsystems (vehicle anchors, tiedown straps, wheelchair, occupant belt) all converging into a single *survivable crash envelope*. The crash pulse (20 g, 85 ms) must be absorbed such that occupant injury criteria remain below tolerance.

## 4.2 Safe Transportation Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Verify WC19 / ISO 7176-19 Label on Chair] --> P2[Position Chair Centrally in Vehicle]
    P2 --> P3[Engage 4-Point Tie-down: Front Straps First]
    P3 --> P4[Tension Lower Straps to Remove Slack]
    P4 --> P5[Engage Rear Tie-down Straps]
    P5 --> P6[Tension All Straps Until Chair is Rigid]
    P6 --> P7[Apply 3-Point Belt to Occupant]
    P7 --> P8[Anchor Pelvic Belt to VEHICLE Floor, NOT Chair]
    P8 --> P9[Anchor Shoulder Belt to B-Pillar]
    P9 --> P10[Verify Belt Lies Flat, No Twists, Across Pelvis]
    P10 --> P11[Confirm All Buckles Locked and Audible Click]
    P11 --> P12[Posture Belt on Chair Snug but Not Restrictive]
    P12 --> P13[Ready to Drive]
```

## 4.3 Crash Force Vector Resolution (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Vehicle Direction of Travel] --> V2[Frontal Impact Pulse 20 g Forward]
    V2 --> F1[Wheelchair Anchored by 4 Straps]
    F1 --> F2[Front Straps Pull in Tension]
    F1 --> F3[Rear Straps May Go Slack in Frontal Crash]
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> O1[Occupant Decelerated by Shoulder + Lap Belt]
    O1 --> O2[Spinal Load Distributed Across Pelvis Sternum]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Define WTORS. List any four essential components of a Wheelchair Tie-down and Occupant Restraint System.**

**Model Answer:**

WTORS (Wheelchair Tie-down and Occupant Restraint System) is the complete engineered assembly used to secure both a wheelchair and its occupant inside a motor vehicle during normal driving and crash conditions, ensuring crash loads are transferred from the occupant → chair → tiedown straps → vehicle floor without injury.

Four essential components:

1. **Lower anchor points** on the wheelchair frame (2 front, 2 rear) engineered to ISO 7176-19.
2. **Tie-down straps** with cam-buckle or ratchet tensioners (4-point system).
3. **Vehicle floor anchor rails** with positive-locking connectors.
4. **3-point occupant belt** — lap belt + shoulder belt — anchored to the vehicle chassis (not the chair).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Why must the occupant's pelvic belt be anchored to the vehicle and not to the wheelchair frame?**

**Model Answer:**

The occupant restraint belt must be anchored to the **vehicle chassis** rather than the wheelchair frame for three critical reasons:

1. **Crash-load transfer path:** The vehicle floor is the ultimate structural anchor. Anchoring to the wheelchair frame means the chair itself must resist the entire crash load, which may exceed its frame strength and cause collapse.
2. **Belt geometry:** The belt angle is engineered to lie across the bony pelvis (ASIS) and sternum. Anchoring to the chair can alter this geometry, allowing "submarining" where the occupant slides under the lap belt.
3. **Standards compliance:** ISO 10542-1 explicitly mandates that the occupant belt be anchored to the vehicle chassis. Anchoring to the chair is a non-conformance that voids WTORS certification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **4-point tie-down system** for wheelchair transportation. Describe the function of each strap and the geometry constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheelchair user of mass **80 kg** (including the chair) is transported in a vehicle that experiences a **frontal crash with peak deceleration of 18 g**. Calculate: (i) the total crash force on the occupant, (ii) the per-anchor load in a 4-point tiedown system, and (iii) whether a tiedown strap with ultimate tensile strength of **20,000 N** meets the ISO 10542 safety factor of **1.5**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**4-Point Tie-down System Description:**

The 4-point tie-down is the standard securement configuration per ISO 10542-1. It uses **four straps** — two anchoring the front lower frame of the wheelchair and two anchoring the rear lower frame — all connected to the vehicle's floor anchor rails.

**Strap-by-strap function:**

- **Two Front Lower Straps:** Resist rearward motion of the chair during frontal impact. Angled between 30°–45° from horizontal to limit vertical lift. Must not interfere with the occupant's feet or the chair's front casters.
- **Two Rear Lower Straps:** Resist forward motion of the chair during rear impact. Also angled between 30°–45° from horizontal, attached to the rear securement points.
- **Geometry Constraint:** Each strap angle from vertical must not exceed **45°**. Angles greater than 45° create excessive vertical lift that can unseat the occupant.

**Sketch description (to be drawn in exam):**
- Top-down view of vehicle floor with chair centered.
- Four straps radiating at 30°–45° from chair corners.
- Lap belt going from vehicle floor (anchor points outside chair) up across pelvis.
- Shoulder belt going from B-pillar across chest to buckle on floor.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $m = 80 \, \text{kg}$, $n = 18$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $F_{\text{ult}} = 20{,}000 \, \text{N}$

**(i) Total crash force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{crash}} &= m \cdot n \cdot g \\
                 &= 80 \times 18 \times 9.81 \\
                 &= 14{,}126.4 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Per-anchor load (4-point equal share):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{anchor,each}} &= \frac{F_{\text{crash}}}{4} \\
                       &= \frac{14{,}126.4}{4} \\
                       &= 3{,}531.6 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Safety factor check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF &= \frac{F_{\text{ult}}}{F_{\text{applied}}} \\
   &= \frac{20{,}000}{14{,}126.4} \\
   &= 1.416
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** $SF = 1.416 \; < \; 1.5$ → **FAILS the ISO 10542 minimum.** The strap is **not acceptable**; one must select a strap with $F_{\text{ult}} \geq 21{,}189.6 \, \text{N}$ to meet the 1.5 safety factor.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Alternative Module Choice]

**(a)** Describe the **ISO 7176-19 crash test protocol** for wheelchairs. What are the pass/fail criteria? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **biomechanical injury criteria** that govern wheelchair occupant restraint design. Explain why the lap belt must anchor to the vehicle and not the chair, and how "submarining" is prevented. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**ISO 7176-19 Crash Test Protocol:**

ISO 7176-19 (also adopted as ANSI/RESNA WC-19) specifies that any wheelchair intended for use as a seat in a motor vehicle must be crash-tested under the following protocol:

1. **Test Fixture:** The wheelchair is mounted on a sled with a 75 kg Hybrid III dummy seated in it.
2. **Frontal Impact:** Sled accelerated to produce a **20 g peak deceleration** with a pulse duration of approximately 50–120 ms.
3. **Rear Impact:** Sled accelerated to **15 g** peak deceleration.
4. **Tiedown Condition:** The chair is secured with a 4-point tiedown and a 3-point occupant belt per ISO 10542-1.

**Pass/Fail Criteria:**

- ✅ The wheelchair frame and tiedown anchors must remain intact.
- ✅ No component shall intrude into the occupant's survival space (defined by a 150 mm radius around the dummy).
- ✅ All measured injury criteria must stay within bio-limits: HIC(d) ≤ 1000, Nij ≤ 1.0, lumbar compression ≤ 6,000 N, femur load ≤ 10,000 N.
- ✅ The chair must not become detached from the tiedown during the test.

**Wheelchairs that pass are labelled "WC19 / ISO 7176-19 compliant"** and have four designated securement points marked with a hook symbol.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Biomechanical Injury Criteria:**

The restraint system is designed to limit the following occupant loads to survivable levels:

| Criterion | Limit | Body Region |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| HIC(d) | 1000 | Head injury |
| Nij | 1.0 | Neck injury |
| Lumbar compression | 6,000 N | T12 vertebra |
| Femur compression | 10,000 N | Thigh bone |

**Why Lap Belt Must Anchor to Vehicle:**

Anchoring the lap belt to the **wheelchair frame** causes three failures:

1. The chair becomes the load path; the frame may buckle.
2. The belt angle drops below the safe 30°–75° from horizontal range, allowing the pelvis to slide under it.
3. The crash load is concentrated at the chair's belt-attachment weld, which is not rated for restraint loads.

**Prevention of Submarining:**

"Submarining" is the forward sliding of the pelvis under the lap belt, causing severe abdominal and lumbar injuries. It is prevented by:

1. **Correct belt angle:** The lap belt must anchor at **30°–75° from horizontal**, ideally around 45°–60°.
2. **Pelvic bony lock:** The belt must lie flat across the **anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS)** — the bony ridge of the pelvis — preventing the soft abdomen from being the load path.
3. **Tight initial fit:** No slack in the lap belt at the start of the journey.
4. **Pre-tensioners:** Some modern WTORS include pyrotechnic pre-tensioners that remove slack at the onset of a crash.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert g to m/s².** A frequent 1–2 mark loss. Always multiply by **9.81** explicitly.
> 2. **Dividing crash force by 4 without stating the assumption** that the load is shared symmetrically. State this assumption, or compute per-anchor rating = total force (worst case).
> 3. **Anchoring the lap belt to the wheelchair** in a diagram — this is an instant 2-mark deduction in descriptive answers.
> 4. **Writing "20g"** in a calculation without showing the **196.2 m/s²** substitution. Examiners want to see the unit conversion step.
> 5. **Confusing ISO 7176-19 (wheelchair) with ISO 10542 (WTORS).** These are *different* standards. Memorise both.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ Safe wheelchair transport is governed by **ISO 7176-19** (chair) + **ISO 10542** (WTORS).
- ✅ Standard configuration: **4-point tiedown + 3-point belt**.
- ✅ Reference crash load: **20 g frontal, 15 g rear**, with a **75 kg** dummy.
- ✅ Lap belt anchors to **vehicle floor**, NOT to the wheelchair frame.
- ✅ Each tiedown anchor must be rated for **the full crash load** (not divided) due to asymmetric loading.
- ✅ Strap angle from vertical must be **≤ 45°**.
- ✅ Required **safety factor ≥ 1.5** for tiedown hardware.
- ✅ Spinal compression limit: **6,000 N at T12**.
- ✅ Femur compression limit: **10,000 N**.
- ✅ "Submarining" is prevented by correct belt angle and bony pelvic lock.
- ✅ Kinetic energy in a 60 km/h crash: **~10.4 kJ** for a 75 kg occupant — absorbed in ~**85 ms**.
- ✅ Crashworthy chairs are labelled **"WC19"** with a hook symbol at securement points.
- ✅ Three layers of safety: **Crashworthy chair → Tie-down → Occupant belt** — all three must function.
- ✅ Wheelchairs without WC19 label must NOT be used as vehicular seats.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation (LTM Devices)** are *non-powered, mechanically simple assistive devices* designed to compensate for upper-limb impairments (limited reach, grasp, dexterity, range of motion, or strength) by **extending the user's effective biomechanical workspace**, **modifying the interface between hand and object**, and **stabilizing joints or objects** during activities of daily living (ADLs), work, or recreation.

In the strict KTU/REHAB engineering taxonomy, these belong to the **Class I (Non-Powered) Orthotic/Assistive Functional Devices** — devices that require no external energy source (battery, compressed gas, AC mains) and rely entirely on the **user's own body mechanics** to function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Extending Your Body" Intuition

Imagine you are wearing thick winter gloves and trying to pick up a coin from the floor. Your fingers are clumsy, your reach is too short, and your grip is weak. Now imagine someone hands you three tools:

1. **Tongs (a reacher)** → extends your arm without effort
2. **Tweezers (a gripper/built-up handle)** → multiplies your finger strength
3. **Blu-Tack (non-slip mat)** → holds the coin in place so you don't need a pincer grip at all

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Low-Tech Manipulation Aids (Memorize this):**
> - **EXTEND** the reach (lengthen the lever arm)
> - **AMPLIFY** the grip (multiply force via grip modification)
> - **STABILIZE** the task (eliminate the need for fine motor control)

This is the entire philosophy of low-tech manipulation aids — they **redesign the task** rather than **re-engineer the human body**.

### 1.3 Classification of Manipulation Activities (KTU Framework)

Manipulation is defined as the **coordinated prehension, transport, and release of objects** using the upper limb. The KTU 2024 module framework categorizes manipulation aids by the ADL domain they support:

| Domain | Typical Limitation | Representative Low-Tech Aid |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-Feeding** | Poor grasp, tremor, hemiparesis | Built-up cutlery, plate guard, swivel spoon, non-slip mat |
| **Dressing** | Limited shoulder ROM, no fine pinch | Button hook, sock aid, dressing stick, long-handled shoe horn |
| **Grooming/Bathing** | Cannot reach back/feet | Long-handled sponge, soap-on-a-rope, adapted toothbrush |
| **Writing/Communication** | Tremor, weak pinch, poor grip | Pencil grip cuff, weighted pen, typing stick |
| **Kitchen/Work** | Limited grip strength, one-handed use | Rocker knife, jar opener, cutting board with nails/spikes |
| **Recreation/Leisure** | Inability to hold cards, page turners | Card holder, automatic page turner, fishing rod holder |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these under the **"Manipulation Triad"**: *Reach Aids + Grasp Aids + Stabilization Aids*. Any exam answer on this topic should explicitly reference this triad for full marks.

### 1.4 Key Quantitative Performance Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Effective Functional Reach (EFR):** typically **60–90 cm** for a standard adult; reachers can extend this to **~80–120 cm**.
- **Required Pinch Grip Force (Normal):** **15–25 N** for ADL objects; arthritic/elderly users may only generate **5–10 N** — built-up handles reduce this demand by **40–70%**.
- **Handle Diameter Standard (Neutral Wrist):** **3.0–3.5 cm** is biomechanically optimal; deviations increase torque demand on the wrist.
- **Friction Coefficient (μ) of Non-Slip Mats:** **0.6–0.9** (silicone/rubber on smooth surface) — sufficient to resist typical plate-slide forces of **2–5 N**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biomechanical Foundation — Why These Devices Work

The human upper limb is a **multi-link serial chain of levers** controlled by a limited set of muscles. When pathology (stroke, arthritis, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, amputation) disrupts one link, the entire chain becomes non-functional. Low-tech manipulation aids work by **altering the boundary conditions** of these levers so that the *remaining* functional capacity of the user is sufficient to complete the task.

The three primary biomechanical principles exploited are:

1. **Lengthening of the Effective Lever Arm (Reach Extension)**
   - The functional reach of the arm is governed by the sum of the segment lengths plus joint range of motion. A reacher adds a rigid extension, transforming a *body-bound* lever into a *tool-extended* lever. The mechanical trade-off is that the **user must apply force farther from the fulcrum**, which is why reachers are often combined with a **trigger-actuated gripper** to recover the lost grip function.

2. **Modification of the Grasp Interface (Grip Amplification)**
   - A normal cylindrical grasp applies force through a small contact patch with the fingertips. A *built-up handle* enlarges the effective diameter, which:
     - Recruits more fingers (and often the palm) into the grip.
     - Reduces the required **flexor tendon moment** to maintain static friction.
     - Places the wrist closer to its **neutral position**, reducing strain.

3. **External Stabilization of the Object or the User (Static Substitution)**
   - If a user cannot *hold* an object, the device can *hold it for them* (e.g., a plate guard contains food on a plate; a vise grip clamps a workpiece). This eliminates the need for active stabilization, freeing the user's limited motor resources for the *manipulation* component only.

### 2.2 The Underlying Static Equilibrium Model

For any manipulation aid, we apply the **principle of static equilibrium** to the user's hand–tool–object system. The two critical equations are:

**Force Equilibrium** (no net linear acceleration):

$$\sum F_{x} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum F_{y} = 0$$

**Moment Equilibrium** (no net rotational acceleration, taken about the joint of interest):

$$\sum M_{joint} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{object} \cdot d_{object} = F_{muscle} \cdot d_{muscle}$$

where $d$ denotes the **moment arm** (perpendicular distance from the joint axis to the line of action of the force).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield equation the KTU board has tested (or is highly likely to test) for low-tech manipulation aids. Memorize the variables and units — not just the symbols.

| # | Equation / Rule | LaTeX Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sliding Condition (Non-slip Mat)** | $F_{applied} \le \mu \cdot N$ | $F_{applied}$: lateral force on object (N); $\mu$: coefficient of friction (–); $N$: normal force (N) | Determines minimum non-slip mat friction needed to hold a plate, cup, or cutting board stationary. |
| 2 | **Mechanical Advantage of a Reacher** | $MA = \dfrac{L_{reacher}}{L_{trigger}}$ | $L_{reacher}$: handle-to-jaw distance (m); $L_{trigger}$: trigger-to-finger distance (m) | Quantifies how much finger force is amplified at the gripper jaws. |
| 3 | **Required Grip Force for Cylinder** | $F_{grip} \ge \dfrac{W}{2 \mu_{skin}}$ | $W$: weight of object (N); $\mu_{skin}$: skin-object friction (–) | Shows why *enlarging the handle* (so that the entire palm contacts) increases $\mu_{skin}$ and reduces required $F_{grip}$. |
| 4 | **Wrist Torque (Built-up Handle)** | $\tau_{wrist} = F_{grip} \cdot \dfrac{d_{handle}}{2}$ | $F_{grip}$: grip force (N); $d_{handle}$: handle diameter (m) | Demonstrates that a **larger handle diameter** increases wrist torque demand — the trade-off the clinician must balance. |
| 5 | **Range-of-Motion Compensation (Reacher)** | $ROM_{required} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h_{object}}{d_{reacher}}\right)$ | $h_{object}$: vertical height of object (m); $d_{reacher}$: horizontal distance (m) | Predicts whether a user with limited shoulder flexion (e.g., 90°) can reach an object using a reacher of given length. |
| 6 | **One-Handed Leverage (Jar Opener)** | $F_{out} = F_{in} \cdot \dfrac{L_{handle}}{L_{lid}}$ | $F_{in}$: applied hand force (N); $F_{out}$: torque on lid (N); $L_{handle}$: handle length (m); $L_{lid}$: lid radius (m) | Design equation for jar openers, key turners, and faucet aids. |
| 7 | **Dressing Stick Effective Reach** | $L_{eff} = L_{arm} + L_{stick} - L_{deficit}$ | $L_{eff}$: total effective reach (m); $L_{arm}$: user arm length (m); $L_{stick}$: stick length (m); $L_{deficit}$: ROM deficit equivalent (m) | Calculates whether a dressing stick compensates fully for a shoulder limitation. |
| 8 | **Optimal Handle Diameter (Radomski)** | $d_{opt} \approx 0.030 \text{ to } 0.035 \text{ m}$ | Empirical range, derived from carpal tunnel pressure studies | The "magic" 30–35 mm rule for built-up handle diameter. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The **most-tested equations** in KTU exams from this module are **#2 (Reacher MA)**, **#3 (Cylinder Grip Force)**, **#4 (Wrist Torque)**, and **#6 (One-Handed Leverage)**. Be prepared to derive or apply any of these.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not classroom-only abstractions. They are used by:

- **Occupational therapists (OTs)** during wheelchair and ADL assessments.
- **Rehabilitation engineers** at institutions like NIEPMD (Chennai) and NIMHANS (Bangalore) for custom device fabrication.
- **Industrial designers** at firms like *Etac*, *Performance Health*, and *Patterson Medical* when commercializing reachers and built-up utensils.
- **Biomechanics researchers** publishing in journals like *Journal of Rehabilitation Research & Development* and *Assistive Technology*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this section is long:** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests *derivation ability* under Bloom's Level 3 (Apply) and Level 4 (Analyze). Below are the four most likely derivations a board examiner will set, with **every algebraic step** explicitly written out.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mechanical Advantage of a Trigger-Operated Reacher

A reacher is essentially a **first-class lever** (with the trigger finger acting as the effort, the gripper jaws as the load, and a linkage pivot as the fulcrum). We wish to find how much the user's finger force $F_{finger}$ is amplified at the jaws $F_{jaw}$.

**Step 1 — Draw the free-body diagram.** Consider the rigid handle of the reacher in static equilibrium. The pivot (linkage fulcrum) is point O. The finger applies force $F_{finger}$ at perpendicular distance $L_{trigger}$ from O. The jaws transmit force $F_{jaw}$ at perpendicular distance $L_{reacher}$ from O on the opposite side.

**Step 2 — Apply the moment equilibrium equation about O.** This eliminates the unknown reaction force at the pivot, leaving only the two applied forces:

$$\sum M_O = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{finger} \cdot L_{trigger} - F_{jaw} \cdot L_{reacher} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $F_{jaw}$ in terms of $F_{finger}$.**

$$F_{jaw} \cdot L_{reacher} = F_{finger} \cdot L_{trigger}$$

$$F_{jaw} = F_{finger} \cdot \dfrac{L_{trigger}}{L_{reacher}}$$

**Step 4 — Define Mechanical Advantage (MA).** By definition, MA is the ratio of output force to input force:

$$MA = \dfrac{F_{jaw}}{F_{finger}} = \dfrac{L_{trigger}}{L_{reacher}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style).** A reacher has $L_{reacher} = 0.40 \text{ m}$ and $L_{trigger} = 0.05 \text{ m}$. A user with weak fingers can apply $F_{finger} = 8 \text{ N}$.

$$MA = \dfrac{0.05}{0.40} = 0.125$$

$$F_{jaw} = 8 \text{ N} \times 0.125 = 1.0 \text{ N}$$

**Interpretation:** This reacher is a **force-reducing** device (MA < 1). The user gives up force to gain **reach**. To lift a 1 kg object (≈ 9.81 N), the user would need $F_{finger} = 9.81 / 0.125 \approx 78.5 \text{ N}$ — confirming the **fundamental trade-off**: *longer reachers amplify reach but reduce gripping force*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Grip Force on a Cylindrical Handle

A user holds a cylindrical object (e.g., a cup) of weight $W$ in a vertical pinch grasp. We wish to find the minimum grip force $F_{grip}$ needed to prevent slipping.

**Step 1 — Identify the forces.** The object weight $W$ acts downward. The user's fingers apply normal forces $N$ inward on each side of the cylinder. The friction force at each finger contact is $f = \mu_{skin} \cdot N$, directed upward to oppose the weight.

**Step 2 — Apply force equilibrium in the vertical direction.** Two fingers (or two contact points) each provide friction, so total friction = $2 \mu_{skin} N$:

$$\sum F_{y} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 \mu_{skin} N - W = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the normal force at one finger.** The grip force $F_{grip}$ is the normal force applied by the finger:

$$F_{grip} = N = \dfrac{W}{2 \mu_{skin}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.** A cup with $W = 2 \text{ N}$ (≈ 200 g) is held with $\mu_{skin} = 0.30$ (skin on smooth plastic).

$$F_{grip} = \dfrac{2 \text{ N}}{2 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{2}{0.60} \approx 3.33 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Effect of a built-up handle.** A built-up handle increases the contact area so that the **entire palm** contacts the surface, raising effective $\mu_{skin}$ to ≈ 0.55 (palm skin is softer and conforms more):

$$F_{grip,new} = \dfrac{2 \text{ N}}{2 \times 0.55} \approx 1.82 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{Force reduction} = \dfrac{3.33 - 1.82}{3.33} \times 100\% \approx 45.4\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Hook:** This 45% reduction is a *classic KTU answer*. Always state the **percentage reduction** explicitly when comparing interventions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Jar Opener Lever Mechanics (Second-Class Lever)

A one-handed jar opener is a **second-class lever**. The lid is the load, the hand applies the effort at the handle end, and the pivot is at the opposite end of the lever.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.** Let the pivot O be at one end. The lid contacts the lever at perpendicular distance $L_{lid}$ from O. The hand applies force $F_{in}$ at perpendicular distance $L_{handle}$ from O.

**Step 2 — Moment equilibrium about O.**

$$\sum M_O = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{in} \cdot L_{handle} - F_{out} \cdot L_{lid} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the output force on the lid.**

$$F_{out} = F_{in} \cdot \dfrac{L_{handle}}{L_{lid}}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Advantage.**

$$MA = \dfrac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \dfrac{L_{handle}}{L_{lid}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.** A jar lid has radius $L_{lid} = 0.04 \text{ m}$ (40 mm). The opener handle is $L_{handle} = 0.12 \text{ m}$. A user applies $F_{in} = 20 \text{ N}$.

$$MA = \dfrac{0.12}{0.04} = 3.0$$

$$F_{out} = 20 \text{ N} \times 3.0 = 60 \text{ N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical translation:** A user who can only produce 20 N of one-handed grip force can now apply 60 N of torque to the lid — sufficient to break the static friction seal of a typical 5–6 cm jar lid (which requires 50–80 N of torque to initially break).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Wrist Torque Trade-off with Handle Diameter

A user grips a handle of diameter $d_{handle}$. The fingers close around the handle, applying a normal force $F_{grip}$ that produces a moment about the wrist joint equal to the grip force times the handle radius.

**Step 1 — Identify the moment arm.** When the fingers wrap around a cylinder, the line of action of the grip force is tangent to the cylinder, and the perpendicular distance from the wrist joint (or, more precisely, from the metacarpophalangeal joint axis) to this line is approximately equal to the **radius** of the handle.

$$d_{moment} \approx \dfrac{d_{handle}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the wrist torque equation.**

$$\tau_{wrist} = F_{grip} \cdot \dfrac{d_{handle}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Show the trade-off with a numerical comparison.** For an object requiring $W = 5 \text{ N}$ of grip support with $\mu_{skin} = 0.5$:

$$F_{grip} = \dfrac{5}{2 \times 0.5} = 5 \text{ N}$$

Compare a thin pen ($d = 0.01 \text{ m}$) and a built-up handle ($d = 0.035 \text{ m}$):

$$\tau_{thin} = 5 \text{ N} \times \dfrac{0.01}{2} = 0.025 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\tau_{built} = 5 \text{ N} \times \dfrac{0.035}{2} = 0.0875 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** The built-up handle increases wrist torque by a factor of **3.5×**. This is why:
- Built-up handles are *good* for users with poor pinch strength (lower $F_{grip}$ needed).
- But built-up handles are *bad* for users with wrist arthritis (higher $\tau_{wrist}$ causes pain).

The clinical compromise is the **30–35 mm optimal handle diameter**, which balances both factors.

---

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix

The following matrix maps **user limitation** → **recommended device class** → **primary biomechanical principle exploited**. This is high-yield for the 14-mark essay questions.

| User Limitation (KTU Term) | Clinical Example | Recommended Device Class | Primary Principle | Secondary Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Limited reach (shoulder ROM < 120°)** | Post-mastectomy, rotator cuff tear | Reacher / dressing stick | Lever arm extension | Postural compensation |
| **Weak pinch grip (< 10 N)** | Rheumatoid arthritis, elderly | Built-up handle utensils | Grip interface modification | Reduced finger flexor demand |
| **Poor fine motor control (tremor, ataxia)** | Parkinson's, cerebral palsy | Weighted pen, swivel spoon | Inertial damping + gravity assist | Smooths motion trajectory |
| **Hemiparesis (one-hand use)** | Stroke survivor | Rocker knife, one-handed cutting board | Task redesign + object stabilization | Eliminates bimanual requirement |
| **Limited wrist extension (< 20°)** | Colles' fracture, wrist fusion | Angled utensils, pistol-grip tools | Joint angle accommodation | Neutral wrist posture |
| **Inability to supinate forearm** | Brachial plexus injury | Self-feeding devices with angled shafts | Substitute pronation/supination | Uses shoulder internal rotation |
| **No grip at all (C5–C6 tetraplegia)** | High-level SCI | Universal cuff, mouthstick, environmental control | Passive attachment | Shifts function to wrist/elbow extension |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid block below maps the **functional architecture** of the low-tech manipulation aid system, including the user–device–object interaction loop and the classification hierarchy required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Manipulation Aid System

```mermaid

    graspCat --> graspDev2[Universal Cuff]

```


### 4.3 Design Trade-off Triangle for Handle Diameter

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    optA -->|Balances| optCon2[Moderate Wrist Torque]
```
