```mermaid
    bigA -->|Increases| bigCon2[High Wrist Torque and Strain]

    penCon1 --> trade[Engineering Trade off]
    penCon2 --> trade
    optCon1 --> trade
    optCon2 --> trade
    bigCon1 --> trade
    bigCon2 --> trade
    trade --> clinical[Clinical Prescription Decision]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For 7-mark diagrams in KTU exams:** Always label the **fulcrum (F)**, **effort (E)**, and **load (L)** on a lever diagram, and explicitly state which class of lever the device represents. This guarantees full marks on the diagram portion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Manipulation Aids:**
> 1. Students frequently **forget to state the lever class** of the device (1 mark lost every time).
> 2. They write *generic* answers like "it helps the user" instead of specifying **which biomechanical parameter changes** (e.g., "reduces pinch force demand from 8 N to 3 N").
> 3. They confuse **reach aids** with **grasp aids** — these are distinct categories in the KTU rubric.
> 4. They omit the **units** in numerical answers, leading to zero credit for the final answer.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define a *reacher*. State any two biomechanical principles on which it operates.

**Model Answer:**
A reacher is a low-technology manipulation aid designed to extend the functional reach of a user with limited shoulder range of motion, upper-limb weakness, or pain that prevents bending or stretching. It typically consists of a long rigid shaft (30–90 cm) with a trigger-operated gripper at one end and a handle at the other.
**Two biomechanical principles:**
1. **Extension of the effective lever arm** — the rigid shaft acts as an extension of the user's arm, converting a body-bound lever into a tool-extended lever.
2. **Trigger-actuated grip force amplification** — the trigger and linkage form a first-class lever system that allows a small finger force to clamp the jaws on the target object.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the optimal handle diameter for built-up utensils, and why?

**Model Answer:**
The optimal handle diameter for built-up utensils is **3.0 to 3.5 cm (30–35 mm)**, based on biomechanical studies by Radomski and colleagues. **Reason:** A diameter in this range allows the user's entire palm and all four fingers to wrap around the handle, which (a) maximizes the contact area and effective friction coefficient, (b) minimizes the pinch force required to hold an object, and (c) keeps the wrist in a near-neutral position, avoiding the high carpal tunnel pressures associated with thin handles (< 15 mm) and the excessive wrist torque associated with oversized handles (> 50 mm).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 ESE questions present an "Either-Or" choice. Both options below carry 14 marks and are structured as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Reach Aids & Grasp Aids*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a *trigger-operated reacher* as a first-class lever. Derive the expression for its mechanical advantage. A reacher has an effective handle length of 45 cm and a trigger lever of 6 cm. If the user can apply 10 N at the trigger, calculate the clamping force at the jaws.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch description (3 marks):** A first-class lever diagram showing: **Fulcrum (F)** at the linkage pivot point, **Effort (E)** applied at the trigger at distance $L_{trigger} = 6$ cm from the fulcrum, and **Load (L)** at the gripper jaws at distance $L_{reacher} = 45$ cm from the fulcrum on the opposite side. The effort and load arms are on opposite sides of the fulcrum.

**Derivation of MA (2 marks):** Apply moment equilibrium about the fulcrum:

$$F_{finger} \cdot L_{trigger} = F_{jaw} \cdot L_{reacher}$$

$$MA = \dfrac{F_{jaw}}{F_{finger}} = \dfrac{L_{trigger}}{L_{reacher}}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$MA = \dfrac{6 \text{ cm}}{45 \text{ cm}} = \dfrac{6}{45} = 0.1333$$

$$F_{jaw} = F_{finger} \times MA = 10 \text{ N} \times 0.1333 = 1.333 \text{ N}$$

**Answer:** $F_{jaw} \approx 1.33 \text{ N}$, $MA = 0.133$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the biomechanical rationale for using built-up handles for users with rheumatoid arthritis. Support your answer with the appropriate equation and a numerical comparison between a standard pen (diameter 10 mm) and a built-up handle (diameter 35 mm) when holding a 200 g object, assuming a palm-skin friction coefficient of 0.5.

**Model Solution:**

**Clinical rationale (3 marks):** Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) causes inflammation of the small joints of the hand, particularly the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints, resulting in weak pinch grip, joint pain, and reduced ability to generate finger flexor force. Built-up handles reduce the pinch force required to hold an object by increasing the effective contact area and the friction coefficient $\mu_{skin}$, thereby transferring the load-bearing function from the inflamed finger joints to the larger, less-affected palm and wrist musculature.

**Equation (1 mark):** $F_{grip} = \dfrac{W}{2 \mu_{skin}}$

**Calculations (3 marks):** Object weight: $W = m \cdot g = 0.200 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 1.962 \text{ N}$

For a standard pen ($d = 10 \text{ mm}$, fingertip contact, $\mu \approx 0.30$):
$$F_{grip,pen} = \dfrac{1.962}{2 \times 0.30} = 3.27 \text{ N}$$

For a built-up handle ($d = 35 \text{ mm}$, full-palm contact, $\mu \approx 0.50$):
$$F_{grip,built} = \dfrac{1.962}{2 \times 0.50} = 1.962 \text{ N}$$

**Force reduction:**
$$\Delta F = 3.27 - 1.962 = 1.308 \text{ N}$$
$$\text{Percentage reduction} = \dfrac{1.308}{3.27} \times 100\% \approx 40.0\%$$

**Answer:** The built-up handle reduces required grip force by approximately **40%**, from 3.27 N to 1.962 N.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Stabilization Aids & One-Handed Devices*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a *one-handed cutting board* designed for a hemiparetic user. Sketch its features and justify each design element biomechanically.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch description (2 marks):** A rectangular cutting board (≈ 30 × 40 cm) with the following features clearly visible: (i) two or three stainless-steel or plastic **spikes/nails** in one corner, (ii) a **raised L-shaped fence** on one edge, (iii) **non-slip rubber feet** on the underside, and (iv) optional **suction-cup base** for additional stability.

**Design element justification (5 marks):**
1. **Spikes/Nails** — Mechanically hold the food (e.g., vegetable, bread, fruit) stationary. This **eliminates the need for a stabilizing hand**, which the hemiparetic user cannot provide. Biomechanically, the spikes convert the food from a *mobile* object to a *fixed* object, allowing the user to apply cutting force with a single functional hand.
2. **Raised Fence** — Provides a vertical stop against which food can be positioned and held during slicing. This acts as a *third hand* and ensures uniform slice thickness.
3. **Non-slip rubber feet** — Increase the friction coefficient between the board and the countertop, preventing the board itself from sliding during cutting. The friction force required equals $\mu N$, where $N$ includes the user's downward pressure on the food.
4. **Suction-cup base (optional)** — Provides additional normal force, raising $\mu N$ above the cutting reaction force.

This device is a **stabilization aid** within the KTU manipulation-aid triad.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A one-handed jar opener is built as a second-class lever with a handle length of 15 cm and grips a jar lid of diameter 8 cm. If a user can apply 25 N at the handle, calculate (i) the mechanical advantage and (ii) the force transmitted to the lid. Comment on whether this is sufficient to open a jar requiring 70 N to break the seal.

**Model Solution:**

**Equations (1 mark):** Second-class lever, moment equilibrium:
$$F_{in} \cdot L_{handle} = F_{out} \cdot L_{lid}$$
$$MA = \dfrac{L_{handle}}{L_{lid}}$$

**Given values (1 mark):**
- $L_{handle} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $L_{lid} = 0.04 \text{ m}$ (half of 8 cm diameter)
- $F_{in} = 25 \text{ N}$

**Calculation of MA (2 marks):**
$$MA = \dfrac{0.15}{0.04} = 3.75$$

**Calculation of $F_{out}$ (2 marks):**
$$F_{out} = 25 \text{ N} \times 3.75 = 93.75 \text{ N}$$

**Comparison and conclusion (1 mark):**
$$F_{out} = 93.75 \text{ N} > 70 \text{ N (required)}$$
The opener produces 93.75 N at the lid, which exceeds the 70 N required to break the vacuum seal. **The user can successfully open the jar** with a 25 N applied force — a 3.75× force amplification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mistake in Q.A(b) and Q.B(b):** Students often confuse the **lid diameter** with the **lid radius** when computing the moment arm. The lever arm is from the pivot to the line of action of the force, which is the **radius**, not the diameter. This single error costs 2 marks.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Low-tech manipulation aids are *non-powered* devices that modify reach, grasp, or stabilization — they require no batteries or external energy.
- **Manipulation Triad (Memorize):** Reach Aids + Grasp Aids + Stabilization Aids.
- **Three Lever Classes:**
  - **First-class (F–E–L):** Reacher, scissors, forearm crutch.
  - **Second-class (F–L–E):** Jar opener, wheelbarrow, nutcracker.
  - **Third-class (F–E–L with E between):** Angled spoon, tweezers, fishing rod.
- **Optimal Handle Diameter:** **30–35 mm** — balances pinch force reduction against wrist torque increase.
- **Reacher MA equation:** $MA = L_{trigger} / L_{reacher}$ — usually **< 1** (reach over force).
- **Jar opener MA equation:** $MA = L_{handle} / L_{lid}$ — usually **> 1** (force over reach).
- **Cylinder grip force:** $F_{grip} = W / (2 \mu_{skin})$ — built-up handles increase $\mu_{skin}$.
- **Key friction coefficient range:** Non-slip silicone mat on smooth surface: $\mu \approx 0.6$ to 0.9.
- **Standard functional reach:** 60–90 cm; reachers extend this to 80–120 cm.
- **Always state** the lever class, the units, and the percentage improvement in any numerical answer.
- **Clinical contraindications:** Built-up handles are *not* suitable for users with active wrist arthritis (increased torque = increased pain) or severe carpal tunnel syndrome.
- **KTU 2024 high-yield examples:** Reacher, dressing stick, built-up cutlery, rocker knife, universal cuff, button hook, long-handled sponge, jar opener, plate guard, non-slip mat.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation (SPEMAM)** are engineered mechatronic systems that combine electrical actuation, mechanical linkages, sensors, and intelligent control algorithms to restore or augment the upper-limb manipulative capabilities of individuals with physical disabilities (amputation, spinal cord injury, neuromuscular disorders, or stroke-induced hemiplegia). These devices operate as **external (extracorporeal) or implanted (prosthetic/orthotic) manipulators** governed by biosignal-driven or residual-effort control strategies.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic spans the **manipulator end of the human-assist device spectrum** — distinguishing it from mobility aids (wheelchairs) and sensory aids (CIs, retinal implants). The key design pillars are:

1. **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** matching the natural kinematic chain of the human arm (shoulder: 3 DOF, elbow: 1 DOF, wrist: 2 DOF, hand: ~20 DOF).
2. **Actuation** using DC servomotors, BLDC motors, or pneumatic artificial muscles (PAMs).
3. **Intention detection** via surface EMG (sEMG), EEG (BCI), sip-and-puff, head-tracking, or residual joint motion.
4. **End-effector design** ranging from simple grippers to anthropomorphic multi-fingered hands.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes the difference between **functional manipulators** (restore specific tasks) and **general-purpose manipulators** (autonomous activity-of-daily-living execution).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mechanical Shadow"

Imagine your arm as a **conductor leading an orchestra** — the brain issues nuanced commands, and muscles execute them. For a person with a missing or paralyzed arm, this conductor exists but the orchestra is gone. A **Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aid** is like an **electronic shadow of the missing arm**: it has motors instead of muscles, gears instead of tendons, sensors instead of nerves, and a microcontroller instead of a spinal cord. The user's intent (a tiny electrical whisper from a remaining muscle) is amplified into a powerful, coordinated mechanical movement.

A simpler analogy: a **power steering system in a car** — the driver's small steering input is amplified by hydraulics/electrics. Similarly, a weak EMG signal from a residual limb is amplified into a full grip force in a myoelectric hand.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Favorite Terms)

- **Payload capacity:** typically **1.5 kg to 5 kg** for ADL-focused arms.
- **Reach envelope:** **0.5 m to 1.2 m** for desktop-mounted manipulators.
- **Grip force:** **10 N to 100 N** for prosthetic hands.
- **Response latency:** **< 300 ms** for real-time usability.
- **Autonomy (battery):** **8 to 16 hours** of intermittent use.
- **Weight of end-effector:** **< 0.5 kg** (preferred for prosthetics).
- **Backdrivability / compliance:** critical for safety during human contact.

### 1.4 Classification Overview

| Class | Example | User Group |
|---|---|---|
| **Prosthetic Hand/Arm** | i-Limb, Bebionic, Michelangelo | Transradial/transhumeral amputees |
| **Powered Orthosis / Exoskeleton** | Armeo Power, MyoPro | Stroke, spinal cord injury |
| **Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arm (WMRA)** | Manus ARM, JACO, iARM | High-level SCI (C1–C4) |
| **FES-Based Manipulator** | Freehand, NESS H200 | C5–C6 SCI with intact lower motoneurons |
| **Sip-and-Puff / Head-Controlled Arm** | QualiWORLD, ENVIRO-US | Locked-in / high tetraplegia |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Five Logical Subsystems

Every SPEMAM system, irrespective of type, decomposes into these five functional blocks:

1. **Intent Acquisition Layer (Sensors)**
   - Surface EMG electrodes (sEMG) — detect motor unit action potentials.
   - EEG caps (BCI) — detect motor imagery (mu-rhythm desynchronization).
   - Force-sensitive resistors (FSR) on a body-driven harness.
   - Sip-and-puff pressure transducers (for ventilator-dependent users).
   - Inertial Measurement Units (IMU) for head/shoulder tilt.

2. **Signal Conditioning & Feature Extraction**
   - Bandpass filtering (sEMG: **20 Hz – 500 Hz**; EEG: **0.5 Hz – 30 Hz**).
   - Rectification → smoothing → RMS or MAV (Mean Absolute Value) computation.
   - Threshold-based or pattern-recognition classification (LDA, SVM, ANN).

3. **Controller (Microcontroller / DSP / FPGA)**
   - Implements the **proportional–integral–derivative (PID)** or **model-based impedance controller**.
   - Translates classified intent into joint-space reference trajectories.
   - Runs safety interlocks (force limits, thermal cutoff, emergency stop).

4. **Actuation Layer**
   - DC/BLDC motors with harmonic-drive or planetary gearheads.
   - Pneumatic Artificial Muscles (PAMs) — McKibben type.
   - Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) wires for finger flexion.
   - Series Elastic Actuators (SEA) for compliant interaction.

5. **End-Effector (Manipulator)**
   - 1-DOF gripper (hook) for simple grasp.
   - 2-DOF pincer (thumb + index).
   - Multi-DOF anthropomorphic hand (5 fingers, ~6 active DOF).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why five blocks?** This is the **KTU-recommended "Sense → Think → Act"** pedagogical framework, identical to the mechatronics V-model taught in EST130/EST102.

### 2.2 Kinematic Foundation — The Arm as a Chain

The human arm is modeled as a **serial kinematic chain**. Using **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters**, the end-effector pose is computed via successive homogeneous transformations.

**Forward Kinematics (FK) — General Form:**

$$
T_{n}^{0}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}^{i-1}(\theta_i)
$$

where each link transform is:

$$
A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Inverse Kinematics (IK)** for a 2-DOF planar reach (shoulder + elbow) reduces to the **law of cosines** for the elbow angle:

$$
\theta_{elbow} = \pi - \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L_1^{2} + L_2^{2} - r^{2}}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)
$$

with $r = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}$ and $\theta_{shoulder} = \tan^{-1}(y/x) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L_2 \sin\theta_{elbow}}{L_1 + L_2\cos\theta_{elbow}}\right)$.

### 2.3 Control Loop — Impedance / Admittance Formulation

Modern assistive arms use **impedance control** (Hogan, 1985) to regulate the dynamic relationship between force and position, essential when the arm contacts humans or fragile objects.

$$
M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_d) = F_{ext}
$$

- $M$ → virtual inertia.
- $B$ → virtual damping.
- $K$ → virtual stiffness.
- $F_{ext}$ → external contact force measured by a force/torque sensor at the wrist.

By tuning $K$, the arm can be made **stiff** (precise positioning) or **compliant** (safe interaction).

### 2.4 Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) — Muscle as the Actuator

When the natural neuromuscular pathway is intact below the lesion (e.g., C5–C6 SCI), FES activates the user's *own* muscles to produce grasp/release. The current–recruitment relationship is:

$$
I(t) = I_{th} + k \cdot \text{RMS}_{EMG}(t) + I_{ramp}(t)
$$

- $I_{th}$ → threshold current to recruit the first motor unit (typically **3–8 mA**).
- $k$ → gain mapping voluntary effort to stimulation amplitude.
- $I_{ramp}$ → slow ramp (**ramp time ≈ 2–5 s**) to prevent spasm and fatigue.

Pulse parameters: **frequency 20–50 Hz, pulse width 200–400 µs, biphasic balanced charge**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** Students must know the difference between **open-loop FES** (no feedback, prone to fatigue) and **closed-loop FES** (uses EMG or angle feedback to adapt stimulation). Closed-loop is the exam-favored answer.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Equation / Expression | Units / Typical Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Kinematics | $T_n^0 = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i^{i-1}(\theta_i)$ | dimensionless 4×4 matrix | End-effector pose from joint angles |
| Elbow IK (2-link) | $\theta_e = \pi - \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L_1^{2}+L_2^{2}-r^{2}}{2L_1L_2}\right)$ | radians | Planar reach planning |
| Impedance Control | $M\ddot{x}+B\dot{x}+K(x-x_d)=F_{ext}$ | N, N·s/m, N/m | Safe human–robot contact |
| sEMG RMS | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^{2}}$ | µV (typically 50–500 µV) | Feature extraction for classification |
| Grip Force / Current | $F_{grip} = \alpha I - \beta$ | N vs. mA (linear regime) | Prosthetic hand control |
| FES Charge / Phase | $Q = I \cdot PW$ | µC (must balance to < 20 µC) | Prevent electrochemical tissue damage |
| Battery Endurance | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Wh | Design choice for portable arms |
| Series Elastic Force | $F = K_{SEA}(x_m - x_l)$ | N | SEA torque sensing |
| Workspace Volume | $V_w = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\int_{0}^{\pi}\!\int_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}} r^{2}\sin\phi\, dr\, d\phi\, d\theta$ | m³ | Reach envelope metric |
| Power Consumption | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W | Joint actuation sizing |

> **Units Convention:** All angles in **radians** unless specified; distances in **meters**; forces in **Newtons**; torques in **N·m**; currents in **amperes**; frequencies in **Hz**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Two-Link Planar Inverse Kinematics for a Wheelchair-Mounted Arm

**Problem Setup:** A WMRA has a shoulder joint $S$ (origin) and an elbow joint $E$. Upper-arm length $L_1$, forearm length $L_2$. The end-effector is commanded to reach point $P(x, y)$ in the shoulder frame.

**Step 1 — Compute the radial distance $r$ from shoulder to target.**

$$
r = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}
$$

This is the straight-line distance. The arm must satisfy the **reachability constraint**:

$$
r \leq L_1 + L_2 \quad \text{(reachable)}, \qquad r \geq \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \quad \text{(workspace boundary)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Law of Cosines to triangle $S$–$E$–$P$.**

The side opposite to the elbow angle $\theta_2$ (interior angle) is $r$. So:

$$
r^{2} = L_1^{2} + L_2^{2} - 2L_1L_2 \cos(\pi - \theta_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Use the trigonometric identity $\cos(\pi - \theta_2) = -\cos\theta_2$.**

$$
r^{2} = L_1^{2} + L_2^{2} + 2L_1L_2\cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\cos\theta_2$ and hence $\theta_2$ (elbow joint).**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{r^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2L_1L_2}
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{r^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2L_1L_2}\right)
$$

This is the **"elbow-up" configuration**. For elbow-down, $\theta_2 \to -\theta_2$.

**Step 5 — Compute the shoulder angle $\theta_1$.**

Using the geometry of the triangle, the angle from the x-axis to the upper arm is:

$$
\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L_2\sin\theta_2}{L_1 + L_2\cos\theta_2}\right)
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style):** Let $L_1 = 0.35$ m (upper arm), $L_2 = 0.30$ m (forearm). Target $P(0.40, 0.20)$ m.

$$
r = \sqrt{0.40^{2} + 0.20^{2}} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.20} \approx 0.4472 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{0.20 - 0.35^{2} - 0.30^{2}}{2 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 0.30} = \frac{0.20 - 0.1225 - 0.09}{0.21} = \frac{-0.0125}{0.21} \approx -0.0595
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}(-0.0595) \approx 93.41^{\circ} \approx 1.631 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.20}{0.40}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.30 \cdot \sin(93.41^{\circ})}{0.35 + 0.30 \cdot \cos(93.41^{\circ})}\right)
$$

$$
= 26.57^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.30 \cdot 0.9982}{0.35 + 0.30 \cdot (-0.0595)}\right) = 26.57^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2995}{0.3322}\right)
$$

$$
= 26.57^{\circ} - 42.03^{\circ} = -15.46^{\circ} \approx -0.270 \text{ rad}
$$

**Verification (forward kinematics check):**

$$
x_{computed} = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 0.35\cos(-15.46^{\circ}) + 0.30\cos(77.95^{\circ}) \approx 0.337 + 0.063 \approx 0.400 \text{ m} \;\checkmark
$$

$$
y_{computed} = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 0.35(-0.267) + 0.30(0.978) \approx -0.093 + 0.293 \approx 0.200 \text{ m} \;\checkmark
$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — sEMG-Driven Grip Force Mapping for a Myoelectric Hand

**Step 1 — Acquire sEMG** from two antagonistic muscle sites (e.g., flexor digitorum superficialis and extensor digitorum communis) at 1 kHz sampling.

**Step 2 — Compute the RMS feature** in a sliding window of length $N = 150$ samples (150 ms).

$$
\text{RMS}[k] = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=k-N+1}^{k} v_i^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear mapping to grip force.** Below threshold $T_{th}$, the hand is idle. Above, the grip force follows:

$$
F_{grip} = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{RMS} < T_{th} \\ \alpha \cdot (\text{RMS} - T_{th}) + F_{min}, & \text{RMS} \geq T_{th} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Anti-phase differential control.** The opposing muscle's RMS subtracts to provide proportional opening:

$$
F_{open} = \beta \cdot \text{RMS}_{extensor}, \qquad F_{grip}^{net} = F_{grip} - F_{open}
$$

This is the **classic Otto-bock / Touch Bionics single-site dual-control scheme** that KTU examiners love.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Workspace Volume of a 2-DOF Spherical Arm

For a shoulder providing azimuth $\theta_s \in [0, 2\pi)$ and elevation $\phi_s \in [0, \pi/2]$, with arm length $L$, the reachable workspace volume is:

$$
V_w = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\!\int_{0}^{L} r^{2}\sin\phi\, dr\, d\phi\, d\theta
$$

$$
= \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin\phi\, d\phi \cdot \int_{0}^{L} r^{2}\, dr
$$

$$
= (2\pi) \cdot [-\cos\phi]_{0}^{\pi/2} \cdot \left[\frac{r^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{L}
$$

$$
= 2\pi \cdot (0 - (-1)) \cdot \frac{L^{3}}{3} = \frac{2\pi L^{3}}{3}
$$

For $L = 0.6$ m:

$$
V_w = \frac{2\pi (0.6)^{3}}{3} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.216}{3} \approx 0.452 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

This sets a **quantitative benchmark** for evaluating whether a WMRA can reach a table, a cup, a doorknob, etc.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Manipulation Aid

| User Condition | Recommended Class | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Transradial amputation (below elbow) | Myoelectric hand | Intact proximal muscles; sEMG is reliable |
| Transhumeral amputation (above elbow) | Myoelectric arm + socket | Need shoulder + elbow actuators |
| C5–C6 SCI (intact shoulder, biceps weak) | FES hand (e.g., Freehand) | Natural muscles still excitable |
| C1–C4 SCI (no voluntary upper-limb control) | WMRA with sip-and-puff / head control | External arm, indirect control |
| Stroke with residual EMG | Powered exoskeleton (Armeo) | Augments existing motion |
| Locked-in syndrome | BCI-driven arm or sip-and-puff arm | Only eye/breath signals available |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between a body-powered and a myoelectric prosthetic hand. Mention two advantages and one limitation of the latter.*

**Model Answer:**
A **body-powered** prosthetic hand uses a cable-and-harness system driven by the user's shoulder/scapular motion; it has no external power source. A **myoelectric** hand uses **surface EMG signals** from residual muscles to control DC motors inside the hand. *Advantages of myoelectric:* (i) Higher grip force (~100 N) without body strain; (ii) More natural appearance and proportional control. *Limitation:* Heavier, requires battery charging (8–16 h), and more expensive (~₹ 5–15 lakh). **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES)? List any two upper-limb FES systems commercially available.*

**Model Answer:**
**FES** is a technique that uses low-level electrical currents (typically 20–50 Hz, 200–400 µs pulse width) to activate paralyzed muscles whose lower motor neurons are intact, restoring functional movement such as grasp/release. *Two commercial systems:* (i) **Freehand System** (NeuroControl Corp.) for C5–C6 SCI; (ii) **NESS H200 / Bioness** wireless hand stimulator. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a myoelectric prosthetic hand**. Explain the role of sEMG signal acquisition, feature extraction, and classification in determining hand open/close commands. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-link planar manipulator (upper arm $L_1 = 0.30$ m, forearm $L_2 = 0.25$ m) is mounted on a wheelchair. The end-effector must reach a cup located at coordinates $(0.35, 0.15)$ m in the shoulder frame. Calculate the **shoulder and elbow joint angles** required for the elbow-up configuration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Working:**

- **sEMG Acquisition:** Two electrode pairs placed over the **flexor digitorum superficialis** (closes hand) and **extensor digitorum communis** (opens hand) of the residual limb capture motor-unit action potentials (amplitude 50–500 µV, bandwidth 20–500 Hz).
- **Signal Conditioning:** Instrumentation amplifier (gain ≈ 1000), 50 Hz notch filter, bandpass filter.
- **ADC & Feature Extraction:** 12-bit ADC at 1 kHz; compute **Root Mean Square (RMS)** or **Mean Absolute Value (MAV)** in 150 ms sliding windows.
- **Classification:** Threshold-based on-off control (single-site) or **LDA / SVM / ANN** for multi-class grasp patterns (lateral, pinch, power).
- **Motor Command:** PWM signal drives a 6 V / 12 V DC motor geared 100:1; cable transmits force to fingers.
- **Sensors:** FSR on fingertips detect slip; closed-loop adjusts grip force.

*Key formula:* $F_{grip} = \alpha(\text{RMS} - T_{th}) + F_{min}$. **(7 marks — 1 for block diagram, 2 for sEMG path, 2 for feature/class, 2 for motor/sensor path)**

**(b) IK Calculation:**

Given: $L_1 = 0.30$ m, $L_2 = 0.25$ m, target $(0.35, 0.15)$ m.

Step 1: Compute radial distance.

$$
r = \sqrt{0.35^{2} + 0.15^{2}} = \sqrt{0.1225 + 0.0225} = \sqrt{0.1450} \approx 0.3808 \text{ m}
$$

Step 2: Check reachability. $L_1 + L_2 = 0.55$ m $\geq 0.3808$ m ✓. $|L_1 - L_2| = 0.05$ m $\leq 0.3808$ ✓.

Step 3: Elbow angle.

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{r^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2L_1L_2} = \frac{0.1450 - 0.09 - 0.0625}{0.15} = \frac{-0.0075}{0.15} = -0.05
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}(-0.05) \approx 92.87^{\circ} \approx 1.621 \text{ rad (elbow-up)}
$$

Step 4: Shoulder angle.

$$
\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.15}{0.35}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.25 \sin(92.87^{\circ})}{0.30 + 0.25\cos(92.87^{\circ})}\right)
$$

$$
= 23.20^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.25 \cdot 0.9988}{0.30 + 0.25 \cdot (-0.05)}\right) = 23.20^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2497}{0.2875}\right)
$$

$$
= 23.20^{\circ} - 40.97^{\circ} = -17.77^{\circ} \approx -0.310 \text{ rad}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_{shoulder} \approx -17.77^{\circ}$, $\theta_{elbow} \approx 92.87^{\circ}$. **(7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for elbow angle, 2 for shoulder angle, 1 for verification)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **concept of impedance control** in assistive robotic arms. Why is it preferred over pure position control when the arm interacts with humans? Derive the governing equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheelchair-mounted robotic arm uses **sip-and-puff** control. The hard puff (HP) command selects a mode, soft puff (SP) cycles, and sip (S) executes. The user issues the sequence **HP → SP → SP → S** to grasp a glass. List the functional state transitions and state the **safety interlocks** that must be implemented in such a system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Impedance Control:**

Pure **position control** forces the end-effector to track a trajectory rigidly, which is **unsafe** for human contact (rigid collision → injury). **Impedance control** (Hogan, 1985) regulates the *dynamic relationship* between force and position, making the arm behave like a **mass–spring–damper**.

The governing equation (in 1-DOF for clarity):

$$
M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_{d}) = F_{ext}
$$

where $M$ is the virtual mass, $B$ the virtual damping, $K$ the virtual stiffness, $x$ the actual position, $x_d$ the desired position, and $F_{ext}$ the external contact force.

- If the user wants a **stiff** arm (precise insertion), $K$ is increased.
- If the user wants a **compliant** arm (safe around a person), $K$ is reduced and $B$ is increased (smoother response).

The wrist-mounted **6-axis force/torque sensor** measures $F_{ext}$, and the controller computes the corrective joint torque $\tau = J^{T}F_{ext}$ (where $J$ is the Jacobian). **(7 marks — 2 for concept, 3 for derivation, 2 for safety justification)**

**(b) Sip-and-Puff State Machine:**

Sequence: HP → SP → SP → S.

State transitions:

| Step | Input | Action | New State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HP (hard puff, > 25 cmH₂O, 0.5 s) | Mode-select: enter **Grasp** mode | STANDBY → MODE_GRASP |
| 2 | SP (soft puff, 5–10 cmH₂O) | Cycle through gripper options: Pinch | MODE_GRASP → PINCH_READY |
| 3 | SP (soft puff) | Cycle again: Power grip | PINCH_READY → POWER_READY |
| 4 | S (sip, negative pressure) | **EXECUTE**: close gripper with proportional force | POWER_READY → CLOSING → HELD |

**Safety Interlocks (mandatory):**

1. **Force cap:** If FSR reads > 30 N on a glass, freeze motor and reverse direction.
2. **Thermal shutdown:** Motor driver temperature > 85 °C → cut PWM, beep alert.
3. **Emergency stop:** Triple-sip within 2 s → cut all power to motors.
4. **Slip detection:** Sudden drop in force > 50 % within 100 ms → re-grasp.
5. **Puff-pressure watchdog:** Continuous puff > 3 s → ignore input (prevent stuck button).
6. **Battery low cutoff:** V < 10.5 V (for 12 V pack) → disable arm, retain wheelchair drive. **(7 marks — 3 for state machine, 4 for interlocks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Writing `cos(π − θ)` instead of `−cos(θ)`** in IK derivations costs 1 mark. The negative sign is *load-bearing*.
> 2. **Confusing sEMG (surface, non-invasive) with intramuscular EMG (needle)**. For prosthetics, the syllabus means **sEMG**. Mixing them = −1 mark.
> 3. **Stating FES pulse width as 2–5 s** — that's the *ramp time*, not the pulse width. Pulse width is **200–400 µs**. Examiners catch this.
> 4. **Omitting the Jacobian in impedance control** — without $\tau = J^{T}F_{ext}$, the answer is incomplete (−1 mark).
> 5. **Writing "|x|" with vertical pipes in KTU answer scripts** — use "mod x" or write "absolute value of x" to avoid formula-parser issues in digital valuation.
> 6. **Forgetting units** on numerical answers: $\theta$ in rad vs. degrees. State both.
> 7. **Drawing a generic "robot" instead of a labeled block diagram** with sensor → controller → actuator → feedback loop. Generic diagrams = 0 marks for that sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Five-block architecture** is universal: Sensor → Conditioner → Classifier → Controller → Actuator → Feedback.
- [x] **Myoelectric** = sEMG-driven; **Body-powered** = cable-driven; **FES** = stimulates the user's own muscles; **WMRA** = external robotic arm.
- [x] **sEMG bandwidth:** 20 Hz – 500 Hz; **sampling rate:** ≥ 1 kHz; **feature:** RMS or MAV in 150 ms windows.
- [x] **DH parameters** define a serial chain; **FK** gives end-effector pose, **IK** gives joint angles for a target pose.
- [x] **Impedance control** $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_d) = F_{ext}$ is the gold standard for human-safe interaction.
- [x] **FES parameters:** 20–50 Hz, 200–400 µs, biphasic, charge-balanced; threshold current 3–8 mA; ramp 2–5 s.
- [x] **Sip-and-puff** uses pressure transducers; HP = mode, SP = cycle, S = execute; triple-sip = E-stop.
- [x] **Workspace volume** of a 2-DOF spherical arm: $V_w = \frac{2\pi L^{3}}{3}$ (hemisphere).
- [x] **Elbow-up IK:** $\cos\theta_2 = \frac{r^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2L_1L_2}$; mind the **negative sign** inside the cosine.
- [x] **Reachability:** $|L_1 - L_2| \leq r \leq L_1 + L_2$ — always verify before computing IK.
- [x] **Prosthetic hand metrics:** grip force 10–100 N, latency < 300 ms, weight < 0.5 kg, autonomy 8–16 h.
- [x] **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** add a spring between motor and load — improves force control and shock tolerance.
- [x] **Compliance vs. stiffness** is tunable in impedance control — choose compliance when humans are nearby.
- [x] **Closed-loop FES** (with EMG/angle feedback) reduces muscle fatigue — preferred in KTU 2024 answers over open-loop.
- [x] **Pattern recognition classifiers** (LDA, SVM, ANN) replace simple thresholding for multi-grasp myoelectric hands.
- [x] **Safety first:** force cap, thermal cut-off, slip detection, E-stop, battery low cutoff — name at least 3 in any 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Aids to Daily Living
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic Aids to Daily Living

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADLs)** are electronic devices, systems, and integrated platforms that enable individuals with disabilities, chronic illnesses, or age-related impairments to perform **Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)** and **Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADLs)** with maximum independence, safety, and dignity.

In strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, EADLs are classified under the broader domain of **Assistive Technology (AT)** as per the **WHO ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** framework. They bridge the gap between the user's residual functional capacity and the demands of the physical/social environment by translating minimal voluntary physical output (a sip, a blink, a breath, a single switch press) into complex control commands for household appliances, communication devices, mobility systems, and entertainment units.

**Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)** — Basic self-care tasks: feeding, dressing, grooming, toileting, bathing, ambulation.

**Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADLs)** — Complex environmental interaction tasks: cooking, shopping, telephone use, money management, medication management, housekeeping.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Universal Remote for the Body" Analogy**
> Imagine your entire home — lights, fan, TV, telephone, door lock, curtains — is a collection of independent devices, each with its own remote control, each requiring you to physically walk over, press a button, or speak into a microphone. For a person with a spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, ALS, or advanced muscular dystrophy, every one of these micro-tasks becomes a wall.
> An **EADL system is essentially a "Universal Translator + Universal Remote"** that:
> 1. **Listens** to whatever tiny, reliable signal the user's body can still produce (one finger, a sip-puff, an eye blink, a voice whisper, a head turn).
> 2. **Translates** that signal into a standard digital command.
> 3. **Relays** the command via wired/wireless link to the target appliance.
> 4. **Confirms** the action back to the user via auditory, visual, or tactile feedback.
> 
> In short: **EADLs convert "I intend to..." into "The device does..."** without requiring the user to physically reach, grasp, or manipulate the device.

### 1.3 Core Classification of EADLs (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

| **S.No** | **Category** | **Primary User Group** | **Example Devices** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Environmental Control Units (ECU) | SCI, ALS, CP, Muscular Dystrophy | X-10 based home automation, mains controllers |
| 2 | Augmentative & Alternative Communication (AAC) | Aphasia, Autism, Laryngectomy | Speech-generating devices (SGD), text-to-speech tablets |
| 3 | Electronic Aids for the Visually Impaired (EAVI) | Blindness, Low Vision | Screen readers, refreshable Braille displays, OCR scanners |
| 4 | Electronic Aids for the Hearing Impaired (EAHI) | Deafness, Hard of Hearing | Digital hearing aids, cochlear implants, alerting devices |
| 5 | Cognitive / Prompting Aids | Traumatic Brain Injury, Dementia, Intellectual Disability | Memo devices, scheduling apps, GPS wayfinders |
| 6 | Mobility-Related Electronic Aids | SCI, Amputation, MS | Power wheelchair controllers, sip-and-puff wheelchairs |
| 7 | Computer Access Aids | Upper-limb impairment, RSI | Adaptive keyboards, eye-trackers, head pointers, sip-puff mice |
| 8 | Robotic Daily Living Aids | Severe motor impairment | Robotic arms (JACO, iARM), feeding robots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics Used in EADL Design (must know for KTU):**
> - **Activation Force:** typically **0.2 N to 2 N** for hand-operated switches.
> - **Activation Travel:** **0.5 mm to 5 mm** for ultra-light switches.
> - **Latency (Response Time):** **< 200 ms** for safety-critical tasks (door locks, call systems); **< 500 ms** acceptable for entertainment.
> - **Scanning Rate:** **0.5 Hz to 5 Hz** for single-switch row/column scanning.
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** target **> 20 dB** for voice-controlled ECUs in typical room noise.
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** **> 10,000 hours** for fixed ECUs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic EADL System

Every EADL, irrespective of its end application, can be decomposed into **five universal functional blocks**. Understanding this pipeline is a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark question** area.

| **Block No.** | **Block Name** | **Function** | **Example Implementations** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Input Transducer (Sensor)** | Captures the user's residual voluntary signal | Mechanical switch, sip-puff pressure sensor, EMG electrode, EOG electrode, microphone, camera |
| 2 | **Signal Conditioning & Processing** | Amplifies, filters, digitizes, and classifies the signal | Instrumentation amplifier, bandpass filter, ADC, microcontroller, DSP |
| 3 | **Command Decoder / Translator** | Maps the processed signal to a specific device command | Lookup table, finite-state machine, neural-network classifier (for voice/gesture) |
| 4 | **Output / Transmission Stage** | Delivers the command to the target appliance | X-10 power-line carrier, IR transmitter, Zigbee/Bluetooth/WiFi radio, relay driver |
| 5 | **Feedback Stage** | Confirms action to user | LED, buzzer, voice prompt, refreshable Braille, haptic vibration |

### 2.2 Detailed Sub-System Theory

#### A. Environmental Control Units (ECU)

An ECU is the **flagship EADL**. It allows a single user-input signal to control multiple household appliances through a centralized controller.

**Operating Principle — The X-10 Power-Line Carrier (PLC) Protocol:**
The X-10 protocol (industry-standard, frequently asked) superimposes a **120 kHz** burst (representing binary data) onto the standard **50 Hz / 60 Hz** AC mains wiring. Each appliance module has a unique **House Code (A–P)** and **Unit Code (1–16)**. Commands are transmitted as a sequence of 11-bit binary frames.

The transmission equation for the data rate is:

$$R_{b} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{bit}}} = \frac{1}{T_{50/60}} = f_{\text{mains}}$$

Where $R_b$ is the effective binary data rate limited by the mains zero-crossings, $T_{\text{bit}}$ is the bit duration, and $f_{\text{mains}}$ is **50 Hz (India/Europe)** or **60 Hz (USA)**. Hence, X-10 transmits data at a maximum of **50 bits/second** — extremely slow but adequate for simple ON/OFF commands.

#### B. Single-Switch Scanning

For users who can operate **only one switch** (e.g., one finger, one sip, one blink), the EADL employs a **scanning technique** to sequentially highlight options until the user activates the switch at the desired option.

**Types of Scanning:**

| **Scanning Type** | **Pattern** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Linear | Row-by-row, left-to-right | Simple AAC devices |
| Row-Column | Highlight row first, then column | Telephone dialing, TV channel selection |
| Block | Group of options → individual option | Large menus, wheelchair driving |
| Auditory | Options spoken aloud via TTS | Blind users |

**Scanning Cycle Time:**

$$T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot T_{\text{dwell}}$$

Where $N$ is the number of options and $T_{\text{dwell}}$ is the dwell time per option. KTU-mandated $T_{\text{dwell}}$: **1 s to 2 s** for motor-impaired users, **0.5 s** for cognitively able users.

#### C. Voice-Activated ECUs

These rely on **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)**. Modern systems use **Hidden Markov Models (HMM)** or **Deep Neural Networks (DNN)** for phoneme classification. Key design parameters:

- **Word Error Rate (WER):** target **< 5%** in quiet, **< 15%** in noise.
- **Speaker-Dependent vs Speaker-Independent:** EADLs are usually **speaker-dependent** (trained on user's voice) for higher accuracy in noisy home environments.
- **Robustness:** Uses **spectral subtraction** or **Wiener filtering** for noise reduction.

The fundamental SNR equation governing ASR accuracy:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$

#### D. Electronic Aids for the Visually Impaired (EAVI)

Three primary technology pillars:

1. **Optical Character Recognition (OCR) Pipeline:**
   Image capture → Pre-processing (binarization, deskew) → Character segmentation → Feature extraction → Classification (Tesseract OCR, CNN-based) → Text-to-Speech (TTS) output.

2. **Refreshable Braille Displays:**
   A linear array of **6-dot or 8-dot piezoelectric Braille cells**. Each cell is raised/lowered by a piezo actuator. The pin rise time is given by:

$$t_{\text{rise}} = \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{\frac{L \cdot m}{k}}$$

Where $L$ is the inductance, $m$ is the moving mass of the pin, and $k$ is the piezo stiffness. KTU-typical: $t_{\text{rise}} <$ **30 ms** for natural reading speed.

3. **Electronic Travel Aids (ETA):**
   - **Ultrasonic cane (Sonic Pathfinder / Miniguide):** uses pulse-echo time-of-flight.

$$d = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$$

where $v_{\text{sound}} \approx$ **343 m/s** at 20 °C, $t_{\text{echo}}$ is round-trip time. Typical range: **0.5 m to 8 m**.

#### E. Electronic Aids for the Hearing Impaired (EAHI)

1. **Digital Hearing Aids:** Microphone → ADC → DSP (multi-band compression, noise reduction, feedback cancellation) → DAC → Receiver (miniature speaker). Key performance parameter: **gain (dB SPL input to dB SPL output)**.
2. **Cochlear Implants:** Bypass the damaged hair cells; electrodes directly stimulate the auditory nerve. The stimulus current amplitude is:

$$I_{\text{stim}}(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f_{\text{carrier}} t)$$

where $A$ is the safe-charge amplitude (typically **100 µA to 1 mA**) and $f_{\text{carrier}}$ is the pulse-repetition rate (typically **500 Hz to 5000 Hz**).

3. **Alerting Devices:** Use **flashing lights, bed-shakers, and vibrating pagers** as substitutes for auditory signals (doorbell, smoke alarm, baby cry).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for EADL Module (Module 3)**

| **S.No** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Application** | **Typical Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot T_{\text{dwell}}$ | Total scanning time for single-switch AAC | seconds |
| 2 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | Voice-ECU noise immunity | dB |
| 3 | $d = v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}} / 2$ | Ultrasonic ETA obstacle distance | metres |
| 4 | $v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound vs temperature | m/s, °C |
| 5 | $R_b = f_{\text{mains}}$ | X-10 PLC data rate (limited by AC) | bits/second |
| 6 | $I_{\text{stim}}(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$ | Cochlear implant biphasic pulse | µA, Hz |
| 7 | $t_{\text{rise}} = (\pi/2)\sqrt{Lm/k}$ | Braille display piezo pin rise time | seconds |
| 8 | $P_{\text{acoustic}} = I \cdot A$ | Hearing aid acoustic output power | Watts |
| 9 | $G_{\text{HA}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{out}}/V_{\text{in}})$ | Hearing aid voltage gain | dB |
| 10 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Nyquist criterion for hearing aid ADC | Hz |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart-Home + Healthcare Convergence:** Modern EADLs are integrated into **IoT-based smart homes** (Google Home, Amazon Alexa paired with smart plugs), giving motor-impaired users voice-driven control of lights, AC, and appliances.
- **Telerehabilitation:** EADLs now upload usage data to cloud servers, allowing remote occupational therapists to monitor patient independence levels.
- **Insurance & Disability Classification:** Countries use EADL prescription as an objective metric for **ADL disability scoring** in disability certification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Derivation — Single-Switch Row-Column Scanning for a 16-Option AAC Device

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**. We will fully work it out.

**Problem Setup:** A user with quadriplegia can activate only **one** switch. The EADL has **16** commands arranged as a **4 × 4 matrix** (TV on/off, channel up/down, volume up/down, etc.). The system uses **row-column scanning**. Derive the average selection time and optimize the dwell time.

**Step 1 — Define the problem variables.**

Let $N_r$ = number of rows = 4, $N_c$ = number of columns = 4, $N = N_r \times N_c = 16$ total options, and $T_d$ = dwell time per option (seconds).

**Step 2 — Row scanning phase.**

The scanner first highlights **Row 1**, then Row 2, Row 3, Row 4, in a cyclic manner. The time to reach Row $i$ depends on its position.

**Step 3 — Probability assumption (uniform random access).**

Assume the user wants any of the 16 options with equal probability. So the probability of the desired row being row $i$ is:

$$P(R_i) = \frac{N_c}{N} = \frac{4}{16} = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Average row scanning time.**

The expected number of row dwells before hit is the average of $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$:

$$E[\text{row dwells}] = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4}{4} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5$$

Hence the **average row scanning time**:

$$T_{\text{row, avg}} = 2.5 \cdot T_d$$

**Step 5 — Column scanning phase (after the row is hit).**

Within the selected row, 4 columns are scanned. The expected number of column dwells is also 2.5. So:

$$T_{\text{col, avg}} = 2.5 \cdot T_d$$

**Step 6 — Total average selection time.**

$$T_{\text{select, avg}} = T_{\text{row, avg}} + T_{\text{col, avg}} = 2.5\,T_d + 2.5\,T_d = 5\,T_d$$

**Step 7 — Plug in KTU-standard dwell time.**

For a motor-impaired user, take $T_d =$ **2 s** (safe, low-fatigue dwell).

$$T_{\text{select, avg}} = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 8 — Throughput.**

Number of selections per minute:

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{60 \text{ s/min}}{T_{\text{select, avg}}} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 \text{ selections/minute}$$

**Step 9 — Optimization insight.**

To reduce $T_{\text{select, avg}}$, one can use **adaptive scanning** — start with slow dwell, then accelerate after first switch-hits. This brings the effective $T_d$ down to ~**1 s**, doubling throughput to **12 selections/minute**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final analytical result (for answer-script writing):**
> $$T_{\text{select, avg}} = \left(\frac{N_r + 1}{2} + \frac{N_c + 1}{2}\right) \cdot T_d = \frac{N_r + N_c + 2}{2} \cdot T_d$$
> For $N_r = N_c = 4$, $T_d =$ 2 s, this yields **10 s** per selection.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Analytical Derivation — Ultrasonic ETA Distance and Safety Limit

**Problem:** A blind user uses an ultrasonic ETA with $f_{\text{ultrasonic}} =$ **40 kHz** transducer. Derive the maximum measurable distance, the minimum measurable distance, and the theoretical angular resolution.

**Step 1 — Speed of sound in air at room temperature (20 °C):**

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 20 = 343 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum measurable distance (limited by echo attenuation and pulse repetition).**

For typical piezoelectric 40 kHz transducers, useful echo amplitude exists up to ~**8 m**.

$$d_{\max} \approx 8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum measurable distance (limited by transducer ring-down time).**

After transmit, the transducer rings for $t_{\text{ring}} \approx$ **1 ms**. The blind zone:

$$d_{\min} = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{ring}}}{2} = \frac{343 \times 0.001}{2} \approx 0.17 \text{ m} \approx 17 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Time-of-flight for a 2 m obstacle:**

$$t_{\text{echo}} = \frac{2 d}{v_{\text{sound}}} = \frac{2 \times 2}{343} \approx 11.66 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Spatial resolution (limited by pulse width).**

For a typical pulse width $\tau =$ **1 ms**:

$$\Delta d = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{343 \times 0.001}{2} = 0.17 \text{ m}$$

This means two obstacles closer than 17 cm cannot be resolved — a critical safety limitation.

**Step 6 — Beam divergence angle.**

For a transducer of diameter $D =$ **16 mm** at 40 kHz, the wavelength is:

$$\lambda = \frac{v_{\text{sound}}}{f} = \frac{343}{40{,}000} = 8.575 \text{ mm}$$

Divergence half-angle (Fraunhofer approximation):

$$\theta \approx 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D} = 1.22 \times \frac{8.575}{16} \approx 0.654 \text{ rad} \approx 37.5°$$

This wide beam is a problem — it cannot resolve the angular position of the obstacle. **Phased-array ETAs** (e.g., KASPA, UltraCane) reduce $\theta$ to ~**3° to 5°** by using multiple transducers with electronic beam-steering.

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix for KTU Answer Writing

| **Parameter** | **Mechanical Switch ECU** | **Voice-Activated ECU** | **Sip-Puff ECU** | **Eye-Gaze ECU** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Required residual function** | One finger / hand | Voice | Oral/mouth pressure | Eye movement |
| **Latency** | 50 ms | 500–1500 ms | 100 ms | 50–200 ms |
| **Reliability in noise** | Excellent | Poor–Moderate | Excellent | Good |
| **Cognitive load on user** | Low | Low | Low–Moderate | Moderate |
| **Cost (₹ approx)** | 5,000–15,000 | 15,000–50,000 | 20,000–60,000 | 1,50,000–5,00,000 |
| **Best suited for** | CP, MD, partial SCI | Laryngectomy, MS | High-level SCI (C3–C4) | ALS, high SCI |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic EADL Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user1[User with Disability]:::user --> input1[1 Input Transducer Switch SipPuff Microphone EOG EMG Eye Tracker]:::sensor
    input1 --> cond1[2 Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter ADC]:::process
    cond1 --> proc1[3 Microcontroller DSP Command Decoder]:::process
    proc1 --> dec1{4 Command Decoder Lookup Table}:::logic
    dec1 --> out1[4a Output Driver Relay IR LED X10 Zigbee Bluetooth WiFi]:::output
    out1 --> app1[5 Target Appliance Lights TV Fan Door Phone Wheelchair]:::appliance
    app1 --> fb1[6 Feedback Stage LED Buzzer Voice Haptic Braille]:::feedback
    fb1 --> user1
    classDef user fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000

```


### 4.3 Signal Flow of a Voice-Activated ECU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mic1[Microphone Array]:::sensor --> pre1[Preamp 40 dB]:::process
    pre1 --> adc1[ADC 16 bit 16 kHz]:::process
    adc1 --> nr1[Noise Reduction Wiener Filter]:::process
    nr1 --> fe1[Feature Extraction MFCC 13 coefficients]:::process
    fe1 --> asr1[ASR Engine HMM or DNN]:::process
    asr1 --> cmd1[Command Decoder ON OFF CH UP VOL UP]:::logic
    cmd1 --> tx1[Wireless Transmitter IR Zigbee WiFi]:::output
    tx1 --> app1[Target Appliance]:::appliance
    app1 --> spk1[Speaker Voice Prompt]:::feedback
    spk1 --> user1[User Confirmation]:::user
```
