<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the term "Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADL)". List any four categories of EADLs with one example each. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
EADLs are electronic devices/systems that enable individuals with disabilities to perform Activities of Daily Living (ADL) and Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADL) independently by translating minimal residual user input into complex control commands.

Four categories:
1. **Environmental Control Units (ECU)** — e.g., X-10 based home automation.
2. **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** — e.g., Speech Generating Device (SGD).
3. **Electronic Aids for the Visually Impaired** — e.g., Refreshable Braille display.
4. **Electronic Aids for the Hearing Impaired** — e.g., Digital hearing aid.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2. Explain the principle of single-switch row-column scanning used in EADLs. Why is dwell time critical? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
In single-switch scanning, the EADL sequentially highlights each row, then each column of an option matrix, and the user activates the switch when the desired option is highlighted. Dwell time is the duration each option is highlighted.

Dwell time is critical because:
- **Too short:** user may miss the desired option (false selections).
- **Too long:** total selection time increases → user fatigue and low throughput.
- Standard: **1–2 seconds** for motor-impaired users.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 3]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a generic Electronic Aid to Daily Living system. Explain each block in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 5 × 5 matrix single-switch scanning EADL has a dwell time of 1.5 s. Calculate (i) the average row-scanning time, (ii) the average column-scanning time, and (iii) the average total selection time. Comment on the throughput. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 Marks):**

```
[User] → [Input Transducer] → [Signal Conditioning] → [Command Decoder] → [Output Driver] → [Target Appliance] → [Feedback] → [User]
```

1. **Input Transducer:** Converts user's residual voluntary signal (finger, sip, voice, eye) into an electrical signal. Example: pressure transducer for sip-puff, microphone for voice.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifies, filters, and digitizes the signal. Includes instrumentation amplifier, bandpass filter, and ADC.
3. **Command Decoder:** Maps the processed input to a specific device command. Implemented using a microcontroller/DSP running a lookup table or a trained ML model.
4. **Output Stage / Driver:** Drives the target appliance via relay, X-10 PLC, IR LED, or wireless (Zigbee/Bluetooth/WiFi).
5. **Feedback:** Confirms action to user via LED, buzzer, voice prompt, or haptic vibration, closing the loop.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $N_r = 5$, $N_c = 5$, $T_d = 1.5$ s.

**(i) Average row scanning time:**

$$E[\text{row dwells}] = \frac{N_r + 1}{2} = \frac{5 + 1}{2} = 3$$

$$T_{\text{row, avg}} = 3 \times T_d = 3 \times 1.5 = 4.5 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Average column scanning time:**

$$E[\text{col dwells}] = \frac{N_c + 1}{2} = \frac{5 + 1}{2} = 3$$

$$T_{\text{col, avg}} = 3 \times T_d = 3 \times 1.5 = 4.5 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Average total selection time:**

$$T_{\text{select, avg}} = T_{\text{row, avg}} + T_{\text{col, avg}} = 4.5 + 4.5 = 9 \text{ s}$$

**Throughput:**

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{60 \text{ s/min}}{9 \text{ s/selection}} = 6.67 \text{ selections/minute}$$

**Comment:** The throughput of ~6.7 selections/min is acceptable for safety-critical commands (call nurse, door open) but slow for entertainment. Adaptive scanning could double this.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is an Environmental Control Unit (ECU)? Explain the operating principle of the **X-10 power-line carrier protocol** with a diagram. List its advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **minimum and maximum measurable distance** of an ultrasonic Electronic Travel Aid (ETA) operating at 40 kHz, given the transducer ring-down time is 1.2 ms. Assume room temperature 25 °C. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ECU & X-10 Protocol (7 Marks):**

An **Environmental Control Unit (ECU)** is an EADL that allows a physically disabled user to control multiple household appliances (lights, fans, TV, telephone) from a single input device.

**X-10 Operating Principle:**
The X-10 protocol transmits digital commands over the existing AC mains wiring by superimposing **120 kHz** bursts on the **50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (USA)** AC waveform at the **zero-crossing points**. Each command frame is **11 bits** long and addresses a specific appliance by **House Code (A–P)** and **Unit Code (1–16)**.

```
[Transmitter MCU] → [120 kHz Burst Generator] → [AC Mains Wiring] → [Receiver Module] → [Appliance Relay]
```

**Advantages:**
- No new wiring needed.
- Low cost, simple.
- Reliable for ON/OFF control.

**Limitations:**
- Very low data rate (**50 bits/s** in India).
- Susceptible to mains noise.
- Cannot transmit complex analog data.

**(b) Ultrasonic ETA Distance Derivation (7 Marks):**

Given: $f = 40$ kHz, $t_{\text{ring}} = 1.2$ ms, $T = 25$ °C.

**Step 1 — Speed of sound at 25 °C:**

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331 + 15 = 346 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum measurable distance (blind zone):**

$$d_{\min} = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{ring}}}{2} = \frac{346 \times 0.0012}{2} = \frac{0.4152}{2} \approx 0.2076 \text{ m} \approx 20.8 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum measurable distance:**

$$d_{\max} \approx 8 \text{ m}$$ (limited by echo attenuation in air for 40 kHz).

**Step 4 — Spatial resolution:**

$$\Delta d = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{346 \times 0.0012}{2} \approx 0.208 \text{ m} \approx 21 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The blind zone and resolution both equal ~21 cm. Obstacles closer than 21 cm cannot be detected — a significant safety limitation. A wider transducer bandwidth or chirp-FM modulation can reduce this.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Before Writing Your Exam:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ECU with ECU (Engine Control Unit)** in automotive — KTU expects the **medical/assistive** meaning.
> 2. In X-10 derivations, students often forget that the **mains frequency** ($f_{\text{mains}}$) limits the data rate — examiners award 2 marks specifically for the line $R_b = f_{\text{mains}}$.
> 3. For scanning questions, **always state the probability assumption** (uniform random selection); omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 4. In ultrasonic derivations, students frequently write $t_{\text{echo}} = d/v$ instead of $d = v t_{\text{echo}}/2$ (forgetting the round-trip factor of 2). This is the **#1 deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 5. For AAC questions, distinguish between **AAC (Augmentative)** — used to supplement existing speech, vs **AAC (Alternative)** — used to replace absent speech. Examiners love this distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Quick Recap for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ☐ **EADL = Electronic Aids to Daily Living** — devices that translate minimal residual user input into complex appliance/communication control.
- ☐ **5 universal functional blocks:** Input → Conditioning → Decoder → Output → Feedback.
- ☐ **ECU = Environmental Control Unit** — flagship EADL for home appliance control.
- ☐ **X-10 Protocol** transmits **120 kHz bursts** on AC mains at **zero-crossings**; data rate = **$f_{\text{mains}}$ = 50 bits/s** in India.
- ☐ **Single-switch scanning:** Linear, Row-Column, Block, Auditory; standard dwell time **1–2 s**.
- ☐ **Selection time formula:** $T_{\text{select, avg}} = (N_r + N_c + 2) T_d / 2$.
- ☐ **Voice-ECU** uses ASR (HMM/DNN); target WER **< 5%** quiet, **< 15%** noisy; SNR target **> 20 dB**.
- ☐ **Refreshable Braille** uses piezo actuators; pin rise time formula $t_{\text{rise}} = (\pi/2)\sqrt{Lm/k}$; target **< 30 ms**.
- ☐ **Ultrasonic ETA:** $d = v_{\text{sound}} t_{\text{echo}} / 2$; $v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6T$; blind zone from transducer ring-down.
- ☐ **Cochlear implant stimulation:** biphasic current pulses; amplitude **100 µA – 1 mA**; pulse rate **500 Hz – 5 kHz**.
- ☐ **Hearing aid gain:** $G = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{out}}/V_{\text{in}})$ in dB.
- ☐ **AAC** = Augmentative and Alternative Communication; SGDs, text-to-speech tablets.
- ☐ **Power wheelchair controllers:** joystick, sip-puff, head-array, chin-control, eye-gaze.
- ☐ **Robotic aids:** JACO arm (7 DOF), Obi feeding robot, iARM.
- ☐ **Standard activation force** for hand switches: **0.2 N – 2 N**; travel **0.5 mm – 5 mm**.
- ☐ **Safety latency** for EADLs: **< 200 ms** for door locks, **< 500 ms** acceptable for entertainment.
- ☐ **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ for hearing aid ADC (typically 16 kHz sampling for 8 kHz audio bandwidth).
- ☐ **WHO ICF framework** underpins EADL prescription philosophy in KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Aids - Principles of Assistive Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robotic Aids - Principles of Assistive Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assistive Robot (KTU Definition):** An **assistive robot** is a mechatronic, computer-controlled, sensor-integrated robotic system designed to augment, supplement, or restore functional capabilities of individuals with physical, sensory, or cognitive impairments, thereby improving their independence, quality of life, and social participation.

According to the **ISO 13482** standard (the governing international standard for personal care robots), assistive robots are classified as a subset of **service robots** that perform tasks "of direct benefit to a person with a physical disability, an elderly person, or a person with a temporary or permanent impairment."

### Sub-classification (KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus)

| Sub-class | Functional Purpose | Clinical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Rehabilitation Robot** | Restore lost motor function post-stroke/spinal injury | Lokomat, Armeo Power |
| **Prosthetic Robot** | Replace a missing limb with active mechatronic device | Modular Prosthetic Limb (MPL) |
| **Mobility-Assistive Robot** | Aid locomotion & navigation | Smart wheelchairs, exoskeletons |
| **Surgical/Clinical Robot** | Assist surgeon with precision tasks | Da Vinci Surgical System |
| **Socially Assistive Robot (SAR)** | Provide companionship, cognitive therapy | PARO, NAO |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of an assistive robot as a **"smart mechanical helper dog"** that doesn't just fetch things — it watches your muscles, listens to your brain signals, and decides *for itself* how much force to apply so you don't drop a glass of water or fall while walking. The key difference from a normal industrial robot is that it *cooperates* with a human body, rather than working in a fenced cage away from people.

### Why This Matters in KTU Context
- The **2024 Scheme** emphasizes *human-robot interaction (HRI)* as a core competency, meaning students must understand the robot as a **partner**, not a tool.
- Mark-bearing questions frequently test the difference between **Assistive, Rehabilitation, and Surgical** robots.

## 1.3 Foundational Building Blocks of an Assistive Robot

An assistive robot is built from **five universal pillars**:

1. **Sensors** — eyes, ears, skin (EMG, EEG, force, vision, IMU).
2. **Actuators** — muscles (DC motors, servo motors, pneumatic muscles, Series Elastic Actuators).
3. **Mechanical Structure** — skeleton (links, joints, end-effector, gripper).
4. **Controller** — brain (microcontroller, DSP, FPGA running PID/fuzzy/neural algorithms).
5. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** — the communication channel (joystick, voice, BCI, sip-and-puff).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** "**S**ensors, **A**ctuators, **M**echanics, **C**ontrol, **H**MI" → acronym **SAMCH** — five fingers of every assistive robotic hand.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) Loop

Every assistive robot operates on a closed-loop cognitive cycle:

### Step-by-Step Logic
- **SENSE (Perception):** Sensors acquire real-time data: joint angles, applied forces, muscle EMG signals, environmental obstacles.
- **PLAN (Cognition):** The controller's algorithm interprets intent — e.g., detecting that the user wants to reach for a cup via EMG pattern recognition.
- **ACT (Execution):** Actuators move the joints in a coordinated trajectory, applying appropriate force/torque.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The KTU 2024 module outcomes specifically require students to "explain the control hierarchy in assistive robotic systems." The SPA loop is the answer.

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Core Metric

$$\text{DOF} = \text{Number of Independent Motions} = 6n - \lambda$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of rigid bodies (links)
- $\lambda$ = number of joint constraints

For a **human arm** (simplified): 7 DOF (3 shoulder + 1 elbow + 3 wrist). An assistive robot mimicking it must match or exceed 7 DOF for natural motion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU ESE 2024 — Memorize the Table Below:**

| Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom | $DOF = 6n - \lambda$ | $n$=links, $\lambda$=constraints | dimensionless | Mechanism design |
| Forward Kinematics (DH) | $T = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$ | DH parameters | homogeneous matrix | End-effector pose |
| Inverse Dynamics (Torque) | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | $M$=inertia, $C$=Coriolis, $G$=gravity | N·m | Actuator sizing |
| Series Elastic Actuator Force | $F = k_s \, \Delta x$ | $k_s$=spring stiffness, $\Delta x$=deflection | N | Compliant actuation |
| PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = gains | V or PWM | Motor control |
| EMG-Driven Activation | $a(t) = \alpha \cdot \text{RMS}\{EMG(t)\}$ | $\alpha$=scale factor | normalized 0–1 | Prosthetic intent decoding |
| Safety Force Limit (ISO) | $F_{max} \leq 150$ N (quasi-static contact) | — | N | Biocompatibility |
| Coulomb Friction | $F_f = \mu N$ | $\mu$=coefficient, $N$=normal force | N | Gripper analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Rule Reminder:** In KTU answer sheets, never use the pipe $\vert$ symbol inside math tables — write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ instead. This is the same KTU valuation rule applied to your markdown notes.

## 2.4 Actuation Principles — Deeper Dive

### 2.4.1 Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)
A spring placed *between* the motor and the load. This is the **gold standard** in rehabilitation robotics (used in MIT's Anklebot, Agility Robotics' Cassie).

$$\tau_{output} = \tau_{motor} - k_s (\theta_m - \theta_{joint})$$

The spring's deflection allows **force control** rather than position control — critical for safe human interaction.

### 2.4.2 Pneumatic Muscle Actuator (PMA)
A rubber bladder surrounded by braided mesh that contracts when inflated. Mimics biological muscle's force-length relationship.

$$F = P \cdot A_{eff} \cdot (1 - \epsilon^2)$$

Where $\epsilon$ is the contraction strain. Used in **RAMSES** and **Soft Arm** rehabilitation robots.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Wheelchair robots** use the SPA loop with LIDAR + IMU sensors.
- **Exoskeletons (e.g., ReWalk, Ekso GT)** use SEA + PID control to assist gait.
- **Surgical robots** use the DH transform chain to convert surgeon's hand motion into scaled-down instrument motion.
- **BCI-driven prosthetics** use EEG pattern classification (via CNN/LDA) to predict user intent in real time.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Assistive Arm

Consider a planar rehabilitation arm with two revolute joints. Using **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters**.

### Step 1 — Define the DH Parameter Table

| Link $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | 0 | $L_1$ | 0 |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | 0 | $L_2$ | 0 |

### Step 2 — General Homogeneous Transform

$$A_i = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Link 1 Parameters ($\alpha_1 = 0, d_1 = 0$)

$$A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Link 2 Parameters

$$A_2 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Multiply $T = A_1 \cdot A_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A_1 \cdot A_2 \\[6pt]
&= \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Extract End-Effector Position

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{ee} &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\[4pt]
y_{ee} &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This result is the foundation for *inverse kinematics* — solving for $\theta_1, \theta_2$ given a desired $(x_{ee}, y_{ee})$. KTU frequently asks to derive the elbow-up/down analytical solution:
> $$\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$$
> $$\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L_2 \sin\theta_2}{L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2}\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Series Elastic Actuator Force Equation

### Step 1 — Define System
A motor applies torque $\tau_m$ on a shaft connected to a torsional spring of stiffness $k_s$ (N·m/rad). The spring is in turn connected to the joint.

### Step 2 — Spring Deflection
$$\Delta\theta = \theta_m - \theta_{joint}$$

### Step 3 — Force/Torque Transmitted to User
$$\tau_{output} = k_s \cdot \Delta\theta = k_s(\theta_m - \theta_{joint})$$

### Step 4 — Incorporate Motor Inertia and Damping
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_m - \tau_{output} &= J_m \ddot{\theta}_m + b_m \dot{\theta}_m \\[4pt]
\tau_m - k_s(\theta_m - \theta_{joint}) &= J_m \ddot{\theta}_m + b_m \dot{\theta}_m
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Practical Implication
By **measuring $\Delta\theta$** with a position encoder, the controller **computes $\tau_{output}$** — enabling true force control, which is essential for safe human-robot physical interaction.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is SEA preferred over rigid actuation in rehabilitation robots?", cite two reasons from this derivation:
> 1. **Inherent compliance** ($\Delta\theta$ acts as a mechanical low-pass filter, absorbing impact).
> 2. **Accurate force measurement** (Hooke's law is linear and well-characterized).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Robot Selection for Clinical Use

| Clinical Need | Robot Class | Key Sensor Stack | Control Strategy | KTU Reference Year |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gait re-education post-stroke | Wearable exoskeleton | IMU + Force Sensitive Resistor + EMG | Adaptive oscillators + PID | 2023 |
| Upper-limb therapy | End-effector planar robot (e.g., InMotion ARM) | Position encoders + Force sensor | Impedance control | 2022 |
| Amputee daily living | Myoelectric hand (e.g., i-Limb) | Surface EMG (multi-channel) | Pattern recognition (LDA) | 2024 |
| Wheelchair-bound navigation | Smart wheelchair (e.g., Whill) | LIDAR + Ultrasonic + RGB-D camera | SLAM + Obstacle avoidance | 2023 |
| Cognitive therapy in autism | SAR (e.g., NAO) | Camera + Microphone + Tactile | Dialogue FSM + Emotion classifier | 2024 |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Intent\nEMG / EEG / Voice] --> B[Sensors\nEMG IMU Force Camera]
    B --> C{Controller\nMicrocontroller / DSP}
    C --> D[High-Level Planner\nTask Interpreter]
    D --> E[Low-Level Controller\nPID / Impedance]
    E --> F[Actuators\nDC Motor / SEA / Pneumatic]
    F --> G[Mechanical Linkage\nRobot Arm / Exoskeleton]
    G --> H[Physical Interaction\nwith Patient]
    H --> I[Feedback Sensors]
    I --> B
    H --> J[User Perception\nMovement / Assistance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Trace the loop. The user issues an *intent* (left). The sensors pick it up, the controller interprets, the actuator executes, the robot physically moves the patient, and feedback closes the loop. This is the *single most diagrammed concept* in KTU ESE Module 3.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Control Architecture of an Assistive Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1: Mission Planning\nTask goal - pick up glass] --> L2[Level 2: Behavior Coordination\nSequencing subtasks]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Motion Planning\nTrajectory generation]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Servo Control\nJoint torque and position]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: Hardware Layer\nMotors and sensors]
    L5 -.feedback.-> L4
    L4 -.feedback.-> L3
    L3 -.feedback.-> L2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Insight:** A common KTU Part B (14 marks) question asks to "draw and explain the control hierarchy." Use the **5-level decomposition above** — Mission → Behavior → Motion → Servo → Hardware — and you will score full marks.

## 4.3 Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Motor\nHigh-speed DC] --> G[Gearbox\nReduces speed,\nincreases torque]
    G --> S[Torsional Spring\nCompliance element\nk_s]
    S --> J[Joint Output\nConnects to robot link]
    ENC1[Motor Encoder\ntheta_m] --> CTRL[Microcontroller\nComputes F = k_s * delta theta]
    ENC2[Joint Encoder\ntheta_joint] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DRV[Motor Driver\nPWM signal]
    DRV --> M
    J --> ENV[Human User\nForce interaction]
    ENV --> J
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two encoders?** The *difference* between the two encoder readings is the spring deflection $\Delta\theta$, which (by Hooke's law) gives the **force applied to the human**. This is the *only* safe way to control physical contact force in real time.

## 4.4 Classification Tree of Assistive Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Assistive Robot System] --> R1[Rehabilitation]
    R --> R2[Prosthetic]
    R --> R3[Mobility]
    R --> R4[Surgical]
    R --> R5[Socially Assistive SAR]
    R1 --> R1a[Lokomat - Gait]
    R1 --> R1b[Armeo - Upper limb]
    R2 --> R2a[Myoelectric hand]
    R2 --> R2b[Powered knee]
    R3 --> R3a[Smart wheelchair]
    R3 --> R3b[Exoskeleton]
    R4 --> R4a[Da Vinci]
    R4 --> R4b[Mako]
    R5 --> R5a[PARO seal]
    R5 --> R5b[NAO humanoid]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define an assistive robot. List any four key components of an assistive robotic system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An assistive robot is a mechatronic device that aids individuals with disabilities in performing daily activities or rehabilitation tasks through computer-controlled actuation, sensors, and intelligent control algorithms. *(1.5 marks)*

> **Four Key Components:** *(0.375 each = 1.5 marks)*
> 1. **Sensors** (EMG, IMU, force, vision)
> 2. **Actuators** (DC motors, SEAs, pneumatics)
> 3. **Controller** (microcontroller/DSP with PID/impedance/AI algorithms)
> 4. **Human-Machine Interface** (joystick, voice, BCI)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the role of a Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) in rehabilitation robots? Mention any two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Role:** The SEA is an actuation element that places a **compliant spring** between the motor and the joint output. It enables accurate **force/torque control** by measuring spring deflection. *(1.5 marks)*

> **Two Advantages:** *(0.75 each = 1.5 marks)*
> 1. **Mechanical compliance** absorbs shocks and impact, protecting the patient's joints.
> 2. **Stable force feedback** via Hooke's Law $F = k_s \Delta x$, enabling safe physical human-robot interaction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *(a)* Explain the Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) architecture of an assistive robot with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Derive the forward kinematics of a 2-DOF planar manipulator using the Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) method and write the end-effector position equations. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — SPA Architecture (7 marks)

The **Sense-Plan-Act (SPA)** paradigm is a closed-loop control framework that governs how an assistive robot interacts with a human user.

**1. Sense (Perception) Layer** *(1.5 marks)*
- Sensors acquire real-time physiological and environmental data.
- **Examples:** Surface EMG electrodes detect muscle activation intent; IMU measures limb orientation; force sensors measure contact pressure; cameras identify objects.

**2. Plan (Cognition) Layer** *(2 marks)*
- The central controller (microcontroller/DSP/PC) interprets the sensed data using:
  - Pattern recognition (LDA, SVM, neural networks) for EMG decoding
  - Task-level state machines for high-level goal management
  - Path-planning algorithms (A*, RRT) for navigation
- This stage answers: *"What does the user want to do?"*

**3. Act (Execution) Layer** *(2 marks)*
- Low-level controllers (PID, impedance, admittance) drive the actuators.
- Actuators produce controlled motion, which is transmitted to the user's limb.

**4. Feedback Closure** *(1.5 marks)*
- Continuous sensor feedback updates the plan, adapting to disturbances, muscle fatigue, or environmental changes.

*Diagrammatic representation:* (Refer to Section 4.1 above — full credit if the student reproduces the SPA loop with labeled blocks.)

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 2-DOF Forward Kinematics (7 marks)

**Given:** Two revolute joints with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ in a horizontal plane.

**Step 1: DH Parameter Table** *(1 mark)*

| Link $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | 0 | $L_1$ | 0 |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | 0 | $L_2$ | 0 |

**Step 2: Individual Transforms** *(2 marks)*

$$
A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
A_2 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Total Transform** *(2 marks)*

$$
T = A_1 \cdot A_2 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: End-Effector Position** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{ee} &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\[4pt]
y_{ee} &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This describes a **2-link planar arm** and serves as the foundation for the inverse kinematics problem (solving for $\theta_1, \theta_2$ given $(x_{ee}, y_{ee})$).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a Series Elastic Actuator (SEA). Why is it preferred for safe human-robot interaction? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Explain the different types of assistive robots (rehabilitation, prosthetic, mobility, surgical, socially assistive) with one clinical example each. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — Series Elastic Actuator (7 marks)

**Block Diagram Description** *(2 marks)*
- A high-speed DC motor drives a gearbox whose output shaft is connected to a **torsional spring** of stiffness $k_s$.
- The other end of the spring connects to the **joint output** that physically interacts with the human.
- Two encoders: one at the motor side ($\theta_m$), one at the joint side ($\theta_j$).
- A microcontroller reads the encoder difference, computes spring force using Hooke's law, and closes the loop by driving the motor via a PWM driver.

**Working Principle** *(3 marks)*

The spring deflection is:

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta_m - \theta_{joint}
$$

The torque transmitted to the human user is:

$$
\tau_{output} = k_s \cdot \Delta\theta
$$

The motor dynamics satisfy:

$$
\tau_m - \tau_{output} = J_m \ddot{\theta}_m + b_m \dot{\theta}_m
$$

**Why Preferred for Safe HRI** *(2 marks)*
1. **Mechanical compliance:** The spring acts as a *mechanical low-pass filter*, absorbing sudden impacts (e.g., a user spasm or stumble) that would otherwise damage a rigid actuator.
2. **Accurate force sensing:** Real-time force measurement via spring deflection enables true **impedance/admittance control** — the robot behaves like a programmable virtual spring-damper, ensuring the patient is never harmed.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — Classification of Assistive Robots (7 marks)

**1. Rehabilitation Robots** *(1.5 marks)*
- *Purpose:* Restore lost motor function through repetitive, task-specific training.
- *Example:* **Lokomat** — a gait-training exoskeleton used for spinal cord injury patients.

**2. Prosthetic Robots** *(1.5 marks)*
- *Purpose:* Replace missing limbs with sensor-driven, actuated devices.
- *Example:* **i-Limb Ultra** — a myoelectric hand that decodes surface EMG signals to control individual finger movements.

**3. Mobility-Assistive Robots** *(1.5 marks)*
- *Purpose:* Aid locomotion and navigation.
- *Example:* **ReWalk exoskeleton** — a wearable lower-limb robot that enables paraplegic users to stand and walk.

**4. Surgical Robots** *(1 mark)*
- *Purpose:* Provide surgeons with enhanced precision, dexterity, and minimally-invasive access.
- *Example:* **Da Vinci Surgical System** — performs tele-operated laparoscopic surgery with 7-DOF instruments.

**5. Socially Assistive Robots (SAR)** *(1.5 marks)*
- *Purpose:* Provide companionship and cognitive engagement.
- *Example:* **PARO** — a therapeutic robotic baby seal used in dementia and autism therapy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Rehabilitation** and **Prosthetic** robots. *Rehabilitation* = assisting recovery of an *existing* limb. *Prosthetic* = replacing a *missing* limb. The marker will deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 3:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Assistive robot = mechatronic system aiding people with disabilities (per ISO 13482).
- ☐ **Five Pillars (SAMCH):** Sensors, Actuators, Mechanics, Control, HMI.
- ☐ **SPA Loop:** Sense → Plan → Act, with continuous feedback.
- ☐ **DOF formula:** $DOF = 6n - \lambda$. Human arm ≈ 7 DOF.
- ☐ **Forward Kinematics:** Uses DH parameters; gives end-effector position from joint angles.
- ☐ **Inverse Kinematics:** Solves joint angles from desired end-effector pose.
- ☐ **Dynamics equation:** $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$.
- ☐ **SEA Equation:** $\tau = k_s(\theta_m - \theta_{joint})$ — preferred for compliant HRI.
- ☐ **PMA Equation:** $F = P \cdot A_{eff} \cdot (1 - \epsilon^2)$ — muscle-like pneumatic actuator.
- ☐ **PID Control:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$.
- ☐ **EMG Decoding:** $a(t) = \alpha \cdot \text{RMS}\{EMG(t)\}$ — converts muscle signal to actuator command.
- ☐ **Five Robot Classes:** Rehabilitation, Prosthetic, Mobility, Surgical, SAR.
- ☐ **Safety Standard:** ISO 13482 — quasi-static contact force $\leq 150$ N.
- ☐ **Hierarchical Control:** 5 levels — Mission → Behavior → Motion → Servo → Hardware.
- ☐ **Two Encoders in SEA:** Motor-side and joint-side — their *difference* gives force.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** If a KTU question mentions *"human safety"* or *"physical interaction,"* always mention **SEA, compliance, and ISO 13482**. If it mentions *"intent recognition,"* always mention **EMG pattern recognition** and **SAMCH architecture**. These keyword pairings are how examiners hunt for marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robots as Personal Assistants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robots as Personal Assistants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Personal Assistive Robot (PAR)** is defined as a mechatronic, sensor-driven, semi-autonomous or autonomous robotic system engineered to augment, substitute, or restore human functional capabilities in Activities of Daily Living (ADL), Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADL), and rehabilitation-oriented tasks for individuals with motor, cognitive, or sensory impairments. Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus framework, these robots occupy the intersection of **medical robotics**, **rehabilitation engineering**, and **ambient assisted living (AAL)** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A Personal Assistant Robot is not merely a service robot — it must possess at least one of the following four properties: (i) physical interaction capability with the user, (ii) adaptive learning from user behavior, (iii) safety-rated autonomy, and (iv) compliance with ISO 13482 (Personal Care Robots).

**Categorical Classification (ISO 13482 – Robots and Robotic Devices — Personal Care Robots):**

| Class | Description | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobile Servant Robot** | Performs service tasks via locomotion | Hospital delivery robot, fetch-and-carry unit |
| **Physical Assistant Robot** | Provides physical support during ADL | Wheelchair-mounted manipulator, exoskeleton |
| **Person Carrier Robot** | Transports a human user | Powered wheelchair with robotic navigation |
| **Medical Robot** | Used in clinical intervention (excluded from 13482) | Surgical robot (Da Vinci), rehabilitation robot |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a friend who never gets tired, has superhuman reach, can lift 10 kg with one hand, never forgets where your keys are, and has the patience to remind you to take your medication 12 times a day — and this friend is built out of motors, microcontrollers, and cameras. **That is a Personal Assistive Robot.**

Think of three everyday scenarios:

- **Scenario A (Mobility-Impaired User):** Mr. Rajan is quadriplegic. He cannot pick up a glass of water. A **wheelchair-mounted robotic arm** acts like an *extension of his own arm* — guided by a sip-and-puff interface or a chin joystick.
- **Scenario B (Elderly User):** Mrs. Lakshmi, age 78, lives alone. She is at risk of falling. A **mobile service robot** patrols the home, detects falls via LiDAR + vision, fetches medicine, and calls her son.
- **Scenario C (Cognitive-Impaired User):** A child with autism learns social cues. A **socially assistive robot (SAR)** like NAO or Pepper acts as a *patient, infinitely repeatable social tutor*.

All three scenarios share one principle: **the robot is a physical prosthesis for capability**, not a replacement of the human.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standardized performance metrics are mandated by KTU board examiners and are recurrent in KTU University Exam questions:

- **Payload Capacity:** typically **1.5 kg – 5 kg** for upper-limb assistive arms (ISO/TS 15066 collaborative robot threshold).
- **Reach Envelope:** **0.7 m – 1.2 m** for desktop-mounted manipulators.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** minimum **5 DoF** (3 for positioning, 1 for orientation, 1 for gripper actuation); advanced arms use **7 DoF** (human-arm redundancy).
- **Maximum Safe Contact Force:** **140 N** (quasi-static, per ISO/TS 15066) on the hand/arm region.
- **Positional Repeatability:** ±**0.1 mm** for clinical-grade manipulators.
- **End-effector closing force:** ≤ **20 N** for compliant grippers on fragile objects.
- **Autonomy Battery Endurance:** **4 – 8 hours** continuous duty for mobile platforms.
- **Round-trip latency (user command → actuation):** ≤ **300 ms** (clinical acceptance threshold).

## 1.4 Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 PEEBT744)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 Learning Outcome Alignment:** This topic directly addresses **CO3** — *“Design and evaluate robotic and mechatronic systems used for personal assistance, rehabilitation, and ambient assisted living.”* Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level: **Apply / Analyze (Level 3–4).**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of a Personal Assistive Robot

A Personal Assistive Robot (PAR) can be decomposed into **five tightly coupled subsystems**. Understanding each subsystem is essential for KTU board questions on design trade-offs.

### Subsystem 1 — Mechanical Structure (Kinematic Chain)

The mechanical backbone is typically a **serial manipulator** mounted on a **mobile base** (wheelchair or differential-drive platform). The arm follows the **Denavit–Hartenberg (D-H) convention** for kinematic modeling.

For an *n*-joint serial chain, the end-effector pose is obtained via forward kinematics:

$$
{}^{0}T_{n} = {}^{0}T_{1}(\theta_1) \cdot {}^{1}T_{2}(\theta_2) \cdot \ldots \cdot {}^{n-1}T_{n}(\theta_n)
$$

where each $\theta_i$ is the joint variable at link $i$.

### Subsystem 2 — Actuation Layer

Three actuation philosophies dominate:

| Actuation Type | Principle | Typical Use Case | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Servo + Harmonic Drive** | Geared electric motor | Wheelchair arm (Kinova Jaco) | High torque, moderate backlash |
| **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | Motor + spring in series | Compliant rehabilitation | Safe HRI, lower stiffness |
| **Pneumatic Muscle (PMA / McKibben)** | Compressed air bladder | Soft exosuits | Compliant, bulky compressor |
| **Voice Coil / Linear Actuator** | Direct-drive electromagnetic | Prosthetic fingers | Low force, high precision |

### Subsystem 3 — Sensory Stack

The robot perceives the user and environment through a multi-modal sensory stack:

- **Proprioception:** Encoders at each joint (typically **14-bit absolute**, giving $\approx 0.022°$ resolution).
- **Exteroception:** RGB-D cameras (e.g., Intel RealSense D455), LiDAR (e.g., 2D Hokuyo, range **0.05 – 30 m**, accuracy ±**30 mm**), ultrasonic arrays.
- **Tactile:** Force-sensitive resistors (FSR), capacitive skin sensors on gripper.
- **Bio-electrical:** EMG surface electrodes, EEG headset (for cognitive intent decoding).
- **Environmental:** IMU (9-DoF: accelerometer + gyroscope + magnetometer), cliff sensors, bumper switches.

### Subsystem 4 — Control Hierarchy

Modern PARs implement a **three-layer control hierarchy**:

1. **Low-Level Joint Control:** PID or computed-torque at **1 kHz**.
2. **Mid-Level Task Control:** Impedance/admittance control, visual servoing at **100 Hz**.
3. **High-Level Deliberative Layer:** Task planner, behavior tree, finite-state machine at **10 Hz**.

### Subsystem 5 — Human-Robot Interface (HRI)

The HRI is the *unique* subsystem distinguishing a PAR from an industrial robot. Common modalities include:

- **Joystick / Sip-and-Puff** (for high-level mobility-impaired users).
- **EOG / EMG / EEG** (for users with severe motor limitations — locked-in syndrome).
- **Voice + Natural Language Understanding** (NLU) (e.g., Google Speech, Whisper).
- **Gaze-tracking** (Tobii Pro Spectrum, **90 Hz**, ±**0.4°** accuracy).
- **Touchscreen GUI + Web interface** (caregiver override).

## 2.2 Why and How — The Reasoning Chain

**Why redundancy matters:** A 7-DoF arm, unlike a 6-DoF industrial arm, can reconfigure internally (elbow-up vs. elbow-down) to *avoid singularities* and *avoid the user's body* during reaching — critical when the user is physically coupled to the robot base.

**How impedance control ensures safety:** Instead of commanding a *position*, the controller commands a *mechanical impedance* (mass-damper-spring) between robot and user. This makes the arm behave like a soft spring under accidental contact.

$$
F_{contact} = M(\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{x}) + B(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}) + K(x_d - x)
$$

where $M$, $B$, $K$ are virtual mass, damping, and stiffness tunable per task.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear verbatim in KTU University Exam solutions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics (chain) | ${}^{0}T_{n} = \prod_{i=1}^{n}{}^{i-1}T_{i}(\theta_i)$ | Homogeneous transform | dimensionless |
| 2 | Workspace sphere volume | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (L_{max})^3$ | $L_{max}$ = max reach | m³ |
| 3 | Joint torque (gravity) | $\tau_g = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g \, r_{c,i} \cos\theta_i$ | $r_c$ = COM distance | N·m |
| 4 | Differential drive odometry | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$ | $b$ = wheelbase | rad |
| 5 | Mobile robot velocity | $v = \dfrac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2 \Delta t}$, $\omega = \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{\Delta t}$ | Linear, angular velocity | m/s, rad/s |
| 6 | Battery endurance | $E = \dfrac{V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah}}{P_{avg}}$ | $V_{bat}$ = voltage, $C_{Ah}$ = capacity | hours |
| 7 | Positional repeatability (statistical) | $R = \pm k \cdot \sigma$ where $k \approx 1.96$ for 95% CI | $\sigma$ = standard deviation | mm |
| 8 | Impedance controller force | $F = M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke$ | $e = x_d - x$ tracking error | N |
| 9 | LiDAR scan time | $t_{scan} = \dfrac{360°}{\dot{\omega}_{scan}}$ | $\dot{\omega}_{scan}$ = angular scan rate | s |
| 10 | Safe contact force limit | $F_{max} = 140$ N (hand region, quasi-static, ISO/TS 15066) | — | N |
| 11 | Power of mobility base | $P_{base} = m_{total} \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \mu + P_{electronics}$ | $\mu$ = rolling resistance | W |
| 12 | Communication latency bound | $\tau_{latency} \leq 300$ ms (clinical guideline) | — | ms |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Personal Assistive Robots are deployed in:

- **Hospital logistics** — TUG robot delivers medications across wards (Aethon Inc.).
- **Home elderly care** — Care-O-bot (Fraunhofer IPA) performs fetch-and-carry and fall detection.
- **Rehabilitation** — InMotion ARM (Bionik Labs) assists stroke patients in repetitive reaching therapy, delivering **800–1000 repetitions/session** vs. **30–40** in manual therapy.
- **Education for special needs** — NAO robot used in autism spectrum therapy (ASK NAO project).
- **Disaster response humanitarian aid** — BEAR robot (Vecna Robotics) lifts wounded soldiers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters to a KTU engineer:** India has an estimated **70 million disabled persons** (Census 2011) and a rapidly aging population. Personal Assistive Robotics is a strategic, high-impact, and high-employment domain within KTU's Outcome-Based Education mandate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Wheelchair-Mounted Planar Arm

We derive the end-effector position of a planar 2R manipulator mounted on a powered wheelchair. This is the *single most common* derivation in KTU board papers for personal-assistant robotics.

### Step 1 — Define Link Parameters

Let:
- Link 1 length = $L_1$ (shoulder-to-elbow)
- Link 2 length = $L_2$ (elbow-to-gripper)
- Joint angle 1 (shoulder) = $\theta_1$ measured from horizontal X-axis
- Joint angle 2 (elbow) = $\theta_2$ measured from link-1 extension

### Step 2 — Write the Homogeneous Transform for Link 1

$$
{}^{0}T_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Write the Homogeneous Transform for Link 2

$$
{}^{1}T_{2} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Composite Transform

$$
{}^{0}T_{2} = {}^{0}T_{1} \cdot {}^{1}T_{2}
$$

Let us multiply the rotation blocks first. Using the angle-addition identity $\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2$, and similarly for sine:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Extract the Position Vector (Forward Kinematics Output)

The end-effector position $(x, y)$ in the wheelchair base frame is:

$$
x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the End-Effector Orientation

$$
\phi_{ee} = \theta_1 + \theta_2
$$

### Step 7 — Derive the Jacobian Matrix (Required for Velocity Control)

Differentiate the position equations with respect to time:

$$
\dot{x} = -L_1\dot{\theta}_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = L_1\dot{\theta}_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

Rewriting in matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}}_{J(\theta)} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The matrix $J(\theta)$ is the **Jacobian** — it maps joint velocities to end-effector velocities and is critical for KTU problems on **singularities**, which occur when $\det(J) = 0$.

### Step 8 — Compute the Determinant (Singularity Condition)

$$
\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2
$$

**Conclusion:** The arm enters a **singular configuration** when $\theta_2 = 0°$ or $\theta_2 = 180°$, i.e., when the arm is fully stretched out or fully folded back. At these configurations, the end-effector loses one degree of freedom and infinite joint velocities may be commanded — a critical safety hazard in patient-mounted arms.

## 3.2 Derivation — Battery Endurance for a Mobile Assistive Robot

### Step 1 — Define Energy in a Battery

$$
E_{bat} = V_{bat} \times C_{Ah} \quad [\text{Wh}]
$$

### Step 2 — Define Average Power Draw

A mobile PAR has four power sinks: drive motors, manipulator motors, computation (GPU/NPU), and sensors.

$$
P_{avg} = P_{drive} + P_{manipulator} + P_{compute} + P_{sensors}
$$

For a typical KTU-design example:
- $P_{drive} = 2 \times 24\text{ V} \times 5\text{ A} = 240$ W (two motors)
- $P_{manipulator} = 60$ W (6 servos average)
- $P_{compute} = 35$ W (Jetson Orin Nano)
- $P_{sensors} = 15$ W (LiDAR + RealSense + IMU)

$$
P_{avg} = 240 + 60 + 35 + 15 = 350 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Endurance

Using a 24 V, 50 Ah LiFePO₄ battery:

$$
E_{bat} = 24 \times 50 = 1200 \text{ Wh}
$$

Endurance:

$$
t_{endurance} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{1200}{350} \approx 3.43 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply a Safety Derating (80% Depth of Discharge for LiFePO₄)

$$
t_{usable} = 0.8 \times 3.43 = 2.74 \text{ hours}
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** A KTU student designing a mobile PAR must either **oversize the battery** (to ≥ 70 Ah) or **reduce drive power** (e.g., use a holonomic base with lower-power hub motors) to achieve the 4-hour clinical endurance target.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Assistive Robot Architecture

| Selection Criterion | Wheelchair-Mounted Arm | Mobile Manipulator | Fixed Tabletop Arm | Wearable Exoskeleton |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User mobility requirement** | User already in wheelchair | Independent ambulation | Bedside / desk use | User can stand/walk |
| **Reach volume** | High (mobile base) | Very high | Limited (≤ 0.7 m) | Coupled to body |
| **Computational cost** | Moderate | High (SLAM + nav) | Low | Very high (real-time) |
| **Safety complexity** | High | Very high (mobile) | Moderate | Extreme (wearable) |
| **Battery endurance** | Inherits wheelchair battery | Needs its own battery | Plugged in | Needs ultralight battery |
| **Cost (USD, 2024)** | 8,000 – 25,000 | 15,000 – 80,000 | 3,000 – 10,000 | 30,000 – 150,000 |
| **Typical KTU example** | Kinova Jaco | Care-O-bot 4 | iArm | ReWalk, Ekso GT |
| **Cognitive impairment support** | No | Yes (if socially interactive) | Limited | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board examiners love this matrix.** Memorize the trade-off between mobility, safety complexity, and cost. In KTU long-answer questions, you will be asked to *justify* an architecture selection against user profile and ADL requirements.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Overall System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[User Intent Source] --> B1{HRI Modality}
    B1 --> B1a[Joystick Sip-Puff]
    B1 --> B1b[EMG EEG]
    B1 --> B1c[Voice NLU]
    B1 --> B1d[Gaze Tracker]
    B1a --> C1[High Level Task Planner]
    B1b --> C1
    B1c --> C1
    B1d --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Mid Level Impedance Controller]
    D1 --> E1[Joint Servo Drivers]
    E1 --> F1[Mechanical Manipulator Arm]
    F1 --> G1[End Effector Gripper]
    S1[RGB-D Camera] --> V1[Perception Module]
    S2[LiDAR] --> V1
    S3[Force Torque Sensor] --> V1
    S4[IMU] --> V1
    V1 --> D1
    F1 --> S3
    P1[Safety Supervisor ISO 13482] --> D1
    P1 --> E1
    P1 --> B2[Emergency Stop Hardware]
    P1 --> M1[Caregiver Dashboard Web]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Fetch-and-Carry Task

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant H as HRI Layer
    participant P as Task Planner
    participant V as Vision Module
    participant M as Manipulator Controller
    participant A as Actuator Hardware
    U->>H: Voice command "Bring me water"
    H->>P: Parsed intent: object=cup, location=fridge
    P->>V: Locate cup and fridge
    V-->>P: 3D pose returned
    P->>M: Plan trajectory to cup
    M->>A: Joint torque commands
    A-->>M: Encoder feedback
    M->>A: Grasp with compliant force
    P->>M: Plan trajectory to user
    A-->>U: Delivers cup
    P->>M: Release object
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Safety State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Operating: User command received
    Operating --> Paused: Caregiver override
    Paused --> Operating: Resume command
    Operating --> EStop: Force exceeds 140 N
    EStop --> Recovery: Hardware reset
    Recovery --> Idle: Self-test pass
    Recovery --> Fault: Self-test fail
    Fault --> [*]: Service required
    Operating --> Fault: Battery below 10 percent
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Five Subsystem Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MECHA[MECHANICAL SUBSYSTEM]
        M1[Base Frame]
        M2[Shoulder Joint]
        M3[Elbow Joint]
        M4[Wrist Joint]
        M5[Gripper End Effector]
    end
    subgraph ACT[ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM]
        A1[Servo Motor 1]
        A2[Servo Motor 2]
        A3[Servo Motor 3]
        A4[Gripper Motor]
        A5[Haptic Feedback]
    end
    subgraph SENS[SENSORY SUBSYSTEM]
        S1[Encoders]
        S2[IMU]
        S3[Force Torque]
        S4[RGB-D Camera]
        S5[Microphone Array]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
        C1[Joint PID at 1 kHz]
        C2[Impedance Controller at 100 Hz]
        C3[Task Planner at 10 Hz]
    end
    subgraph HRI[HUMAN ROBOT INTERFACE]
        H1[Voice]
        H2[Joystick]
        H3[EMG EEG]
        H4[Caregiver GUI]
    end
    MECHA --- ACT
    ACT --- SENS
    SENS --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT
    HRI --> CTRL
    CTRL --> HRI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block-level architecture explicitly maps the five subsystems introduced in Section 2.1. Notice the **bidirectional arrow between SENS and CTRL** (sensors feed controller; controller commands actuators which perturb the world and re-trigger sensors) and the **symmetric arrow between HRI and CTRL** (the user is *in the loop*, not outside it). This is the canonical structure required in KTU design questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Each question carries **3 marks**, expected answer length is one clean paragraph plus one diagram or formula.

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Define a Personal Assistive Robot. List any four functional requirements mandated by ISO 13482 for personal care robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Personal Assistive Robot (PAR) is a mechatronic system designed to perform service, support, or rehabilitation tasks in close physical or social proximity to a human user with disability or age-related functional decline.

Four ISO 13482 requirements are:
1. **Risk assessment** with documented hazard analysis for every operational mode.
2. **Power and force limiting** — quasi-static contact force ≤ **140 N** on the hand.
3. **Emergency stop** — both hardware (redundant) and software stop reachable within **300 ms**.
4. **Stability verification** — the robot must not tip over under maximum payload and dynamic braking conditions.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — any two ISO requirements; 1 mark — additional two ISO requirements.)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — December 2022]

**Differentiate between a Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arm and a Mobile Manipulator as personal assistants. State one clinical advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wheelchair-Mounted Arm | Mobile Manipulator |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobility** | Coupled to user's powered wheelchair | Independent autonomous base |
| **Advantage** | No extra navigation needed; user controls reach directly | Can leave the user's side to fetch objects independently |
| **Disadvantage** | Reach limited to wheelchair footprint | Higher safety risk due to autonomous navigation |

Clinical advantage examples:
- **Wheelchair arm:** Preserves user sense of agency since motion is directly commanded.
- **Mobile manipulator:** Enables 24/7 monitoring and reduces caregiver burden.

*(1 mark — correct differentiation; 1 mark — advantage of wheelchair arm; 1 mark — advantage of mobile manipulator.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each sub-part typically carries **7 marks**.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five major subsystems** of a Personal Assistive Robot. Discuss the role of impedance control in ensuring safe human-robot interaction.

**Q. (b)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator mounted on a wheelchair has link lengths $L_1 = 0.35$ m and $L_2 = 0.30$ m. The shoulder joint angle is $\theta_1 = 45°$ and the elbow joint angle is $\theta_2 = 30°$. Compute:
- (i) The end-effector position $(x, y)$ in the wheelchair base frame.
- (ii) The Jacobian matrix at this configuration.
- (iii) The determinant and verify whether the configuration is singular.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(a) (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the five-subsystem block diagram as shown in **Section 4.4** of these notes. Label clearly: Mechanical, Actuation, Sensory, Control, HRI.

**Subsystem explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Subsystem:** Serial kinematic chain providing reach. Typical configurations: 5-DoF, 6-DoF, or 7-DoF redundant arm.
2. **Actuation Subsystem:** DC servos, harmonic drives, or series elastic actuators. Provides torque/velocity at each joint.
3. **Sensory Subsystem:** Encoders, IMU, force-torque sensor, RGB-D camera, microphone — provides feedback.
4. **Control Subsystem:** Three-layer hierarchy — joint-level PID, mid-level impedance, high-level task planner.
5. **HRI Subsystem:** Voice, joystick, EMG/EEG, gaze — translates user intent into robot commands.

**Impedance Control (1 mark):** Impedance control models the robot as a virtual mass-damper-spring system, ensuring that accidental contact produces a bounded force. The relationship is $F = M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke$, where the gains are tuned per task. This makes the robot intrinsically compliant and safe.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $L_1 = 0.35$ m, $L_2 = 0.30$ m, $\theta_1 = 45°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Compute the composite angle and cosines/sines:**

$$
\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 45° + 30° = 75°
$$

$$
\cos 45° = 0.7071, \quad \sin 45° = 0.7071
$$

$$
\cos 30° = 0.8660, \quad \sin 30° = 0.5000
$$

$$
\cos 75° = 0.2588, \quad \sin 75° = 0.9659
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — End-effector position:**

$$
x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
x = 0.35 \times 0.7071 + 0.30 \times 0.2588 = 0.2475 + 0.0776 = 0.3251 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
y = 0.35 \times 0.7071 + 0.30 \times 0.9659 = 0.2475 + 0.2898 = 0.5373 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Jacobian entries:**

$$
J_{11} = -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) = -0.35(0.7071) - 0.30(0.9659) = -0.2475 - 0.2898 = -0.5373
$$

$$
J_{12} = -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) = -0.30 \times 0.9659 = -0.2898
$$

$$
J_{21} = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 0.35(0.7071) + 0.30(0.2588) = 0.2475 + 0.0776 = 0.3251
$$

$$
J_{22} = L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 0.30 \times 0.2588 = 0.0776
$$

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5373 & -0.2898 \\ 0.3251 & 0.0776 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Determinant:**

$$
\det(J) = J_{11} \cdot J_{22} - J_{12} \cdot J_{21}
$$

$$
\det(J) = (-0.5373)(0.0776) - (-0.2898)(0.3251)
$$

$$
\det(J) = -0.0417 + 0.0942 = 0.0525 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Singularity check:**

Since $\det(J) = 0.0525 \neq 0$, the configuration is **NON-SINGULAR**. The arm has full 2-DoF mobility and can execute arbitrary end-effector velocities in any direction within its reachable workspace.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to compute $J_{12}$ with a positive sign. Recall: $J_{12} = -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ because the partial derivative of $L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ w.r.t. $\theta_2$ introduces a factor of $-L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$. Forgetting this negative sign gives an incorrect determinant and incorrectly classifies the configuration. Lose **2 marks** if you make this mistake.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **classification of personal care robots** as per ISO 13482. For each class, give one clinical example and state one design constraint.

**Q. (b)** Design the **battery subsystem** of a mobile assistive robot with the following specifications:
- Drive motors: 2 × 24 V, 4 A each, duty cycle 60%
- Manipulator: 24 V, 2 A average
- Compute (Jetson Orin Nano): 19 V, 2.5 A (via DC-DC from 24 V)
- Sensors (LiDAR + camera + IMU): 24 V, 0.6 A
- Required endurance: ≥ 4 hours with 80% depth-of-discharge derating

Compute the required battery capacity in Ah and recommend a chemistry.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(a) (7 marks)

**Classification (3.5 marks — 0.5 per cell):**

| Class | Example | Design Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobile Servant** | TUG hospital delivery robot | Must navigate autonomously; safety-rated obstacle detection within 2 m |
| **Physical Assistant** | Kinova Jaco wheelchair arm | Quasi-static contact force ≤ 140 N; redundant DoF to avoid user body |
| **Person Carrier** | Powered wheelchair with auto-nav | Tip-over stability under braking; max speed ≤ 1.5 m/s indoors |
| **Medical (excluded from 13482)** | Da Vinci surgical robot | Sterile, fail-safe actuators, surgeon-in-the-loop |

**Discussion (3.5 marks):**

Each class imposes a different safety philosophy. Mobile servants must have **perimeter-scanning LiDAR** with 360° coverage and emergency stops reachable from any user position. Physical assistants must implement **impedance control** to ensure soft contact. Person carriers require **dynamic stability analysis** including worst-case braking deceleration. Medical robots fall under the stricter **IEC 80601-1** family and are typically outside the personal-care scope. The KTU design implication is that *the classification dictates the safety subsystem architecture and budget*.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Compute Average Current per Subsystem:**

$$
I_{drive} = 2 \times 4 \text{ A} \times 0.6 = 4.8 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{manipulator} = 2 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{compute} = 2.5 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{sensors} = 0.6 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Total Average Current:**

$$
I_{total} = 4.8 + 2 + 2.5 + 0.6 = 9.9 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Total Power at 24 V:**

$$
P_{total} = 24 \text{ V} \times 9.9 \text{ A} = 237.6 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Required Energy for 4 Hours:**

$$
E_{req} = 237.6 \text{ W} \times 4 \text{ h} = 950.4 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Apply 80% Depth-of-Discharge Derating:**

$$
E_{battery} = \dfrac{E_{req}}{0.80} = \dfrac{950.4}{0.80} = 1188 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Compute Battery Capacity in Ah:**

$$
C_{Ah} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{V_{bat}} = \dfrac{1188}{24} = 49.5 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Recommendation:** Round up to a commercially available **24 V, 50 Ah LiFePO₄** battery pack. Justification (1 mark):
- **LiFePO₄** offers 2000+ cycles (vs. 500 for lead-acid), 95% round-trip efficiency, no thermal runaway risk.
- 50 Ah satisfies the 4-hour endurance with a small margin.
- For medical applications, LiFePO₄ is preferred over Li-ion (LiCoO₂) for safety.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** A frequent mistake is to forget the **duty cycle factor** for the drive motors. Many students compute $I_{drive} = 8$ A instead of $4.8$ A. This leads to an oversized battery specification and costs you marks on engineering judgment. Always read carefully whether a continuous or duty-cycled current is given. Another common error: forgetting the **depth-of-discharge derating**, which produces an undersized battery that fails prematurely in the field. Lose **2 marks** for either error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin to your study wall!)**

- [ ] **Definition first:** PAR = sensor-driven, semi/autonomous robot for ADL/IADL/rehab per ISO 13482.
- [ ] **Four ISO classes:** Mobile Servant, Physical Assistant, Person Carrier, Medical (excluded).
- [ ] **Five subsystems:** Mechanical, Actuation, Sensory, Control, HRI.
- [ ] **Three-layer control:** Joint PID (1 kHz) → Impedance (100 Hz) → Task Planner (10 Hz).
- [ ] **Forward kinematics formula:** ${}^{0}T_{n} = \prod_{i=1}^{n}{}^{i-1}T_{i}(\theta_i)$ — memorize it.
- [ ] **Jacobian determinant:** $\det(J_{2R}) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2$ — **zero at singular**.
- [ ] **Impedance law:** $F = M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke$.
- [ ] **Safe contact force:** ≤ **140 N** (ISO/TS 15066, hand region).
- [ ] **Clinical latency budget:** ≤ **300 ms** user command → actuation.
- [ ] **End-effector closing force:** ≤ **20 N** for fragile object handling.
- [ ] **Battery rule of thumb:** Apply 80% DoD derating for LiFePO₄; multiply load current × endurance × 1.25.
- [ ] **End-effector DOF minimum:** 5 (3 position + 1 orientation + 1 gripper).
- [ ] **Redundancy (7-DoF):** Allows elbow-up/down to avoid singularities and obstacles.
- [ ] **Standards triad:** **ISO 13482** (personal care), **ISO/TS 15066** (collaborative force limits), **IEC 80601-1** (medical electrical).
- [ ] **Differential drive kinematics:** $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)/b$ and $v = (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)/2\Delta t$.
- [ ] **Major commercial examples:** Kinova Jaco (arm), Care-O-bot 4 (mobile), InMotion ARM (rehab), ReWalk (exoskeleton).
- [ ] **Indian context:** ~70 million disabled (Census 2011) + aging population = huge assistive robotics demand.
- [ ] **Examiner loves:** block diagrams, forward kinematics derivations, Jacobian singularities, impedance control equation, ISO standards citations, and battery sizing problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
