> 3. **Cane length formula** — Use $L = 0.45 H$ **or** the wrist-crease rule. Do **not** mix the two or write vague answers like "half the body height".
> 4. **Off-loading percentage** — Standard cane = 25 %; Quad cane = 50 %. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Long cane ≠ walking cane** — The white long cane is an **orientation aid**, not a weight-bearing device. Writing "used by blind people for balance" loses marks; the correct phrasing is "environmental scanning and obstacle detection".
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always quote cane length in **cm**, force in **N** or **%BW**, and tip diameter in **mm**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Mobility and Orientation Aids – Canes (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3):**

- [ ] A **cane** is a single-point-contact mobility aid; an **axillary crutch** has a top pad.
- [ ] Standard cane off-loads **≤ 25 % BW**; quad cane off-loads **≤ 50 % BW**.
- [ ] Cane is held in the **contralateral (opposite) hand** to the weak leg.
- [ ] Prescribed length: $L = 0.45 H$ (height method) **or** floor-to-wrist-crease (15°–30° elbow flexion).
- [ ] **Three-point gait** sequence: **Cane → Weak leg → Strong leg**.
- [ ] **Quad cane** has 4 feet; **narrow base = 15 cm**, **wide base = 20 cm**.
- [ ] **Forearm (Lofstrand) cane** has a forearm cuff — used when the grip is weak.
- [ ] **Long white cane** is for **orientation**, not weight-bearing; **red-and-white** = deaf-blind.
- [ ] **Tip ferrule** must have $\mu \geq 0.8$; diameter 12–19 mm.
- [ ] Standards: **ISO 11334-1** (single-point cane), **ISO 11334-4** (multi-leg cane), **ISO 9999**.
- [ ] Shaft material: aluminium 6061-T6 (most common) or carbon-fibre (premium lightweight).
- [ ] **Euler buckling load** governs shaft sizing: $P_{\text{cr}} = \pi^{2} E I / (K L)^{2}$.
- [ ] Contraindications: severe cerebellar ataxia, bilateral upper-limb weakness, cognitive impairment.
- [ ] Energy cost reduction with cane use: **8–15 %** vs unassisted gait.
- [ ] Adjustable canes use a **push-button telescopic** lock; folding canes use a **bungee-cord hinge**.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Mobility and Orientation Aids: Canes** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternative Mobility Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Alternative Mobility Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Alternative Mobility Devices?

**Alternative Mobility Devices (AMDs)** are a broad class of assistive technology engineered to restore, augment, or substitute human locomotion in individuals with impaired mobility arising from congenital disability, spinal cord injury (SCI), amputation, neuromuscular disease, osteoarthritis, stroke (cerebrovascular accident), or age-related debility. They are the practical engineering interface between the user's residual functional capacity and the built environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Alternative mobility devices encompass *wheeled mobility systems* (manual wheelchairs, powered wheelchairs, mobility scooters), *ambulation aids* (canes, crutches, walkers), *prosthetic limbs* (artificial replacements for missing limbs), and *orthotic devices* (external biomechanical supports). Each is matched to the user through a clinical prescription process aligned with the **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** framework of the WHO.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mobility Pyramid"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of mobility options as a **pyramid of independence**.
> - At the **base (highest independence)**: a fully able-bodied person walking unaided.
> - One step up: a person using a **single-point cane** (minimal support, highest mobility).
> - Higher: **crutches, walkers, manual wheelchairs, powered wheelchairs**, and finally at the top of need, **prosthetics and orthotics** that directly interface with the musculoskeletal system.
>
> The goal of an assistive technologist is to match the user to the **lowest level of device** that safely restores their environmental access. Over-prescribing a powered chair when a cane would do is as harmful as under-prescribing a powered chair for someone who cannot self-propel.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Alternative Mobility Devices

| Tier | Device Class | User Functional Level | Energy Cost (METs) |
|------|--------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Single-point cane** | Mild balance deficit, one good hand | 3.5 – 4.0 |
| 2 | **Quad cane / Hemi-walker** | Moderate hemiparesis, poor grip | 4.0 – 4.5 |
| 3 | **Axillary / Forearm crutches** | Bilateral LE weakness, no WB on one limb | 4.5 – 6.0 |
| 4 | **Standard / Rollator walker** | Poor balance, need bilateral UL support | 3.5 – 5.0 |
| 5 | **Manual wheelchair** | Cannot ambulate; good UL strength | 3.0 – 4.5 (self-prop) |
| 6 | **Powered wheelchair** | Cannot self-propel; cognitive function intact | 2.0 – 2.5 |
| 7 | **Mobility scooter** | Cannot walk long distances; trunk control | 2.5 – 3.0 |
| 8 | **Prosthetic limb (Transtibial/Transfemoral)** | Amputation; intact residual limb | 4.0 – 8.0 (varies) |
| 9 | **Orthosis (AFO, KAFO)** | Joint instability, muscle weakness | 3.5 – 5.5 |

> **Key Metric Highlighted:** The **Metabolic Equivalent of Task (MET)** is the standard physiological cost measure, where 1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min (resting energy expenditure). Normal walking costs ~3.0 METs; hemiplegic gait can exceed 6.0 METs.

### 1.4 The ICF Framework — KTU High-Yield Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **WHO ICF Model** classifies mobility disability across three domains — **(1) Body Functions/Structures, (2) Activities, (3) Participation**. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to map a clinical condition (e.g., T10 complete paraplegia) to the appropriate device tier using this framework. Devices are not chosen for the diagnosis alone but for the **activity limitation** and **participation restriction** they aim to address.

### 1.5 The Prescriptive Process — Who Decides?

The rehabilitation team comprises:
1. **Physiatrist (PM&R physician)** — Team lead, writes the prescription.
2. **Prosthetist/Orthotist (CP/CO certified)** — Designs and fabricates P&O devices.
3. **Physical Therapist (PT)** — Gait training, device tuning.
4. **Occupational Therapist (OT)** — ADL training, wheelchair skills.
5. **Rehabilitation Engineer** — Custom fabrication, electronics, controls.
6. **User & Caregiver** — Final arbiters of usability and acceptance.

> [!NOTE]
> A **correctly prescribed** mobility device reduces secondary complications (pressure ulcers, shoulder overuse injury, scoliosis, contractures) by over **60%** in long-term wheelchair users, per WHO Rehabilitation 2030 data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manual Wheelchairs — The Engineering Subsystems

A manual wheelchair is a **human-powered, wheeled mobility base** with five engineered subsystems:

1. **Frame** (Rigid vs. Folding; aluminum, titanium, or carbon fiber).
2. **Seating & Posture Support** (Cushion, backrest, lateral trunk supports).
3. **Wheels** (Drive wheels, casters, push-rims).
4. **Propulsion Drive Train** (Direct push on hand-rim; lever-drive; geared hub).
5. **Control Interface** (Brakes, tipping levers, anti-tippers).

### 2.2 Theoretical Principle — Rolling Resistance and Propulsion Mechanics

The user must overcome four resistive forces to propel a manual wheelchair:
1. **Rolling resistance** of the tires.
2. **Internal friction** of bearings and caster pivots.
3. **Camber-induced turning resistance** (the angled drive wheels generate a side-force component).
4. **Aero + grade + inertia** during acceleration.

#### 2.2.1 Rolling Resistance Formula

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot N$$

Where:
- $F_{rr}$ = rolling resistance force (N)
- $\mu_{rr}$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless, typically **0.008 – 0.015** for pneumatic wheelchair tires on smooth flooring)
- $N$ = normal force on the wheel (N) = effective load on drive wheels

#### 2.2.2 Push-Rim Biomechanics (Kinetics of Propulsion)

The **tangential force** the user applies to the hand-rim is amplified through the wheel radius ratio:

$$T_{hand} = F_{push} \cdot r_{rim}$$

$$T_{wheel} = F_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}$$

At equilibrium (no acceleration of the wheel):

$$F_{wheel} = F_{push} \cdot \frac{r_{rim}}{r_{wheel}}$$

> **Mechanical Insight:** Since $r_{rim} < r_{wheel}$, the **mechanical advantage is less than 1** (typically 0.83 – 0.91). The user must therefore apply a *larger* push-rim force than the force delivered to the floor — a direct cause of **shoulder overuse injury** (rotator cuff pathology) in long-term manual chair users.

#### 2.2.3 Power Output Requirement

$$P_{prop} = F_{total} \cdot v$$

Where $v$ is the wheelchair linear velocity (~0.8 – 1.4 m/s for typical self-propulsion). A 70 kg user on a 12 kg chair climbing a 5% grade requires:

$$P_{prop} \approx (m_{user} + m_{chair}) \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \cdot v \approx 80 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 1.0 \approx 39.2 \text{ W}$$

This is the *minimum sustained* output a SCI user must generate — equivalent to a slow bicycle ride.

### 2.3 Powered Wheelchairs — The Electrical & Control Subsystems

A powered wheelchair (PWC) is a **battery-electric mobility base** with:

| Subsystem | Component | Spec Range (KTU) |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| **Power Source** | Sealed Lead-Acid (SLA) or Li-ion | 24 V or 36 V, 30 – 75 Ah |
| **Motors** | DC permanent-magnet or brushless DC | 2 × 150 – 450 W |
| **Drive Train** | Direct gear, belt, or gear-hub | Reduction ratio 8:1 – 30:1 |
| **Controller** | Microprocessor-based PWM | 50 – 100 A peak |
| **Input Device** | Joystick, sip-and-puff, head-array, chin-control | Force-sensing or position-sensing |
| **Wheels** | Drive wheel + caster (RWD/MWD/FWD) | 8" – 14" |

### 2.4 Powered Wheelchair Dynamics

The governing motion equation on a flat surface:

$$M \cdot a = F_{motor} - F_{rr} - F_{aero} - F_{grade}$$

Where:
- $M$ = total mass (user + chair + batteries) in kg
- $a$ = linear acceleration (m/s²)
- $F_{motor}$ = total tractive force from both drive motors
- $F_{grade} = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$

Maximum range on a single charge is governed by battery capacity:

$$E_{batt} = V \cdot Ah \quad (\text{Wh})$$

$$\text{Range} = \frac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta}{P_{draw}} \quad (\text{km})$$

Where $\eta$ = drive-train efficiency (0.6 – 0.8) and $P_{draw}$ = average power draw during typical mixed driving.

### 2.5 Crutch Gait — Force & Energy Analysis

Crutches transfer body weight through the upper limbs. The **axillary load** is given by:

$$L_{ax} = W \cdot \frac{d_{heel-ax}}{d_{ax-fingertip}}$$

Where:
- $L_{ax}$ = load borne by axillary pad (N)
- $W$ = body weight (N)
- $d_{heel-ax}$ = horizontal distance from heel to axillary pad
- $d_{ax-fingertip}$ = distance from axillary pad to fingertip (handle)

> **Design Rule of Thumb:** Maintain $d_{heel-ax} \approx 15$ cm and $d_{ax-fingertip} \approx 38$ cm so that axillary load does not exceed ~40% body weight during three-point gait. Higher loads cause **brachial plexus compression ("crutch palsy")**.

### 2.6 Prosthetic Limb Engineering — The Socket–Pylon–Foot Triad

A lower-limb prosthesis consists of:
1. **Socket** — Custom-laminated interface to the residual limb.
2. **Suspension** — Suction, pin-lock, lanyard, or seal-in liner.
3. **Pylon / Shank** — Structural connector (aluminum, titanium, carbon fiber).
4. **Terminal device** — Foot (SACH, single-axis, multi-axis, energy-storing) for transtibial; or knee + foot for transfemoral.

The **vertical ground reaction force (GRF)** must be transmitted through the socket without producing pressure > 200 kPa at any point on the residual limb (capillary closure threshold).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula and boundary condition tested in PEEBT744 Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables | KTU Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot N$ | $\mu_{rr}$, $N$ | N |
| 2 | Push-rim mechanical disadvantage | $F_{wheel} = F_{push} \cdot r_{rim}/r_{wheel}$ | Radii | N |
| 3 | Propulsion power | $P = F_{total} \cdot v$ | Force, velocity | W |
| 4 | Grade resistance | $F_{grade} = M g \sin\theta$ | Mass, slope | N |
| 5 | Battery energy | $E = V \cdot Ah$ | Voltage, capacity | Wh |
| 6 | PWC range | $R = E \cdot \eta / P_{draw}$ | Energy, efficiency, power | km |
| 7 | Crutch axillary load | $L_{ax} = W \cdot d_{heel-ax}/d_{ax-fingertip}$ | Distances | N |
| 8 | Capillary closure pressure | $P_{skin} < 200$ kPa | — | kPa |
| 9 | Walker stability (tipping) | $M_{stability} = W \cdot b/2$ | Base width $b$ | N·m |
| 10 | Metabolic cost of transport (CoT) | $CoT = E_{metab} / (m \cdot d)$ | Energy, mass, distance | J/(kg·m) |
| 11 | Heart-rate reserve (exercise intensity) | $HRR = HR_{max} - HR_{rest}$ | — | bpm |
| 12 | Transtibial socket pressure | $\sigma = F/A$ | Force, contact area | kPa |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same biomechanical and electromechanical principles that govern alternative mobility devices are also applied in:
- **Robotic exoskeletons** (Ekso GT, ReWalk) — controlled FES + actuator hybrid.
- **Human–machine interfaces** for drones and wheelchairs via shared neural control.
- **Rehabilitation robotics** (Lokomat, ARMEO) — quantitative gait/upper-limb retraining.
- **Smart canes** with ultrasonic and inertial sensors for visually impaired users.
- **Active prosthetics** with microprocessor-controlled knee joints (C-Leg, Rheo Knee).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rolling Resistance of a Manual Wheelchair on a Flat Surface

### Given
- User mass: $m_u = 70$ kg
- Chair mass: $m_c = 12$ kg
- Total mass: $M = 82$ kg
- Tire coefficient of rolling resistance: $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$
- Drive wheel radius: $r_w = 0.30$ m
- Push-rim radius: $r_{rim} = 0.26$ m
- Self-propulsion speed: $v = 1.0$ m/s
- Bearing and caster internal coefficient (combined): $\mu_{int} \approx 0.005$

### Step 1 — Total Weight (Normal Force)
The total normal force on the drive wheels (assuming a level surface and the rear wheels bear ~85% of the weight):

$$N = M \cdot g = 82 \cdot 9.81 = 804.42 \text{ N}$$

### Step 2 — Force on Drive Wheels Only
With load distribution factor $\alpha = 0.85$:

$$N_{drive} = \alpha \cdot N = 0.85 \cdot 804.42 = 683.76 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3 — Rolling Resistance Force
$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot N_{drive} = 0.012 \cdot 683.76 = 8.205 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4 — Internal Friction Force (Bearings & Casters)
$$F_{int} = \mu_{int} \cdot N = 0.005 \cdot 804.42 = 4.022 \text{ N}$$

### Step 5 — Total Propulsive Force Required at Constant Speed
$$F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{int} = 8.205 + 4.022 = 12.227 \text{ N}$$

### Step 6 — Power Required at the Wheel
$$P_{wheel} = F_{total} \cdot v = 12.227 \cdot 1.0 = 12.23 \text{ W}$$

### Step 7 — Power Required at the Push-Rim (Accounting for Mechanical Disadvantage)
$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{r_{rim}}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{0.26}{0.30} = 0.867$$

$$P_{push} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{12.23}{0.867} = 14.11 \text{ W}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The user must expend ~14 W of metabolic power at the hands for *flat-ground* cruising. Climbing a 5% grade triples this demand. This is why long-term manual chair users experience **shoulder impingement** and why powered mobility is prescribed when UL preservation is a clinical priority.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Powered Wheelchair Battery Range

### Given
- Battery: 2 × 12 V, 35 Ah SLA in series → 24 V, 35 Ah
- Total mass: $M = 110$ kg (user + chair + batteries)
- Drive train efficiency: $\eta = 0.70$
- Average power draw during mixed indoor/outdoor driving: $P_{draw} = 120$ W
- Tire $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$ on indoor tile

### Step 1 — Total Battery Energy
$$E_{batt} = V \cdot Ah = 24 \cdot 35 = 840 \text{ Wh}$$

### Step 2 — Usable Energy (80% Depth-of-Discharge for SLA)
$$E_{usable} = 0.80 \cdot 840 = 672 \text{ Wh}$$

### Step 3 — Range at Constant Speed
$$R = \frac{E_{usable} \cdot \eta}{P_{draw}} = \frac{672 \cdot 0.70}{120} = \frac{470.4}{120} = 3.92 \text{ km}$$

> **Critical Pitfall:** Students often forget the depth-of-discharge factor for SLA batteries (lithium is ~90%). A 24 V 35 Ah battery *cannot* deliver its full 840 Wh in practice — a KTU favourite trap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Crutch Axillary Load — Three-Point Gait

### Given
- Body weight: $W = 700$ N (≈ 71.4 kg user)
- Heel to axillary pad: $d_{heel-ax} = 15$ cm = 0.15 m
- Axillary pad to fingertip: $d_{ax-fingertip} = 38$ cm = 0.38 m

### Step 1 — Axillary Load Formula
$$L_{ax} = W \cdot \frac{d_{heel-ax}}{d_{ax-fingertip}}$$

$$L_{ax} = 700 \cdot \frac{0.15}{0.38} = 700 \cdot 0.395 = 276.3 \text{ N}$$

### Step 2 — Fraction of Body Weight on Axilla
$$\%BW = \frac{L_{ax}}{W} \cdot 100 = \frac{276.3}{700} \cdot 100 = 39.5\%$$

### Step 3 — Hand-Grip Load (Complement of Axillary)
$$L_{hand} = W - L_{ax} = 700 - 276.3 = 423.7 \text{ N}$$

> **Clinical Rule:** The axillary pad should never bear > 50% of body weight; ideally keep below 40%. Excess axillary load → **crutch palsy** (compression of brachial plexus, especially C8–T1 nerves).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Prosthetic Socket Interface Pressure

### Given
- Vertical GRF during mid-stance on a transtibial prosthesis: $F = 700$ N
- Effective socket–limb contact area: $A = 350 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.035 \text{ m}^2$

### Step 1 — Average Interface Pressure
$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{700}{0.035} = 20{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 20 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 2 — Comparison with Capillary Closure Threshold
$$\sigma_{avg} = 20 \text{ kPa} < P_{cap} = 200 \text{ kPa} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Clinical Reasoning:** Although average pressure is well below the capillary closure threshold, **peak pressures** at bony prominences (tibial tuberosity, fibular head, distal end of tibia) can locally exceed 200 kPa. This is why modern sockets use **total-surface-bearing (TSB)** designs with silicone liners to distribute pressure evenly.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Device Selection

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **KTU exam favourite**. The question typically presents a clinical scenario (e.g., "60-year-old with right hemiparesis post-stroke") and asks for the appropriate device with justification.

| Clinical Presentation | Functional Level | First-Choice Device | Rationale |
|-----------------------|------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Single below-knee amputation, healthy contralateral limb | K-Level 3–4 | **Transtibial prosthesis with energy-storing foot** | Variable cadence community ambulation |
| Bilateral above-knee amputation | K-Level 1–2 | **Wheelchair (manual or powered)** | Prosthetic gait energy cost prohibitive |
| T10 complete paraplegia, intact UL | K-Level 2 | **Manual ultralight rigid wheelchair** | Independence, low weight, shoulder preservation |
| C4 complete tetraplegia | K-Level 0 | **Powered wheelchair with chin or sip-and-puff control** | No UL function for self-propulsion |
| Elderly, mild balance deficit, intact cognition | Falls risk | **Quad cane or standard walker** | Maximum base of support per unit cost |
| Rheumatoid arthritis, painful wrists | — | **Forearm (Lofstrand) crutches** | Weight borne on forearm cuff, sparing wrist |
| Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, age 12 | K-Level 2 | **Powered wheelchair** | Manual propulsion no longer sustainable; preserve UL |
| Parkinson's disease, festinating gait | Frequent falls | **Standard walker or rollator with brakes** | Cognitive cue, wide base, hand-brake for fall arrest |

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Walker Stability Against Tipping

A walker tips when the **line of gravity** falls outside the base of support. The restoring moment is:

$$M_{restore} = W \cdot \frac{b}{2}$$

Where $b$ is the base width of the walker (front-to-back or side-to-side, whichever is smaller). To prevent tipping during one-handed reaching:

$$M_{restore} > M_{destabilize} = F_{reach} \cdot h$$

Standard ANSI/RESNA stability test requires that a static forward tip only occur beyond **15°** of forward tilt — this corresponds to a minimum base depth of ~50 cm for an average adult.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mobility Device Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Presents with Mobility Limitation] --> B{Functional Ambulation Assessment}
    B -- Can walk with mild deficit --> C{Cognitive Status Intact?}
    B -- Cannot ambulate safely --> D{Upper Limb Function Adequate?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Single-Point or Quad Cane]
    C -- No --> F[Standard Walker with Supervision]
    D -- Yes --> G[Manual Wheelchair]
    D -- No --> H[Powered Wheelchair]
    G --> I{Intended Use Environment}
    I -- Indoor mostly --> J[Lightweight Manual Chair]
    I -- Mixed indoor outdoor --> K[Ultralight Rigid Chair]
    I -- Long distance community --> L[Powered Wheelchair]
    H --> M{Control Site Available}
    M -- Hand --> N[Standard Joystick PWC]
    M -- Chin --> O[Chin-Controlled PWC]
    M -- Sip-and-puff --> P[Pneumatic Switch PWC]
    M -- Head motion --> Q[Head Array PWC]
    E --> R[Canes Family]
    F --> S[Walkers Family]
    J --> T[Wheelchair Family]
    K --> T
    L --> T
    N --> T
    O --> T
    P --> T
    Q --> T
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Powered Wheelchair Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Input Device] --> B[Joystick Signal Conditioner]
    B --> C[Microprocessor Controller]
    C --> D[PWM Driver Stage]
    D --> E[Left Drive Motor]
    D --> F[Right Drive Motor]
    E --> G[Left Drive Wheel]
    F --> H[Right Drive Wheel]
    G --> I[Wheelchair Chassis Motion]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Tactile and Visual Feedback to User]
    J --> A
    C --> K[Battery Management System BMS]
    L[24V SLA or Li-ion Battery Pack] --> K
    K --> D
    C --> M[Fault Detection and Safety Cutoff]
    M --> C
```

## 4.3 Prosthetic Limb Subsystem Map (Transfemoral)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Residual Limb] --> B[Suspension System]
    B --> C[Custom Laminated Socket]
    C --> D[Connector / Pyramid Adapter]
    D --> E[Pylon Shank]
    E --> F[Microprocessor Knee Unit]
    F --> G[Torque Sensor and Controller]
    G --> H[Pneumatic or Hydraulic Cylinder]
    H --> I[Multi-axis Energy Storing Foot]
    I --> J[Ground Reaction Force]
    J --> K[Stance and Swing Phase Control]
    K --> F
    G --> L[Onboard Battery Pack]
    L --> G
    F --> M[Remote Programming Interface]
    M --> F
```

## 4.4 Crutch Force Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Body Weight W] --> B[Axillary Pad Point A]
    A --> C[Handgrip Point H]
    B --> D[Reaction Force Ra Vertical]
    B --> E[Reaction Force Ra Horizontal]
    C --> F[Reaction Force Rh Vertical]
    C --> G[Reaction Force Rh Horizontal]
    D --> H[Net Vertical Equilibrium]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Floor Reaction at Crutch Tip]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two reactions $R_a$ (axillary) and $R_h$ (handgrip) are the engineering variables you solved in **Section 3.3**. The diagram shows the **four unknown reactions** the body generates at the crutch, which are summed to give the floor reaction at the crutch tip.

## 4.5 Gait Cycle vs. Mobility Device Compatibility

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normal Gait Cycle] --> B[Stance Phase 60 percent]
    A --> C[Swing Phase 40 percent]
    B --> D[Heel Strike]
    B --> E[Mid Stance]
    B --> F[Push Off]
    C --> G[Acceleration]
    C --> H[Swing Through]
    D --> I{Requires Heel Strike Stability?}
    E --> J{Requires Mid Stance Load Transfer?}
    F --> K{Requires Push Off Energy Return?}
    I --> L[Energy Storing Foot Prosthesis]
    J --> M[Solid AFO or KAFO]
    K --> N[Carbon Fiber Spring Foot]
    I --> O[Standard SACH Foot May Fail]
    E --> P[Articulated AFO Required]
```
