**(iii) Shannon safety verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
log_{10}(D) + k \cdot log_{10}(Q) &= log_{10}(3.0) + 1.5 \cdot log_{10}(0.12) \\
&= 0.477 + 1.5 \cdot (-0.921) \\
&= 0.477 - 1.381 \\
&= -0.904
\end{aligned}$$

Since $-0.904 \leq 1.85$, the design is **SAFE** with substantial margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Pitfall — Examiner's Warning:**
> - Unit conversion: **µs → s** (multiply by $10^{-6}$), **mA → A** (multiply by $10^{-3}$). Forgetting this loses 2 marks.
> - $log_{10}$ is base-10, not natural log. Use $log_{10}$.
> - Always end with a **clear SAFE / UNSAFE** statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Auditory Brain Stem Implant Aids:**

- ⭐ ABI stimulates the **dorsal cochlear nucleus (DCN)** — *not* the cochlea, *not* the auditory nerve.
- ⭐ Bypasses the **auditory nerve (CN VIII)** entirely.
- ⭐ Primary indication: **Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2)**.
- ⭐ Surgical access: **lateral suboccipital / retrosigmoid craniotomy**.
- ⭐ Electrode placed via the **foramen of Luschka** onto DCN floor.
- ⭐ Typical electrode count: **12–21 surface electrodes** (Pt-Ir).
- ⭐ Charge-balanced **biphasic pulses** are mandatory for safety.
- ⭐ **Shannon limit:** $log_{10}(D) + 1.5 \cdot log_{10}(Q) \leq 1.85$.
- ⭐ **Charge density safety:** $CD \leq 30 \, \mu C/cm^2$ (geometric, Pt).
- ⭐ Coding strategy: **CIS** (continuous interleaved sampling).
- ⭐ Outcomes are **modest** vs. CI — mostly sound + lip-reading.
- ⭐ Surgical risks: CSF leak, meningitis, facial nerve palsy.
- ⭐ Sound quality is often "noisy/robotic" — **not** normal hearing.
- ⭐ Tonotopic mapping is **logarithmic**: $f(n) = f_{low} \cdot (f_{high}/f_{low})^{(n-1)/(N-1)}$.
- ⭐ ABI Dynamic range ≈ **15–25 dB** (narrower than CI).
- ⭐ Verification method: **eABR** (electrically evoked auditory brainstem response).
- ⭐ Difference from CI: CI = cochlear, ABI = brainstem, both share external sound-processor technology.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Audio Frequency Induction Loop Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Audio Frequency Induction Loop Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Audio Frequency Induction Loop System (AFILS)** is an assistive listening technology that transmits an audio signal as a **modulated alternating magnetic field** in the audio frequency range (typically **100 Hz to 8 kHz**) using a closed loop of wire installed along the perimeter of a room or designated listening area. The magnetic field is received directly by the **Telecoil (T-coil)** pickup inside a hearing aid, enabling users with hearing impairment to perceive the audio source with significantly improved clarity and Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) compared to acoustic listening in noisy environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AFILS is also designated internationally as an **Induction Loop System** or **Hearing Loop System**, and is governed by the international standard **IEC 60118-4** (the mandatory performance benchmark).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Think of an AFILS as a **"silent, invisible loudspeaker for hearing aids."** A normal loudspeaker pushes air molecules to create sound waves that everyone hears — but a hearing aid user often struggles because the sound mixes with the ambient room noise (echo, chatter, air conditioning). 

An induction loop solves this by:
1. **Translating** the desired audio into a magnetic field instead of air vibrations.
2. The magnetic field is **picked up only by a hearing aid switched to the 'T' (Telecoil) position**.
3. The user's hearing aid then converts this magnetic field *directly* into electrical audio, completely bypassing the noisy room acoustics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are inside a noisy marketplace trying to hear a friend. Instead of shouting, you hand them a personal earpiece plugged into their ear that receives your voice via invisible magnetic waves — that is exactly what an AFILS does for a hearing-impaired listener inside a looped room.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Magnetic Field Strength (RMS) | **100** | mA/m |
| Peak Field Strength (for sine wave) | **400** | mA/m |
| Frequency Response Bandwidth (minimum) | **100 to 5,000** | Hz |
| Recommended Bandwidth | **100 to 8,000** | Hz |
| Loop Impedance Range | **0.5 to 5.0** | Ω |
| Maximum Background Magnetic Noise (RMS) | **-22** | dB re 1 A/m |
| International Compliance Standard | **IEC 60118-4** | — |
| US Compliance Standard | **ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act)** | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The AFILS relies on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** combined with **Ampère's Circuital Law**.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic:

1. **Audio Capture:** A microphone or direct audio feed (e.g., from a public address mixer) captures the desired speech signal $v_{in}(t)$.
2. **Amplification:** A specialized low-impedance, current-output **Loop Amplifier (Loop Driver)** boosts the audio signal to a high current level $i_{loop}(t)$.
3. **Magnetic Field Generation:** This current $i_{loop}(t)$ flows through a closed loop of wire that runs around the perimeter of the listening area. By Ampère's Law, this current generates an **alternating magnetic field** $H(t)$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop.
4. **Reception via Telecoil:** A hearing aid user switches their device to the 'T' position. The T-coil inside the hearing aid is a small induction coil (typically thousands of turns of fine wire around a permeable ferrite core). By Faraday's Law, the changing magnetic flux $\Phi(t)$ through the T-coil induces an EMF (voltage) in the coil windings.
5. **Audible Reproduction:** This induced voltage is amplified by the hearing aid's internal amplifier and converted to sound via the hearing aid's receiver (miniature loudspeaker), delivering the audio directly into the user's ear canal.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Magnetic Transmission:** Acoustic transmission in air degrades with distance (inverse-square law), reflections, and ambient noise. Magnetic field coupling, however, is **contained** within the loop boundary, immune to room acoustics, and provides a flat frequency response inside the coverage zone.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of the System

### 2.2.1 Magnetic Field from a Loop (Ampère's Law Application)

For a simple circular loop of radius $r$ carrying current $I$, the magnetic field strength $H$ at the **center** of the loop is:

$$H = \frac{I}{2r}$$

For a **rectangular perimeter loop** with width $w$ and length $l$, the field strength at the geometric center is approximately:

$$H \approx \frac{I}{\pi \sqrt{w^2 + l^2}} \cdot N$$

where $N$ is the number of turns (usually $N = 1$ for perimeter loops, $N = 2$ or more for figure-8 or phased-array loops).

### 2.2.2 Induced Voltage in the Telecoil (Faraday's Law)

The voltage induced in the T-coil is governed by:

$$V_{TC} = -N_{TC} \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

where the magnetic flux through the T-coil is:

$$\Phi = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot H \cdot A_{TC}$$

Combining these, for a sinusoidal magnetic field $H(t) = H_0 \sin(\omega t)$:

$$V_{TC} = -N_{TC} \cdot \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot A_{TC} \cdot \omega \cdot H_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

The RMS induced voltage is therefore:

$$V_{TC,RMS} = N_{TC} \cdot \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot A_{TC} \cdot \omega \cdot H_{RMS}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The induced voltage is **proportional to frequency** ($\omega = 2\pi f$). This means the loop system must apply a **pre-emphasis correction** (high-frequency boost in the amplifier) to compensate for the natural $+6$ dB/octave slope of the T-coil, ensuring flat audio response from $100$ Hz to $8$ kHz.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Loop magnetic field (center, circular) | $H = \dfrac{I}{2r}$ | Circular loop design |
| Loop magnetic field (center, rectangular) | $H \approx \dfrac{I \cdot N}{\pi \sqrt{w^2 + l^2}}$ | Perimeter loop design |
| Induced voltage in T-coil | $V_{TC} = N_{TC} \mu_0 \mu_r A_{TC} \omega H$ | Telecoil sensitivity |
| Reference field strength (RMS) | $H_{ref} = 100\ \text{mA/m}$ | IEC 60118-4 target |
| Peak field strength (sine) | $H_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot H_{RMS} = 400\ \text{mA/m}$ | Amplifier saturation limit |
| Required loop current | $I_{loop} = H_{target} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{w^2+l^2}}{N}$ | Amplifier sizing |
| Frequency response (T-coil slope) | $+6\ \text{dB/octave}$ | Pre-emphasis design |
| Wire resistance (copper loop) | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A_{wire}}$ | Power loss calculation |
| Maximum spillover attenuation | $\geq 24\ \text{dB}$ outside loop | Privacy/compliance |
| Background noise limit (RMS) | $\leq -22\ \text{dB}\ \text{re}\ 1\ \text{A/m}$ | IEC compliance |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Venues:** Theatres, lecture halls, airports, banks, places of worship, and hospitals.
- **Transportation:** Ticket counters, airport gates, train information desks.
- **Teleconferencing & Courtrooms:** Where legal accuracy of speech is critical.
- **Home Use:** Personal listening loops installed under cushions or around TV sets.
- **Drive-throughs & Taxis:** Vehicle-mounted loops for hearing aid users at service windows.
- **Niche Use:** Museum audio guides via portable neck-loop receivers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Magnetic Field Strength of a Rectangular Perimeter Loop

### Objective
Derive the magnetic field strength $H$ at the center of a rectangular loop carrying current $I$ with $N$ turns.

### Given
- Loop dimensions: width $w$, length $l$
- Loop current: $I$
- Number of turns: $N$
- Loop material: Non-magnetic (air core), so $\mu_r \approx 1$

### Step 1: Express the geometry

Each straight segment of the rectangular loop is a finite straight wire of finite length. We must integrate the **Biot-Savart contribution** from each segment.

The magnetic field at distance $r$ from a finite straight wire of length $L$ carrying current $I$ is:

$$H = \frac{I}{4\pi r} \left( \sin\theta_1 + \sin\theta_2 \right)$$

where $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are the angles from the perpendicular to the endpoints of the wire.

### Step 2: Apply symmetry

For a rectangular loop with center at origin, each side is at perpendicular distance $w/2$ from the center (for the two "short" sides) or $l/2$ (for the two "long" sides). By symmetry, the perpendicular components of the field from opposite sides **add**, while the parallel components **cancel**.

### Step 3: Evaluate field from one short side

The short side has length $w$. The perpendicular distance from center is $w/2$. End angles are:

$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{l/2}{w/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{l}{w}\right)$$

Therefore, contribution from one short side:

$$H_{short} = \frac{I}{4\pi (w/2)} \cdot 2\sin\left(\tan^{-1}\frac{l}{w}\right) = \frac{I}{2\pi w} \cdot \frac{l}{\sqrt{w^2+l^2}}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate field from one long side

By symmetry (swapping $w \leftrightarrow l$):

$$H_{long} = \frac{I}{2\pi l} \cdot \frac{w}{\sqrt{w^2+l^2}}$$

### Step 5: Sum all four sides

There are 2 short sides and 2 long sides. Total field at center:

$$H = N \cdot \left[ 2 H_{short} + 2 H_{long} \right]$$

$$H = N \cdot \frac{I}{\pi \sqrt{w^2+l^2}} \left[ \frac{l}{w} \cdot \frac{1}{1} + \frac{w}{l} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

Wait, let us redo the algebra carefully:

$$H = 2 \cdot \frac{I}{2\pi w} \cdot \frac{l}{\sqrt{w^2+l^2}} + 2 \cdot \frac{I}{2\pi l} \cdot \frac{w}{\sqrt{w^2+l^2}}$$

$$H = \frac{I}{\pi w} \cdot \frac{l}{\sqrt{w^2+l^2}} + \frac{I}{\pi l} \cdot \frac{w}{\sqrt{w^2+l^2}}$$

$$H = \frac{I}{\pi \sqrt{w^2+l^2}} \cdot \left( \frac{l}{w} + \frac{w}{l} \right)$$

### Step 6: Simplify to canonical form

The expression inside parentheses simplifies:

$$\frac{l}{w} + \frac{w}{l} = \frac{l^2 + w^2}{w \cdot l}$$

Therefore:

$$H = \frac{I \cdot (w^2 + l^2)}{\pi \sqrt{w^2+l^2} \cdot w \cdot l} \cdot N$$

$$H = \frac{I \cdot N}{\pi \cdot w \cdot l} \cdot \sqrt{w^2 + l^2}$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{H = \frac{I \cdot N \cdot \sqrt{w^2+l^2}}{\pi \cdot w \cdot l}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** This is the formula used by loop installers to size the amplifier. If $H_{target} = 100$ mA/m is desired at the center of a $10$ m $\times$ $20$ m room, then $I \approx 314$ mA for a single-turn ($N=1$) loop.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Loop Current from IEC 60118-4 Compliance

### Objective
Compute the amplifier current needed to deliver **$100$ mA/m RMS** at the geometric center of a $8\ \text{m} \times 12\ \text{m}$ rectangular loop, single-turn ($N=1$).

### Step 1: Identify target field

$$H_{target} = 100\ \text{mA/m} = 0.1\ \text{A/m}$$

### Step 2: Substitute dimensions

Let $w = 8\ \text{m}$, $l = 12\ \text{m}$, $N = 1$.

$$\sqrt{w^2+l^2} = \sqrt{64+144} = \sqrt{208} \approx 14.42\ \text{m}$$

$$\pi \cdot w \cdot l = \pi \cdot 8 \cdot 12 = 96\pi \approx 301.59\ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 3: Solve for $I$

Rearranging $H = \dfrac{I \cdot N \cdot \sqrt{w^2+l^2}}{\pi \cdot w \cdot l}$:

$$I = \frac{H_{target} \cdot \pi \cdot w \cdot l}{N \cdot \sqrt{w^2+l^2}}$$

### Step 4: Plug in values

$$I = \frac{0.1 \cdot 301.59}{1 \cdot 14.42}$$

$$I = \frac{30.159}{14.42}$$

$$I \approx 2.09\ \text{A (RMS)}$$

### Step 5: Compute peak current

$$I_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{RMS} = 1.414 \cdot 2.09 \approx 2.96\ \text{A}$$

### Step 6: Compute power dissipated in loop wire

For copper loop wire of length $L_{wire} = 2(w+l) = 2(8+12) = 40\ \text{m}$ and cross-section $2.5\ \text{mm}^2$, resistance per meter at $20^\circ$C is approximately $0.007\ \Omega/\text{m}$:

$$R_{loop} = 0.007 \cdot 40 = 0.28\ \Omega$$

$$P_{loop} = I_{RMS}^2 \cdot R_{loop} = (2.09)^2 \cdot 0.28 \approx 1.22\ \text{W}$$

### Result:
A $2.1$ A RMS amplifier with output stage capable of $3$ A peak into $0.28\ \Omega$ is required. This is well within the capability of a mid-range Class-D loop driver.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Loop Topology Comparison

| Topology | Coverage Pattern | Field Uniformity | Spillover Control | Installation Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Perimeter Loop** | Whole room | Poor (drops 50% at edges) | None | Low |
| **Phased Array Loop** | Targeted (e.g., teller window) | Excellent in zone | Excellent | Medium |
| **Figure-8 Loop** | Two opposing zones | Sharp null in center | Very High | Medium |
| **Multi-turn Loop** | Whole room (low current) | Same as perimeter | None | Low |
| **Superior Loop** | Overhead ceiling loop | Excellent (vertical component) | Excellent | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AFILS End-to-End System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Audio Source A Microphone or PA Mix"]
    srcB["Line Level Input 0 dBu Reference"]
    preamp["Pre Amplifier Stage"]
    eq["Pre Emphasis EQ Plus 6 dB Per Octave"]
    loopdrv["Loop Driver Class D Amplifier"]
    loopwire["Perimeter Loop Wire Single Turn or Multi Turn"]
    magfield["Alternating Magnetic Field H Field"]
    tcoil["Telecoil T Coil Inside Hearing Aid"]
    haamp["Hearing Aid Amplifier and Receiver"]
    eardrum["User Eardrum Audio Output"]

    srcA --> preamp
    srcB --> preamp
    preamp --> eq
    eq --> loopdrv
    loopdrv --> loopwire
    loopwire --> magfield
    magfield --> tcoil
    tcoil --> haamp
    haamp --> eardrum
```

## 4.2 Coverage Zone and Spillover Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    room["Rectangular Listening Room W by L"]
    loopwire["Loop Wire Routed Along Ceiling or Floor Perimeter"]
    good["High Field Strength Zone Greater Than 100 mA per m RMS"]
    weak["Reduced Field Zone Near Edges Approx 60 percent of center"]
    spill["Spillover Zone Outside Loop Field Decay Below 22 dB"]
    user1["Hearing Aid User Inside Loop T Coil Engaged"]
    user2["Non User Outside Loop No T Coil Reception"]

    room --> loopwire
    loopwire --> good
    loopwire --> weak
    loopwire --> spill
    good --> user1
    spill --> user2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Loop Topology Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Choose AFILS Topology"]
    q1{"Is the venue a single large room?"}
    q2{"Is privacy or spillover control critical?"}
    q3{"Is the listening area a small specific zone such as a counter?"}

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> perimeter["Use Perimeter Loop Simple Low Cost"]
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> q3
    q2 -- No --> multi["Use Multi Turn Perimeter Loop Low Current Drive"]
    q3 -- Yes --> phased["Use Phased Array Loop Vertical Field Component"]
    q3 -- No --> superior["Use Superior Loop Overhead Ceiling Mount"]
```

## 4.4 Signal Processing Chain (Block-Level Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mic["Input Stage Balanced Microphone or Line In"]
    bpf["Bandpass Filter 100 Hz to 8 kHz"]
    comp["Automatic Gain Control Compressor Limiter"]
    preemph["Pre Emphasis Network Plus 6 dB Per Octave High Frequency Boost"]
    classd["Class D Current Amplifier Low Output Impedance"]
    sense["Current Sense Resistor Feedback Loop for Constant Current"]
    loopout["Loop Output Terminals to Perimeter Cable"]
    monitor["Headphone Monitor Output for Operator Verification"]

    mic --> bpf
    bpf --> comp
    comp --> preemph
    preemph --> classd
    sense --> classd
    classd --> loopout
    loopout --> monitor
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define an **Audio Frequency Induction Loop System (AFILS)**. List the international standard governing its performance and the reference magnetic field strength it mandates.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An Audio Frequency Induction Loop System (AFILS) is an assistive listening system that transmits an audio signal as an alternating magnetic field within the audio frequency band (typically 100 Hz to 8 kHz) using a loop of wire installed along the perimeter of a listening area. The magnetic field is received by the telecoil (T-coil) inside a hearing aid, allowing hearing-impaired users to listen directly without being affected by ambient room acoustics. It is governed by the **IEC 60118-4** international standard, which mandates a reference field strength of **100 mA/m RMS** (or 400 mA/m peak) at the center of the looped area.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is a **pre-emphasis network of +6 dB/octave** used in a loop driver amplifier?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The pre-emphasis network compensates for the natural frequency response of the hearing aid's telecoil. According to Faraday's law, the voltage induced in the T-coil is proportional to the angular frequency ($\omega = 2\pi f$) of the magnetic field, resulting in a +6 dB/octave rising slope. Without pre-emphasis, high frequencies would be disproportionately louder than low frequencies, producing an unnatural sound. The pre-emphasis applies a +6 dB/octave **boost to high frequencies in the amplifier** (or equivalently, attenuation to lows), so that the **overall response at the eardrum is flat** across the audio band, ensuring faithful audio reproduction.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** 

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **working principle of an Audio Frequency Induction Loop System**. Discuss the role of each block. (7 marks)

**(b)** A rectangular room measures **15 m × 25 m**. A single-turn perimeter loop ($N = 1$) is to be installed. Calculate the **loop current required** to produce the IEC 60118-4 reference field strength of 100 mA/m RMS at the center. Also compute the **peak field strength** in mA/m and the **peak loop current** in amperes. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

The AFILS consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Audio Source (1 mark):** A microphone, line-level feed, or PA system output provides the audio signal. May include direct connection to a television, telephone, or conference mixer.

2. **Pre-Amplifier (1 mark):** Boosts the low-level microphone signal to line level. Includes balanced input for noise rejection over long cable runs.

3. **Pre-Emphasis Network (1 mark):** Applies a +6 dB/octave high-frequency boost to compensate for the natural frequency-dependent response of the telecoil (as derived in Section 3).

4. **Compressor / Limiter (1 mark):** Prevents the loop driver from being driven into clipping on loud transients, which would cause distortion heard directly in hearing aids. Maintains intelligible speech by keeping peak levels controlled.

5. **Loop Driver (Class-D Current Amplifier) (2 marks):** Converts the pre-emphasized voltage signal into a high-current output, typically 2–10 A RMS, into the very low-impedance loop (often 0.2–5 Ω). Uses current feedback (not voltage feedback) so that field strength is independent of loop impedance variations.

6. **Perimeter Loop (1 mark):** A closed loop of copper wire (usually 1.5–2.5 mm² cross-section) routed along the floor, ceiling, or skirting of the listening area.

**Working Principle:** The audio current in the loop generates a uniform alternating magnetic field inside the loopped area. Hearing aid users with T-coils switch to 'T' mode and receive the signal directly via induction, completely bypassing room reverberation and ambient noise.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $w = 15$ m, $l = 25$ m, $N = 1$, $H_{target} = 100$ mA/m = $0.1$ A/m.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute geometric term

$$\sqrt{w^2 + l^2} = \sqrt{15^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{225 + 625} = \sqrt{850} \approx 29.15\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute product

$$\pi \cdot w \cdot l = \pi \cdot 15 \cdot 25 = 375\pi \approx 1178.10\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply formula

$$I = \frac{H_{target} \cdot \pi \cdot w \cdot l}{N \cdot \sqrt{w^2+l^2}}$$

$$I = \frac{0.1 \cdot 1178.10}{1 \cdot 29.15} = \frac{117.81}{29.15}$$

$$\boxed{I_{RMS} \approx 4.04\ \text{A}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Peak field strength

$$H_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot H_{RMS} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 100 \approx 141.4\ \text{mA/m}$$

Wait — note: the IEC standard specifies the **average reference field** as 100 mA/m RMS. The peak is $100\sqrt{2} = 141.4$ mA/m for a pure sine. However, the standard also uses a **speech-weighted 400 mA/m peak** to ensure adequate margin for speech peaks.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Peak loop current

$$I_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{RMS} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 4.04 \approx 5.71\ \text{A}$$

**Final Answer:** The loop driver must deliver approximately **4.04 A RMS** (or **5.71 A peak**) to achieve the IEC 60118-4 reference field strength at the center of the room.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the **Perimeter Loop**, **Phased Array Loop**, and **Figure-8 Loop** topologies in terms of coverage, uniformity, spillover control, and typical applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **voltage induced in a hearing aid telecoil** placed at the center of a circular loop of radius $r$ carrying sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$. Assume the telecoil has $N_{TC}$ turns, cross-sectional area $A_{TC}$, and is wound on a ferrite core of relative permeability $\mu_r$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Comparative Study (7 marks):**

| Topology | Coverage (2 marks) | Uniformity (2 marks) | Spillover (1.5 marks) | Application (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Perimeter Loop** | Covers entire room uniformly (lowest uniform field is 60% of center value, still exceeds 100 mA/m). | Field strength is highest at center and drops toward edges; falls as $1/r^2$ vertically. | Poor — magnetic field leaks well beyond the room. | Theatres, lecture halls, places of worship, airports. |
| **Phased Array Loop** | Two parallel loops driven in phase produce a **vertical H-field** with sharp nulls outside the looped region. | Very uniform within the targeted zone. | Excellent — field decays by **≥ 24 dB** within 1.5 m of loop edge. | Bank teller windows, ticket counters, private consultation desks. |
| **Figure-8 Loop** | Two loops arranged in figure-of-eight produce opposing field directions, creating a sharp null at the crossover. | Uniform along the long axis of the figure-8. | Excellent — strong privacy by design. | Adjacent rooms sharing a wall where spillover must be avoided (e.g., adjacent courtrooms). |

**Part (b) — Derivation of Induced Voltage (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Magnetic field at center of circular loop of radius $r$:

$$H(t) = \frac{i(t)}{2r} = \frac{I_0 \sin(\omega t)}{2r}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Magnetic flux density $B$ in ferrite core:

$$B(t) = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot H(t) = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I_0 \sin(\omega t)}{2r}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Magnetic flux through one turn of T-coil:

$$\Phi(t) = B(t) \cdot A_{TC} = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I_0 A_{TC} \sin(\omega t)}{2r}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Induced EMF by Faraday's Law:

$$V_{TC}(t) = -N_{TC} \cdot \frac{d\Phi(t)}{dt}$$

$$V_{TC}(t) = -N_{TC} \cdot \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I_0 A_{TC}}{2r} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

$$V_{TC}(t) = -\frac{N_{TC} \cdot \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot I_0 \cdot A_{TC} \cdot \omega}{2r} \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Peak voltage:

$$V_{TC,peak} = \frac{N_{TC} \cdot \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot I_0 \cdot A_{TC} \cdot \omega}{2r}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** RMS voltage:

$$V_{TC,RMS} = \frac{V_{TC,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{N_{TC} \cdot \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot I_0 \cdot A_{TC} \cdot \omega}{2\sqrt{2} \cdot r}$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{TC}(t) = -\frac{N_{TC} \mu_0 \mu_r A_{TC} \omega I_0}{2r} \cos(\omega t)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to multiply by $\sqrt{2}$ when converting RMS to peak, leading to a 2-mark loss on the numerical question.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In derivation questions, students frequently skip the intermediate step showing $\Phi = B \cdot A$ and jump directly from $H$ to $V$, losing 2 marks for missing the **flux linkage** concept.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to state **Ampère's Law** and **Faraday's Law** explicitly by name. Examiners award 1 mark each for naming these laws in the working principle.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Writing the loop field formula as $H = I/r$ (missing the factor of 2). The correct formula is $H = I/(2r)$ at the center of a circular loop.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **H** (magnetic field strength, A/m) with **B** (magnetic flux density, Tesla). Use $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AFILS** transmits audio as an **alternating magnetic field** picked up by a **telecoil (T-coil)** in hearing aids.
- Governing standard: **IEC 60118-4**, with reference field **100 mA/m RMS**.
- Working band: **100 Hz to 8 kHz** (preferably up to 8 kHz).
- Magnetic field of a circular loop at its center: $H = I / (2r)$.
- Magnetic field of a rectangular loop at its center: $H \approx I \cdot N \cdot \sqrt{w^2+l^2} / (\pi \cdot w \cdot l)$.
- Induced voltage in T-coil: $V_{TC} \propto \omega \cdot H$ (proportional to frequency).
- **+6 dB/octave pre-emphasis** is mandatory in the amplifier to flatten the T-coil's natural rising slope.
- A **Class-D current-mode amplifier** is preferred for its high efficiency and constant-current behavior.
- **Three main loop topologies:** Perimeter, Phased Array, Figure-8.
- **Phased arrays** provide the best **spillover control** (≥ 24 dB attenuation outside loop).
- **Perimeter loops** give the widest coverage but spill magnetic field outside the room.
- **Background magnetic noise** must be ≤ **−22 dB re 1 A/m** for compliance.
- A **compressor/limiter** is required to prevent amplifier clipping that would distort hearing aid output.
- Copper loop wire resistance: typically $0.2$ to $1\ \Omega$ — the amplifier must be current-output, not voltage-output.
- Loop current for IEC compliance is typically **2 to 10 A RMS**, depending on room size.
- **Telecoil sensitivity** is standardized as a T-coil **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** figure.
- **Loop amplifiers** often include a **headphone monitor** so operators can verify signal quality directly.
- **NEC / Earthing considerations:** Loop wires should not be run parallel to and close to mains cables to avoid 50/60 Hz hum injection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IR Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IR Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are IR Systems in the Context of Assistive Medical Devices?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)**
> An **Infrared (IR) System**, in the context of assistive medical devices, refers to any engineered biomedical system that utilizes electromagnetic radiation in the infrared wavelength region (typically **0.7 µm to 1000 µm**) to perform diagnostic, therapeutic, monitoring, or compensatory functions for individuals with physical or sensory impairments. These systems exploit the unique interaction of IR radiation with biological tissues — including emission, reflection, transmission, and absorption — to deliver non-invasive clinical support.

The infrared spectrum is conventionally subdivided into three biologically relevant bands:

| Band | Wavelength Range | Primary Medical/Assistive Utility |
|------|------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Near-Infrared (NIR)** | 0.7 µm – 1.4 µm | Pulse oximetry, NIR spectroscopy (NIRS), IR remote communication |
| **Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR)** | 1.4 µm – 3 µm | Tissue imaging, vein detection |
| **Mid-Wave Infrared (MWIR)** | 3 µm – 8 µm | Thermal imaging, fever screening |
| **Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR / Thermal IR)** | 8 µm – 15 µm | Thermography, night-vision aids, physiotherapy |
| **Far-Infrared (FIR)** | 15 µm – 1000 µm | Therapeutic heating, FIR saunas, wound healing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The human body emits peak IR radiation at approximately **9.4 µm** (per **Wien's Displacement Law** at skin temperature of ~306 K). This is why LWIR cameras (8–14 µm) are the dominant choice for medical thermography and assistive IR vision systems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heat-Vision Goggles" Mental Model

Imagine you are standing in a completely dark room wearing a special pair of goggles. You cannot see with your eyes, but through the goggles, you can see the warm outline of every person and object — a glowing map of heat signatures. This is precisely what an **IR thermal camera** does.

Now extend this analogy to assistive technology:

- 🔥 **A blind user wearing an IR-assisted navigation aid** perceives warm human bodies and warm doorways in pitch darkness — just like the goggles.
- 💉 **A nurse using an IR vein finder** sees a high-contrast thermal map of veins glowing beneath the skin, making needle insertion effortless.
- 🩺 **A physiotherapist using a thermographic camera** detects inflammation (hot spots) in a patient's inflamed joint long before it becomes visually obvious.
- ♨️ **A patient with arthritis receiving FIR therapy** experiences deep-tissue warming as the invisible IR energy penetrates the skin and is absorbed by muscles and joints.

In every case, the underlying principle is the same: **IR radiation is invisible to the human eye, but a properly engineered sensor system can convert it into information, images, or therapeutic heat** — making IR an exceptionally versatile tool in the assistive medical device ecosystem.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Memory)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| **c** | Speed of light in vacuum | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| **h** | Planck's constant | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| **k_B** | Boltzmann constant | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| **σ** | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | **5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** |
| **T_skin** | Average human skin temperature | **306 K (33 °C)** |
| **λ_max,skin** | Peak IR emission wavelength of skin | **≈ 9.4 µm** |
| **ε_skin** | Human skin emissivity | **≈ 0.98** |

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the human skin has an **emissivity of 0.98** — almost a perfect blackbody (1.0). This is why thermal cameras can reliably measure skin temperature without complex emissivity correction, and why IR thermography is so effective for medical diagnosis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of IR Radiation

IR systems operate on four foundational physical phenomena:

### (A) Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation
Every object above absolute zero (0 K) emits electromagnetic radiation with a spectral distribution described by Planck's law. For a perfect blackbody:

$$B_{\lambda}(T) = \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{\frac{h c}{\lambda k_{B} T}} - 1}$$

Where:
- $B_{\lambda}(T)$ = Spectral radiance (W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·m⁻¹)
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength (m)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $c$ = Speed of light = **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

### (B) Wien's Displacement Law
This law tells us **at which wavelength** a blackbody emits most intensely:

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

**Why this matters for assistive devices:** For human skin at $T = 306$ K, the peak emission is:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{306} \approx 9.47 \text{ µm}$$

This falls squarely in the **LWIR (8–14 µm)** atmospheric window — making uncooled LWIR microbolometer cameras the dominant technology for medical thermography.

### (C) Stefan-Boltzmann Law
The total radiant exitance (power emitted per unit area) is:

$$M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

Where $\varepsilon$ is the emissivity (0 to 1) and $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.

**Real-world utility:** A small change in skin temperature (e.g., due to inflammation) produces a measurable change in emitted IR power, which is what a thermal camera detects. A 1 K rise in skin temperature increases emitted power by approximately:

$$\frac{\Delta M}{M} \approx 4 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{306} \approx 1.3\%$$

This 1.3% change is well within the detection capability of modern uncooled IR detectors (Noise Equivalent Temperature Difference, **NETD < 50 mK**).

### (D) Beer-Lambert Law (for NIR Spectroscopy in Brain Monitoring)
For Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS), used in brain-computer interfaces and cerebral oxygenation monitoring:

$$I = I_{0} \cdot e^{-\mu_{a} \cdot d}$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = Incident light intensity
- $I$ = Transmitted/reflected intensity
- $\mu_a$ = Absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹)
- $d$ = Optical pathlength (cm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "optical window" of biological tissue lies between **650 nm and 1300 nm**, where light penetrates deepest. This is why NIR (700–900 nm) is preferred for brain oximetry and muscle oxygenation monitoring.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> This cheat-sheet table compiles every equation you are expected to know for KTU assessments on IR systems. Memorize the symbol definitions and units.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Units | Application in Assistive Devices |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Planck's Law | $B_\lambda = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$ | $B_\lambda$: spectral radiance | W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·m⁻¹ | Sensor calibration |
| 2 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\lambda_{max}$: peak wavelength | m·K | Choosing detector band |
| 3 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | $M$: radiant exitance, $\varepsilon$: emissivity | W·m⁻² | Thermal imaging physics |
| 4 | Beer-Lambert Law (modified) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot d$ | $A$: absorbance, $c$: concentration | dimensionless | Pulse oximetry, NIRS |
| 5 | Sensor Responsivity | $\Re = \frac{V_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Ratio of output voltage to input power | V/W | IR detector characterization |
| 6 | NETD | $NETD = \frac{4 \sqrt{ab \cdot \Delta f}}{A \cdot \Re \cdot \tau \cdot (dM/dT)}$ | $a,b$: pixel dimensions, $A$: area, $\tau$: transmission | mK (millikelvin) | Camera sensitivity |
| 7 | Heat Transfer by FIR Therapy | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | $m$: mass, $c$: specific heat | J | Dosimetry in physiotherapy |
| 8 | Penetration Depth (FIR) | $\delta = \sqrt{\frac{k}{\rho c \omega}}$ | $k$: thermal conductivity, $\rho$: density, $\omega$: angular freq. | m | Therapeutic heating depth |
| 9 | Pulse Oximetry Ratio | $R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$ | Ratio of ratios | dimensionless | SpO₂ calculation |
| 10 | Photon Energy | $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of one photon | J (or eV) | Sensor selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In the table above, the absolute value notation has been replaced with **parentheses** or **descriptive text** to prevent markdown table syntax corruption. Always use the variable's full descriptive name in exams.

---

## 2.3 Working Principle Block — How an IR System Operates

A generic IR assistive system executes the following pipeline:

1. **IR Source / Emitter Stage** — The patient (body), a FIR lamp, or an external IR LED provides the radiation.
2. **Atmospheric/Optical Path Stage** — Lenses, filters, and mirrors shape and direct the IR beam; atmospheric absorption (mainly by water vapor and CO₂) must be minimized.
3. **Detector Stage** — Converts IR photons into an electrical signal. Common detectors:
   - **Thermopiles** (broadband, uncooled, low cost)
   - **Pyroelectric detectors** (motion-sensitive, used in occupancy aids)
   - **Microbolometers** (uncooled LWIR imaging arrays)
   - **Photodiodes (InGaAs, Ge)** (NIR/SWIR spectroscopy)
   - **Cooled HgCdTe (MCT)** (high-performance MWIR imaging)
4. **Signal Processing Stage** — Amplification, filtering, A/D conversion, and feature extraction.
5. **Display/Actuator Stage** — Visual display, audio cue, vibration feedback, or therapeutic heating delivery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Peak Emission Wavelength of Human Skin

We want to derive the IR wavelength at which human skin emits the maximum radiation. This is essential for selecting the correct detector band for medical thermography cameras.

### Step 1 — State Wien's Displacement Law
Wien's displacement law in its standard form is:

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b$$

Where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K is Wien's displacement constant. This is a direct consequence of differentiating Planck's law with respect to $\lambda$ and setting the derivative to zero.

### Step 2 — Solve for $\lambda_{max}$

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Skin Temperature
The average surface temperature of healthy human skin is:

$$T_{skin} = 306 \text{ K} \quad (33\,^\circ\text{C})$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}{306 \text{ K}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{306}$$

Performing the long division explicitly:

$$\lambda_{max} = 0.009470 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = 9.4705 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 9.47 \text{ µm}}$$

### Step 5 — Physical Interpretation
This wavelength falls inside the **LWIR atmospheric transmission window (8–14 µm)**, where atmospheric attenuation of IR radiation is minimal. Consequently, an uncooled microbolometer camera operating in the 8–14 µm band will capture the strongest possible signal from a human body, making it the **technology of choice** for fever screening, breast thermography, vascular disorder diagnosis, and assistive IR vision devices.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Radiant Power Emitted by Human Skin

We want to compute the total IR power emitted per square meter of human skin, then estimate the total radiated power from an average human body.

### Step 1 — State Stefan-Boltzmann Law
For a real (non-ideal) surface:

$$M = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^{4}$$

### Step 2 — Identify Skin Emissivity
The emissivity of human skin in the LWIR band is exceptionally high (essentially a near-blackbody):

$$\varepsilon_{skin} \approx 0.98$$

This is because biological tissue strongly absorbs IR radiation across the LWIR range due to water content.

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$M = 0.98 \cdot (5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}) \cdot (306 \text{ K})^{4}$$

### Step 4 — Compute $T^4$ Step-by-Step

$$T^{2} = 306^{2} = 93636 \text{ K}^{2}$$

$$T^{4} = (93636)^{2} = 8.7668 \times 10^{9} \text{ K}^{4}$$

### Step 5 — Multiply Constants

$$M = 0.98 \cdot 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 8.7668 \times 10^{9}$$

Compute the constants' product first:

$$5.670 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 8.7668 \times 10^{9} = 5.670 \cdot 8.7668 \cdot 10^{1} = 49.708 \cdot 10^{1} = 497.08 \text{ W·m}^{-2}$$

Then apply emissivity:

$$M = 0.98 \cdot 497.08 = 487.13 \text{ W·m}^{-2}$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$\boxed{M_{skin} \approx 487 \text{ W·m}^{-2}}$$

### Step 7 — Total Body Radiated Power
For an average adult body surface area of approximately $A = 1.75$ m²:

$$P_{total} = M \cdot A = 487 \cdot 1.75 \approx 852 \text{ W}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Insight:** This calculation explains why a thermal camera can easily detect humans in complete darkness — a human radiates roughly **850 watts** of IR power, equivalent to a small space heater! This is more than sufficient signal for modern uncooled microbolometer sensors with NETD ≈ 50 mK.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity of Emitted Power to Skin Temperature

This derivation demonstrates why IR thermography is clinically useful — small temperature changes produce detectable IR signal changes.

### Step 1 — Differentiate Stefan-Boltzmann Law
For a constant emissivity:

$$\frac{dM}{dT} = 4 \varepsilon \sigma T^{3}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Derivative at $T = 306$ K

$$\frac{dM}{dT} = 4 \cdot 0.98 \cdot 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \cdot (306)^{3}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $306^3$

$$306^{3} = 306 \cdot 306 \cdot 306 = 93636 \cdot 306 = 28,652,616 \text{ K}^{3} \approx 2.865 \times 10^{7} \text{ K}^{3}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply All Factors

$$\frac{dM}{dT} = 4 \cdot 0.98 \cdot 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 2.865 \times 10^{7}$$

$$= 3.92 \cdot 5.670 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 2.865 \cdot 10^{7}$$

$$= 3.92 \cdot 5.670 \cdot 2.865 \cdot 10^{-1}$$

Computing the numeric product:

$$3.92 \cdot 5.670 = 22.226$$

$$22.226 \cdot 2.865 = 63.677$$

$$63.677 \cdot 10^{-1} = 6.368$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{dM}{dT} \approx 6.37 \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-1}}$$

### Step 5 — Physical Meaning
A **1 K rise in skin temperature** (e.g., from inflammation) increases the emitted IR power by about **6.37 W per square meter**. Even more importantly, the **fractional change** is:

$$\frac{\Delta M}{M} = \frac{6.37}{487} \approx 0.0131 = 1.31\%$$

This is the fundamental sensitivity that makes IR thermography clinically viable.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which IR System for Which Clinical Need?

| Clinical / Assistive Need | IR Sub-Band | Recommended Detector | Typical Device Example | Key Performance Metric |
|---------------------------|-------------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Fever screening (COVID-style) | LWIR 8–14 µm | Uncooled microbolometer | Handheld thermal camera | ±0.5 °C accuracy |
| Blind/low-vision navigation | LWIR 8–14 µm | Microbolometer array | IR vision aid | Frame rate ≥ 9 Hz |
| Vein visualization | NIR 850 nm | CMOS/CCD + IR LED | Vein finder | Contrast ≥ 2:1 |
| Brain oxygenation | NIR 700–900 nm | Silicon photodiode | fNIRS headset | SpO₂ ± 2% |
| Pulse oximetry | Red (660 nm) + NIR (940 nm) | Dual photodiode | Fingertip pulse oximeter | SpO₂ ± 2% |
| Arthritis physiotherapy | FIR 15–100 µm | FIR ceramic emitter | FIR lamp / sauna | Penetration 2–4 cm |
| Wound healing | FIR 5–25 µm | FIR emitter pad | Therapeutic FIR blanket | Temperature 40–45 °C |
| Diabetic foot screening | LWIR 8–14 µm | Thermal array camera | Podiatric thermography | ΔT sensitivity 0.3 °C |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic IR-Based Assistive Medical Device

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Body Surface or IR Source] --> B[IR Radiation Emission Stage]
    B --> C[Optical Collection Stage - Lens and Filter Assembly]
    C --> D[Atmospheric Path - Minimal Water Vapor and CO2 Absorption]
    D --> E[IR Detector Array - Microbolometer or Photodiode]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning - Amplification and Filtering]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    G --> H[Digital Signal Processing Unit - DSP or Microcontroller]
    H --> I{Output Mode Selection}
    I --> J[Visual Display - LCD or OLED Screen]
    I --> K[Audio Cue Generation - Buzzer or Speaker]
    I --> L[Tactile Feedback - Vibration Motor]
    I --> M[Therapeutic Heating Delivery - FIR Lamp Driver]

    style A fill:#ffe4b5
    style B fill:#ffd699
    style E fill:#ffb380
    style H fill:#ff9966
    style I fill:#ff8080
    style J fill:#ffb3b3
    style M fill:#ff9999
```

---

## 4.2 Pulse Oximeter Functional Flow

flowchart LR
    A[Red LED 660 nm] --> C[Fingertip Probe]
    B[NIR LED 940 nm] --> C
    C --> D[Photodetector]
    D --> E[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    E --> F[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 5 Hz]
    F --> G[Microcontroller ADC]
    G --> H[SpO2 Calculation Algorithm]
    H --> I[Display Numeric SpO2 and Pulse Rate]

    style A fill:#ffcc99
    style B fill:#cc99ff
    style H fill:#99ccff
    style I fill:#99ff99
```

## 4.3 IR Thermography Camera Signal Processing Chain

    A[Human Body Thermal Emission 9.4 µm Peak] --> B[Germanium Lens LWIR 8 to 14 µm]
    B --> C[Microbolometer Focal Plane Array FPA]

    D --> E[14 bit Analog to Digital Converter]

```
