```mermaid
    L5[Level 5 Smartwatch Wearable]
    L6[Level 6 Smart Home Assistant]
    L7[Level 7 AI Conversational Bot]
    L8[Level 8 Implantable Neural Prosthetic]
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> L8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define Cognitive Assistive Technology. List FOUR examples of devices used to aid memory in patients with cognitive impairment. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cognitive Assistive Technology (CAT)** refers to electronic, mechanical, or computational systems engineered to **augment or substitute impaired cognitive functions** such as memory, attention, planning, and problem-solving, particularly in individuals with neurological injury or neurodegenerative disease.

**Four examples of memory-aid devices:**

1. **NeuroPage System** — one-way pager for prospective memory reminders.
2. **Voice Recorders / Digital Notebooks** — for retrospective recall of conversations.
3. **Smart Pill Dispensers** (e.g., MedMinder) — timed medication reminders with caregiver alerts.
4. **Smart Home Assistants** (Amazon Echo / Google Home) — voice-activated memory retrieval for dementia patients.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Smartphones with reminder apps, SenseCam lifelogging cameras, smartwatches with vibratory cues.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between Prospective and Retrospective Memory Aids with one example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Prospective Memory Aid | Retrospective Memory Aid |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Helps user remember to perform a **future** intended task | Helps user **retrieve** previously stored information |
| **Brain Function Substituted** | Future-intention recall (hippocampus-dependent) | Past-event recall (episodic memory) |
| **Example Device** | NeuroPage Pager, Pill Dispenser | Voice Recorder, SenseCam, Photo Diary |
| **Trigger Type** | Time-based or event-based cue | User-initiated retrieval |

---

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**(a)** Explain the **engineering architecture and operational working** of an Electronic Memory Aid (EMA) with a neat functional block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **NeuroPage system** in detail — its working principle, clinical evidence, and limitations as a memory aid. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer to (a):**

The **Electronic Memory Aid (EMA)** is a purpose-built or software-defined device engineered to **offload the cognitive burden of memory** from a neurologically impaired user onto a reliable technological system.

**Functional Architecture (Four Modules):**

**1. Input Module:**
- Voice microphone for hands-free entry
- Manual keypad/touchscreen for caregiver programming
- Real-Time Clock (RTC) for time-triggered cues
- Optional caregiver web portal (cloud input)

**2. Storage Module:**
- Non-volatile flash memory (**≥ 8 GB**)
- Stores structured tasks: **{Task Description, Trigger Time, Repeat Pattern, Priority}**
- Holds user profile, preferences, and medication schedule

**3. Processing Module:**
- Microcontroller/SoC (e.g., ARM Cortex-M class)
- Runs **scheduling algorithm** comparing RTC time to stored tasks
- **Priority engine** ranks pending cues
- **Escalation logic** triggers caregiver alerts on missed acknowledgements

**4. Output Module:**
- LCD/LED display for textual cue
- Speaker/buzzer delivering ≥ **65 dB SPL** alert
- Vibration motor for tactile cue (essential for hearing-impaired)
- GSM/Wi-Fi module for caregiver SMS/app push

**Operational Working:**
The caregiver or user pre-loads tasks via input. At the preset time, the RTC fires an interrupt, the processor retrieves the matching task, and the output module delivers a multi-modal cue. The user acknowledges via button — completing a **closed feedback loop**. Missed acknowledgements escalate to caregivers.

---

**Model Answer to (b):**

**NeuroPage System — Detailed Discussion:**

**Origin & Principle:**
NeuroPage, developed in the 1990s (pioneered by researchers including **Eva Svendsen** and clinical collaborators), is a **one-way alphanumeric pager system** designed specifically for prospective memory support in patients with **traumatic brain injury (TBI), stroke, and dementia**.

**Working Principle:**
1. A **caregiver or therapist** accesses a central PC-based scheduling software.
2. Tasks/reminders (e.g., "Take your medicine at 2 PM") are entered with date-time.
3. At the scheduled time, the message is transmitted via **paging network** to the patient's pager.
4. The pager **vibrates and displays** the message on a small LCD screen.
5. Patient performs the task and (optionally) confirms.

**Clinical Evidence:**
- **Wilson et al. (1997)** landmark study showed that brain-injured patients completed **up to 80% more intended tasks** with NeuroPage than without.
- Strong evidence base across multiple single-case experimental designs.
- Cited as the **gold-standard** in cognitive prosthetics literature.

**Limitations:**
- **One-way communication** (no acknowledgment of completion).
- Dependent on **paging network coverage** (now declining globally).
- **No multimedia** — text only.
- Requires **caregiver or therapist** to program — not fully user-independent.
- Becoming **obsolete** as paging networks shut down; replaced by smartphone-based equivalents.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**(a)** Classify the various types of **memory aid devices** with examples. Explain any ONE in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cognitive-impaired patient correctly completed **3 out of 10 prescribed daily tasks** without a memory aid. With a smart speaker–based reminder system, completion improved to **9 out of 10**. Calculate the **Recall Accuracy Improvement (ARI)** and comment on its clinical significance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer to (a):**

**Classification of Memory Aid Devices:**

**1. Based on Memory Type Supported:**
- **Prospective Memory Aids** — NeuroPage, Pill Dispensers, Calendar Apps
- **Retrospective Memory Aids** — Voice Recorders, Digital Diaries, SenseCam
- **Episodic Memory Aids** — Lifelogging cameras, GPS-tagged photo apps
- **Procedural Memory Aids** — Step-by-step task sequencers (e.g., COGORTH)
- **Semantic Memory Aids** — Digital contact directories with photos

**2. Based on Form Factor:**
- Handheld, Wearable, Environmental, Implantable

**Detailed Explanation — Smart Home Assistant (e.g., Amazon Echo / Google Home):**

- A **voice-activated**, always-listening smart speaker placed in the user's home.
- The user can **store reminders**: *"Alexa, remind me to take my blood pressure medicine every day at 8 AM."*
- At the trigger time, the device emits an **audible chime and voice announcement**.
- The user can **query** past reminders: *"Alexa, what did I forget yesterday?"*
- Particularly suitable for **mild cognitive impairment and early dementia** because it requires **no manual dexterity** — only voice.
- **Limitations:** Requires Wi-Fi, raises **privacy concerns** (always-listening microphone), and is not portable.

---

**Model Answer to (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$C_{baseline} = 3, \quad C_{device} = 9, \quad N_{total} = 10$$

**Step 1:** Apply the ARI formula.

$$ARI = \dfrac{C_{device} - C_{baseline}}{C_{baseline}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$ARI = \dfrac{9 - 3}{3} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.

$$9 - 3 = 6$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final ratio.

$$ARI = \dfrac{6}{3} \times 100 = 200\%$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result: ARI = 200%** — meaning the smart speaker **tripled** the patient's baseline task completion rate. Clinically, an ARI ≥ 25% is considered significant; **200% is highly significant**, demonstrating that the cognitive aid provides dramatic functional benefit and is strongly indicated for continued prescription.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Memory Aid Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing prospective vs retrospective memory** — Examiners frequently test this. Remember: **prospective = future task**, **retrospective = past event recall.**
> 2. **Forgetting to mention cue modality** — A complete answer must specify whether the device uses **auditory, visual, or tactile** cues (multi-modal cues score higher marks).
> 3. **Skipping battery/network constraints** — Engineers must discuss power, connectivity, and clinical usability constraints.
> 4. **Not citing clinical evidence** — When discussing NeuroPage, ALWAYS cite the **Wilson et al. study** and the **80% improvement figure**.
> 5. **In numerical problems** — Marks are lost when students forget the **×100** conversion to percentage in ARI calculation.
> 6. **Confusing ARI with compliance rate (CR)** — These are different metrics; CR measures **device usage**, ARI measures **task completion improvement**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Devices to Aid Memory**

- **CAT** = Cognitive Assistive Technology — augments impaired cognitive function.
- **Memory aids** are also called **cognitive prosthetics** or **cognitive orthotics**.
- **Two main memory types targeted:** Prospective (future tasks) and Retrospective (past events).
- **Four functional modules** of any EMA: Input → Storage → Processing → Output.
- **NeuroPage** = one-way pager-based prospective memory aid; **gold-standard benchmark**; **80% task improvement** in clinical trials.
- **Smart Home Assistants** (Alexa, Google Home) = voice-activated, best for mild dementia.
- **Smart Pill Dispensers** = locked compartments with timed alerts — best for polypharmacy.
- **Smartwatches** = discreet vibratory cues — best for active adults and tech-aware users.
- **SenseCam** = lifelogging wearable camera for episodic memory reconstruction.
- **ARI formula:** $ARI = \dfrac{C_{device} - C_{baseline}}{C_{baseline}} \times 100$
- **Effective Memory Offload:** $E_{offload} = 1 - \dfrac{M_{user}}{M_{total}}$
- **Minimum alert loudness:** **65 dB SPL**; **Maximum acceptable cue delay:** **5 seconds**.
- **Working memory capacity (biological limit):** **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller).
- **Forgetting curve:** $R = e^{-t/S}$ (Ebbinghaus) — justifies need for external reminders.
- **Target patient populations:** TBI, stroke, Alzheimer's, dementia, Parkinson's, ADHD, intellectual disability.
- **Modern trend:** Integration of **AI/LLM-based conversational bots** for conversational memory retrieval — emerging research area.
- **Design considerations:** Discreetness, battery life, ease of use for elderly, cost, caregiver burden, multi-modal cues.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Time Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time Management** is the strategic process of planning, organizing, allocating, and exercising conscious control over the amount of time spent on specific activities, especially those aimed at increasing effectiveness, productivity, and professional efficiency. In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices (PEEBT744)**, time management refers to the systematic methodology adopted by biomedical engineers, clinical technologists, rehabilitation specialists, and project managers to prioritize tasks related to device design, regulatory documentation, clinical validation, patient interaction, maintenance cycles, and product lifecycle management within constrained healthcare environments.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** professional competency framework, time management is positioned as a transversal employability skill, mapped to **Course Outcome CO4** of Module 4 — *"Appraise the managerial, ethical, and entrepreneurial dimensions of biomedical engineering practice."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):**
> Time Management is grouped under the broader umbrella of *Professional Skills and Project Management in Biomedical Engineering*. Students are expected to demonstrate the ability to plan R&D timelines, clinical trials, and regulatory submissions (like CDSCO / FDA 510(k) pathways) using formal time management frameworks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgical Tray" of Your Day

Imagine a surgeon who has **exactly 4 hours** before an operation. She does not randomly pick instruments; she arranges her tray by:
- **What is urgent** (scalpel, sutures) — done first
- **What is important but not urgent** (sterile drapes, monitoring leads) — prepared in parallel
- **What can be delegated** (assistant arranging the OT) — handed off
- **What can be eliminated** (unused retractors) — removed from the tray

**Your 24 hours is the surgical tray.** Time management is the discipline of laying out your day with the same surgical precision so that critical tasks (like calibrating a ventilator or submitting a CDSCO dossier) are completed on time, important tasks are scheduled, and time-wasters are eliminated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Time Management is the art and science of converting time from a non-renewable, finite resource into a structured, prioritized, and outcome-oriented productivity framework — particularly critical in biomedical engineering where device development cycles, patient safety windows, and regulatory deadlines are non-negotiable."*

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Time Management

| Metric | Standard Value / Convention | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Working hours per day (KTU / WHO standard) | **8** | hours |
| Working days per week | **5 to 6** | days |
| Total productive hours per week | **40 to 48** | hours |
| Parkinson's Constant (work expands) | Indefinite | — |
| Pareto Principle (80/20 rule) | **80% output from 20% input** | ratio |
| Adult attention span (avg.) | **20 to 25** | minutes |
| Eisenhower quadrant cycle | **Urgent × Important** | 2 × 2 matrix |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Time Management (Why & How)

#### **Pillar 1: Clarity (The "What")**
- **Why:** Without knowing what matters, every minute spent is blind effort.
- **How:** Define goals using the **SMART** framework — **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound**.
- Biomedical example: *"Submit the bench-test report for the prosthetic knee prototype by 5 PM Friday"* — meets all five SMART criteria.

#### **Pillar 2: Prioritization (The "Which First")**
- **Why:** Not all tasks are equal. Some have hard deadlines (regulatory), some have high impact (patient safety testing).
- **How:** Apply prioritization matrices — **Eisenhower Matrix**, **ABC Analysis**, **Pareto Principle**, **MoSCoW method**.

#### **Pillar 3: Execution & Review (The "How Well")**
- **Why:** Planning without execution is hallucination.
- **How:** Use **time-blocking**, **Pomodoro technique**, **Gantt charts**, and weekly reviews.

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Models

#### **Model A: The Eisenhower Matrix (Urgent-Important Grid)**

Tasks are classified into four quadrants:

- **Quadrant I — Urgent & Important (DO NOW):** Crises, regulatory deadlines, device recall response.
- **Quadrant II — Important, Not Urgent (SCHEDULE):** R&D brainstorming, long-term design reviews, preventive maintenance.
- **Quadrant III — Urgent, Not Important (DELEGATE):** Routine emails, certain meetings, scheduling logistics.
- **Quadrant IV — Not Urgent & Not Important (ELIMINATE):** Mindless scrolling, redundant meetings, excessive documentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Eisenhower Matrix is the **most frequently tested** time-management framework in KTU ESE. Memorize the four quadrants and their action verbs: **DO, SCHEDULE, DELEGATE, ELIMINATE.**

#### **Model B: The Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**

**Vilfredo Pareto's observation:** Approximately **80% of results come from 20% of efforts.**

In assistive medical device context:
- **20%** of design features deliver **80%** of the patient's functional benefit.
- **20%** of the documentation consumes **80%** of the regulatory submission time.

#### **Model C: ABC Analysis**

Tasks are graded:
- **A tasks (High value, ~60% of results):** Must be done today.
- **B tasks (Medium value, ~30% of results):** Should be done this week.
- **C tasks (Low value, ~10% of results):** Nice to do, can be deferred.

#### **Model D: Parkinson's Law**

> *"Work expands so as to fill the time available for its completion."*

**Implication:** Setting tight, artificial deadlines improves focus and output. A 5-hour task, if allotted 10 hours, will likely consume 10 hours.

#### **Model E: The Pomodoro Technique**

- Work in focused blocks of **25 minutes** (called a *Pomodoro*).
- Take a **5-minute** break.
- After **4 Pomodoros**, take a **15 to 30 minute** longer break.
- Resets attention span, prevents cognitive fatigue during device calibration or documentation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Formula | Expression / Standard | Unit | Application in Biomedical Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time Allocation Ratio | $T_{\text{task}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{total}} \times W_{\text{priority}}}{\sum W_i}$ | hours | Allocating clinical validation vs. documentation time |
| Productivity Index | $P_i = \dfrac{O_{\text{achieved}}}{O_{\text{planned}}} \times \dfrac{T_{\text{planned}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$ | dimensionless | Measuring R&D engineer output |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{value-added}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | percent | % of day on productive biomedical tasks |
| Pareto Output | $O_{80} = 0.8 \cdot O_{\text{total}}$ from $E_{20} = 0.2 \cdot E_{\text{total}}$ | ratio | 80% of device impact from 20% of features |
| Pomodoro Cycle | $T_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \times (25 + 5) - 5 = 115 \text{ min}$ | minutes | Focused device testing blocks |
| Parkinson's Effective Time | $T_{\text{eff}} = k \cdot T_{\text{allotted}}$ where $k \in [0.6, 0.8]$ | hours | Tight deadlines reduce slack |
| SMART Goal Score | $S = \prod_{i=1}^{5} s_i$ where $s_i \in \{0,1\}$ | 0 or 1 | Binary check on goal quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Board Tip:** The Productivity Index $P_i$ is a **favorite 3-mark question**. If $P_i > 1$, the task was completed faster or better than planned — this is the engineering ideal.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

1. **Regulatory Submissions (FDA 510(k), CDSCO):** Time-managed Gantt charts prevent missed submission windows — failure to file within 90 days results in withdrawal.
2. **Clinical Trials:** Patient recruitment, device fitting, and follow-up visits must be staggered to avoid burnout.
3. **Hospital Equipment Maintenance:** Preventive maintenance scheduling (PM) reduces downtime of ventilators, dialysis machines, and infusion pumps.
4. **Startup Founders:** An assistive-tech founder must allocate time across R&D, fundraising, and clinical validation — typically using a 40-30-30 rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Time Allocation Using Weighted Priority

**Problem Statement:** A biomedical project manager must allocate 8 working hours across four tasks:
- Task A: Calibration report (priority weight $W_A = 5$)
- Task B: Regulatory paperwork (priority weight $W_B = 4$)
- Task C: Team meeting (priority weight $W_C = 2$)
- Task D: Inventory check (priority weight $W_D = 1$)

**Step 1 — Define the general allocation formula:**

$$
T_i = \dfrac{T_{\text{total}} \times W_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of all priority weights:**

$$
\sum W_j = W_A + W_B + W_C + W_D
$$

$$
\sum W_j = 5 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 12
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula to each task:**

$$
T_A = \dfrac{8 \times 5}{12} = \dfrac{40}{12} \approx 3.33 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_B = \dfrac{8 \times 4}{12} = \dfrac{32}{12} \approx 2.67 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_C = \dfrac{8 \times 2}{12} = \dfrac{16}{12} \approx 1.33 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_D = \dfrac{8 \times 1}{12} = \dfrac{8}{12} \approx 0.67 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify that allocations sum back to the total:**

$$
T_A + T_B + T_C + T_D = 3.33 + 2.67 + 1.33 + 0.67
$$

$$
= 8.00 \text{ hours} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Interpretation:** Task A (calibration report) receives the largest time slice because it carries the highest priority weight. Task D (inventory) is squeezed to under 45 minutes, reflecting its low urgency — a classic **Pareto-aligned** allocation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Productivity Index Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A biomedical engineer **planned** 10 hours for writing a regulatory submission and actually **completed** it in **8 hours**, achieving **12 of the 15** planned output units (e.g., document sections). Calculate the Productivity Index $P_i$.

**Step 1 — Recall the productivity formula:**

$$
P_i = \dfrac{O_{\text{achieved}}}{O_{\text{planned}}} \times \dfrac{T_{\text{planned}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the output ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{O_{\text{achieved}}}{O_{\text{planned}}} = \dfrac{12}{15} = 0.8
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the time ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{T_{\text{planned}}}{T_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{10}{8} = 1.25
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both ratios:**

$$
P_i = 0.8 \times 1.25 = 1.0
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$
P_i = 1.0 \implies \text{Baseline productivity (target achieved exactly)}
$$

$$
P_i > 1.0 \implies \text{Over-achievement (faster + more output)}
$$

$$
P_i < 1.0 \implies \text{Under-achievement (slower or less output)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A $P_i = 1.0$ in a regulatory context means the engineer hit the *quality* target but missed the *time-compression* opportunity. World-class biomedical project managers aim for $P_i \in [1.2, 1.5]$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Time-Wasters vs. Time-Savers in Assistive Medical Device Workflows

| Activity Domain | Time-Waster (Quadrant III / IV) | Time-Saver (Quadrant I / II) | KTU-Mapped CO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Device design | Endless CAD re-modelling without freeze | Freeze design at version control milestone | CO4 |
| Documentation | Re-writing same report daily | Use pre-approved ISO 13485 templates | CO4 |
| Meetings | Open-ended, no agenda | Time-boxed (30 min), agenda-circulated | CO4 |
| Patient trials | Ad-hoc scheduling | Staggered, calendar-blocked visits | CO4 |
| Email | Hourly inbox checking | 2 scheduled email windows (10 AM, 4 PM) | CO4 |
| Procurement | Waiting on vendors passively | Pre-approved vendor list with SLAs | CO4 |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Pareto-Weighted Task Selection

**Step 1 —** List all 10 tasks for the week.
**Step 2 —** Estimate impact value $V_i$ for each (scale 1–10).
**Step 3 —** Estimate time cost $t_i$ for each (in hours).
**Step 4 —** Compute efficiency ratio $E_i = V_i / t_i$.
**Step 5 —** Sort tasks in **descending** $E_i$ order.
**Step 6 —** Execute top 20% of tasks first — this delivers ~80% of weekly value.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Eisenhower Matrix Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0["Incoming Task or Activity"]
    T1{"Is it Urgent?"}
    T2{"Is it Important?"}
    Q1["Q1: URGENT and IMPORTANT — DO NOW. Example: ventilator calibration"]
    Q2["Q2: NOT URGENT but IMPORTANT — SCHEDULE. Example: long-term R&D roadmap"]
    Q3["Q3: URGENT but NOT IMPORTANT — DELEGATE. Example: routine email triage"]
    Q4["Q4: NOT URGENT and NOT IMPORTANT — ELIMINATE. Example: redundant meetings"]
    OUT["Document action in time log"]

    T0 --> T1
    T1 -- YES --> T2
    T1 -- NO --> Q4
    T2 -- YES --> Q1
    T2 -- NO --> Q3
    Q1 --> OUT
    Q2 --> OUT
    Q3 --> OUT
    Q4 --> OUT
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Time Management System for Biomedical Projects

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step A: Define SMART Goals"]
    B["Step B: List All Weekly Tasks"]
    C["Step C: Apply ABC or Pareto Weights"]
    D["Step D: Eisenhower Quadrant Sort"]
    E["Step E: Time Block on Calendar"]
    F["Step F: Execute with Pomodoro"]
    G["Step G: Weekly Review and Adjust"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G -. feedback loop .-> A
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Parkinson's Law and Productivity Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Receive Task T"]
    P2["Estimate True Effort E"]
    P3["Set Artificial Deadline D shorter than E"]
    P4["Execute with High Focus"]
    P5{"Deadline Met?"}
    P6["Reward and Record Productivity Index"]
    P7["Analyze Slack Time, Reduce D Further"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 -- YES --> P6
    P5 -- NO --> P7
    P7 --> P3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define Time Management. List any four techniques used for effective time management.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Time Management is the process of planning and exercising control over the amount of time spent on specific activities to increase effectiveness, efficiency, and productivity. In biomedical engineering, it refers to systematically organizing R&D, clinical, regulatory, and patient-care tasks within fixed deadlines.

**Four Techniques:**
1. **Eisenhower Matrix** — classifies tasks by urgency and importance.
2. **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** — focus on the 20% of tasks delivering 80% results.
3. **ABC Analysis** — grades tasks as A (high), B (medium), or C (low) priority.
4. **Pomodoro Technique** — 25-minute focused work blocks with short breaks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to define the term first before listing. Always **define**, then list. Marks are split: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for techniques.

---

**Q2. What is Parkinson's Law? How does it apply to biomedical device project management?** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Parkinson's Law states that *"work expands so as to fill the time available for its completion."*

**Application in Biomedical Device Projects:**
1. If a regulatory dossier is allotted 90 days, engineers unconsciously slow their pace to fill it; setting an artificial 60-day deadline increases focus.
2. In clinical trials, tight patient-recruitment windows force better screening and consent processes.
3. In R&D, allocating a "design freeze" deadline 30% earlier than realistic compels early trade-off resolution.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[A] (a)** Explain the **Eisenhower Matrix** in detail. With a suitable example from the assistive medical device industry, classify the following four activities into the four quadrants and justify your answer:
1. Responding to a Class I device recall notice.
2. Quarterly review of the R&D design pipeline.
3. Replying to a vendor's routine quotation email.
4. Browsing social media during working hours.

**[A] (b)** Discuss the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** with reference to time management in biomedical engineering projects. Compute the time savings if a 200-hour validation cycle is compressed using the Pareto approach.

**Model Solution:**

**[A] (a) — 7 Marks**

**Eisenhower Matrix Explanation (4 marks):**

The Eisenhower Matrix is a 2×2 decision tool introduced by U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower. Tasks are classified on two axes:
- **X-axis: Urgency** (time-sensitive)
- **Y-axis: Importance** (impact on goals)

The four quadrants yield four distinct actions:
- **Q1 (Urgent + Important) → DO:** Crises and hard deadlines.
- **Q2 (Not Urgent + Important) → SCHEDULE:** Strategic, long-term tasks.
- **Q3 (Urgent + Not Important) → DELEGATE:** Interruptions that don't require your expertise.
- **Q4 (Not Urgent + Not Important) → ELIMINATE:** Distractions and time-wasters.

**Classification with Justification (3 marks):**

| Activity | Quadrant | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Class I device recall response | **Q1 — DO NOW** | Life-threatening patient risk; legally mandated; must be executed immediately. |
| 2. Quarterly R&D pipeline review | **Q2 — SCHEDULE** | Strategically vital but not urgent today; allocate a future 2-hour block. |
| 3. Vendor quotation reply | **Q3 — DELEGATE** | Time-sensitive but operationally low value; assign to procurement assistant. |
| 4. Social media browsing | **Q4 — ELIMINATE** | Neither urgent nor important; pure time-waster. |

---

**[A] (b) — 7 Marks**

**Pareto Principle Theory (4 marks):**

The Pareto Principle, named after economist **Vilfredo Pareto**, observes that roughly **80% of consequences come from 20% of causes**. In biomedical engineering:

- 80% of patient functional recovery comes from 20% of the device's core features (e.g., a prosthetic limb's grip strength dominates user satisfaction).
- 80% of the time spent on a regulatory dossier is consumed by 20% of its most complex sections (e.g., clinical evaluation report).

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- Total validation cycle: $T_{\text{total}} = 200$ hours
- Pareto principle: 80% of *value* comes from 20% of *time*

**Step 1 — Time that delivers 80% value:**

$$
T_{80\%} = 0.20 \times T_{\text{total}} = 0.20 \times 200 = 40 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Time savings vs. the original cycle:**

$$
T_{\text{saved}} = T_{\text{total}} - T_{80\%} = 200 - 40 = 160 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage efficiency gain:**

$$
\% \text{ gain} = \dfrac{T_{\text{saved}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{160}{200} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Conclusion:** By focusing exclusively on the high-value 20% of validation tasks, the team captures **80% of the regulatory value** while saving **160 hours** — equating to **4 full working weeks** of engineering time redirected to innovation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[B] (a)** What is **ABC Analysis**? Apply it to allocate time for the following tasks of a biomedical startup founder in an 8-hour workday: investor pitch (weight 4), prototype debugging (weight 5), vendor meeting (weight 2), social media post (weight 1).

**[B] (b)** Define **SMART goals**. Draft a SMART goal for launching an assistive wheelchair prototype and explain each criterion.

**Model Solution:**

**[B] (a) — 7 Marks**

**ABC Analysis Definition (2 marks):**

ABC Analysis is a prioritization technique that classifies tasks into three categories based on their value-to-time ratio:
- **A tasks (high priority, ~60% impact):** Done today.
- **B tasks (medium priority, ~30% impact):** Done this week.
- **C tasks (low priority, ~10% impact):** Deferred or eliminated.

**Task Classification (2 marks):**

| Task | Weight | Category |
|---|---|---|
| Prototype debugging | 5 | **A** |
| Investor pitch | 4 | **A** |
| Vendor meeting | 2 | **B** |
| Social media post | 1 | **C** |

**Time Allocation Using Weighted Formula (3 marks):**

$$
T_i = \dfrac{T_{\text{total}} \times W_i}{\sum W_j}
$$

**Step 1:** $\sum W_j = 5 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 12$

**Step 2:**

$$
T_{\text{prototype}} = \dfrac{8 \times 5}{12} = \dfrac{40}{12} \approx 3.33 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_{\text{pitch}} = \dfrac{8 \times 4}{12} = \dfrac{32}{12} \approx 2.67 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_{\text{vendor}} = \dfrac{8 \times 2}{12} = \dfrac{16}{12} \approx 1.33 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_{\text{social}} = \dfrac{8 \times 1}{12} = \dfrac{8}{12} \approx 0.67 \text{ hours}
$$

**Verification:** $3.33 + 2.67 + 1.33 + 0.67 = 8.00$ hours ✓

---

**[B] (b) — 7 Marks**

**SMART Goals Definition (2 marks):**

**SMART** is a goal-setting framework ensuring objectives are:
- **S** — Specific
- **M** — Measurable
- **A** — Achievable
- **R** — Relevant
- **T** — Time-bound

**SMART Goal for Assistive Wheelchair Prototype (5 marks):**

> *"Design, fabricate, and clinically validate a foldable, motor-assisted assistive wheelchair prototype weighing under 15 kg by **30 November 2025**, achieving a minimum user-satisfaction score of **8/10** across **at least 10** differently-abled test users in the Kochi district."*

**Criterion-wise Explanation:**

| Letter | Criterion | Explanation in this Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Specific | Clearly defines *what* (foldable wheelchair) and *for whom* (differently-abled users). |
| **M** | Measurable | Weight ≤ 15 kg, satisfaction ≥ 8/10, sample ≥ 10 users. |
| **A** | Achievable | Feasible with current 3D-printing and brushless-motor tech. |
| **R** | Relevant | Aligns with KTU Module 4 outcome on assistive device innovation. |
| **T** | Time-bound | Hard deadline of 30 November 2025. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Time Management Questions:**
> 1. Students often **omit the numerical calculation** in Pareto or productivity-index questions, costing 3–4 marks instantly. Always show the full arithmetic.
> 2. Do **not confuse Eisenhower with Pareto** — they are distinct tools. Eisenhower classifies; Pareto prioritizes by value.
> 3. In ABC Analysis, the weight assignment **must be justified**, not arbitrary. Always state why one task outweighs another (e.g., patient safety impact).
> 4. In SMART goals, every letter **must have an explicit example** — vague statements lose marks.
> 5. **Units matter:** Hours ≠ Days. Always declare units in time-allocation problems.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Time Management = planning + control of time for productivity.
- ✅ **Eisenhower Matrix:** 4 quadrants → **DO, SCHEDULE, DELEGATE, ELIMINATE** (most-tested concept).
- ✅ **Pareto Principle:** **80% output from 20% input**; compute savings = $T_{\text{total}} \times 0.8$.
- ✅ **ABC Analysis:** Weights A > B > C; allocate via $T_i = \dfrac{T_{\text{total}} \times W_i}{\sum W_j}$.
- ✅ **Parkinson's Law:** Work fills time → set tight artificial deadlines.
- ✅ **Pomodoro:** 25 min work + 5 min break, repeat 4×, then long break.
- ✅ **SMART Goals:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- ✅ **Productivity Index:** $P_i = \dfrac{O_{\text{achieved}}}{O_{\text{planned}}} \times \dfrac{T_{\text{planned}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$.
- ✅ **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{value-added}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Biomedical relevance:** Always frame examples in device design, regulatory submission, clinical trials, or patient interaction.
- ✅ **Board exam tip:** Numerical questions need full steps; theoretical questions need definitions + 4 distinct points.
- ✅ **CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO4** of PEEBT744 (Professional Skills & Ethics).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prompting/Cueing/Coaching Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prompting/Cueing/Coaching Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prompting Devices (KTU 2024 Definition):** Prompting devices are a category of cognitive assistive technology (CAT) that delivers pre-programmed or context-sensitive stimuli (verbal, visual, auditory, tactile, or multimodal) to initiate, sustain, or guide a user through the sequential steps of an *Activity of Daily Living (ADL)* or *Instrumental Activity of Daily Living (IADL)* when the user's intrinsic memory, executive function, or attentional resources are impaired.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Assistive Medical Devices, prompting/cueing/coaching devices form a **tripartite cognitive support hierarchy**, where each tier represents an escalating level of user autonomy and an escalating level of system intelligence:

| Tier | Mechanism | User Agency | System Role |
|------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Prompting** | Direct instruction ("Now brush your teeth") | Passive executor | Commander |
| **Cueing** | Indirect hint (a sound at medication time) | Active interpreter | Reminder |
| **Coaching** | Adaptive, feedback-driven guidance | Active learner | Collaborator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cognitive Assistive Technology (CAT)** in KTU 2024 syllabus is formally classified as a subset of **Smart Assistive Devices (SAD)**. Prompting/Cueing/Coaching devices are **soft-restraint free**, **non-invasive**, and considered **rehabilitative** rather than palliative.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy for B.Tech Students

Imagine you are writing a long KTU semester examination, and the question paper is extraordinarily complex with seven interlocking sub-parts. What if the invigilator was allowed to whisper the *next sub-part* in your ear only when your eyes lifted from the previous answer? That whispered hint is a **cue**. What if the invigilator instead *read out the entire sub-part to you* — that is a **prompt**. What if the invigilator instead *asked you a guiding question* (e.g., "What formula did you learn in Module 3?") and waited for your reply before proceeding — that is **coaching**.

In every case, the user has the cognitive ability to perform the task, but their internal *executive controller* (the prefrontal cortex, working memory) is either too slow, too easily distracted, or too fatigued to chain the steps together unaided. The external device becomes a **portable executive controller**.

## 1.3 Clinical Population Served

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists the following primary user groups:

- **Dementia of Alzheimer's Type (AD/ADRD)** — most prevalent user base, ~55 million cases globally (WHO, 2023).
- **Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI)** survivors with executive dysfunction.
- **Post-Stroke Aphasia & Apraxia** patients.
- **Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI)** and age-related memory decline.
- **Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities (IDD)** including Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD).
- **Pediatric ADHD** — where coaching devices double as attention trainers.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard clinical metrics used in KTU question papers for evaluating these devices include the **Disability Assessment for Dementia (DAD)**, the **Lawton IADL Scale**, and the **Allen Cognitive Level Screen (ACLS-5)**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

From a **biomedical engineering** perspective, a prompting/cueing/coaching device is essentially a **closed-loop cyber-physical system** with a human-in-the-loop. The system must:

1. **Sense** the user's context (location, time, activity state).
2. **Reason** about whether and how to intervene.
3. **Actuate** the prompt through an appropriate modality.
4. **Learn** from the user's response to optimize future prompts.

This is a textbook example of a **Sensor → Processor → Actuator → Feedback (SPAF)** loop, identical in architecture to a closed-loop drug delivery infusion pump, but with the human cortex as the controlled variable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Tripartite Hierarchy — Operational Definitions

### 2.1.1 Prompting Devices
A prompt is an **explicit, complete instruction** delivered at a specific moment. It is non-negotiable in tone and typically contains a *verb-object* structure (e.g., "Take the white pill"). Prompting devices are the most clinician-driven and are used when the user has **lowest residual cognitive bandwidth**.

**Engineering Subtypes:**
- **Verbal Prompters** — pre-recorded audio (e.g., voice on a smartwatch).
- **Visual Prompters** — pictorial/animated step-by-step instructions on a tablet.
- **Haptic Prompters** — wrist-worn vibration pattern encodings.
- **Audible Beacons** — non-speech sounds (chime, bell) used in ambient prompting environments.

### 2.1.2 Cueing Devices
A cue is an **indirect trigger** that activates a *previously learned* association. The user's own memory does the work; the cue is just a *retrieval cue*. The classic cognitive science principle is **Encoding Specificity**: memory retrieval is most effective when the retrieval context matches the encoding context.

**Engineering Subtypes:**
- **Time-of-Day Cues** (e.g., medication alarm).
- **Location-Based Cues** (e.g., Bluetooth beacon near pillbox).
- **Activity-Based Cues** (e.g., door sensor triggers "Did you lock the door?").
- **Social Cues** (e.g., video call from caregiver).

### 2.1.3 Coaching Devices
A coach **observes, prompts, evaluates, and adapts**. It uses the user's performance history to decide *whether* to step in and *how much* assistance to provide. Coaching is the most sophisticated tier and aligns with the **Vygotskian Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD)** — the system scaffolds the user just enough to perform at the next level, not more, not less.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaffolding Principle (Vygotsky, 1978):** Effective coaching provides *just-in-time, just-enough* assistance. Over-prompting causes **learned helplessness**; under-prompting causes **task abandonment**. The engineering goal is to maintain the user at the *flow state* (Csikszentmihalyi).

## 2.2 Architectural Layers of a Modern Prompting/Cueing/Coaching Device

A KTU-style block diagram should always be drawn with these four layers:

**Layer 1 — Context Acquisition Layer (Sensors):**
- PIR motion sensors, door contact sensors, RFID tag readers, smartwatches, computer vision (pose estimation, object detection), indoor positioning (UWB, BLE beacons), microphone (voice activity detection).

**Layer 2 — Cognitive State Inference Layer (Processor):**
- Rule-based expert systems, Bayesian networks, fuzzy logic controllers, Hidden Markov Models (HMMs), and increasingly, on-device TinyML models (e.g., TensorFlow Lite Micro).

**Layer 3 — Prompt Generation Layer (Actuator):**
- Text-to-Speech (TTS) engines, LCD/OLED displays, vibration motors, smart speakers, augmented-reality glasses (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens in research prototypes).

**Layer 4 — Feedback & Adaptation Layer:**
- Touch/gesture response, voice confirmation, computer-vision check of task completion, supervised reinforcement learning.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Principle | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$ | RT = reaction time (s), n = number of choices | Determines the maximum number of prompt options to present without overwhelming the user. Typical: $n \leq 4$ for dementia. |
| 2 | **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$ | MT = movement time (s), D = distance to target, W = target width | Sizes touchscreen buttons on prompting tablets. |
| 3 | **Steering Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \frac{L}{W}$ | T = time to traverse a tunnel, L = tunnel length, W = tunnel width | Designs guided-path confirmation workflows. |
| 4 | **Forgetting Curve (Ebbinghaus):** $R = e^{-t/S}$ | R = retention, t = elapsed time, S = memory strength | Calculates optimal prompt timing. S is empirically fit; for AD patients S $\approx$ 8-15 minutes. |
| 5 | **Spaced Repetition Interval:** $I_{n+1} = I_{n} \cdot EF$ | I = inter-prompt interval, EF = easiness factor (typically 1.3-2.5) | Optimizes coaching repetition schedules. |
| 6 | **Task Completion Time:** $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{step,i} + T_{prompt,i} + T_{ack,i})$ | All in seconds, N = number of steps | Models end-to-end ADL completion time. |
| 7 | **Cognitive Load (Sweller):** $CL = a \cdot IR + b \cdot ER + c \cdot GR$ | IR = intrinsic load, ER = extraneous load, GR = germane load | Informs whether to switch prompt modality to reduce extraneous load. |
| 8 | **Precision/Recall for Activity Recognition:** $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP},\; R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | TP/FP/FN from confusion matrix | Evaluates context-sensing layer performance. |
| 9 | **Battery Energy Budget:** $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | E in Joules (or Wh), V in Volts, I in Amps, t in seconds | Sizes battery for wearable prompters (typical 3.7 V Li-Po, 400 mAh for 7-day standby). |
| 10 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio for Audio Cues:** $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | SNR in dB | Sets volume for auditory prompts; minimum **15 dB above ambient noise** for dementia users. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Threshold for Audible Cues in KTU problems:** SNR $\geq$ **15 dB** is the published minimum for users with age-related hearing loss (presbycusis). For haptic cues, the device should produce $\geq$ **1.3 g peak acceleration** at the wrist to be perceived without being startling.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These systems are deployed in:
- **Smart Homes** for aging-in-place (e.g., MIT's PlaceLab, ORCATECH).
- **Rehabilitation Robotics** (e.g., the COACH system, University of Toronto).
- **Hospital Discharge Programs** to reduce 30-day readmission in CHF and COPD patients.
- **Workplace Accommodations** for TBI survivors returning to employment.
- **Special Education Classrooms** for students with ASD (e.g., visual schedule boards, Proloquo2Go).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Prompt Timing from the Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Framework (KTU-style):** A clinician wants to deliver a prompt to a patient to take antihypertensive medication. The patient's memory strength for this task has been clinically estimated as $S = 12$ minutes. At what elapsed time $t$ should the device fire its first prompt so that retention is exactly 50%?

**Step 1 — Recall the Ebbinghaus retention function:**

$$
R(t) = e^{-t/S}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given memory strength $S = 12$ minutes:**

$$
R(t) = e^{-t/12}
$$

**Step 3 — Impose the design constraint $R(t) = 0.5$ (50% retention is the standard KTU engineering trigger threshold for non-critical prompts):**

$$
0.5 = e^{-t/12}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$
\ln(0.5) = -\frac{t}{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $\ln(0.5)$:**

$$
\ln(0.5) = -0.6931
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$
-0.6931 = -\frac{t}{12}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $t$:**

$$
t = 0.6931 \times 12 = 8.318 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering rounding and clinical interpretation:**

$$
t \approx 8.3 \text{ minutes} \approx 8 \text{ min } 19 \text{ s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For an Alzheimer's patient with a typical memory strength of $S = 8$ minutes, the same 50%-retention prompt would fire at $t = 5.5$ minutes. This is the quantitative basis for *context-sensitive* prompting: as the disease progresses, the system shortens the prompt interval automatically.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sizing a Touchscreen Button Using Fitts' Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Framework:** You are designing a one-button "I have taken my pill" confirmation screen for a dementia user. The user's finger-to-button distance is $D = 60$ mm. The Kinect-based study by Tien et al. (2013) found that a target width of at least $W = 40$ mm is required for a 90% hit rate in cognitively impaired users. Using Fitts' law with $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit, calculate the expected movement time.

**Step 1 — Write Fitts' law:**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $D = 60$ mm and $W = 40$ mm:**

$$
MT = 50 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 60}{40}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the argument of the logarithm:**

$$
\frac{2 \times 60}{40} = \frac{120}{40} = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\log_{2}(3)$:**

$$
\log_{2}(3) = \frac{\ln(3)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{1.0986}{0.6931} \approx 1.585 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by the slope $b$:**

$$
150 \times 1.585 = 237.75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Add the intercept $a$:**

$$
MT = 50 + 237.75 = 287.75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 7 — Express in seconds:**

$$
MT \approx 0.288 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** for design reporting:**

$$
ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) = 1.585 \text{ bits}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A movement time of $\sim$ 0.29 s is within the **HCI norm of 0.3-0.6 s** for tablet taps. The index of difficulty of 1.585 bits is comfortably below the **7-bit limit** (per the Fitts' law usability theorem, MacKenzie, 1992). If $W$ were reduced to 20 mm, ID would rise to 2.585 bits and miss rates would increase for cognitively impaired users.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hick's Law for Prompt Menu Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Framework:** A smartwatch prompts a user to confirm a completed ADL. Designers are debating between a 2-option screen ("Yes" / "No") and a 4-option screen ("Yes" / "No" / "Repeat Instructions" / "Call Caregiver"). Using Hick's law with $a = 100$ ms and $b = 150$ ms, compare the decision times.

**Step 1 — State Hick's law:**

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)
$$

**Step 2 — Case A: 2 options, $n = 2$:**

$$
RT_{A} = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}(2+1) = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}(3)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\log_{2}(3) \approx 1.585$:**

$$
RT_{A} = 100 + 150 \times 1.585 = 100 + 237.75 = 337.75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Case B: 4 options, $n = 4$:**

$$
RT_{B} = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}(4+1) = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}(5)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $\log_{2}(5) \approx 2.322$:**

$$
RT_{B} = 100 + 150 \times 2.322 = 100 + 348.3 = 448.3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage increase:**

$$
\Delta RT = \frac{448.3 - 337.75}{337.75} \times 100\% \approx 32.7\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** Increasing options from 2 to 4 raises decision time by ~33%. For dementia users, the additional choice may also raise *intrinsic cognitive load* (Sweller's framework) and trigger confusion. **KTU design recommendation: keep $n \leq 2$ for AD users; $n \leq 4$ for MCI users.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Tiers

| Analytical Axis | Prompting | Cueing | Coaching |
|-----------------|-----------|--------|----------|
| **User Initiation** | None (device leads) | Partial (user interprets) | Full (user + system co-lead) |
| **Cognitive Demand on User** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **System Complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest (ML required) |
| **Adaptivity** | None | Low | High |
| **Risk of Learned Helplessness** | High | Low | Lowest |
| **Typical Cost (USD, 2024)** | $50-$300 | $100-$800 | $1000-$5000+ |
| **Battery Power (Wearable)** | Low | Low | Medium-High |
| **Example Device** | Picture-based ADL board | Mediplanner clock | COACH, Autominder, Guide |
| **KTU Syllabus Section** | 4.1.1 | 4.1.2 | 4.1.3 |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Battery Energy Budget for a Wearable Prompter

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Framework:** A wrist-worn prompter uses a 3.7 V Li-Po battery rated 400 mAh. The device draws $I = 25$ mA average current (mix of display, BLE radio, vibration motor). How many days of continuous use can the patient expect before recharge?

**Step 1 — Convert battery capacity from mAh to coulombs (As):**

$$
Q = 400 \text{ mAh} = 0.4 \text{ Ah} = 0.4 \times 3600 = 1440 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to energy in watt-hours:**

$$
E = V \times Q = 3.7 \text{ V} \times 0.4 \text{ Ah} = 1.48 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute device power consumption:**

$$
P = V \times I = 3.7 \times 0.025 = 0.0925 \text{ W} = 92.5 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute runtime in hours:**

$$
t = \frac{E}{P} = \frac{1.48 \text{ Wh}}{0.0925 \text{ W}} = 16 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to days (assuming 16 h/day active use):**

$$
t = \frac{16 \text{ h/day}}{16 \text{ h}} = 1 \text{ day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This naive calculation gives only 1 day of runtime. In practice, duty-cycling the radio and using an OLED display (rather than LCD) extends runtime to **5-7 days**. KTU 2024 problems often require students to *propose* such duty-cycling to demonstrate engineering optimization.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — A Smart Prompting/Cueing/Coaching Device

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete **Sensor → Processor → Actuator → Feedback (SPAF)** architecture, including the **Cognitive State Inference Engine** and the **Adaptation Layer** that distinguish coaching devices from mere prompters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[USER ACTIVITY LAYER\nPhysical Movements and ADL Steps] --> A2[CONTEXT SENSORS\nPIR Door Contact RFID BLE UWB CV Mic]
    A2 --> B1[SIGNAL CONDITIONING\nFiltering Amplification ADC]
    B1 --> B2[FEATURE EXTRACTION\nMotion Vectors Audio Cues Object Labels]
    B2 --> C1[COGNITIVE STATE INFERENCE\nBayesian HMM Fuzzy Logic TinyML]
    C1 --> C2[DECISION ENGINE\nShould System Intervene]
    C2 -->|Yes| D1[PROMPT SELECTOR\nChoose Modality and Content]
    C2 -->|No| A1
    D1 --> E1[VISUAL ACTUATOR\nTablet OLED AR Glasses]
    D1 --> E2[AUDITORY ACTUATOR\nSpeaker TTS Voice]
    D1 --> E3[HAPTIC ACTUATOR\nVibration Motor Buzzer]
    E1 --> F1[USER RESPONSE\nTouch Voice Gesture]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[OUTCOME EVALUATOR\nTask Completed Yes or No]
    G1 -->|Yes| H1[POSITIVE FEEDBACK\nPraise and Log]
    G1 -->|No| H2[REPROMPT WITH HIGHER ASSISTANCE\nScaffolded Prompt]
    H1 --> I1[ADAPTATION LAYER\nUpdate User Model]
    H2 --> I1
    I1 --> C1
    H2 --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The two feedback loops are what differentiate a *coaching* device from a mere prompter. A prompter exits the loop after the prompt is delivered; a coach observes the response, evaluates success, and re-enters the cognitive state inference engine with updated beliefs about the user. This is the engineering embodiment of the **closed-loop learning paradigm** in rehabilitation engineering.

---

## 4.2 State Machine for Prompt Modality Escalation

This diagram models how a coaching device *escalates* the level of assistance when a user fails to respond. The state machine follows the **Least-to-Most Prompting (LTM)** hierarchy used in special education and adapted for dementia care.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STATE_A_IDLE
    STATE_A_IDLE --> STATE_B_CUE: Trigger Fires
    STATE_B_CUE --> STATE_C_PROMPT: No Response in 15 s
    STATE_C_PROMPT --> STATE_D_VERBAL: No Response in 15 s
    STATE_D_VERBAL --> STATE_E_COACH: No Response in 15 s
    STATE_E_COACH --> STATE_F_ESCALATE: No Response in 30 s
    STATE_F_ESCALATE --> STATE_G_CAREGIVER: No Response in 60 s
    STATE_B_CUE --> STATE_H_SUCCESS: User Acknowledges
    STATE_C_PROMPT --> STATE_H_SUCCESS: User Acknowledges
    STATE_D_VERBAL --> STATE_H_SUCCESS: User Acknowledges
    STATE_E_COACH --> STATE_H_SUCCESS: User Acknowledges
    STATE_H_SUCCESS --> [*]
    STATE_G_CAREGIVER --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Each state has a *timeout window* (15-60 s). The LTM (Least-to-Most) hierarchy respects user dignity by first trying a *gentle* non-speech cue before escalating to a *direct verbal prompt* and finally to a *caregiver alert*. The timer values are typical KTU exam parameters.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between prompting, cueing, and coaching in the context of cognitive assistive devices. Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Prompting** is the delivery of a direct, complete, time-stamped instruction that requires minimal interpretation by the user. The user is the passive executor. *Engineering example:* a smart speaker that announces "Take your morning pill now" at 8:00 AM.
- **Cueing** is the delivery of an indirect signal that relies on the user's intact associative memory to recall the correct behavior. The user is the active interpreter. *Engineering example:* a wristwatch vibration at medication time, with no spoken content.
- **Coaching** is the delivery of adaptive, feedback-driven guidance that modifies its behavior based on the user's response history. The user and the system *co-lead* the activity. *Engineering example:* the COACH system (Cognitive Orthosis for Assisting aCtivities in the Home) developed at the University of Toronto, which uses computer vision to observe handwashing and offers progressively more specific verbal cues.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three design considerations that an engineer must address while designing a prompting device for a dementia patient.**

**Model Answer (any three):**

1. **Modality Selection:** The output modality (visual, auditory, haptic) must be matched to the user's residual sensory and cognitive capacity. Multi-modal prompts (e.g., vibration + voice) increase compliance by ~30% in AD patients.
2. **Timing and Frequency:** Prompts must follow the Ebbinghaus retention curve; over-prompting causes agitation, under-prompting causes task abandonment.
3. **Cognitive Load:** The prompt text must be short, in the user's native language, and free of jargon (Hick's law: $n \leq 2$ options).
4. **Failure Recovery:** The system must have a clear escalation pathway to a human caregiver when the user does not respond within a defined window.
5. **Privacy and Dignity:** Devices must be *opt-in* and not stigmatize the user in social settings (e.g., wearables that look like ordinary watches).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** write answers that are purely clinical or psychological. KTU 2024 Scheme marks are heavily weighted toward **engineering analysis** (sensor selection, signal processing, control loop design, power budgeting). A purely descriptive answer typically scores **5-6 out of 14**.
> 2. **Do not** confuse prompting with cueing in your diagrams. Always label the *modality* and the *trigger condition* explicitly.
> 3. **Do not** omit the *feedback loop*. A coaching device that does not adapt to user response is, by definition, merely a prompter, and you will lose 3-4 marks.
> 4. **Do not** state formulas without defining the variables and units. KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks for undefined variables.
> 5. **Always** include a labeled block diagram of your proposed system; this fetches **2-3 marks** even if the rest of the answer is average.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labeled block diagram, describe the architecture of a wearable prompting device for an Alzheimer's patient. Explain the role of the *context-sensing layer*, the *decision engine*, and the *actuator layer*. List at least three sensors and three actuators you would use, justifying each choice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A clinician prescribes a memory aid to remind a patient to drink water every $T$ minutes. The patient's memory strength for this task is $S = 10$ minutes. Using the Ebbinghaus retention function $R(t) = e^{-t/S}$, determine the maximum allowable inter-prompt interval $T$ such that retention is at least 70%. State your answer in minutes and seconds. If the device battery is rated $3.7$ V, $600$ mAh, and the average current draw is $30$ mA, calculate the runtime in hours.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram and architecture (7 marks):**

The architecture follows the SPAF (Sensor-Processor-Actuator-Feedback) loop. The student should present a diagram with the following four layers:

- **Layer 1 — Context Sensing Layer:** Three sensors minimum.
  1. **PIR Motion Sensor** (e.g., Panasonic EKMC) — detects presence in a room. *Justification:* low-power (≤ 1 mA), low-cost, ubiquitous.
  2. **BLE Beacon Receiver (e.g., Nordic nRF52832)** — indoor positioning. *Justification:* triangulation accuracy ≤ 1.5 m, integrates with the smartphone.
  3. **9-DOF IMU (e.g., Bosch BMI270)** — wrist motion, gait pattern. *Justification:* detects if the user is walking or stationary.

- **Layer 2 — Decision Engine (Processor):** A microcontroller (e.g., STM32L4) running a fuzzy-logic rule base, e.g.,
  - IF time = 10:00 AND location = kitchen THEN prompt "Drink water".
  - IF user has not moved for 60 min THEN prompt "Stand and walk".

- **Layer 3 — Actuator Layer:** Three actuators minimum.
  1. **OLED Display (0.96 inch, 128x64)** — for visual prompts.
  2. **Miniature Speaker (8 ohm, 0.5 W) with TTS** — for verbal prompts.
  3. **Eccentric Rotating Mass (ERM) motor** — for haptic vibration cues.

- **Layer 4 — Feedback Layer:** Touch/button confirmation, voice activity detection.

Marks breakdown: Diagram 3, sensor list 2, actuator list 2.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the retention constraint:**

$$
R(T) = e^{-T/S} \geq 0.7
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $S = 10$ min:**

$$
e^{-T/10} \geq 0.7
$$

**Step 3 — Take natural log (note: both sides are positive and $\ln$ is monotonic):**

$$
-\frac{T}{10} \geq \ln(0.7)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $\ln(0.7)$:**

$$
\ln(0.7) = -0.3567
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
-\frac{T}{10} \geq -0.3567
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by -1 (flip inequality):**

$$
\frac{T}{10} \leq 0.3567
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $T$:**

$$
T \leq 3.567 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 8 — Express in minutes and seconds:**

$$
T_{max} = 3 \text{ min } + 0.567 \times 60 \text{ s} = 3 \text{ min } 34 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 9 — Energy budget for the battery:**

$$
E = V \times Q = 3.7 \text{ V} \times 0.6 \text{ Ah} = 2.22 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 10 — Device power:**

$$
P = V \times I = 3.7 \times 0.030 = 0.111 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 11 — Runtime:**

$$
t = \frac{E}{P} = \frac{2.22}{0.111} = 20 \text{ h}
$$

**Final Answers:**

- $T_{max} \approx 3$ min $34$ s.
- Runtime $\approx 20$ hours (less than 1 day; duty cycling is recommended).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **COACH system** (Cognitive Orthosis for Assisting aCtivities in the Home). Describe its prompting strategy, the role of computer vision, and how it differs from a traditional audio prompter. Mention the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** formulation used to choose the next prompt.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A touchscreen-based prompting tablet has a 7-inch display (diagonal 17.78 cm, 16:9 aspect ratio). The horizontal resolution is 1024 pixels. The design team must place a "Confirm Task Done" button that is at least $W = 35$ mm wide. The finger-to-button distance is $D = 45$ mm. Using Fitts' law with $a = 60$ ms and $b = 170$ ms, calculate the index of difficulty, the expected movement time, and recommend whether the button is large enough for a 90% acceptance rate among cognitively impaired users (reference threshold: ID $\leq$ 2 bits).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) COACH System (7 marks):**

The COACH system was developed at the University of Toronto by **Mihailidis, Boger, Craig, and Hoey** (circa 2008). It assists dementia patients with **handwashing**, a multi-step ADL. Key engineering features:

1. **Computer Vision Module:** A ceiling-mounted camera tracks the user's hand, the soap dispenser, the faucet, and the towel. A marker-based or deep-learning-based pose estimator infers the *current step* in the handwashing sequence (e.g., "turning on tap", "soaping hands", "rinsing").
2. **Audio Prompting Module:** A speaker delivers verbal cues tailored to the inferred step.
3. **Decision Engine — Markov Decision Process (MDP):** The state $S$ is the *inferred activity step*; the action $A$ is the *type of prompt* (none / generic / specific). The reward $R$ is positive when the user completes the step, negative when the user needs repeated prompting. The policy $\pi(s)$ selects the *minimum-necessary* prompt that maximizes expected long-term reward. This is the formal embodiment of the **least-to-most** prompting philosophy.
4. **Adaptation:** The system uses **Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP)** extensions to handle sensor noise and uncertainty in the user's state.

Differences from a traditional audio prompter:
- COACH is **context-aware** (vision-driven), not time-based.
- COACH is **adaptive** (policy updates with experience).
- COACH is **multi-modal** but vision-centric; traditional prompters are typically audio-only.

**(b) Numerical Fitts' Law problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Index of Difficulty (ID):**

$$
ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 45}{35}\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{90}{35}\right) = \log_{2}(2.571)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\log_{2}(2.571)$:**

$$
\log_{2}(2.571) = \frac{\ln(2.571)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{0.9445}{0.6931} \approx 1.363 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute movement time:**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 60 + 170 \times 1.363 = 60 + 231.71 = 291.71 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with threshold:**

$$
ID = 1.363 \text{ bits} \leq 2.0 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

The button design is **adequate** for cognitively impaired users. The ID of 1.363 bits is well within the recommended 2-bit threshold, and the expected movement time of 291.71 ms is below the 600-ms HCI acceptance boundary. The width $W = 35$ mm corresponds to approximately $35 / 0.423 = 82.7$ pixels, which the design team can round up to **84 pixels** for a safety margin.

**Final Answers:**

- ID $\approx$ 1.36 bits.
- MT $\approx$ 0.29 s.
- Button design is **recommended**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Prompting/Cueing/Coaching Devices**

- **Three-Tier Hierarchy:** Prompt (direct) → Cue (indirect) → Coach (adaptive). Memorize one example of each.
- **SPAF Loop:** All modern systems follow **Sensor → Processor → Actuator → Feedback**.
- **Key Sensors:** PIR, BLE beacon, IMU, camera, microphone.
- **Key Actuators:** OLED/LCD, speaker with TTS, ERM/LRA vibration motor.
- **Ebbinghaus Equation:** $R(t) = e^{-t/S}$ — used to compute prompt timing.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(2D/W)$ — used to size buttons and confirm UI affordances.
- **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$ — used to limit menu options ($\leq 2$ for AD).
- **Least-to-Most Prompting (LTM):** Cue → Prompt → Verbal → Coach → Caregiver. Always respect user dignity.
- **Mnemonic for Modality Hierarchy (highest acceptance first):** **VAH** = Visual → Auditory → Haptic. Multi-modal is best.
- **COACH System:** Computer vision + MDP/POMDP for handwashing in dementia.
- **Battery Sizing:** Always use $E = VIt$ with realistic duty-cycling assumptions.
- **Threshold Values to Memorize:** SNR $\geq$ 15 dB for audio; vibration $\geq$ 1.3 g; Fitts' ID $\leq$ 2 bits; Hick's $n \leq 2$ for AD.
- **Clinical Scales:** DAD, Lawton IADL, ACLS-5.
- **Failure Recovery:** Every prompting system must have an explicit *escalation to caregiver* state.
- **Ethical Principle:** Devices are *opt-in*, *dignity-preserving*, and *non-stigmatizing*.
- **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse the prompting tier with the cueing tier in diagrams; always label triggers and modalities.
